'To meet the needs of severe environments, difficult installation conditions,
'and/or high temperatures as typified by pipelines, offshore driling platforms,
tunnels, dams, nuclear containment vessels, steam lines, gas turbines, etc, strain
gages enclosed permanently in protective metal housings which are attached by
spot welding to the specimen have been developed." These gages incorporate a
'umber of unique feature (see Fig. 416). The strain filament and heavier (0007-
in diameter) leadout wire ate of untized construction with a carefully controlled
taper: this minimizes joint fatigue problems and erratic electric connections.
Active and dummy filaments are in close proximity; the dummy gage experiences
'no strain since the helix angle of its winding matches Poisson's ratio. Both
filaments are encased in a strain tube made by welding a tubular shell to a fat
flange. The filament is mechanically coupled to the strain tube, but electrically
isolated from it by highly compacted magnesium oxide powder, by using a high-
speed centrifuge and swaging operation. When the Mange is spot-welded to the
Altech Co. iyo Indust. Cat

specimen, the specimen strain is faithfully transmitted to the strain tube and in
turn by the powder to the gage filament for both tensile and compressive stresses
'Our next device is not a strain gage, but is included here because ofits use in
'experimental stress and failure analysis and the increasing importance of fracture
mechanics methods. In such studies. the measurement in standard test specimens,
of erack length and growth with time is of great interest. The KRAK-GAGE!
Uses a bonded thin metal foi a in ordinary strain gages, but of a geometry suited
to the particular fracturesmechanics specimen under study. A constant current is
supplied to the gage terminals. As the crack grows, the gage resistance increases,
producing a voltage which increases linearly with crack length. Gages with full-
Scale lengths of 5 to 100 mm are available. Another non-strain-gage device that
seems appropriate to mention here is the fatigueslfe gage." This gage appears
similar to @ bonded-fil strain gage and is manufactured by similar processes;
however, its electrical behavior is quite different. For cyclic straining of a fixed
amplitude, gage resistance permanently and irreversibly (but not linearly) in-
creases with each cycle until fatigue failure occurs. The starting resistance is
usually 100.0, while the end-oflfe resistance is about 108 0. Ifthe strain ampli-
tude is increased, the resistance change is greater per cycle. Applications of this
device to fatigue-falure studies are discussed in Dorsey.
Differential Transformers
Figure 417 shows schematic and circuit diagrams for translational and rotational
linear variable differetial-transformer (LVDT) displacement pickups. The exci
tation of such devices is normally a sinusoidal voltage of 3 to 15 V rms amplitude
and frequency of 60 to 20.000 Hz. The two identical secondary coils have induced
in them sinusoidal voltages of the same frequency as the excitation; however. the
'amplitude varies with the position of the iron core. When the secondaries are
connected in series opposition, a null postion exists (x, 20) at which the net
'output e, is esentially zero, Motion of the core from null then causes a larger
mutual inductance (coupling) for one coil and a smaller mutual inductance for
the other, and the amplitude of ¢, becomes a nearly linear function of core
position for a considerable range either side of null, The voltage <, undergoes a
TAO phase shift in going through null. The output ¢, is generally out of phase
with the excitation ¢,.: however, this varies with the frequency of ¢,.. and for
'each diferentia transformer there exists a particular frequeney (numerical value
Supplied by the manufacturer! at which this phase shift is zero. Ifthe differential
transformer is used with some readout system that fequites a small phase shit
between ¢, and ¢,, (some carrier-amplifier systems require this. excitation at the
correct frequency can solve this problem. Ifthe output voltage is applied directly
{oan ac meter of an oscilloscope, this phase shift is not a problem,

Since the frequency response of (eJe,.Kio) has a phase angle of +90 deg at low
frequencies and -90" at high, somewhere in between it will be zero, as mentioned
earlier. I, for some reason, the excitation frequency cannot be adjusted to this
Nalue, the same effect may be achieved for a given frequency by one of the
methods! shown in Fig. 4.19,
While the output voltage atthe null postion is ideally zero, harmonics in the
'excitation voltage and stray capacitance coupling between the primary and sec-
'ondary usually result in a small but nonzero null voltage. Under usual conditions
this is less than 1 percent of the full-scale output voltage and may be quite
acceptable. Methods of reducing this null when itis objectionable are availabe
First, the preferred connection shown in Fig. 420a should be used if a balanced
(center-apped) excitation-voltage source is available. The grounding shown tends
to reduce capacitance-coupling effects. Ifa eentertapped voltage source is not
available, the arrangement of Fig. 4.20b can be used. With the core at the null
postion and the output-measuring device connected, the potentiometer is ad-
usted until the minimum null reading is obtained. The values of R and Ry are
hot critical, but should be as low as possible without loading (drawing excessive
'current from) the excitation source

'The output of a differential transformer is a sine wave whose amplitude is
Proportional to the core motion. If this output is applied to an ac voltmeter the
meter reading can be directly calibrated in motion units, This arrangement is
Perfectly satisfactory for measurement of static or very slowly varying displace.
'ments, except that the meter wil give exactly the same reading for displacements
'of equal amount on either side of the null since the meter is not sensitive to the
180" phase change at null. Thus we cannot tell 0 which side of null the reading
applies without some independent check. Furthermore if rapid core motions are
to be measured, the meter cannot follow or record them, and an oscillograph oF
'oscilloscope must be used as a readout device. These instruments record the
actual waveform of the output as an amplitude-modulated sine wave, which is
'usually undesirable. What is desired is an output-voltage record that looks like
the mechanical motion being measured. To achieve the desired results, demodu-
lation and filtering must be performed; if itis necessary to detect unambiguously
the motions on both sides of nul, the demodulation must be phase-sensitive
Many different circuits are available for performing these operations. We show
here only one arrangement, which is quite simple. To use this approach, all four
'output leads of the LVDT' must be accessible some have the series-opposition
connection internal to the case and would thus not be applicable to the following,
discussion).
Figure 421a shows the circuit arrangement for phase-sensitive demodulation
using semiconductor diodes. Ideally, these pass current only in one direction;
thus when fis positive and e is negative, the current path is efgedhe, while when J
i negative and e positive, the path is ehdgfe. The current through R is therefore
always from c tod. A similar situation exists in the lower diode bridge. For static
or very slowly varying core displacements, the voltage e, may be applied directly
toa de voltmeter, The meter will at as an electromechanical low-pass filter, with
'the needle assuming a position corresponding to the average value ofthe rectified
sine wave e,. If motions both sides of null are to be measured, a meter with zero
in the center of the scale will eliminate the need for switching lead wites when e,
'oes negative. When rapid core motions are to be measured, this de meter ar
angement is useless since the meter movement cannot follow variations more
rapid than about 1 Hz. Itis then necessary to connect e, of Fig. 42tc to the input
'of a low-pass filter which will pass the frequencies present in x, but reject all those
(higher) frequencies produced by the modulation process. The design of such a
filter is eased by making the LVDT excitation frequency much higher than the x,
frequencies.
Ifa frequency ratio of 10: 1 or more is feasible, a simple RC filter as in Fig.
4.220 may be adequate. The output of this filter then becomes the input to an
oscillograph or oscilloscope. For example, suppose we wish to measure tran.
sient x, whose Fourier transform has dropped to insignificant magnitude forall
frequencies higher than 1,000 Hz. Suppose also an LVDT system with an exct-
ation frequency of 1,000 He is available. The frequencies produced by the modu
lation process will thus lie in the band 19,000 to 21,000 Hz. Suppose that we
desire the "ripple" due to frequencies at 19,000 Hz and higher to be no more

"The fullrange stroke of commercially available translational LYDTs ranges
{rom about +£0005 to about +3 in, with other sizes available as specials. The
nonlinearity of standard units is of the order of 0.5 percent of full scale, with 0.1
percent possible by selection. Sensitivity with normal excitation voltage of 3 0 6
Vis of the order of 06 to 30 mY per 0.001 in, depending on frequency of
excitation (higher frequency gives more sensitivity) and stroke (smaller strokes
Usually have higher sensitivity) Some special units have sensitivity as high as 1 to
415 V per 0.001 in, Since the coupling variation due to core motion is a con-
tinuous phenomenon, the resolution of LVDTS is infinitesimal, Amplification of
the output voltage allows detection of motions down to a few microinches. There
is no physical contact between the core and the col form: thus there is no friction
for wear. There are, however, small radial and longitudinal magnetic forees on the
ore if tis not centered radially and at the null position. These ate in the nature
'of magnetic "spring" forces in that they inetease with motion from the equilib
'um point. They are rarely more than 0.1g to 0.3g and thus are often negligible
Rotary LVDTs have a nonlinearity of about +1 percent of full scale for travel of
40" and £3 percent for +60. The sensitivity is of the order of 10 t0 20
mV/deg The moving mass (core) of LVDTs is quite small, ranging from less than
'ug in small units to $9 oF more in larger ones. There isa small radial clearance
(air gap) between the core and the hole in which it moves. Motion in the radial
tection produces a small output signal, but this undesirable transverse sens
tivity is usually less than I percent of the longitudinal sensitivity

'The dynamic response of LVDTs is limited mainly by the excitation fre-
quency, sinoe it must be much higher than the core-motion frequencies $0 as to
'be able to distinguish between them in the amplitude-modulated output signal
For adequate demodulation and filtering, a frequency ratio much less than 10 deg 1
presents problems. Since few differential transformers are designed to be excited
bby more than 20,000 H, the useful range of motion frequencies is limited 10
about 2.000 Hz. This is adequate for many applications. Although the trans-
former principle of the LVDT clearly requires ac excitation, so-called DCDTs are
Available from manufacturers. The "DC part of the terminology refers to the
fact thatthe user applies only a de excitation to the device. The apparent discrep-
ancy is resolved when we discover that microelectronic technology has allowed us
'to build into the transducer case a complete electronic system including oscillator
(produces ac excitation from de power), demodulator, amplifier, and low-pass
filter (See Fig. 423), LVDTs also have been combined with pneumatic servo-
'mechanisms to achieve a noncontacting displacement probe (see Fig. 4.60) LVDT-
to-digital converters (similar to the synchro/digital converter of Fig. 427) are also
available."

'Synchros and Induction Potentiometers
'The term "synchro" is applied toa family of ac electromechanical devices which,
in various forms, perform the functions of angle measurement, voltage and/or
angle addition and subtraction, remote angle transmission, and computation of
rectangular components of vectors. In tis section we are concerned with only the
Angle-measuring function; equipment for performing the other functions is
'covered in later appropriate chapters
'Synchros for angle measurement are most utilized as components of servo
mechanisms (automatic motion-contrl feedback systems) whete they are used 10
measure and compare the actual rotational position of a load with its com-
'manded position, as in Fig. 424, To perform this function, two different types of
Synchros the contro transmitter and the control transformer, are used. The error
'oltage signal e, is an a¢ voltage of the same frequency as the excitation and of
amplitude proportional (for small error angles) to the error angle Oy ~ Oy. Its
phase changes by 180 deg at the null point; thus the direction of the error is de~
tected, When Oy = Op, the error voltage (and thus the amplifier output and motor
input) is zero and the system stays at rest. Ifa command rotation Uy is now put
'4 Dand the motor will rotate so as to return Oy to correspondence with Oy
1 physical constructions of the control transmitter and control transformer
are identical except that the transmitter has a salient-pole ("dumbbell") rotor
'while the transformer has a eylindrial rotor. The construction is similar to that

of a wound-rotor induction motor. Figure 42Sa shows the coil arrangement of
the transmitter alone. Basically, rotation ofthe rotor changes the mutual induct-
'ance (coupling) between the rotor coil and the stator coils, Fora given stator coil,
the open-circuit output voltage i sinusoidal in time and varies in amplitude with
olor position, also sinusoidally as shown in Fig. 4.25b, The three voltage signals
from the stator coils uniquely define the angular position of the rotor. When
these three voltages are applied to the stator coils of a control transformer, they
produce a resultant magnetomotive force aligned in the same direction as that of
the transmitter rotor. The rotor of the transformer acts as a "search coil" in
detecting the direction ofits stator field. If the axis of this coil is aligned with the
field, the maximum voltage is induced into the transformer rotor coil. Ifthe axis
is perpendicular to the field, 2ero voltage is induced, giving the null position
'mentioned above. The output-voltage amplitude actualy varies sinusoidally with
'the misalignment angle, but for small angles the sine and the angle are nearly
equal, giving a linear output,
{Im an induction potentiometer, there is one winding on the rotor and one on
the stator. (Additional dummy windings are sometimes used to improve accuracy,
however) Both these windings are concentrated; thus for simplicity we show

them as single-turn coils in Fig. 4.26. The primary winding (rotor is excited with
alternating current. This induces a voltage into the secondary (stator). The ampli-
tude of this output voltage varies with the mutual inductance (coupling) between
the two coils, and this varies with the angle of rotation. For single-turn (con-
'centrated) coils the variation with angle would be sinusoidal and only a small
Tinear range around null would be obtained. By carcfully distributing the rotor
nd stator windings linear relation for up to +90" rotation may be obtained,
While synchros and induction pots could be designed to work at a varity of
excitation frequencies, standard commercial units generally are available only for
60 or 400 Hz, The physical size ranges from about $- to 3-in diameter. Sensi
tivities of both syncheos and induction pots are of the order of 1 V/deg rotation
'while the residual voltage at null is of the order of 10 to 100 mV. For a standard
Synchro transmitter-transformer pair, the misalignment of the two shafts when
fotated from an originally established electrical null to any other null position
within a complete rotation is of the order of 10 angular minutes. This type of
'error puts a basi limit on the positioning accuracy of servosystems using syn=
thos. Synchro pairs and induction pots are capable of continuous rotation,
although the linear range of induction pots is limited to about +60 to +90"
'Within this range the nonlinearity is of the order of 0.25 percent
Just as in LVDTs, synchros and induction pots require some sort of phase
sensitive demodulation (o obiain a signal of the same form as the mechanical-
'motion input. When used in ac servomechanisms, the conventional two-phase ac
servomotor itself accomplishes this function without any additional equipment.

In a strictly measurement (as opposed to control) application, some sort of phase-

'mechanical loading ofthese rotary components on the measured system is mainly
the inertia of the rotor.

'Variable-Inductance and Variable-Reluctance Pickups
Closely related to LVDTs and synchros, but in practice distinguished from them
by name, is @ family of motion pickups variously called. variable-induetance,
variable-reluetance, or variable-permeance (permeance is the reciprocal of re-
Tuctance) pickups or transducers. The terminology used for these pickups is not

-- 278 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 272
248 weasunine eves
uniform or necessarily descriptive of their basic principles of operation, We are
concerned here mainly with describing some common examples rather than
trying to develop a systematic nomenclature,
Figure 4280 shows the arrangement of a typical translational variable-
inductance transducer. Outwardly the physical size and shape are very similar to
those of an LVDT. Again, there is a movable iron core which provides the
'mechanical input, However, only two inductance coils are present; they generally
form two legs of a bridge whichis excited with alternating current of 5 to 30 V at
60 to 5.000 Hz. With the core at the null position, the inductance ofthe two coils
is equal. the bridge is balanced, and e, is zero. A core motion from null causes &
cchange in the reluctance of the magnetic paths for each of the coils, increasing
fone and decreasing the other. This reluctance change causes a proportional
change in inductance for each coil, a bridge unbalance, and thus an output
voltage ¢,. By careful construction, ¢, can be made a nearly linear function of x,
over the rated displacement range,
'Two alternative methods of forming the bridge circuit are shown in Fig.
428, The total transducer impedance (Z, plus Z,) at the excitation frequency is
'of the order of 100 to 1,000 9. The resistors R are usually about the same value
aas Z and Z,, and the input impedance of the voltage-measuring device ate,
should be at least IOR. If the bridge output must be worked into a low
impedance load, R must be quite small. To get high sensitivity, high excitation
camercapeed

voltage is needed; this causes @ high power loss (heating) in the resistors R, To
Solve this problem, a centertapped transformer circuit may be used. Here the
bridge is mainly inductive, and less power is consumed with corresponding less
heating.
'Such variable-inductance transducers are available in strokes of about 0.1 to
as much as 200 in. The resolution is infinitesimal, and the nonlinearity ranges
from about 1 percent of full scale for standard units to 002 percent for special
units of rather long stroke, Sensitivity is of the order of 5 to 40 Viin. Rotary
'Versions using specially shaped rotating cores have a nonlinearity of the order
OF to 1 pervent of full scale over a £45" range, The sensitivity is about
01 Vides.
Figure 429 shows another common version of the variable-reluctance prin-
ciple. This particular application is an accelerometer for measurement of accel
fons in the range +4. Since the force required to accelerate a mass is pro-
portional to the acceleration, the springs supporting the mass in Fig. 429 deflect
fachatian 1oy,.000088
Coane i
'kis
igme 429 Varbleretetance

-- 280 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 274
250 weasurine nevis
in proportion to the acceleration; thus a displacement measurement allows an
acceleration measurement, The mass is of iron and thus serves as both an inertial
clement for transducing acceleration to force and a magnetic circuit element for
transducing motion to reluctance,
We consider the complete instrument here since it has several features of
general interest with regard to displacement measurement. Ordinarily, such an
instrument would be constructed so that the iron core would be halfway between
the two E frames when the acceleration was zero, thus giving zero output vollage
for zero acceleration. However, to deteet motion on both sizes of zer0 (corre.
sponding to plus or minus accelerations), a fairly involved phase-sensitive de-
'modulator would be required. It was desired to save the cost, weight, and space
of this demodulator, and so another solution (which can also be used with
LYDTs and similar devices) was proposed. With zero-acceleration input, the iron
'ore and springs were adjusted so that the core was offset to one side by an
'amount equal to the spring deflection corresponding to 4y acceleration. Thus,
with no acceleration applied, the output voltage was not zero but some specific
value (25 V in this particular case). Then, when + 4g of acceleration was applied,
the output went to 50 V; and when -4y was applied, the output went to zero. In
this way a relatively simple demodulator and filter circuit can be used to provide
itection-senstive motion measurement, The main drawback of this scheme
(orhich argues against its use except when necessary) is the loss of linearity: This is
because the greatest linearity is found around the null postion. Thus for a given
total stroke iti better to put one-half of it on each side of null rather than all on
'one side, as in the above scheme.
Let us return to the basic motion-measuring principle of Fig. 429. The
Primary coils set up a flux dependent on the reluctance ofthe magnetic path. The
'main reluctance isthe air gap. When the core i in the neutral position, the Aux is
the same for both halves of the secondary coil; and since they ate connected in
series opposition, the net output voltage is zero. A motion of the core increases
the reluctance (air gap) on one side and decreases it on the other, causing more
voltage to be induced into one haf ofthe secondary coil than the other and thus
4 net output voltage. Motion inthe other direction causes the reverse action, with
4 180. phase shift occurring at null. The output voltage is half-wave, non-phase-
sensitive rectified (demodulated) and filtered to produce an output of the same
form as the acceleration input. Ifthe 25-V output for zero-acecleration input is
'objectionable, it can be bucked out with a 2.5-V battery of opposite polarity
connected externally to the accelerometer. The actual full-scale motion of the
'mass in this particular instrument is just a few thousandths of an inch, which
Bives @ displacement sensitivity for the variable-eluctance clement of almost
1,000 Vin,
The final variable-reluctance clement we consider is the Microsyn,' a rotary
component shown in Fig. 430 and widely used in sensitive gyroscopic instru.

ments. The sketch shows the instrument in the null position where the voltages

fom the null (say clockwise) increases the reluctance (decreases the induced
voltage) of coils | and 3 and decreases the reluctance (increases the voltage) of,
Coils 2 and 4, thus giving a net output voltage e,. Motion in the opposite
direction causes a similar effect, except the output voltage has a 180 deg phase shift
Ia direction-sensitive de output is required, a phase-sensitive demodulator is
necessary.
"The excitation voltage is $ to $0 V at 60 to 5000 Hz. Sensitivity is of the
order of 02 to 5 V/degree rotation. Nonlinearity is about 0.5 percent of fll scale
for +7 rotation and 1.0 percent for +10". The null voltage is extremely small,
Tess than the output signal generated by 0.01" of rotation; thus very small mo-
tions can be detected. The magnetic-reaction torque is also extremely small. Since
there are no coils on the rotor, no slip rings (with their attendant friction) are
needed.
Eddy-Current Noncontacting Transducers
In this type of transducer (Fig. 431), the probe usually contains two coils, one
(active) which is influenced by the presence of a conducting target and a second
(balance) which serves to complete a bridge circuit and provide temperature
'vompensation. Bridge excitation is high-frequency (about 1 MHz) alternating cur-
rent. Magnetic flux lines from the active coll pass into the conductive target surface,
producing in the target eddy currents whose density is greatest atthe surface and
Which become negligibly small about three "skin depths" below the surface. Figure
1432! gives formulas for computing skin depth 5 and graphs ofthese formulas for
* Kaman Sciences Corp Clardo Springs, Colo, Ap Note 108, 179.

-- 282 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 276
[Blank page or no OCR text detected]

-- 283 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 277
[MOTION AND DIMENSIONAL MEASUREMENT. 283,
the common excitation frequency of 1 MHz. While thinner targets can be sue-
'cessfully employed, a minimum of three skin depths is recommended to reduce
temperature effects. As the target comes closer to the probe, the eddy currents
'become stronger, which changes the impedance of the active coil and causes a
bridge unbalance related to target position. This unbalance voltage is demodu-
lated, low-pass filtered (and sometimes linearized) to produce a de output pro-
portional to target displacement, The high excitation frequency not only allows
the use of thin targets, but also provides good system frequency response (up to
100 KH)
Probes are commercially available with full-scale ranges from about 0.25
(probe about 2-mm diameter, 20 mm long) to 30 mm (76 mm-diameter, 40 mm
Tong) nonlinearity of 0.5 percent, and maximum resolution 0.0001 mm. Targets
are not supplied with the probes since the majority of applications involve non-
Contact measurement of existing machine parts (thus the part itself serves as
target) Since target material, shape et, influence output, generally its necessary
to statically calibrate the system with the specific target to be used. For non-
'conductive targets, you must fasten a piece of conductive material of suficient
thickness to the surface. Commercially available adhesive-backed aluminum-foil
tape? is convenient for this purpose. The recommended measuring range of @
fiven probe begins at a "standofl? distance equal to about 20 percent of the
probe's stated range. That is, a probe rated at O to | mm range should be used at
get-probe distances of 0.2 to 1.2 mm.
Flat targets should be about the same diameter as the probe or large, if
possible. Targets larger than the probe have little effect on the output; however,
'output drops to about 50 percent for target diameter one-half of probe diameter*
Curved-surface targets such as the periphery of a circular shaft behave similarly
to flat surfaces if the shaft diameter exceeds four transducer diameters.* Special
four-probe systems? for measuring orbital motions of rotating shafts and various
centering and alignment operations are available. While rotating-shaft measure
ments are routinely accomplished, special care may be necessary 10 deal with
Jectrical runout"®in shafts made of ferromagnetic material (such as steel). This
refers to a variation in magnetic permeability around the periphery of the shaft
{resulting from inhomogeneities of heat treatment, hardness, etc) which causes an
electrical output even for a perfectly true-running shaft, thus giving false motion
readings. If electrical runout is excessive filtering. differential measurements using
two diametrially opposed probes, nickel plating of the sha, or shaft-surface
{riding to remove hardness variations may help,

AA rotational or translatory motion may be used in many ways to change the

converted to a usable electrical signal by means of a variety of circuitry. We
'consider here a few typical applications. Capacitance-type pickups often require
clectronics somewhat more complex than for the more common types of trans.
ducers. However, their mechanical simplicity, very small mechanical loading
etlcts, and high sensitivity make them attractive in a number of applications, and
some commercial general-purpose devices now available are quite convenient to

The use of a variable differential (three-terminal) capacitor with a bridge
circuit is shown in the Equibar' differential pressure transducer of Fig. 4.34
'Spherical depressions ofa depth of about 0001 in are ground into the glass disks:
then these depressions are gold-coated to form the fixed plates of a diferent
capacitor. A thin, stainless-steel diaphragm is clamped between the disks and
serves as the movable plate. With equal pressures applied to both ports, the
iaphragm is in a neutral position, the bridge is balanced, and e, is zero. If one
pressure is greater than the other, the diaphragm deflets in proportion, giving an
'Output at e, in proportion to the differential pressure. For the opposite pressure
difference, e, exhibits a 180 deg phase change. A direction-sensitive de output can be
obtained by conventional phase-senstive demodulation and filtering. Balance
resistors necessary for initially nulling the bridge are not shown in Fig. 4.34 This
'method (as opposed to that of Fig. 4.3) allows measurement of static deflections.
Such dlfferential-capacitor arrangements also exhibit considerably greater lin-
cearity than do singlecapacitortypes.®

For vibratory displacements, e, will be an amplitude-modulated wave as in
Fig. 436. The average value of this wave alter rectification is still the mean
Separation of the plates and still can be read by the same meter as used for static

at 10 kHz. The output ofthis filter is applied to a peak-to-peak voltmeter directly

capacitance plate and covering the full-scale displacement ranges of 0.001, 01008,
001, and 0.5 in, respectively. The overall system accuracy is of the order of 2
percent of fall scale. Any lat conductive surface may serve as the second plate of
the variable capacitor. Thus in vibrating machine parts, the parts themselves
the variable capacitor. Thus in vibrating machine parts, the parts themselves often
perform this function. The resolution of 0.5 percent of full scale indicates that
(with the O.01-in fullscale probe) it is possible to detect motion as small as $ in.

differential manner as shown. Voltages e, and e, switch back and forth at about
400 kHz between the excitation voltage (say 6 V de) and ground; the output
voltage is the average (low-pass filtered) difference between e, and e3. At the

ue, which produces no output, Motion changes the relative width (time dur-

'negative average value depending on whether x, was positive or negative, Circuit
behavior is as follows. The four solid-state switches are switched simultaneously
by the output of two op-amp comparators whenever ey or e passes through the
fixed reference voltage of 3 V. Note in the diagram that we are observing the
circuit at an instant when es is connected to ground (es = 0) and capacitor Cy is
discharged, while C, is being charged by the 6-V supply through R, with a time
constant RC. As C; charges, ¢ rises exponentially (nearly linearly for early
times); and when e, reaches 3 V (the reference voltage level), the two inputs of
comparator 4 are now equal, causing its output to throw all four switches to thei
'opposite positions. This "instantaneously" discharges C; and begins a simil
charging process for C. Thus an oscillation is established whose period t, + ts
(about 25 ys in the referenced device) is determined by R\C, + Cs, and é,¢, But
whose duty cycle varies as C, and C; change, In steady stat, the output voltage
is the average of e, ~ e;. Thus

Figure 438 shows a capacitive displacement transducer marketed as a high=
temperature strain gage. A differential capacitor in the form of three thin rings is
tused, with the two inner rings supported by a temperature-compensating rod
'made of the same material as the test specimen. Since the measuring axis is

the specimen is compensated. Maximum strain is + 20,000 je, corresponding to a
total displacement of £002 in over the In gage length (a model with-in gage

Piezoclect
'When certain solid materials are deformed, they generate within them an electric
charge. This effect is reversible in that if a charge is applied, the material will
mechanically deform in response. These actions are given the name piezoelectric

-- 292 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 286
262 weasumino devicrs
(pi-820-clecri) effect." This electromechanical energy-conversion principle is ap-
plied usefully in both directions. The mechanical-input/lectrical-output direction
{is the basis of many instruments used for measuring acceleration, force, and.
pressure, It also can be uilzed as a means of generating high-voltage low-current
electric power, such as is used in spark-ignition engines and electrostatic dust
filters. The eletrical-input/mechanical-output direction is applied in small vibra-
tion shakers, sonar systems for acoustic ranging and direction detection, industrial
ultrasonic nondestructive test equipment, pumps for ink-jet printers, ultrasonic
flowmeters, and micromotion actuators?
'The materials that exhibit a significant and useful piezoelectric effect fall into
three main groups: natural quartz, rochelle salt) and synthetic (lithium sulfate, am-
'onium dihydrogen phosphate) crystals, polarized ferroelectric ceramics (barium
titanate, ete), and certain polymer films." Because of their natural asymmetric
structure, the crystal materials exhibit the eflct without further processing. The
ferroelectric ceramics must be artificially polarized by applying a strong electric
field to the material (while itis heated to a temperature above the Curie point of
'that material) and then slowly cooling with the field sill applied. (The Curie tem
'peratures the temperature above which a material loses its ferroelectric properties;
thus it limits the highest temperature at which such materials may be used.) When
the external field is removed from the cooled material, a remanent polarization is
retained and the material exhibits the piezoelectric eect.
'The piezoelectric effect can be made to respond to (or cause) mechanical
deformations of the material in many different modes, such as thickness ex-
pansion, transverse expansion, thickness shear, and face shear. The mode of
'motion effeced depends on the shape and orientation ofthe body relative to the
crystal axes and the location of the electrodes. Metal electrodes are plated onto,
selected faces ofthe piezoelectric material so that lead wires can be attached for
bringing in or leading out the electric charge. Since the piezoelectric materials are
insulators, the electrodes also become the plates of a capacitor. A piezoclecric
clement used for converting mechanical motion to electric signals thus may be
thought of asa charge generator and a capacitor. Mechanical deformation gener-
ates a charge; this charge then result ina definite voltage appearing between the
electrodes according to the usual law for capacitors, E = Q/C. The piezoelectric
effect is direction-sensitive in that tension produces a definite voltage polarity
hile compression produces the opposite,
We illustrate the main characteristics of piezoelectric motion-to-voltage
'transducers by considering only one common mode of deformation, thickness
expansion. For this mode the physical arrangement is as in Fig. 4.39. Various
double-subscripted physical constants numerically deseribe the phenomena oc.
'curring. The convention is that the first subseript refers to the direction of the

With the above brief introduction as background, we proceed to consider
piezoelectric elements as displacement transducers. The ultimate purpose is gen-
'rally fore, pressure, or acceleration measurement, but here we consider only the
'conversion from displacement to voltage. For analysis purposes it is necessary to
'consider the transducer, connecting cable, and associated amplifier as a unit. The
transducer impedance is generally very' high; thus the amplifier is usually a
high-impedance type used for buffering purposes rather than voltage gain.
'Charge amplifiers (sce Chap. 10) ae commonly used also, The cable capacitance
'ean be significant, especially or long cables, For the transducer alone, if static
deflection x, is applied and maintained, a transducer terminal voltage will be
developed but the charge will slowly leak off through the leakage resistance of the
transducer. Since Ri is generally very large (the order of 10" 0), this decay
would be very slow, perhaps allowing at least a quasi-static response. However,
when an external voltage-measuring device of low input impedance is connected
to the transducer, the charge leaks off very rapidly, preventing the measurement
of static displacements. Even relatively high-impedance amplifiers generally do
not allow static measurements. Some commercially available' systems using
quartz transducers (very high leakage resistance) and electrometer input ampli-
fiers (very high input impedance) achieve an effective total resistance of 10!*
which gives a suficiently slow leakage to allow static measurements.

"Although pethaps not quite as versatile with respect (o computing and com-
pensating operations as the strain-gage/bridge-cieuit combination, piezoelectric
Elements do provide some similar functions, as exemplified by the three-component
force transducer of Fig. 443. Two types of thin quartz sensing disks are used
there. One type is cut (0 be sensitive to tension/compression, and the other 10
'hear. One par of tension/compression disk is used to measure the z component
'f force, while two paits of shear disks, each properly oriented, measure the x and
} components. Use of pairs of disks rather than single disks simplifies electrode
attachment, eliminates the need for insulating layers which reduce rigidity, and
Increases sensitivity. Note that the disk pairs are electrically in parallel so that the
'Charges produced by each element are summed. This signal-summing action can
be extended to more than two elements and is not limited to elements within a
Single transducer. Thus, say the x component of fore from one transducer could
be electrically summed with the y component from another. While piezoelectric

clements respond to both positive and negative strains (with charge of opposite
Polarity it is mechanically difficult to couple tension stresses into the elements,
since adhesive connections usually are not considered sufficiently reliable. The
usual procedure is thus to install the elements (such as the discs above) under
heavy compressive preload, sufficient to preclude relaxation of the preload under
any expected external tension and large enough to provide reliable fictional trans.
fer of the x and y shear components without slippage. Here the leakage of charge
for steady loads is an advantage, since the preload persists mechanically but pro.
'duces zero output voltage. Applied positive and negative 2 loads then produce
corresponding plus and minus voltages since the output voltage corresponds to
the change in strain away from the preload condition.

Electro-Optical Devices
We describe several displacement-measuring devices that combine optical and
electronic principles. Each is designed for a specific class of applications, but all
share the general advantages of optical measurement,' noncontacting operation
ion pressure of
Our first example, the Fotonic sensor. uses fiber optics to measure displace-
'ments in the range of microinches to tenths of inches with a relatively simple
'optical/electronic arrangement. The probe (Fig. 444), 002 to 0. in in diameter
'and 3 in long, consists of a bundle of several hundred optical fibers, each a few
thousandths of an inch in diameter. The fiber bundle extends for I t0 3 ft to the
clectronics chassis, where itis divided into two equal groups of fibers. One group
(transmitting fibers) is exposed to a light source and thus carves light to the probe
{New Dimensions in Optical Gain. nt. Techs 9 Jane 1980.
2 C Memair.C. Kisingr and H.Adhis, The Fons Sensor, I & Con. Sys p11, une

tip, where light is emitted and reflected by the target surface. While targets of
hgh reflectivity give greater output, even rather dull surfaces can be measured.
'The reflected light is picked up by the other (receiving) group of fiber, transmi
ted to the clectronies package, and focused on a suitable photodetector whose
Clectronics then produees a de output related to probe-target gap. To minimize
"rose-talk between adjacent fibers the fibers are of a core/cladding construction;
{hwo glasses of different refractive index are used to obtain total internal reftction.
Probes of this general type display two useful measuring ranges: front slope
and back slope. At zero gap, no light can escape from the transmitting fibers and
'Output is 220. AS the gap opens up, more ofthe target surface i illuminated and
feflecion increases, giving a very sensitive and nearly linear range of measure-
nent (front slope). As gap increases, finally the entire target i illuminated, giving
peak output, Motion beyond this point causes a reduction in response since both

-- 300 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 294
270 weasuaine evicrs
target illumination and the fraction of reflected light gathered by the sensor
decrease roughly according to an inverse square law. This "back slope" region is
also useful for measurement, but is less lincar and sensitive. Theory shows that
'maximum sensitivity in the front-slope region is achieved by arranging the fibers
in a precise geometric pattern in which each receiving fiber is surrounded by four
transmitting fibers. This is not practical from a manufacturing viewpoint, so a
random distribution, which has been found to give nearly identical results, is
usually used. Three other arrangements (Fig. 444) which allow tradeoffs among
various features are also available, however. Typical characteristics for a stane
dard (random distribution) probe of 0.109-in diameter for the front-slope region
are as follows: sensitivity 1.6 mV in, linear (+ | percent range 0.001 to 0008 in,
Satie resolution 1 yin, frequeney response flat +3 dB from de to 50 kHz, full.
scale output 0 to 10 V de, output-signal ripple 30 mV pep. Since the target surface
is usually an existing machine part, static calibration with that specific surface is
'generally required: however, automatic compensation for slowly changing surface
reflectivity is sometimes possible."
Lasers are the basis of many measurement systems used both in industrial
'manufacturing and research laboratories. Several schemes have been devised
using lasers for motion and dimension measurement. One such? is shown in Fig.
445. Here a single narrow helium-neon laser beam is scanned over a workspace
by a livessided prism rotating at 1800 r/min, giving one measurement scan in
rho s. A special collimating lens produces parallel rays which sweep through the
'workspace ata linear rate proportional to the prism's rotational speed. Thus the
Position within the workspace of the cylindrical target (whose diameter is to be
'measured) is not critical. (Ifthe target moves while itis being seanned, an error
will result: however, the rapid measurement cycle reduces this effect. If target
'motion is oscillatory, averaging can be used to minimize error)
'As a result ofthe shadow cast by the target, the photodetector output voltage
exhibits a "notch" whose width in time is proportional to the target width in
space. Measurement of this time interval (by gating an electronic counter) is
'ade more precise by electrically double-ifferentiating the photodetector signal
{0 produce two narrow spikes. Differentiation also allows ac signal processing
land makes the system less sensitive to gradual illumination changes resulting
from drift in laser power. smoke in the workspace, et. A single system oscillator
typically 18 MHz) is used both as the counter clock and the ac frequeney
feference forthe hysteress-synchronous motor used to rotate the prism. Thus any
drifts in oscillator frequency cause motor-speed changes and counter time-base
changes which nullify each other. Additional circuitry is provided to. rejct

We now briefly treat the laser interferometer. a very precise motion measur
ing system. The use of lightintererence principles as a measurement tool
'certainly can be considered a part of classical physics. Michelson, in the 1890s,
tied the scheme of Fig. 4.46, which is the basis of all subsequent developments.
Using the wave model of light, we would expect the observer to see cycles of light
and darkness as the motion of the movable mirror shifted the phase of beam 2
'with respect to fixed beam 1, causing alternate reinforcement and interference of
the two beams. we know the light wavelength to be, say, 05 x 10" m, then
'cach 0.25 » 10 deg m of mitror movement corresponds to one complete cycle (ight
to-dark to light) of illumination. By counting the number of illumination eycles,
'we can calculate the distance between any two positions of the movable mirror.
This seemingly simple measurement principle is fraught with difficulties that
have kept it a tedious standards-laboratory procedure of limited application
{rather than a practical "machine shop" tool) until the recent appearance of
Several laser-based developments. The laser itself provides a much higher-quality
monochromatic (singe frequency) light source than did earlier "lamps." since its
* Moors op it

-- 302 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 296
2M weasunine devicrs
Taran
Figure 4.6 Original Mishetoniteeometer
light is "coherent" (stays in phase with itself) over much greater distances and its
frequency is very stable and precisely known (to about one part in 10 deg ). When
light travels through air, its frequency is unaffected, but its wavelength changes
whenever air pressure, temperature, or humidity causes alterations in the ait'
'refractive index and thus in the speed of light. The pressure effect is about 4 parts
in 10 deg per millimeter of mercury, while the temperature eect is nearly 1! parts in
10" per degree Celsius. Many applications do not warrant corrections for
these effects: but if they are deemed necessary, some commercial systems! provide
temperature, pressure, and humidity sensors that automaticaly scale the instru
'ment readout to provide continuous correction. Since the lengths of objects being.
'measured are themselves temperature-dependent, automatic sensing of this tem:
Perature and correction of readings to some standard (often 20 deg C) ate of practical
interest and are provided on some systems.
Clever optical, mechanical, and electronic designs have built on the above
simple principles to provide measuring systems of much improved portability,
Precision, range of applicability, and ease of use. Figure 4.47 shows one such
system which we briefly discuss (articles? below give more details) The helium-
neon laser used is unusual in that it produces light at two distinct optical
frequencies f, and fz, both in the neighborhood of 5 x 10" Hy, but separated in
frequency by about 2 MHz and of opposite polarization. As the beam leaves the
laser. iti spit in half. One half goes directly to a polarizer and photodetector to
steate an electric reference signal: the other hall proceeds out to the external
'optics. The reference-beam polarizer gives the two frequencies equal polarizations
so that they may exhibit ordinary constructive and destructive interference. This
imerference can be thought of in terms of the intensity-versus-time graph of Fi

447, There, two waves initially "in phase" get slightly "out of phase" in one
'ele, since the frequencies are slightly different. (Note that the time shift per eycle
is a tery small fraction of one cycle; thus, the waves shown are nearly identical
fand interfere constructively, giving a high illumination to the reference-beam
Photodetector and a large electric output) Destructive interference (giving low
iumination and small electric output) will occur 1.25% 10 cycles (0.25 10"* )
later when the time shift per eycle has accumulated a total phase shift of one-half
cyele. The "light" on the reference photodetector thus "fickers" at a 2-MHz
producing a 2-MHz electric signal.
Turning our attention to the measuring beam, we see that it proceeds out
from the laser and encounters first the fixed remote interferometer's polarizing
'beam splitter, which efficiently reflects the fz component around a cube corner
(prism) and transmits the f, component to the measurement cube corner. I this
cube corner is stationary, no frequency change occurs in f, between the incident
"ind reflected beam. However, if the cube corner moves, the reflected beam ex-
hibits a Doppler shift of frequency proportional to the velocity of motion [about
33 MHzimvs)}. When this f, + Af beam is recombined with the fy beam in the
emote interferometer and impinged on the photodetector, again we get inter-
ference effects that produce an elecric signal of frequency 05 to 15 MHz, de-
pending on the velocity, The reference and measurement signals are ac-amplifed
nd applied to a subtracting counter that reads zero if there is no motion, but
which accumulates counts in proportion to the distance traveled from a chosen
{eference position. For example, a motion at 1 em/s for 1 s (total motion 1 em)
fives 33,000 counts and thus a resolution of about 3 x 10-7 m.

'addition to displacement, Locating the interferometer emote from the laser head

'Some recent improvements? in the above system include a wavelength tracker
for better compensation of environmental error sources. The temperature, pressure,
and humidity sensors of Fig. 447 ate all intended to correct for the effect of these
'variables on wavelength, The tracker eliminates the need for all these sensors by
"tually measuring the wavelength itself; thus it corrects fr all error sourees which
lMfect wavelength, It also provides index-oF refraction data on the air (or other
dimpbicnt gas), useful in certain optical systems. The table of Fig. 448 compares
the achievable accuracies of various compensation schemes. A differential inter
ferometer is combined with an etalon to achieve the wavelength tracking function
Ina differential interferometer, the relative ("differential") motion of two plane
mirrors is measured as in Fig. 448, We again have two laser beams with diferent
frequency and polarization (as in Fig. 4.47) originating at al. The reference beam
{dashed line) follows the path f, 2, 3,..-, 16, while the measuring beam follows
Ui hyc,~.-p. Both beams fist encounter the polarizing beam splitter at .b, where
the relerence beam is reflected while the measuring beam passes through, due to
thet different polarizations. The measuring beam next passes through the quarter~
wave plate, reflets from the measuring mirror, and again passes through the
{quarter-wave plate. The quarter-wave plate is oriented so as to change the polar
ization of the measuring beam so that itis now like that of the original reference
'beam, causing the measuring beam to be reflected at b rather than passing through.
Both of the quarter-wave plates in Fig. 4.48 are used to perform this polarization-

"To measure uniaxial or biaxial motion ina plane perpendicular to the line of
sight, optical trackers of various types are available, Perhaps the most common
technique utilizes an image-dssector tube and electron multiplier with a servo
Tock-on technique. Figure 430 shows the arrangement for a uniaxial measure-
'ment using this approach? The moving object must itself exhibit a "target" (such
Tis a machined edge) with an optical-contrast ratio of at last 3: 1, or else we
fasten to it a black-white target with the dividing line perpendicular to the
direction of motion, Illumination of average room level or more is required and
'must be nonpulsatng. In microscope or telescope optics (depending on the stand-
Off distance and range of motion), an image of the target is focused on the
'photocathode of the image-dissector tube. The rear surface of the photocathode
mits electrons in relation to the light intensity striking the front surface, thus
forming an "electron image" of the target. An applied electric field accelerates the
lectron image down the tube and focuses it on an aperture plate with a tiny
feentral hole. Thus only a small portion of the electron image is seen by the
tlectron multiplier, which produces an output current proportional to the inten-
Sity ofthis image. I the electron multiplier sees "all white." it produces a large
'output; if all black," a small output. I the target black-white dividing line lies
Within the aperture, an intermediate level of output is produced. If we choose,

fenerate an error signal e,, which will go positive or negative depending: on
whether more black or more white is present. Since the image-dissector tube has
4 deflection coil (just like an oscilloscope CRT) which can position the electron,
'mage, we connect our amplified error signal to this coil with polarity such that if
there is "too much black," the coil drives the image in the white direction, and
vice-versa. Thus a feedback system is ereated which keeps the black-white dit-
iding line nearly centered on the aperture at all times. The output signal, pro-
Portional to target displacement x,,is obtained from the amplifier output e,, As
in all servosystems, accuracy and speed require a high loop gain, However, this is
possible here without instability since all dynamic clements in the control loop.
hhave smal lags because ofthe absence of mechanical moving parts,

(xe, m) deflection coils, a target with both horizontal and vertical black-white

'multiplier has only one output signal, not a horizontal and a vertical, and this

in any particular direction. To appreciate the nature of the probiem, consider Fig.

Yertical position is incorrect, but would not be corrected because again the
50:50 area ratio produces no corrective x, deflection-cil signal,

of the phototube between the horizontal and vertical channels of the image
deflection system. Hold amplifiers in each channel preserve the most event data,
while the other channel is being updated, until fresh data are available for that
channel. A single eycle of servosystem action goes as follows: The cycle stats

However, the manufacturing process allows considerable flexibility in size and
seometry; area arrays containing 10,000 elements in a 100 x 100 matrix with
60am center-to-eenter spacing, for example, allow imaging of two-dimensional
'objects. On a single silicon chip, such arrays include a row (or rows) of individual
light-sensitive photodiodes, each with its own charge-storage capacitor and solid-
state multiplex switch. Also contained on the chip is a shift register for serial
'readout ofthe individual clement signals, The diodes transduce incident light into
'charge, which is stored on the capacitor until readout. The charge is proportional
to the product of light intensity and exposure time; however, a saturation level
ddoes exist. For an array as in Fig. 453a, saturation typically occurs at about
6 uW sjem*. Scan frequency (the frequency at which the individual elements are
interrogated and read out) can be varied over a wide range (up to about 40 MHz
in some models), which allows a tradeoff between sensitivity and speed, since
slower sean rates allow the same illumination to produce more charge each scan
cycle

Figure 4.53b shows how a linear array can be used to construct a line-scan
camera for dimensional measurement. The object to be measured is back-lighted
{front lighting is also feasible) so as to produce a light-dark pattern with tran-
sitions at the objec's edges. Conventional optics focus an image on the
Photodiode array. Dimensional resolution at the array is, of course, limited by
the diode spacing, say 0.001 in. However, ifthe optics is such that a 5-in object
produces a I-in image. then resolution of the object's dimension is only 0.005 in
If the object is moving (as is often the case in industrial inspection), then ad-
ditional errors are possible; however, higher scan frequency may alleviate such
problems. Figure 4.53 shows two output signals, "video" and "data." The video
Signal is a "boxcar" (stepwise-changing) function that shows the time-integrated
ilumination of each individual picture element (called pixel) over one scan cycle
'Note thatthe light (and dark) regions do not give an identical response for each
pixel. No illumination field is perfectly uniform; and even if t were, the individual
pixel sensitivities cannot be made perfectly equal. To get an unambiguous dimen-
Sion measurement, the video signal is compared with a judiciously chosen thresh-
'old level to produce distinct switching inthe "data" signal
Many useful variations of the above apparatus are possible. Area scanning,
'can be done with an area array or by using an oseilating mirror to deflect the
image past a linear array. Wide objects ean be measured by using two cameras
separated by a known distance. Color applications are possible by using three
Cameras with proper-color fiers, Cameras can be interfaced with microproces-
Sors which have digital representations of objects stored in ROM (read-only
'memory). The measured objects' "signature" can be compared with a "standard"
sored in ROM to check whether they match, thereby identifying the object or
perhaps detecting defects init
"The lateral-effect photodiode" silicon detector is the basis of « number of
'useful motion-measuring schemes. In the single-axis device of Fig. 44, if terminal
G is grounded, we are using the photovoltaic (PV) mode of operation, while apply
ing a bias voltage (5 to 15 V) there gives the photoconductive (PC) mode. Photo-
'conductive operation may be necessary in the highest-speed applications: it reduces
'device capacitance and thus system RC time constants. For either mode, when the
light spot is atthe central (x= 0) position, currents i, andl ic are equal. When the
light spot moves away from x = 0, one current increases and the other decreases,
with their diference being a nearly linear function of x ifthe currents are fed into
devices of low impedance. Op-amp electronics as shown are convenient for pro-
viding the desired low input impedance (transimpedance amplifier) and the voltage
subtraction (e, ~ ?c) which gives zero output for x = 0. Since light intensity in
'many practical systems may drift with time, temperature, ete, the division of,
4 - ec bY ?4 + ?c is often desirable. Since intensity changes will alter the photo-
currents (and thus e, and ¢c) similarly, the division produces an output signal

'A commercial, ready-to-use motion-measuring system based on a lateral-

Surface to be measuted, which noed not be highly reflective. As the surface moves

Photographic, Videotape, and Holographic Techniques
'The application of still and motion-picture photography often allows qualitative
land quantitative analysis of complex motions that would be difficult by other
methods.' Here we touch briefly on some of the more common applications
Perhaps the simplest application of still photography is the single-ash
top-action" technique.* The objective is to "freeze" a motion at a particular
phase of its occurrence to allow detailed visual study of some physical phe-
'nomenon. The equipment usually employed consists ofa still camera, a strobo-
scopic light source, and some means of triggering a single flash ofthe strobe light
at the desired instant. Ifthe experiment can be performed in a darkened room,
the procedure consists of manually opening the camera shutter, allowing the
[Phenomenon to occur, triggering the light at the desired instant, and then man=
ually closing the shutter. If triggering, focus, and exposure are correct, a photo of
the phenomenon, "frozen" at the instant of the light flash, is obtained. Such
Photos can be most helpful in understanding complex physical processes in fluid
motion or moving machine parts. Of course, the effective freezing of the motion
'depends on the flash duration's being suficiently short compared with the velocity
of the motion. Flash durations of the order of 1 to 3 ys ate readily available
'Thus, for example, a velocity of 300 m/s will cause a "blurring" of 003 to 009 em
at the object. The actual blurring on the film will be this value times the
image/object ratio of the camera setup. Ia 1-em object shows up on the flm as
0.1 cm, for example, the above blurring would amount to 0.003 to 0.009 em on
the film, which generally would be considered acceptable. If the experiment
cannot be carried out in a darkened room, the opening and closing ofthe shutter
"WG. Hyze, "Engineering and Slentite igh Speed Photography." Macmillan, New York,
1969
* "Handbook of Hih Speed Photography." Genera Radio Co, West Concord Mas.

-- 317 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 311
SIONAL MEASUREMENT 287
'must be synchronized with the flash and the open time of the shutter must be
short enough so as not to overexpose the film from the room light.
a displacement-time record is desired, a multiple-ash stil-camera tech
nique may be employed, The setup is essentially the same as above except the
strobe light flashes repetitively at a known rate. The result is a multipl-exposure
photo showing the moving object in successive positions which are separated by
Known increments of time. By including a calibrated length scale in the photo
{preferably in the plane of the motion) numerical values of displacement at speci-
fic time intervals may' be measured.
High-speed motion-piclure photography is used to study motions that oceur
100 rapidly for the eye to analyze properly. This is accomplished by taking the
pictures at high camera picture frequency (rames per second) and then project-
ing the film at a low projector picture frequency. The lowest usable projector
frequency is about 16 frames per second since lower frequencies result in flicker-
ing because the human eye' persistence of vision is about 0.06 s. The highest
usable camera picture frequency depends on the construction of the camera
Relatively small, portable cameras are available with picture frequencies up to
about 20,000 frames per second, and these are in relatively wide use in industry.
Larger, more complex, and expensive cameras' are available where the appli
cation dictates their higher speed. Their picture frequencies are up 10 several
million per second. The time magnification is defined as the ratio of the camera
frequency to the projector frequency: thus if 16 isthe projector Irequency. mi
fications of several hundred thousand to one are achievable while 1,500 1 is not
'unusual in common industrial practice. Aside from picture frequency. the shutter
speed of the camera must be sufficiently high to prevent blurring of the individual
frames, just as in still photography. For 16-mm film a blur of 0005 cm is con:
sidered acceptable. The shutter-speed requirement can be greatly relaxed by the
tse of a synchronized short-duration electronic flash asthe light source, singe the
fash duration then controls blur no matter what the shutter speed. Selection of a
'proper camera picture rate may be judged roughly by the rule that the projection
'of the complete motion to be visually analyzed should take about 2 to 10s. Thus
'4 motion occurring in 0.001 s requires a time magnification of 2000 to 10,000.
For vibratory motions the camera picture rate must be several (preferably 5 to
10) times the highest vibration frequency.
'A convenient alternative 1o high-speed cinematography for applications al-
lowing use of modest (60 oF 120 per sccond) frame rates is found in the special
videotape systems? designed for industrial measurement. These are identical in
principle to units used for "instant replay" in commercial TV. However, their
'etl design provides & 2 t0 4 times higher frame rate, as well as elimination of
* 6. R. Van Horn, Rotting Dram Cameras for High Speed Photography, Flectmopicl Si.

rolling noise bars, jitter, and picture distortion not tolerable in instrumentation
applications. Also, to "freeze" rapid motion, illumination is stroboscopic with
about a 20-ys ash duration, once per frame. Note that time resolution of motion
'depends on the frame rate, not on the strobe duration. Thus these systems com-
pete with photographic methods for only the slower motions: however, many
industrial problems lie in this range. For these cases, they provide considerably
'greater convenience of use and quicker availability of results. One system,* com
bining new developments in video sensors, magnetic tape recording, and digital
processing, provides frame rates to 2,000 per second (fll size) and 12,000 per
second (f siz).
Optical holography refers to a wide varity? of laser-based optical techniques
applicable to vibration measurement, flow visualization, particle diagnostics, ete
Here we briefly describe holographic interferometry for vibration measurement,
'one of the most widely used techniques. Classical (prlaser) interferometry was
limited to measurement of small motions of specularly reflecting (polished) fat
surfaces, whereas current laser techniques can deal with three-dimensional objects
with arbitrarily curved and diffusely reflecting surfaces, in other words, almost
any real object.
Figure 457 shows the essential features of a simple holographic apparatus. A
single laser beam is divided by a beam splitter into two separate beams, which
retain a fixed phase relationship with each other over some distance related to
the coherence length of that particular laser. (Coherence length is the distance
'over which a laser stays in phase with itself A long coherence length allows us to
'measure larger objects. Typically a 20-mW helium-neon laser might have a co-
hherence length of 0.25 m and be capable of measuring objets up to about 0.5 m
in all dimensions) After the laser beam is split, the two beams are each diverged
in beam spreaders so that an area (rather than a "point") can be illuminated.
(Note that the undiverged beams present safety hazards and must be treated with
respect.) One diverged beam, the reference beam, is directed to illuminate a
high-resolution photographic plate, while the other (object) beam illuminates the
measured object, which reflects the light to the same photographie plate, If the
'measured object is stationary, when the object and reference beams recombine at
the photographic plate, because of phase shifts resulting from the different path
lengths of rays illuminating the objec, an intensity pattern called a hologram is
recorded on the plate, which is then chemically developed as in ordinary pho-
tography. The developed plate can be observed with the unaided eye, but bears
'no resemblance tothe object. The intensity pattern spacings(~S x 10 deg mm) are

from the actual object) appears tothe unaided eye
[At this point we have a method (holography) for producing realistic (three-

igre 458 Holographic bation ana
'ot yet a motion-measuring scheme. In holographic interferometry we record two
holograms on the same plate, Ifthe abject has moved andjor deformed between
the two exposures, these two holograms will be different. When we reconstruct
this double hologram, the two patterns will interfere, causing light and dark
fringes superimposed on the objec's image) which now are visible to the unaided
eye and can be photographed. The fringes represent contours of equal displace
'ment along the viewing axis, with each successive fringe corresponding to to}
(depending on the geometry of the optical layout) wavelength of light, 6 to 12 yin
for a helium-ngon laser. To study static and dynamic motions and deformations,
various specific techniques' (static double exposure, dynamic time average, teal
time, pulse?) have been developed. Figure 4.58 shows typical results obtained
in vibration analysis of machine component.? Automated holography work:
stations* are available which provide all the facilities needed to produce and view
holograms of objects smaller than 18 in. These use a thermoplastic recording med
jum which produces ready-to-view holograms 105 after exposure and which can
be erased and reused several hundred times.

Photoclastic, Brittle-Coating, and Moiré Fringe Stress-Analysis Techniques
Since these methods are really strains rather than stress-sensitive and since strain
isa small displacement, we include a brief treatment in this section on displace-
ment measurement
Photoelastic methods' depend on the property of birefringence under load
exhibited by certain natural or synthetic transparent materials. Birefringence
'ouble refraction) under load refers to the phenomenon in which light travels at
different speeds in a transparent material, depending on both the direction of
travel relative o the directions of the principle stresses and the magnitude of the
dilerence between the principal stresses, for a two-dimensional stress field. By
constructing models (from suitable transparent materials) of the same shape as
the part to be stressanalyzed and shining suitably polarized light through them
while they are subjected {0 loads proportional to those expected in actual service,
4 pattern of light and dark fringes appears which shows the stress distribution
throughout the piece and allows numerial calculation of stresses at any chosen
point.
By use of the "frozen stress" technique, the method can be extended 10
three-dimensional problems. A three-dimensional plastic model is subjected to
simultaneous load and high temperature. The load is maintained while the speci
men is slowly cooled to room temperature whercupon the load is released. tis
found that a residual stress pattern identical to that produced by the load is
"fraven" into the specimen. Furthermore. now the model may be carefully sliced
in various directions to produce Mat (two-dimensional) slabs, which ean be pho-
toclastically analyzed to determine three-dimensional stresses. By combining pho-
toclastic and high-speed photographic techniques, the method can be extended to
'dynamic studies such as the propagation of shock waves through solid bodies,
In reflete photoclasticity there is no need to construct a plastic model: the
part itself is given a thin "coating" of photoelastic material. Unfortunately,
Sprayed of brushed coatings, the most convenient application technique, exhibit
too much thickness variation and are used only in rough qualitative studies. For
accurate work, a thin (DOI to 0.125 in) sheet of photoelastic plastic is bonded to
the part with a reflctive adhesive and thus participates inthe surface strain when
the purt i lodded. Polarized light is directed onto the part, reflects from the shiny
undersurface, and produces fringe patterns that are analyzed by a reflection
polariscope. Flat surfaces are easily fitted with available sheet material. Complex
Surfaces, however, require casting of a thin Nat sheet, careful "form fitting" to the
part while the sheet is stil "limp," allowing the formed sheet to harden, and then
'cementing to the part with reflective adhesive.
Photoclastic methods, as compared with bonded resistance strain gages, give
an overall picture of the stress distribution in a part. This is very helpful in
MLM. Frosh, *Photoeatiy" vols 1 and 2, Wiley, New York, 1941, 194; Dally and Rly,
"tsperinental Sess Amys Photoane Sten Ana Tecigues and Prods, Bul. SFC"
240, Photos Ine, Malvern,

-- 322 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 316
292 weasuanic pewers
locating and numerically evaluating stress concentrations and in redesigning the
part for optirium material use. Also the method does not disturb the local stress
field as a stra n gage might,
In the brtle-coating stressanalysis technique,' special lacquerlike material
is sprayed on the actual part to be analyzed and the coating allowed to dry.
Application cf load causes visible cracking of the brittle coating The direction of
the cracking shows the direction of maximum stress, and the spacing of the
cracks indicates magnitude. Under favorable conditions, numerical values of
siress can be calculated from measured erack spacing to about 10 percent accu
racy. The main features of the method are its simplicity, low cost, and speed in
4iving an overall picture of stress distribution. Often itis used in conjunction
with electric-esistance strain gages, with the brittle coating locating the points of
'maximum stiess and its direction so that strain gages can be applied at the
proper places and in the proper orientation for accurate strain meastiement.
Moiré fringe methods? utilize a fine (200 to 2000 lines/in) square grid of
'equidistant lines bonded to the surface to be analyzed. When a master (un
deformed) grid is superimposed (directly or by projection) on the grid as de-
formed by loxding the par, a fringe pattern appears which ean be analyzed to
'obtain surface displacements oF stains
Displacement-to-Pressure (Nozzle-Flapper) Transducer
'The nozzle-fi pper-iransducer principle is widely used in precision gaging equip-
'ment and as 4 basic component of pneumatic and hydraulic measurement and
control apparatus. Figure 459 shows the general arrangement. Fluid at a reg
'ulated pressure is supplied to a fixed-low restriction and a variable-low re-
striction connzeted in series. The variable-low restriction is varied by moving the
"flapper" to change the distance x,. This causes a change in output pressure p,
which, for a mited range of motion, is nearly proportional to x, and extremely
sensitive to it, Thus a pressure-measuring device connected to p, can be cali
brated to read x,. Ideally (pressure-containing chambers rigid; vid incompress-
'ible) a sudden change in x, would cause an instantaneous change inp, Actually,
the dynamics are approximately those of a linea, first-order system for small
changes in x,. The time constant is determined for gases by the compressibility of
the gas; for Iquids the elastic deformation of the pressure-sensing device often
controls,
'We analye the system of Fig. 4.59a for the case of a gaseous medium since a
'majority of practical applications utilize low-pressure (p, ~ 20 to 30 Ibvin® gage)
air as the working fluid. The principle of conservation of mass is applied to the
volume V by stating that during a time interval dr the difference between entering
mass and leav ng mass must show up as an additional mass storage in V.

"Many different versions of the air gaging principle have been developed in
past years. A recent development? which combines air gaging with reed switches
is shown in Fig. 4614, A "pneumatic Wheatstone-bridge" arrangement uses fixed
flow restrictions K, and R, and adjustable-low restriction Ry to measure dis-
placement x. When Ry and Ry are equal assuming Ry = Rz), the pressure drop
feross the freeloating piston {which carries a small permanent magnet) is zero
'and the piston is at its bottom position, Motion x to the left increases Ry and
fcauses a Ap aeross the piston, which moves to its top position when this Ap
exceeds a few millimeters of water. When the piston moves from top to bottom,
magnet motion actuates two magnetic reed switches, one going from open to
'closed, the other doing the reverse, This switching point can be adjusted to occur
'over a range of x positions by adjusting Ry. and the switch actions can be wsed
for a variety of indicating and control tasks.
"The module of Fig. 4614 isa basic building block which can be used to create
fa variety of gaging systems useful in manufacturing, Figure 461b shows one
'adapted to classifying machined parts of dimension W into "undersize," "oversize"
land "in tolerance" groups, with typical tolerance ranges of ++0.01 mm. Repeat
ability as good as 0.2 um and a speed of S measurements per second are claimed,
lunder proper conditions. For undersize parts, pis low and the pistons are in the
position shown, so that switch 1 i closed, switch 3s open, and the red ("undersize")
light is turned on. Think now of gradually increasing W and thus pq. We want
'switch 1 t0 open as W crosses the lower tolerance limit, but switch 2 should
remain open, giving a no-lights condition, signifying a good part. This can be
accomplished by adjusting Ry, and Ry, As W inereases further, we want switch
2 to go from open to closed (lighting the yellow "oversize" lamp) as W crosses
the upper tolerance limit. This can be done with a further adjustment of Ry»

Digital Displacement Transducers (Translational and Rotary Encoders)
Since transducers often communicate with digital computers, transducers with
sigital output would be particularly convenient. However, vey few such devices
exis, $0 usually we use an analog transducer to produce a voltage signal and an
clectronic analog-to-digital converter to realize the desired digital data. Digital
transducers called encoders* do exist, however, for translational and rotary dis-
placement and are in wide use. Three major classes tachometer, incremental,
and absolute-are available in various detail forms; Fig. 463 shows their charac.
teristic output signals. A tachometer encoder has only a single output signal
Which consists of a pulse for each increment of displacement. If motion were
always in one direction, a digital counter could accumulate these pulses to deter-
'mine displacement from a known starting point. However, any reversed motion
'would produce identical pulses, causing errors. Thus this type of encoder usually
is used for speed, rather than displacement, measurement in situations where
rotation never reverses
* ? Hudson, A Guide to Opal Shall Encoder, In. & Cont St, May 197

"The incremental encoder! solves the reverse-motion problem by employing
at least two (and sometimes three) signal-generating elements, By mechanically
displacing the two tracks, one of the electric signals is shifted 4 eycle relative to
the other, allowing detection of motion direction by noting which signal rises
*-Tetniqaes for Digitsing Rotary and Linear Motion, Dynamics Research Corp, Wilmington,
Mase 1976

-- 332 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 326
M02 wsasunise pevicrs
first. Thus the up-down pulse counter can be signaled to subtract pulses whenever
motion reverses. A third output, which produces a single pulse per revolution ata
distinct point, is sometimes provided as a zero reerence. An incremental encoder
has the advantage of being able to rotate through as many revolutions as the
application requires. However, any false pulses resulting from electric noise will
'cause errors that persist even when thé noise disappears, and loss of system
Power also causes total loss of position data with no recovery when power fs
reapplied.
'Absolute encoders generally are limited to a single revolution and utilize
'multiple tracks and outputs, which are read out in parallel to produce a binary
representation of the angular position of the shaft. Since there is a one-to-one
correspondence between shaft position and binary output, position data are re
covered when power is restored after an outage, and transient electric: noise
'causes only transient measurement error
Encoders ofall three types can be constructed as contact devices (stationary
brushes rub on rotating code disk, Fi. 464) or as noncontacting devices using
either magnetic or optical principles. In Fig. 4.64 the readout lamps are shown
