Ultrasonic Transducers
We show three examples of displacement measurement utilizing wave-
Propagation principles and producing digital output. Such sensors often are
called ultrasonic since the signals employed are generally outside the frequency
range of human hearing. Our first example? intended mainly for full-scale ranges
of 1 to 10 ft oF more, utilizes a permanent magnet which moves relative to a
'magnetostrictive wire enclosed in a nonferrous protective tube (see Fig. 468)
Electronic circuitry drives a current pulse through the wire. At the magnet lo-
cation, magnetostrictive action generates in the wire a stress pulse which propa-
ates (0 the receiver location at a fixed speed of about 110,000 in/s. At the
receiver location, a pickup coil senses the arrival of the pulse. The time interval
bbetwcen the initiating current pulse and the arrival of the sensed stress pulse is
clearly proportional to the displacement x, with a sensitivity of about 9 ps/in. By
using the two pulses to gate on and off a counter, a digital output reading with
resolution of 16 bits can be obtained. To get a continuous analog signal, the pulse
is applied repetitively and the transit time interval is used to modulate the width
of a rectangular pulse train. Low-pass filtering of this pulse train produces an
analog voltage whose amplitude is proportional to the pulse width and thus to
'x. The repetition rate is sufficiently high that the low-pass filter can be designed
to be flat (~3 dB) to about 200 Hz for a 2-e-stroke unit, Nonlinearity better than

i fore

(005 percent of fll scale is obtained. This transducer is becoming quite popular
as the feedback sensor in hydraulically actuated servomechanisms for position
control? Here its basic postion signal is augmented by deriving digital velocity
(= AX/At) and acceleration (4 = AV/AN signals. These signals allow imple=
mentation of derivative control modes on the system controlled variable (load
position) which lead to significant improvements in control system performance.
'Our next example is based on the automatic focusing system of a popular
camera. The motor drive which focuses the lens gets its information from an
tltrasonie rangefinder (distance measuring) system. The manufacturer has made
available an experimenters kit and manual? which allow study of ultrasonic
distance measurement; our description is based on this manual. The heatt of the
system isan electrostatic transducer (similar in principle to the capacitor micro-
phone of Chap. 6) which is, however, alternately used as a loudspeaker and a
microphone. The principle ofall such systems, of course. is that of classical sonar
'where an acoustic signal is sent out and its reflected return timed (0 allow
Calculation of the target' distance by use of the known propagation velocity
(1100 {1s for standard aie). Whereas simpler ultrasonic ranging systems (such as
those used for tank lquid-level measurement) might use a single-frequency signal.
the camera rangefinder must deal with targets of variable and unpredictable
shape and size. This led to use of a mulifequency signal to ensure that a
reflected echo would occur reliably. This signal consists of 8 cycles of 60 KHz, 8
cycles of 57 kHz, 16 eyeles of $3 kHz, and 24 cycles of $0 KH? for a total of 56
cyeles and about 1-ms, By using the transducer as a loudspeaker, this electric
'signal is applied, causing similar acoustic signal to propagate toward the target.
Before the echo returns, the transducer connections are electronically switched, so
that it acts as a microphone to transduce the reflected pressure wave into'an
clectric signal whose time of arrival can be detected and the target distance
thereby calculated. The system is designed 10 measure distances over the range
(09 to 35 ft with a resolution of 0.1 f, Since the variation in echo signal strength
for targets from 3 t0 35 ft is almost a million to one, the microphone's tuned
amplifier selF-adjusts its gain (16 settings) and bandwidth (8 settings) to maintain
Signal levels more nearly constant in subsequent circuits. In the kit mentioned
above, distance measurements are made and read out digitally on an LED dis-
play $ times per second: however, cireuit adjustments can speed this up to about
20 measurements per second,
'Our final example is a diitizer* used to pick dimensions off of solid objects
and input the digitized values to various computer-aided design programs. Then
the computer can display an accurate "three-dimensional" picture of the object
fon a graphics terminal; and since the object's geometry is now numerically

specified, the computer can perform any engineering calculations (surface areas,
Volumes, stress calculations, etc) that are in the computer's memory. A pen
whose tip contains a tiny electric spark source is used to trace the surface of the
object. The spark discharge generates a sharp acoustic pulse which propagates in
all directions at known speed. Microphones at the four corners of a 75x 150 em
Plane surface (the pen can be as much as 180 cm away from the plane in the
perpendicular direction) receive the pulses, Timing circuitry allows calculation of
the four slant ranges, from which x, y, z coordinates can be calculated by a
builtin microprocessor or the users own general-purpose computer. Resolution
{s about 0.1 mm and up to 140 points per second can be digitized. Ifthe spark
source is attached at a particular point on a moving object, the translational
'displacement, velocity, and acceleration of this point could be calculated since the
'ime intervals between digitized displacement values are known,
44 RELATIVE VELOCITY, TRANSLATIONAL,
AND ROTATIONAL
We consider here devices for measuring the velocity of translation, along @ line, of
'one point relative to another and the plane rotational velocity about a single axis
'of one line relative to another.
Calibration
'The measurement of rotational (angular) velocity is probably more common than
that of translational velocity. Since translation generally can be obtained from
rotation by suitable gearing or mechanisms, we consider mainly the calibration of
rotational devices. For angular and linear velocities, perhaps the most convenient
calibration scheme uses a combination ofa toothed wheel, a simple magnetic prox-
imity pickup and an eletronic EPUT (events per unit time) meter (se Fig. 469).
'The angular rotation is provided by some adjustable-speed drive of adequate
stability. The toothed iron wheel passing under the proximity pickup produces an
electric pulse each time one tooth passes. These pulses are fed to the EPUT
meter, which counts them over an accurate period (say 1.00000 5), displays the
result visually fora few seconds to enable reading, and then repeats the process.
'The stability ofthe rotational drive is easily checked by observing the variation
of the EPUT meter readings from one sample to another. The inaccuracy of pulse
counting is + 1 pulse plus the error in the counter time base, which is of the order
of | ppm. The overall accuracy achieved depends on the stability of the motion
source, the angular velocity being measured, and the number of teeth on the
wheel. If the motion source were absolutely stable (no change in velocity whal-
fever), very accurate measurement could be achieved simply by counting pulses
'over a long time, since then the average velocity and the instantaneous velocity

-- 339 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 333
MOTION AND DIMENSIONAL MEASUREMENT 309
Figure 469 Veloity-aibration setup.
would be identical. Ifthe motion source has some drift, however, the time sample
must be fairly short, For example, a shaft rotating at 1,000 ¢/min with a 100-
Tooth wheel produces 1,667 pulses in a I-s sample period. The inaccuracy here
'would be I part in 1,667 (the I-ppm time-base error is totally negligible), or 0.06
percent If the shaft rotated at 10 r/min, the error would be 6 percent. Slow
Potations can be measured accurately by such means if the toothed wheel is
placed on a shaft_which is sulliciently geared up from the shaft driving the
transducer being calibrated
"The above procedure uss relatively simple equipment and generally provides
cmirely adequate accuracy. Other simpler and less accurate procedures can be
tmployed if they are adequate for their intended purpose. These usually consist of
Simply comparing the reading of a velocity transducer known to be accurate with
the reading of the transducer to be calibrated when both are experiencing the
same velocity inp.

accentuates any low-amplitude, high-fequency noise present in the displacement
Signal. Thus a carbon-flm potentiometer would be preferable to the wirewound
type, and demodulated and filtered signals from ac transducers may cause trouble
because of the remaining ripple at carrier frequency. Workable systems using
cletrial differentiation are possible, however, with adequate attention to deta

Average Velocity from Measured Ax and Ar
Often a value of average velocity over a short distance or time interval is ade-
(quate, anda continuous velocty/time record is not required. A useful basic
ethod is somehow (optically, magnetically, etc) to generate a pulse when the
moving object passes two locations whose spacing is accurately known. If the
Velocity were constant, any spacing could be used, with large spacing, of course,
Jeading to greater accuracy. I the velocity is varying, the spacing Ax should be
Ssnall enough that the average velocity over AW is not very different from the
NElocty at either end of Ax. The same technique is applicable to rotational
Figure 4.70a shows the application of a variable-reluctance proximity pickup
such as was used in Fig. 4.69. When magnetic material passes close in front of the
Tue of the pickup, the reluctance of the magnetic path changes with time, gener=
sting a voltage in the coil. These pickups are simple and cheap and give a large
Sutput voltage (ofien several volts) under typical operating conditions. The
Sutput voltage inereases with velocity and closeness of the external moving iron
to the pickup. Display of the two pulses on a single sweep of an oscilloscope with
'calibrated time base allows measurement of the average velocity. Greater accu
acy may be achieved by applying the voltage pulses to an electronic time-
Taterval meter. In some velocity-measurement or proximity-detecting. appli
ations, pickups of this type ate unsatisfactory because signal size decreases with
Selovity and is 2ero for a stationary object. We might then use proximity pickups
tithe eddy-current or Hallelfet type. Eddy-current proximity pickups are sim-
ilar in principle to the eddy-current displacement transducers discussed earlier,
'except that a switching (rather than a linearly proportional) output is desired
When a conductive metal object approaches, the pickup's oscillator experiences @
'eduction in amplitude sufficient to (rigger a switching signal.' Hall-effect devices
Ulize the Fall principle of Fig. 4.714, where a suitable material (originally gold,
bat today 4 semiconductor) supplied with constant current produces an output
Voltage , when a transverse magnetic field is applied. Proximity pickups based.
tn this principle may utilize a permanent magnet* attached to the moving object
tr else require a ferrous target whose approach changes the reluctance of an
'nternal magnetic citeuit whose flux the Hall sensor feels.' In either case, the Hall
Clement output voltage triggers a standard TTL logie signal as output. Very low
"elocitics can be measured by electronically timing the interval between passe
reach pair of adjacent gear teeth, holding the previous velocity value until up-
dated by the next tooth passage. Figute 471 shows a version using two mechan-
faaly offct Hall sensors which provide also a direction-of-rotation signal

Stroboscopic Methods!
Rotational velocity may be comveniently measured by using electronic strobo-
scopic lamps which flash at a known and adjustable rate. The light is directed
'onto the rotating member which itself usually has spokes, gear teeth, or some
other feature enabling "lock on." If not, a simple black-and-white paper target
can be attached. The frequency of lamp Mashing is adjusted until the "target
'appears motionless. At this setting, the lamp frequency and motion frequency are
identical, and the numerical value can be read from the lamp's calibrated dial to
fan inaccuracy of about + 1 percent of the reading (0.01 percent in some units
with rystal-controlled time base). The range of lamp frequency of a typical unit?
is 110 to 25,000 flashes per minute. Speeds greater than 25.000 r/min can be
'measured by the following technique. Synchronism can be achieved at any flash-
ing rate r that is an integral submaltipl of the speed to be measured. n. The
flashing rate is adjusted until synchronism is achieved at the largest possible
flashing rate say r,. Then the flashing rate is slowly decreased until synchronism
is again achieved ata rater:. The unknown speed m is then given by
(4105)
For very high speeds, r, and r, are close together, giving poor accuracy. Accue
'acy can be improved by reducing the flashing rate below ry until synchronism is
'again achieved. This procedure can be continued until synchronism is obtained Nv

[AC Tachometer Generators for Rot
'An ac two-phase squirrel-cage induction motor can be used as a tachometer by
'nciting one phase with ils usual ac voltage and taking the voltage appearing at
fhe second phase as output. With the rotor stationary, the output voltage is
'sentially zero. Rotation in one direction causes at the output an ac voltage of
the same frequency as the excitation and of an amplitude proportional to the
instantancous speed. This output voltage isin phase with the excitation. Reversal
fof rotation causes the same action, exeept the phase of the output shifts 180 deg (see
Fig 475). While squirrel-cage rotors sometimes are used, the most accurate units
Employ a drag-eup rotor. This does not change the basic operating character-
istics.
A typical high-accuracy unit is excited by 115-V 400-Hz voltage, has a
sensitivity of 28 V per 1,000 r/min, nonlinearity of 005 percent from 0 to 3,600
'rimin, negligible rotor friction, rotor inertia of 7 g-cm?, and a total weight of
{67 on. Most commercial ae tachometers are designed 10 be used in ac servo-
mechanisms which conventionally operate on either 60 or 400 Hz, and so they
fre generally designed for operation at these frequencies. For general-purpose
fotion measurement, the frequency response of such units is limited (as are all ac
Or "cartct"-type devices) by the carrier frequency to about one-tenth to one-fifth
Of the carrer frequency. It is usually possible, however, to excite a tachometer
'esigned for 400 Hz at considerably higher frequencies if necessary, although
'ome of or all the performance characteristics may change value.

Eddy-Current Drag-Cup Tachometer
Figure 4.76 shows schematically an eddy-current tachometer. Rotation of the
'magnet induces voltages into the cup which thereby produce circulating eddy
'currents in the cup material. These eddy currents interact with the magnet field to
igre 476 Dragcup velocity pickup.

Jun balances the magnetic torque. Thus in steady state the angle 0, is directly
proportional toa, the input velocity. If an electric signal is desired, any low-
Torque displacement transducer can be used to measure. Dynamic operation is
lroverned by the rotary inertia of parts moving with @, spring stiffness and the

45 RELATIVE-ACCELERATION MEASUREMENTS.
"Transducers directly sensitive to relative acceleration are rare. Figure 477 shows
cone type commercially available." When the shaft rotates at constant speed, eddy
'urrents generated in the rotating conductive disk are constant and proportional
to speed. These currents produce a magnetic field which links the two pickup
vil, but no coil voltage is produced because the field is steady. When the shaft
fecelcrates, a changing magnetic field causes coil output voltages which are pro-
portional to acceleration, Speed of response is limited by both electric resonance
in the coils and torsional mechanical resonance. Connecting coils in series in-
Creases sensitivity by a factor of 4 (compared to parallel) but reduces electric
igure 47 Relative acceration pickup.

46 SEISMIC. (ABSOLUTE-) DISPLACEMENT PICKUPS
While the most general motion of a rigid body involves three-dimensional trans-
lation and rotation, the total vector quantities usually are not amenable to direct
'measurement. So we employ an array of uniaxial transducers oriented along,
selected axes to measure orthogonal components of the vectors, which are then
combined by calculation to define the total vector magnitudes and directions.
Figure 4.78 shows the general construction of a seismic-displacement pickup
{or uniaxial translatory or rotary motions. These devices are used almost exclus-

48 SEISMIC- (ABSOLUTE:) ACCELERATION PICKUPS.
(ACCELEROMETERS)
"The most important pickup for vibration," shock, and general-purpose absolute-
motion measurement isthe accelerometer. This instrument is commercially avai-
fable in a wide variety of types and ranges to meet correspondingly diverse appli-
cation requirements. The basis for this popularity lie in the following features
1. Frequency response is from zero to some high limiting value, Steady acceler-
ations can be measured (except in piezoelectric types)
2. Displacement and velocity can be easily obtained by electrical integration,
which is much preferred to differentiation.
3. Measurement of transient (shock) motions is more readily achieved than with
displacement or velocity pickups.
4, Destructive forces in machinery, etc, often are related more closely to acceler-
ation than to velocity of displacement.
* 6, Buadupan E Males and M. Ras "Vibration measurement." M, Nijof, oston, 1986,

-- 354 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 348
324 weASuRING Devic
'The basic accelerometer configuration is again that of Fig. 4.78. The oper-
ating principle is as follows: Suppose the acceleration x, to be measured is
'constant. Then, in steady state, the mass M will be at rest relative to the case, and.
thus its absolute acceleration will also be X, If mass M is accelerating at %, there
'must be some force to cause this acceleration, and if M is not moving relative to
the case, this foree ean come only from the spring. Since spring deflection x, is
proportional to force, which in turn is proportional to acceleration, x, is @
'measure of acceleration ,. Thus absolute-acceleration measurement is reduced
to the measurement ofthe force required to accelerate a known mass (sometimes
called the "proof" mass}. This dependence on mass leads to problems (mainly in
imertial guidance systems, not in vibration measurement) since a mass also experi.
{ences forces due to gravitational fields. Thus an accelerometer cannot distinguish
between a force due to acceleration and a force de to gravity.
'The majority of accelerometers may be classified as either deflection type or
rull-balance type. Those used for vibration and shock measurement are usually
the deflection type whereas those used for measurement of gross motions of
vehicles (submarines, aircraft, spacecraft, etc) may be either type, with the nul
balance being used when extreme accuracy is needed,

<ducer employed, and type of damping provided, Since the desired input is now &,,

'The low-frequency response is limited by the piezoelectric characteristic
<Di(eD +1) while the highcfrequency response is limited by mechanical res.
'nance. The damping ratio ¢ of piezoelectric accelerometers is not usually quoted
by the manufacturer, but can be taken as zero for most practical purposes. The
'accurate (5 percent high at the high-frequency end and $ percent low at the
low-frequency end) frequency range of such an accelerometer is 3/t < « < 0.20,
Accurate low-frequency response requires large s, which is usually achieved by
use of high-impedance voltage amplifiers or charge amplifiers. Systems designed
for response below about 1.0 H7 and subject to temperature transients may
exhibit errors because of the pyroclectric effect present in most piezoelectric
'materials. Here a charge output is produced in response to a temperature input
For systems with negligible response at low frequencies, these temperature.
induced signals (because they are "slow" transients) produce litle output. But
'serious errors may occur if + has been made large to measure low-frequency
accelerations and ifthe accelerometer has not been designed to minimize thermal
effects
'The design details of piezoelectric acelerometers can be varied to emphasize
selected features of performance desired for particular applications. No single
Configuration is ideal forall situations since tradeoffs exist here just ax in all
engineering design, Figure 4:81 shows several designs from a single manufacture
'The basic compression design (Fig. 481a) is the simplest and most rugged and
hhas the best mass/sensitivity ratio; but because the housing acts as an integral
part of the spring/mass system, this type is more sensitive to spurious inputs.
[For piezoclectric accelerometers these include temperature,' acoustic noise,
"base bending" (surface strains caused by bending at the mounting surface,
ctoss-axis motion, and magnetic fields.] The spring is generally preloaded to
ork the piezoelectric material at a more linear portion ofits charge-strain curve.
This preload also allows measurement of both positive and negative accelerations
'without putting the piezo material in tension. That i, the initial preload causes
an output voltage of a certain polarity. However, this immediately leaks off; and
the polarity of subsequent acceleration-induced voltages will follow the direction

single two-conductor cable which carries both amplifier power and the measur
ment signal connects the instrument to a simple constant-current power supply,
which also provides a high-level (a few volts) output signal directly to oscillo-
Scopes of signal analyzers. This arrangement allows greater sensitivity with
Smaller, higherrequency accelerometer, reduces eable-noise effects and length
limitations, and lowers costs. These advantages are traded for a reduced temper

328 weasunINe vewiers
accelerometer itsel) and less versatile signal conditioning the built-in amplifiers
allow litle oF no adjustment)
Piezoelectric accelerometers are available in a very wide range of specifi-
cations from a large number of manufacturers. The sensitivity-frequency response
tradeoff is apparent in typical specifications; a shock accelerometer may have
0.004 pC/g and natural freugency of 250.000 Hz, while @ unit designed for low:
level seismic measurements has 1,000 pC/g and 7,000 Hz. The smallest units
(ceded to reduce mass-loading errors in measurements on light structures) are
about 3 x 3 mm with 0.5-g mass including cable. Response to spurious thermal
'and base-bending inputs for the best isolated-shear designs may be 200 times less
than for designs not optimized in this respect. Triaxial units as small as a 7-mm
cube with I-g mass are available. Uncooled instruments usable over a temper
ature range of ~450 to +1500 deg F typically exhibit a £10 percent sensitivity
change from ~100 to + 1500 deg F and may be designed to survive radiation envi-
ronments typical of nuclear reactors. A system of accelerometers, cabling, and
signal conditioning designed for economical implementation of multichannel
vibration tests uses inexpensive piezo film transducers as plug-in modules to ereate
'uniaxial, biaxial, or triaxial sensing points for benign laboratory environments."
Piezoelectric accelerometers tend to have somewhat larger cross-axis sensi-
tivities than other types; however, this is typically held to about 2 to 4 percent
and usualy is not a critical factor. Some manufacturers indicate the location of
'the cross axis of least transverse sensitivity, allowing the user to orient the instru
'ment so as to minimize this effect in each installation, Accelerometers may be
'mounted with threaded studs (the preferred method), with cement or wax adhes-
ives, or with magnetic holders. The main effect of the various mounting methods
is a reduction of the natural frequency below that of the unmounted accelerome-
ter itself, as a result of elastic and inertial characteristics of the mounting. The
lunmounted resonant frequency is measured by suspending the accelerometer
freely from its cable, exciting the piezo element electrically (sinusoidally), and
finding the frequency at which current and voltage are in phase. Mounted natural
frequency is the frequency of peak voltage output when the accelerometer is
'mechanically vibrated sinusoidally. For a solid stel stud (the optimum mount
ing), the mounted natural frequency is typically about 60 percent of the un-
'mounted. Experience has shown that for measurements above 5 kH2, the stec!
stud mounting is significantly improved when a drop of light oil is applied
between the mating surfaces before torquing the connection tight. Apparently a
sliffer clastic coupling is achieved by the oil filing microscopic voids. at the
interface. When electrically insulated studs are used to reduce electric noise prob-
lems arising from ground loops, the lower stiffness of the insulating material
'causes reduced frequency response. Data on this and other mounting effects are
available in the literature?

tetween the electrodes "pumps" air parallel to the plate surface and through
holes in the plate to provide squecze-ilm damping. Since air viscosity is less
temperature-senstive than oil, the desired 0.7 damping ratio changes only about
15 percent per 100 deg F. A family of such instruments with full-seale ranges from
'202g (4H flat response) to ++ 1,000g (3000 Hz), cross-axis sensitivity fxs than

hin cube weighing 1.7 07.

'Null-Balance- (Servo-) Type Accelerometers?
Sovcalled servoaccelerometers using the principle of feedback have been devel-
'ped for applications requiring greater accuracy than is generally achieved with
{nstruments using mechanical springs as the force-to-displacement transducer. In
these null-balance instruments the acceleration-sensitive mass is kept very close
to the zoro-displacement position by sensing this displacement and generating a
magnetic force which is proportional to this displacement and which always
'Opposes motion ofthe mass from neutral. Ths restoring force plays the same role
4s the mechanical spring force in @ conventional accelerometer. Thus we may
'Consider the mechanical spring to have been replaced by an electrical "spring.
'The advantages derived from this approach are the greater linearity and lack of
hhystereis of the electrical spring as compared with the mechanical one, Also, in
some cases, electrical damping (which can often be made less temperature-
Sensitive than mechanical damping) may be employed. There is also the possi
bility of testing the static and dynamic performance of the device just prior to a
test run by introducing electrically excited test forces into the system. This con-
'enient and rapid remote self-checking feature can be quite important in complex
lind expensive tests where it is extremely important that all systems operate
'Correctly before the test is commenced. These servoaccelerometers usually are
Used for general-purpose motion measurement and low-Irequency vibration, Also
they are particularly useful in acceleration-control systems since the desited value
'of acceleration can be put into the system by introducing a proportional current
{from an external source.

Accelerometers for Inertial Navigation
Inertial navigation is accomplished in principle by measuring the absolute accel-
ration (usually in terms of three mutually perpendicular components of the
fotalacceleration vector) of the vehicle and then integrating these acceleration
Signals twice to obtain displacement from an initial known starting location
'Thus instantaneous position is always known without the need for any communi-
ation with the world outside the vehicle. To keep the accelerometers' sensitive
Sixes always oriented parallel to their original starting position, elaborate stable
platforms using gyroscopic references and feedback systems are necessary. Since
the accelerometers are sensitive also to gravitational force, this force must be
'computed and corrections applied continuously, Since the inertial navigation
System measures absolute motion, systems for navigation over the carth's surface
{Such as for submarines) must include means for compensating for the earth's
'own motions.
'While accelerometers for such navigation systems must operate on essentially
the same basie principles that we considered above, their extreme performance
fequitements and the desi {o obtain integrals of the acceleration rather than
+, Cady, @-Flex Accelerometer Construction and Principe of Operation, TNO, Sundstand
'ata Conta Redmond, Wash. 1984
deg Mode! A Tat Tek, Daa, Sundstrand Data Conta

'acceleration itself lead to special techniques and configurations. The desire for
compatibility with the required data-processing computers (often digital) also
influences the designer's choice of alternatives. The details ofthese applic
'are beyond the seope ofthis book, but may be found in numerous references !
Mechanical Loading of Accelerometers on the Test Object?
The attachment of an accelerometer to a vibrating system results in a change in
the motion measured as compared with the undisturbed ease. We can apply
'general impedance principles to this problem to calculate the significance of this
effect in any particular instance. In doing so, a useful simplification, which is
adequate in most cases isto regard the entire accelerometer as one rigid mass
'equal to the total mass of the instrument. This approximation generally holds
since accelerometers are used below their natural frequency and thus there i little
'relative motion ofthe proof mass and the instrument ease. For simple structures,
such as beams, the shift in resonant frequency due to added mass can be estimated
from theory?
49 CALIBRATION OF VIBRATION PICKUPS
'While the response of vibration pickups to interfering and modifying inputs such
4s temperature, acoustic noise, and magnetic fields is often of interest, ere we are
'concerned with the response to the desired input of displacement, velocity, oF
'acceleration. An excellent reference giving a more complete treatment of this
Subject is available* Here we briefly touch on some of the main points
'The calibration methods in wide use may be classified into three broad types:
constant acceleration, sinusoidal motion, and transient motion. Constant-
'acceleration methods (which are suitable only for calibrating accelerometers) in
clude the tlting-support method and the centrifuge. The tilting-support method
Utilizes the accelerometer's inherent sensitivity to gravity. Static "accelerations"
'over the range +14 may be accurately applied by fastening the accelerometer to

'a tilting support whose tilt angle from vertical is accurately measured. This
method requites that the accelerometer respond to state accelerations: therefore
tmost piezoelectric devices cannot be calibrated in this way. The accuracy of the
method depends on the accuracy of angle measurement and the knowledge of
Tocal gravity. The accuracy is ofthe order of +00003g, In the centrifuge method,
the sensitive axis of the accelerometer is radially disposed on a rotating hor-
{zontal disk so that it experiences the normal acceleration of uniform circular
motion. Static accelerations in the range 0 to 60,001 are achievable with an
lccuracy of +1 percent, The allowable weight ofthe pickup varies from 100 Ib at
100g to 11h at 60.000y.
"The sinusoidal-motion method is exemplified by the calibration facility of the
'National Bureau of Standards. This consists of a modified cletrodynamic vibra
tion shaker which has been carefully designed to provide uniaxial pure sinusoidal
'motion and which is equipped with an accurately calibrated moving-coil velocity
pickup to measure its table motion. If motion is known to be purely sinusoidal,
Knowledge of its velocity and frequency enables accurate calculation of the dis-
placement and acceleration, (The motion frequency is easily obtained with high
'hecuracy by electronic counters) This technique is thus useful for displacement,
'elocty, of acceleration pickups. The particular equipment referred to above can
'calibrate pickups (obtaining both amplitude ratio and phase angle) of a weight up
fo 2Ib over the frequency range 8 to 2.000 Hz. The acceleration range available is
0 to 25y, velocity range is 0 to 50 in/s, and displacement range is 0 t0 0: in
"Accuracy is +1 percent from 8 to 900 Hz and +2 percent from 900 to 2.000 He
Calibration of primary standards such as the National Bureau of Standards
moving-coil velocity pickup above is accomplished by the so-called absolute
'method. with the reciprocity? and laser interferometer? techniques being the most
common. These are both rather involved procedures reserved for standards-
laboratory use. Figure 486 shows typical apparatus forthe laser method, as used
to calibrate a high-precision piezoelectric accelerometer which is then utilized as
fa "transfer standard" to conveniently calibrate other accelerometers by @ com-
parison method. The laser method actually measures displacement amplitude x,
land relies on the purity of the shakers sinusoidal motion, plus acura frequency
'measurement, to infer acceleration amplitude as wx,, The particular accelerome-
ter shown is made with a mirror-fnish surface to reflect the laser light: thus there
is'no need to attach a separate mirror when calibration is performed. When the
'accelerometer has a displacement amplitude x,, the numberof fringes counted by
the phototransistor observing the interferometer output is 8x,/2 for each com
plete vibration eycle, which gives 12.64 cycles/um when a laser with 7 = 06328
Jum is used, For a typical calibration frequency of 160 Hz, a 10 mis* peak
fceleration corresponds to a displacement of 99 jum, which gives 125.1 fringes

4.11 PENDULOUS (GRAVITY-REFERENCED)
ANGULAR-DISPLACEMENT SENSORS,
In a number of applications the measurement of angular displacement relative to
the local vertical (gravity vector) isa useful technique. Examples include sensing
'loments for control systems of road-paving and seraping machines: drainage-
filelaying machines: alignment of construction forms, piles and bridges: and
{itude control of vehicles (such as submarines) and torpedoes or missiles. These
felatively simple instruments (basically plumb bobs with electrical output) can
ometimes replace more complex and expensive gyroscopic instruments which
perform similar functions. Their main disadvantages relative to gyros are their
Sensitivity to interfering translatory acceleration inputs and their dependence on
ty field (They do not work in essentially gravity-ree space)

'commercial sensors do not include the springs K, ; we include them here because
their presence makes possible interesting and potentially useful dynamic beha-
vior. For the usual case of no springs, K, is simply set equal to zero. The damping
'effect is not essential to the theoretical Operation of the device, but is included in
'most practical instruments to reduce oscillations at pendulum frequency caused
by transient interfering inputs. A variety of electrical displacement transducers
may be employed, depending on the required characteristics; a potentiometer is
shown for simplicity. The following assumptions are justiable for most purposes

|. Angles are small enough that the sine and the angle are nearly equal and the
cosine is nearly 1
2. The inertia effect ofthe fluid on the pendulum motion is negligible.
3. The damping effect ofthe Mui is limited to the damping gap.
4. All dry-friction effects in pot wipers, bearings, and slip rings may be neglected
for dynamic analysis,
5. The buoyant force on the pendulum is negligible
6. The springs provide a linear restoring torque.

'The pendulum sensor is unfortunately sensitive to horizontal accelerations,
and so its application is ruled out where such accelerations are large enough t0
'cause a significant output signal. A simple analysis shows that a steady horizontal

'A commercial pendulum using a potentiometer motion pickup has a full
scale range of +8 . natural frequency of 2 Hz damping ratio of 06, and resol-
Ution of 0.1 (O08. if vibration is present Pendulums are also available for
measuring fotation about two mutually perpendicular axes.
Closely related to the pendulum isthe Tiltmeter® sensor of Fig. 490. deg based
'on the ancient carpenters spirit level. A cylindrical glass cavity has a spherical
inner top surface plated with four equally spaced platinum strips which act as
electrodes: the flat bottom surface has a single central pin electrode. The cavity is
partially filed with a conductive liquid, which leaves at the top a bubble that
Covers equal areas of the four top electrodes when the device is level. Opposite
Clectrodes are connected to form from the Muid body two adjacent legs of
bridge. while two matched resistors complete the bridge which is ac-excited by
applying a voltage between the bottom electrode and the common termination
point of the resistors. As the case is tilted about one avis, the bubble. which
Femains fixed with respect t0 the gravity vector, moves to partially uncover one
cap electrode and cover the opposite. Thus the bridge is unbalanced as a result of
the resistance changes of the Buid bodies caused by their changed contact with
the upper electrodes. Two' such bridges are formed to resolve a biaxial tilt into
two perpendicular components, Analysis* shows each axis of the device responds
'essentially as an overdamped simple pendulum to desired tilt input and spurious
inputs of horizontal displacement and acceleration, with the typical frequency
response (f, = | Hz) shown in Fig. 4:90. Sensitivity is extremely high; angles the
'order of 10 deg * degree can be measured. To prevent overranging the device, it must
be level within about 0.05". If angular motions larger than this are expected, we
can build a servocontrolled tilt table, mount the sensor on this table, and use it
as the servo's null detector.
4,12 GYROSCOPIC (ABSOLUTE) ANGULAR-DISPLACEMENT.
AND VELOCITY SENSORS
While gyroscopic instruments have been used in limited mumbers and a
cations (gyracompasses in ships and aircraf,turn-and-bank indicators in aircraft,
tlc) since around World War I, developments during and after World War II
brought them to an extreme degree of refinement, and their use is now common
in military and commercial appications.® In most of these applications the gyr0
is used to measure the motion of some vehicle, either as a permanent part of @
vehicle measurement-control system or as a test instrument temporarily attached
to gather data, Less common, but still important, applications include oil well

Perhaps the simplest gyro configuration is the free gyro shown in Fig. 491
These instruments are used to measure the absolute angular displacement of the
vehicle to which the instrument frame is attached. A single free gyro can measure
rotation about two perpendicular axes, such as the angles 0 and @. This can be
'accomplished because the axis of the spinning gyro wheel remains fixed in space
(if the gimbal bearings are frictionless) and thus provides a reference for the
relative-motion transducers. If the angles to be measured do not exceed about
10", the readings of the relative-displacement transducers give directly the abso-
lute rotations with good accuracy. For larger rotations of both axes, howev
there isan interaction effect between the two angular motions, and the transduce?
dings do not accurately represent the absolute motions of the vehicle, The free
gyro is also limited to relatively short-time applications (les than about $ min)
since gimbal-bearing friction eauses gradual drift (loss of initial reference) of the

[A high sensitivity is achieved by large angular momentum H, and soft spring K,,
although low K, gives a low «,.. Natural frequencies of commercially available
rate gyros are of the order of 10to 100 Hz. The damping ratio is usualy set at 0.3
to 07. Large angular momentum is obtained in small size by using high-speed
{often 24,000 r/min) motors to spin the gyro wheel. Full-scale ranges of about
£10 t0 +1000 degrees are readily available, Resolution of a + 10 degree/s range
instrument is ofthe order of 0.005 degrees. In some high-performance rate gyros,
the mechanical spring is replaced by an "electrical spring" arrangement similar
{0 that used in the servo accelerometer of Fig. 48.
'To measure all three components (roll pitch, and yaw) of angular velocity in
4a vehicle, an arrangement of three rate gyros, such as in Fig. 495, may be
'employed. It should be pointed out that in a rate gyro only the output angle @
must be kept small. This requires use of a very sensitive motion pickoff, but such
are available. The input angle ¢ may be indefinitely large since no matter how
large it gets the spin angular-momentum vector is always perpendicular (except
{or a small error resulting from nonzero 0) to the input angular-velocity vector.

* General Pression lc. Kesftt Di Lite Fa NJ

382 MEASURING Devices
This is done by using the motion signals from the gyros to drive servomecha-
nisms which maintain the platform in a fixed angular orientation, The system
accelerometers are mounted on this platform, and their double-integrated output
is an accurate measure of vehicle motion along the three orthogonal axes since
the platform always moves parallel to it initial orientation.
The rate-integrating gyro has been subjected to extremely intensive and ex-
tensive engineering development to bring its performance characteristics to re
'markable levels while maintaining small size and weight and great resistance to
rugged environments. This has been accomplished by painstaking attention to
'minute mechanical, thermal, and electrical details which would be completely
negligible in a less sophisticated device. Figure 496 shows floated rate-
integrating gyro which incorporates a number of clever design featutes necessary
to high performance. The inner gimbal isin the form of a hollow cylinder con-
taining the gyro wheel and is mounted in precision pivot jewel bearings. A small
radial gap between this cylinder and its housing is filed with damping fluid to
form the damper B. Temperature feedback-control systems regulate electric heat
rs 10 maintain damping-flud viscosity constant. This heating must be carefully
applied so as to minimize convection currents in the damping fluid which would
'exert spurious torques on the inner gimbal. The density of the damping fluid
(which completely surrounds the hollow cylinder) is adjusted so as to obtain
'neutral buoyancy (this explains the "floated gyro" terminology), thereby removing
Fire 496 Fost, ateintepating gyro (Courtesy Honeywell Icy Miteapoli, Man)

-- 383 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 377
LMOTON AND DIMENSIONAL MEASUREMENT 383,
the radial load from the jewel bearings and reducing their already-low friction
even more, A further 10:1 bearing-fiction reduction is achieved by vibrating
(Cdithering") the pivot bearings at low amplitude and high frequency by means of
electrically driven piezo crystals. (Magnetic noncontact bearings" are also some~
times used to reduce friction.) A further advantage of the floated construction is
'an inerease in shock and vibration tolerance since ease shocks are transmitted (0
the inner gimbal through the damping fluid rather than the rather fragile jewel
bearings.
'At original assembly, the inner gimbal is very carefully statically balanced
since any unbalance creates a spurious gravity torque. When such a gyro is buil
into, say, an ICBM, it may be inactive for ong periods, and various environmen-
tal and aging effects can cause a loss of balance. To correct for such unbalance
'without removing the gyro from the missle of even disturbing its hermetic seals,
the eutectic gimbal balance pan is provided. Ifthe gyro is activated by running its
'wheel up to speed, the gyro becomes a very sensitive detector of unbalance since
the inner gimbal will slowly precess under the action of the unbalanced gravity
torque and this motion will be detected by the 4 displacement transducer (signal
generator). To rebalance the gimbal, an electric current is applied to melt a
'eutectic alloy in the balance pan so that the molten metal migrates to move its
'mass center and thereby regain balance. When gimbal precession stops, rebalance
hhas been obtained and the metal is allowed to solidly. Gyro wheel bearings of
the hydrodynamic gas type contribute to long life and low noise since they ride
'on film of gas with no metal-to-metal contact, except momentarily when st
ing or stopping. The permanent-magnet torque generator allows precise electrical
application of inner gimbal torques for sel-esting and correction procedures. An
joelastic" design for the inner gimbal bearing suspension ensures that elastic
deflections caused by case liner accelerations will be in the same direction as the
acceleration, This means that the inertia torque will not have a moment about
the center of mass, which would cause a spurious gyro output
Recent developments in absolute angular-motion sensors have included both
'exotic versions of the classical "spinning wheel" concept and totally new devices
'evoid of gyroscopic effects but often still called "gyros" because they perform
the same measurement function as the traditional mechanical instrument, Elec
trostatie gyros? utilize feedback-type electrostatic systems to levitate a [-om-
diameter beryllium sphere spinning at 146,000 r/min without physical contact
inside a chamber evacuated to 10 * tore. The gyto is a free-otor type with
tiworanis angle-measuring capability for large angles, which makes it especially
suitable for strapdown navigation systems. A deliberate mass unbalance in the
Spinning sphere causes motions related to sphere angular attitude which are
Picked up by a capacitance-displacement sensor that uses eight electrodes ar-
Fanged octahedrally over the spherical housin's inner surface. These electrodes
are Limeshared between the suspension servosystem and the angle-measuring

system, with 20 percent of the duty cycle being devoted to angle measurement.
Extreme accuracy is obtained by calibrating each gyeo to obtain numerical values
for a sophisticated error model containing 56 sources of error. This model is

'echnology) to overcome basic electromechanical hardwace limitations in instru-

'Optical (ring-laser) "gyros" do not use gyroscopic principles at all. Rather,
they measure phase shifts between two laser beams directed around a loop by
mirrors fastened t0 the object whose rotation is to be measured. One beam
travels with the rotation while the other travels against it, causing @ phase shift
Proportional to rotation when the beams are recombined and compared. The
Practical realization of this basic concept uses two active optical oscillators inter-
nal to the loop. rather than an external light source. These oscillators (lasers) are
arranged so that rotation produces a frequency difference between them. In @
typical instrument with a triangular optical path of 84-in perimeter and laser
wavelength of 0.6328 jm, a 1 rad/s rotation rate produces a 65-kHz output
frequency. Sine the time integral of the output-pulse rae is proportional to input
angle, « simple summing of pulses gives a ratesintegrating-gyro type of response
'with a resolution of | count for 3.1 seconds of arc of rotation. The development
'of the no-moving-parts ring-laser gyro appears to be synergistic with that of the
'microcomputer since the two together make the use of so-called strapdown guid
ance systems very attractive. These systems, rather than using complex servo-
systems to maintain the gyro mounting plaiform in a fixed attitude, strap the
yro directly to the vehicle frame and compute, from the gyro signals, the axis
transformation data required to convert from the vehicle reference frame to the
'necessary inertial coordinates, The low-cost, compact microcomputers thus allow
us to substitute computing power for electromechanical hardware
'A recent variation? on the laser gyro principle utilizes a long optical fiber
'wound in a multiturn coil to replace the mirrors. Angular-rate sensors utilizing
fluid (nongyroscopic) principles include the fuidic vortex device? and hot-wire
jet-deflection sensor.* Figure 497 deg shows the latter device, which utilizes vibra-
{ing piezo crystals to circulate a steady laminar Rlow of gas in sealed chamber.
'The gas jet passes over two hot-wire flow sensors (See hot-wire anemometers in

'ease about the input axis causes a lateral deflection of the gas stream, differential
'cooling ofthe wires and a bridge-unbalance voltage proportional to angular rate

In this short section we can only indicate a few major concepts while many
significant details are neglected. A few samples' of the voluminous literature are
'mentioned below.
4.13, COORDINATE-MEASURING MACHINES,
Modern manufacturing processes for discrete pars require fast and accurate mea-
suring devices to check critical dimensions against their specified values. In a
'numerically controlled machining center with an automatic tool changer, one of
the tool positions may be occupied by a gaging probe, so that each part can be
'measured, and corrections made, as that partis being machined. This is called
in-process gaging. When this approach is not possible or desirable, off-line gaging
may be used. Here the parts are not measured until they leave the machining
process and reach a separate gaging station. Each part (or perhaps a statistically
Selected partial sample of the total production) is then measured, and statistical
data on average values and deviations are accumulated. These data ate periodically
'compared with part specifications, and machine-stting corrections are made when
necessary. Many gaging techniques ranging from simple, manually operated ones
to complex, computer-controlled systems have been developed for in-process and.
'off-line gaging? In this section we briefly describe a versatile offline technique
based on locating critical part features in a Cartesian coordinate system,
'\ typical coordinate-measuring machine (CMM) has a probe mounted on &
set of three mutually perpendicular slides such that the probe can be positioned
fat any desired x, y, 2 location within the machine's working space. Different

motions, Both precision rolling-contact bearings and hydrostatic air bearings have
'been successfully used to achieve accuracies as good as about 1 xm fora slide with
[em travel. "Volumetric? (three-dimensional) accuracy is also important but more
dificult to specify and measure. Many machines may be fitted with a precision
'rotary table, allowing even more versatile application. igure 499" shows a sample
'measured part and the kinds of features which may be routinely checked. Small
CMMs of modest accuracy may cost $10,000 to $20,000 while larger, more accurate
machines range up to several hundred thousand dollars (1989 prices.
'Although various types of probes may be used with CMMs first we discuss
the widely used touch-trigger type, invented in the early 1970s. Here the probe is
used only to provide a sensitive and reproducible indication of when the probe
touches the measured part; the three-dimensional position of the probe is actually
read from the slide-position transducers on each machine axis. Thus to measure
the width w of the part shown in Fig. 4.100, we drive the slide from postion 1
in the negative x ditection until contact is made, at which instant the x, 9,
readings will be "frozen" so that we (or a machine memory) can record them. Then
wwe move the slide to position 2 (same y and = readings as position 1) and drive
it in the positive x direction until a "touch" signal again freezes the readings.
Knowing the diameter of the probe's spherical tip, we can easily ealeulate w from
the dillerence betwoen the two x readings. In an actual CMM, the probe may be
positioned anywhere in the working space manually (air bearings and z-axis
'counterweight allow you to grasp the probe body in one hand and easily move
all three slides where you wish) by using a joystick control to manually command
clectric motor drives at preselected speeds or by using computer-commanded
clectric motor servodrives to accomplish the desired moves automatically under
program conteol. This last computer numerical control capability provides @
"LK Too! Divison, Ciaciaat, Milacon, Tempe, Ai

-- 390 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 384
360 seasunine nevis
'powerful measurement tool when itis combined with software to automate the
various geometric calculations needed to extract part features from slide-position
readings. A common and important use of CMMs is to check, for conformance
to specifications, the first machined part in a production run from some numer-
ically controlled machine tool. This verification ofthe part-programming process
'nd all other aspects of machining is necessary before you can confidently proceed
with the production run.
'Asin so many other measurement systems, the addition of powerful computing
capability to basic CMM functions has greatly increased the versatility, ease of
use, speed, and accuracy of these machines. For example, parts of basically
rectangular shape need not be carefully aligned with the fixed x, y, 2 coordinate
system of the CMM before measurements are taken. Rather, the partis fixed in
'any convenient position, and three mutually perpendicular part faces are chosen
to define a part coordinate system with axes x, Jp, 2». By touching the probe 10
three widely spaced points on each part surface, the equations of the three planes
which define the part axes can be numerically calculated and the intersection
point of these planes (origin of xp, yp.) can be solved for. Once the part co-
'ordinate system has thus been located in the machine's axis system, all further
'part measurements can be automatically converted from machine coordinates to
'the more convenient part coordinates. Computing power has thus replaced
tedious physical part-alignment procedure with a convenient mathematical rotation
of the axes. Similar geometric algorithms allow us to determine the center co-
'ordinates and radius of a citeular hole by touching the probe at three or more
points on the hole's circumference. For any of the common geometric features of
'machined parts (lines, planes, circles, eylinders, cones, spheres, ete), analytic
'geometry tells us the minimum number of "touch points" necessary to define that
feature. Because real machined surfaces are never perfect geomettic objets (and
to increase statistical reliability ofthe results), itis conventional to use more touch
points than are theoretically necessary. We then need to use some kind of "least-
squares" algorithm to compute the "best" estimate of the feature's parameters.
Although lines and planes canbe fitted with familiar algorithms, even the seemingly
simple circle will not yield to "ordinary" least-squares curve-fitting techniques. If
readers familiar with standard least-squares curve-fitting routines availablein many
'computer libraries try them on a circle, they will find that
1. The circle is a double-valued function
2. The equations to be solved are nor the usual set of linear algebraic equations.
3 For certain physically possible touch points, impossible results or calculations
arise, such as taking square roots of negative numbers or finding angles whose
'cosines exceed 1.0,
'These problems defeat the conventional linear or nonlinear regression techniques,
and thus special algorithms must be developed.' Algorithms actually used on
"1 Kasa, A ice Fiting Procedare and Its Ero Anas, IEEE Tron, Int. Mest, vl IM-25,
pp. 8-14 March 1976

-- 391 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 385
MOTION AND DIMENSIONAL MEASURE 361
(CMMSs are considered proprietary by the CMM manufacturers, and 1 was unable
to obtain any details.
'We now want to give a little more detail on probe types [passive ("hard"),
'witching ("ouch trigger"), analog-proportional, analog-nulling] and machine
'operating modes (freeAlating manual, driven manual, direct computer-controlled),
Perhaps the simplest operation is free-floating manual mode witha passive probe.
For free-floating operation, the machine slides must be nearly frietion-free (as
with hydrostatic air bearings), since the human operator simply grasps the probe
hholder and moves all three axes, to position the probe at the feature to be mea
sured. The "probe" is nothing but a rigid piece of metal whose shape is appro-
priate to the feature being gaged. For simple width measurement ofa rectangular
part, the probe could just be a cylinder of known diameter which is manually
pressed against the flat part surface (once for each side}. Clearly such a method
will suffer from variability in the force applied by the operator: however, itis
'entirely satisfactory for many applications. The centers of holes are quite nicely
Tocated with a conical probe, which is selicentering as i is gently lowered into
the hole
Perhaps the most widely used probe is the touch-trigger' type, usable in all
three machine operating modes. As explained briefly earlier, this probe isan on/off
switching type which "freezes" the readings of the thre slide motion sensors as the
probe tip touches and is deflected by the part surface, Its most common form is
Shown in Fig. 4.101, The probe stylus is kinematically located in a single unique
position by the six contacts of the three eylindrical rods with the six balls with @
light spring preload maintaining this position when no external forces are applied
to the stylus. The six contacts are electrically wired in series, as shown, and a
constant-current source of about 0.5 mA is connected. The total resistance of the
Six contacts in the neutral position is on the order of a few ohms, making the
'Voltage ¢, a few millivolts, When the probe's spherical tip is deflected against the
spring preload by contact with a measured part, one or more of the contact
resistances increase very greatly with tiny deflections. When the total resistance
exceeds about 3000 0, voltage eg passing through 1.5 V trips. circuit which freezes
fll thee side-position readouts, recording the positon of the probe atthe instant
'of touch. A uniquely favorable feature ofthe probe is its three-dimensional nature;
tip deflections in +x, ++y, +2 directions will all cause triggering, thus the probe
'may approach the measured part from various directions. Note that ~z forces
are opposed, not by the light spring preload, but by the very stiff rod-and-ball
contacts, Thus the -z direction cannot be used for gaging: however, this is rarely
a problem,
'An important detail of probe operation which was ignored in Fig. 4.100 is
probe bending and "pretravel." The probe does not actualy trigger atthe instant
'of touch since it does require a small, bt finite, force and deflection to increase
the electric resistance to the 3000-0 trigger point. Also bending deflection of the
probe (minimized by using shor, stiff probes whenever possible) causes a small
" Reis Ine Stauber

| Ke
unmeasured deflection between touching and triggering. Fortunately, these effects
are largely repeatable and may be corrected by calibration. For example, in Fig.
4.100 before measuring an unknown w, one would measure a precisely known W
(such as a gage block), to find the "effective working diameter" d, of the probe
from the equation

"Then this one d, value can be used to correct for all three effets (ball diameter,
pretravel, and bending) by using the formula
'Actual size = measured size ~ de
{In practice, d, is usually found by touching a calibration sphere at about 10 points
'on the sphere' surface and using a special algorithm to compute d.. This more
Complicated scheme is better since it"exereises" the probe's characteristics in many
directions, making the d, value more correct for a general measurement.
"Although the construction and operation ofthe probe seem quite simple, con
tinuous high accuracy and reliability depend on subile design details and use of
"appropriate materials, Lubricants with special electrical, lubricating and corrosion-
resistant properties, for example, ate used on the ball-rod contacts. Probes used
for in-process gaging with, say, a lathe (rather than in a CMM) use higher spring
preloads to prevent false triggering due to vibration, etc. This degrades some
ther probe characteristics, such as accuracy, but these tradeoffs are necessary
to optimize the probe's overall performance for a specific application. Special
'materials are also used for the rods, balls, and stylus tips. Rod and ball materials
lire considered proprietary, but stylus tips are often synthetic ruby, an aluminum
'Oxide ceramic noted for hardness, smoothness, and dimensional stability. Touch-
trigger probes also have favorable overtracel characteristics. That is in touching
the probe to the part, some motion pas the touch point is unavoidable, especially
jfone is moving the probe manually. For example, a probe with 0.1-gm repeatabil-
ity, O15-g trigger force, and 10-ym tip deflection to trigger may have a safe over-
travel of £25 mm and a maximum probe approach velocity of 7 mm/s. High
probe-approach velocity speeds machine operation and increases throughput but
egeades accuracy. Driven manual operation (operator controls probe motion with
'ajovatick) and direct computer control (probe motion is commanded by computer)
'bviously give better control of probe velocity than does free-floating manual
operation.
"Analog probes give a voltage output proportional to probe deflection from
the null position, and most use LVDTs as the displacement sensor. Analog probes
'ae capable of higher resolution and accuracy than the on off touck-trigger probes,
but tend to be more fragile and expensive with poorer overtravel. Duplicating the
thrce-dimensional capabilities of touch-trigger types leads to rather complicated
'mechanical configurations. Note that to obtain a number for, say, the x location
'of the probe tip, we must now add the reading of the probe to that of the slide
motion sensor. An alternative to this is the mulling operation of the probe/CMM.
'stem, Here the probe's signal (plus or minus from null) is used as an error signal
ina feedback loop which controls the CMM slide position, causing the machine
slide to automatically drive to a position where the probe signal is zero (oF some
"elected bias position), For "three-dimensional" operation, this can lead to quite
complicated servosystems.
Although most CMMs are used to measure diserete part features, asin Fig
499 point-to-point" mode), form documentation for "seulptured surfaces" such
'a turbine blades "contouring" mode) is also possible, though it presents special

-- 394 of 1000 --

364 seasunine pevices
problems.' Here anslog nulling type probe systems have some advantages in
Smoothly following the part surface under servo control as data are taken. In
overall design philosophy, CMM manufacturers have adopted different view.
points. One approach strives for perfection in manufacture and assembly of each
'mechanical component, to achieve overall machine accuracy. Other designers
hhave opted for less stringent requirements on components followed by an indi.
Vidual calibration of each machine to obtain numerical values for a software
ertor-correction scheme built into the CMM's computer. This latter approach is
'often feasible since the major machine errors® tend to be systematic (reproducible)
rather than random. Recently CMM manufacturers and users collaborated in
Producing a national standard? devoted to defining methods for evaluating CMM
Performance. 1 found this document extremely useful and recommend it highly
{to anyone interested in CMM design and operation, Up to this point we have
concentrated on the CMM itself however, the ambient thermal environment in
which the machine operates is critical, as emphasized in B89.1.12M and explored
in detail in another standard* Large, high-accuracy machines present the greatest
'thermal problems, as related in a recent article' Theoretical finite-element studies
'of thermally induced deflections showed that when the temperature of the machine's
bridge was 06 deg C higher than its base, the bridge bowed upward by 104 yam, oF
8 times the desired measuring tolerance. This led to the design of « sophisticated
airflow and temperature-control system which maintains large volumes of the
'machine room air at 20 + 0.06 deg C, a considerable feat of engineering in ise, While
thermal problems are a major factor, barometric pressure, humidity, gaseous and
Particulate concentrations, lighting, and floor vibration may also require careful
'attention. Finally, the integration of CMMs into the total manufacturing process
is reoeving increasing study and promises great benefits as computer-aided design
(CAD), computer-aided manufacture (CAM), and computer-aided inspection (CAD),
are all coordinated

friction, wear, fatigue resistance, cleanliness in food processing, leakage in seals,
paintability scatering loses on optical surfaces, substrate surface flaws in micro-
Circuits, and visual appearance.* The study of surface properties is related to the
larger area called tribology, which includes all aspects of friction, wear, and
lubrication. The deviation of an actual surface from the theoretical ideal is de~
'scribed in terms of lay, flaws, waviness, and roughness (Fig. 4.102). Lay is the
directional aspect of the dominant surface pattern, usually determined by the
production process. Flaws are isolated defects which occur at infrequent interval.
'Waviness plus roughness is called profile and is the total vertical excursion, at a
cross section, from a fixed datum, with waviness being the long-period com-
ponent and roughness the short-petiod (closely spaced) one. In many cases, only

roughness is of interes, so the simpler instruments may provide data on it alone.
'More comprehensive instruments allow one to measure the total excursion (profile)
roughness and/or waviness by selection of instrument modes.
Most surface-finish measurements? are made with stylus instruments; how=
ever, optical types are available (though expensive) when noncontacting methods
are necessary. Typical diamond styli have tip radii of about 0.0001 in, exert about
(0.0001 to 0.001 fbf of force on the specimen, and can reach to the bottom of most
seratch marks more than 0.0002 in wide. High-resolution units for very fine sur-
faces such as gage blocks use 0.00005-in radius and reduced stylus force. The stylus
size cannot be reduced much below these values because the tip pressure begins
to exceed the yield point of most materials, causing plastic deformation of the
'measured surface. This places a fundamental limitation on the horizontal resolu
tion of these instruments. To measure roughness along a chosen line on a surface,
the stylus is dragged along this line ata fixed slow speed (0.0001 to 01 ins), using.
sufficient stylus force and slow enough scan speed that the stylus faithfully follows
the surface. The stylus may be vertically guided by an attached shoe or skid,
hereby the datum for the measurement changes as the shoe follows the local
waviness and the stylus measures mainly roughness. Ifthe total profile is to be
'measured, a shoeless probe is used and an absolute external datum is fixed by the
traversing mechanism (sce Fig. 4.103). When roughness along a curved line must
'be measured (bearing balls, gear teeth, etc}, suitable curved external datum
'mechanisms are used,

For many practical measurements, the roughness profile is processed to obtain
values for certain roughness parameters, which can then be compared numerically
with goals or specifications to decide whether the surface meets some functional
requirement. Relating machine functional requirements (such as wear life, friction
torque, heat generation, scoring and galling, etc) to numerical values of roughness
parameters i at present more art and experience than science and so perhaps is
fruitful area for research. The most common parameters are R, (the average
absolute value of the profile excursions from the mean line) and R, (the rms value),
as defined in Fig 4.105." Instruments offer a selection of sampling lengths L, with
typical set being 0.021, 0.07, 021, 070, and 2.1 in, Usually R, or R, is measured
5 times (over five consecutive values of L), and an average is computed. Some
'other parameters used less frequently (Riu Ry, Row: Ry) Ate also defined in the

'We conclude this section with a brief description of an optical surface-fnish
instrument (see Fig. 4.109)" The instrument uses enhanced phase-measurement
interferometry. Light reflected from the object under test interferes with lhght
reflected from the internal-reference surface, which is smooth within 4 to 8 A (04
{008 nm) or LS A ('supersmooth" reference needed for very smooth test objects.
'An interference fringe pattern is visible through a microscope and is also focused
'ona photodetector array (1024 linear elements forthe two-dimensional instrument,
256 x 256 area clements for the three-dimensional one). The entire intererometer
is mounted on a piezoelectric micropositioner capable of making precise. tiny
increments of motion under computer control. Each successive increment causes
an additional small phase shift in the interferometer and a change in the fringe
pattern, which can be interpreted by the computer in terms of measured
Bojectsurface profile. By using a variety of interferometers (Michelson, Mirau,
Linnik instruments with a range of specifications are available. The profile length
ranges from about 9 t0 0.0667 mm; however, a computer-controlled XY specimen
table can extend this. The spatial sampling interval is from 27 mm to 0.2 ym; the
working distance is 12mm to 0.2mm, The maximum surface heights are 8 to
(0.54 yom, vertical resolution is 1 to 3 A, maximum surface slopes are 0.26" to $4
Extensive software and color graphics are available to compute and display a wide
variety of surface characteristics.
4,15 MACHINE VISION
In the late 1970s, machine (or computer vision systems for laboratory automation
began to be practical, and this field has since shown accelerated growth, Figure
4110® classifies typical applications ofthis technology. The dividing line between
optical gaging and measuring systems, such as those discussed earlier in this
chapter, and "true" machine vision is not always clear-cut, since available defini
tions allow some room for interpretation. Machine vision attempts to duplicate
'some portion of the capabilities of the human eye and brain system for forming
fand interpreting images, and studies of human perception have becn a significant
factor in the development of machine vision. A useful definition of machine vision
requires that at least three basic functions be present: image formation, image
analysis, and image interpretation.
Tmage formation involves a proper combination of illumination type and
camera (seasor). Back-lighting provides a silhouette image of maximum contrast,

which is useful for dimensional measurements and detection of the presence oF
absence of features such as holes. Front lighting may be needed to deal with
features such as labels or bar codes. Detection of three-dimensional features may
require side lighting. Other possibilities include polarized light and "structured"
light. Structured light projects a geometrically defined light/dark pattern onto the
'measured object s0 as to enhance certain features ofthe image. Perhaps the most
tused type of structured light is a simple flat beam or "sheet" of laser light. as
Provided by a rotating or oscillating mirror' or (without moving parts) cylindrical
lenses Projecting such a sheet of light onto a target object produces a line of
reflected light, which follows the contours of the object and whose image can be
Processed to obtain various useful measurements, For example, several such sensors
can be stationed around the periphery of an automobile door to check for proper
fit during assembly by measuring the (one-dimensional) gap (Fig. 4111a). "Con-
touring" of three-dimensional objects is achieved in other applications, as shown,
in Fig. 4.1116, The light sheet optically "slices through" the measured body,
creating a cross-sectional image which can be observed by a suitable camera and
analyzed to obtain desired part dimensions. Motion ofthe target object through

Tight sources include incandescent and fluorescent lamps, fiber optics, lasers,
'ae lamps, and strobe lights. Proper lighting also requires judicious positioning of
the illuminated object. Early vision systems used vidicon cameras as image sensors
to convert light to electric signals and then to a two-dimensional image of the
three-dimensional object. More recently, solid-state cameras using charge-coupled
'device (CCD) oF charge-injected device (CID) photosensors have become popular.

igre 111 Light sect appiations
'When one-dimensional arrays of sensors are used, two-dimensional images can
be formed by scanning (rotating mirror, etc) or by use of the sensed object's own.
motion, as along a conveyer line. Two-dimensional sensor arrays are "sell.
scanning." giving the desired image electronically, without the need for any motion
Each sensor in an array provides its own individual picture element ("pixel"), so
the image is spatially broken up into, say, 1024 elements in a linear array, oF
256 x 256 in an area sensor.
Image analysis begins with preprocessing, where typically the sensor analog
voltages are sampled 30 or 60 times per second and digitized, producing a digital
pixel array which can be stored in memory ("frame grabber"). In assigning a digital
value to each pixel, two main approaches are taken: binary (black/white) and gray
scale. The binary approach compares cach pixel signal with a selected threshold
illumination value and assigns a digital 0 or 1, depending on whether the signal

-- 407 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 401
MOTION AND DIMENSIONAL MEASUREMENT 377
is greater or less than the threshold value. The image is thus greatly simplified
since it now consists only of white" and "black" pitels. Gray-scale systems provide
finer intensity discrimination, typically 64 to 256 "shades of gray" between black
and white (the human eye can discriminate about 64 however, a 256 x 256 area
sensor, using 256 shades of gray and updating at 30 images per second, produces
Ihuge quantities of data, requiring vast memories and long processing times for
analysis. For this reason, and because they are adequate for many simple inspec
tion tasks using silhouette images, binary systems are widely used. To allow use
'of the more versatile gray-scale systems in production applications where high-
speed gaging, sorting, flaw detection, etc, are required, vsion system engineers are
using new superfast computer architectures such as array, pipeline, and systolic
processors especially suitable for image analysis.
Sometimes only a portion of an image is needed to reach a decision, and
"windowing" (electronically masking the unneeded area) can be used to greatly
reduce data requirements, When an image suffers from various forms of degrada
mm (blurring of lines or boundaries, poor contrast, buckground noise, ete),
improvement may be achieved by using techniques such as contrast stretching,
'edge detection, and run-length encoding.*
'Once preprocessing is complete, image analysis in the computer can commence
by deseribing and measuring selected properties of various image features. Usually
the simplest features ace analyzed frst, and more complex ones arc added until
the desired results are obtained. When a sensed par's orientation and distance
from the camera are already known, determination of the part position is fairly
simple. I distance andior orientation must be measured, various techniques? are
'available, but now the analysis becomes more complex. Object motion causes
further dificulies, compounding as more degrees of freedom are allowed: however,
strobe lights may be helpful in freezing images.
Here we discuss only briefly two of the most useful image-analysis techniques:
cedge detection and normalized gray-scale correlation (as used in template
matching). For measured objects whose natural image is not very contrasty and
whose features (which we wish to measure) are thus not sharply defined, an edge-
'enhancement operation on the gray-scale picture may be desirable prior to com-
parison with a threshold value, conversion to a binary image, and pixel counting
for dimensional measurement. For a gray-scale system we can plot (Fig. 4112a)
the light intensity at each pixel as the third (2) dimension of a three-dimensional
surface, where the x, y coordinates locate the pixels in the two-dimensional image
'created by the camera, Figure 4.1125 shows an idealized edge, where the intensity
hhas a sudden and obvious change as we cross the edge. Real images of many
'manufactured parts exhibit a more gradual change in intensity (Fig. 4112c) and
require some kind of enhancement operation to locate features accurately enough

down its location, Computer programs can use combinations of these (ot other)

scale image to a binary one which sharply defines part features for measurement.

G useful for deciding whether the pixel P is an edge pixel, via the scheme of Fig.

spective of the "direction" r (Fig. 4.112) of the edge. One thus scans the Sobel

that is called an edge pixel
In searching an image of an assembled mechanism or in a bin of assorted
parts for the presence of a particular part (Fig. 4.114), the template-matching
technique is widely used. A replica of the object of interest is compared with all
the unknown objects in the field of view, and ifthe match is suficiently close, we
conclude that we have found the sought abject. Since a template match can never
'be perfect because of image noise, spatial and intensity quantization, and uncer-
taintis in the exact shapes of manufactured parts, we need to define some measure
'Dom, n) of the difference between the template and the image intensities when the
template is displaced by Ax = m, Ay =n from a selected image location
y =k. As we scan the template over the image region of interest, Dfm,n) takes
'on various values, one of which will be the smallest. If this smallest value of D
is ess than some preselected threshold value, then we decide that we have a match
'nd also know the location (m,m values) of the sought object. A mean-square

would not be simple line drawings; the sides of the triangle would have some
"width," and the template width would be chosen a little larger than the object
width, to provide some tolerance.) While Eq, (4156) and Fig. 4.114 reveal the basic
'concept of template matching, experience shows the D calculation is not reliable
i the face of real-world complications such as uncontrolled overall seene illumin
tion, ete: Some kind of normalization procedure which produces a relative, rather
'than absolute, value is often of help in such situations. Here the normalized cross
correlation? has been found useful:
