Figure A115 Foc ge and feature points
Before completing this section with a brief discussion of an actual manu-
facturing application of machine vision, we want to again caution the reader about
the need to carefully consider all aspects of any proposed application, since many
subtle traps await the unwary.* Our example uses machine vision to help auto-
'mate the assembly, calibration, and quality control of pane! instruments for auto-
mobiles, specifically a fuel-gage dispay.? The final step in the manufacture of such
'devices involves fastening the pointer to the rotating shaft so that it aligns with
'a preprinted "empty" mark when the electrical input is set at the empty value,
Selecting the proper range resistor for each gage so that the pointer aligns with
the "full" mark when the electric fll-tank signal is applied, and finally inspecting
'each gage For proper position at the empty and full conditions and recording any
deviations from normal for statistical process control purposes. Automation of
these operations without vision systems had been difficult because the many dif-
ferent gage styles in use required many different hardware fixtures, with attendant
Tong changeover times, Standardization on a single gage configuration was rejected
as too restrictive on styling design, The vision system accommodates nearly
'unlimited gage variations by assigning needed modifications to computer software
(easily changed), rather than hardware fixtures.
With the preprinted gage dial fastened securely in a fixture, frst the vision
system determines the location of three "feature points" near the empty mark, as
in Fig 4.115. Actually, two points should be sufficient; use of three gives some
desired redundancy. Next the triangle formed by the three feature points om the
dial is compared with a triangle template stored in computer memory and char-
acteristic ofthat dial pattern. The system now knows where the empty mark on
* RC, Stafford, Induced Metrology Distortions Using Machine Vision Systems pp. 3-38 10
3-4'S H. Lapides and A.C. Englander, Understanding How Images Are Digitized, "Vision
pp 423108-33.
DLE Lemke and D.A. Langdon, Gapsite-Computran! Vision for Avlomotive Isrument
{Catbvation, SAE Paper 83037, 1983,

-- 412 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 406
[382 yeasunING nevices
this particular dial is located, and the system signals some stepping-motor drives
to position the pointer correctly and then stake it permanently to the gage shaft,
'the shaft having been properly positioned by applying the "empty" electric signal
to the gage. The vision system also needs to measure the pointer postion t0
'accomplish this. Then the "full" electric signal is applied, causing the shaft and
pointer to rotate toward the F mark on the dial. Economie manufacture results
'in gage electrical movements (really voltmeters) which exhibit rather large variance
in sensitivity, so most gages will not go accurately to the F mark when the proper
signal is applied. This intentionally allowed variation is corrected (individually for
'each gage) atthe final assembly and calibration stage which we are describing,
'by noting the positon error (the vision system does this also) and then selecting &
compensating resistor to correct the sensitivity. This resistor is automatically
assembled into the gage. Now mechanically and electrically complete, the gage is
'passed {0 an inspection station, where similar vision systems eheck for deviations
from normal and record data for statistical process control. For example, ifthe
selected resistors are not bringing the pointer within tolerance for the "ull" con-
ition, the manufacturing process for the gage electrical movements may have
drifted off specification, requiring compensating changes inthe program for sslct-
ing calibration resistors
This system uses a solid-state camera with 100 x 100 pitels and a Canon
'zoom lens with a frontal collector Jens for magnification, The location accuracy
is +4 pixel, which corresponds to about 0.0025 in at the gage face. Gage
location takes about 40 to 80 ms while pointer location requires about 20 ms
Figure 4.116 gives an overview of the entre operation,

FORCE, TORQUE, AND

SL STANDARDS AND CALIBRATION
Force is defined by the equation F = MA; thus & standard for force depends on
standards for mass and acceleration. Masti considered a fundamental quantity,
and its standatd isa cylinder of platinum-iidam, called the Teternational Kilo:
ram, kept in a vault at Sevres, France Other masses (suchas national standards)
'may be compared with ths standard by means of an equal-arm balance, with a
precision of & few parts in 10 deg for masses of about | kg. Tolerances on various
lasses of standard masses available from NIST may be found in its publication.
'Acceleration is not a fundamental quantity, but cathe is derived from length
and time, two fundamental quantities whose standards are discussed in Chap. 4
'The acecieration of gravity, i @ convenient standard which can be determined
with an accuracy of about T part in 10" by measuring the period and effective
length of a pendulum or by determining the change with time of the spocd of a
frely falling body. The actual value of g varies with location and also slightly
with time (in a periodic predictable fashion) at a given location. It also may
Change (light) unpredictably because of local geological activity. The so-called
standard value of g refers to the value at sea level and 45 deg latitude and is
numerically 980668 ems! The value at any latitude degrees may be computed
"TW, Lashot and L, B. Macuidy Pecsion Laboratory Standards of Mass and Laboratory
Weights, Nath Bur Sid. (U3) Cie S47, se I, 1956; PF Ponti, Mass and Mae Values, Nat
Bur Sd (US, Monograph 13,197

When the numerical value of y has been determined at a particular locality,
the gravitational force (weight) on aecurately known standard masses may be
'computed to establish a standard of force. Ths isthe basis of the "deadweight"
'calibration of force-measuring systems. The National Bureat of Standards capa:
ity (Fig. 5.1") for such calibrations is an inaccuracy of about | part in $000 for
the range of 10 to 1 million If. Above this range, direct deadweight calibration is
'not presently available. Rather, proving rings' or load cells ofa capacity of |
'million bf oF less are calibrated against deadweights, and then the unknown force
© accra in Meastements nd Clbraton, Nal Bl Si (US Tov Nove 262.1965

is applied to a multiple array of these in parallel. The range 1 10 10 millon Ibis
covered by such arrangements with somewhat reduced accuracy. At the low-force
end of the scale, the accuracy of standard masses ranges from about | percent for
'4 mass of 10 * Ibm to 0001 percent forthe 0.1 to 10 Ibm range to 0001 percent
for a 100-16 mass. The accuracy of force calibrations using these masses must be
somewhat less than the quoted figures because of error sources in the experimen-
tal procedure." A commercially available? calibrating machine using deadweights,
knife edges, and levers covers the range of 0 to 10,000 Ibf (or 0 to 50 kN) with an
accuracy of 0.005 percent of applied load and a resolution of +0.0062 percent
of applied load
'The measurement of torque is intimately related to force measurement; thus
torque standards as such are not necessary, since force and length ate sufficient 10
define torque. The power transmitted by a rotating shaft isthe produet of torque
and angular velocity. Angular-velocity measurement was treated in Chap. 4
52 BASIC METHODS OF FORCE MEASUREMENT
'An unknown force may be measured by the following means:
1. Balancing it against the known gravitational force on a standard mass, either
directly or through a system of levers
2. Measuring the acceleration of a body of known mass to which the unknown
force is applied
3. Balancing it against a magnetic force developed by interaction of a current-
carrying coll and a magnet
4. Transducing the force to a fluid pressure and then measuring the pressure
5. Applying the force to some elastic member and measuring the resulting de-
fection
6. Measuring the change in precession of a gyroscope caused by an applied
torque related to the measured force
7. Measuring the change in natural frequency of a wire tensioned by the force
In Fig. $2, method 1 is illustrated by the analytical balance, the pendulum
scale, and the platform scale. The analytical balance, while simple in principle,
'requires careful design and operation to realize its maximum performance.' The
'beam is designed so that the center of mass is only slighty (afew thousandths of
fan inch) below the knife-edge pivot and thus barely in stable equilibrium, This
'makes the beam deflection (which in sensitive instruments is read with an optical
* Catiration of Fore Mesvuring struments for Veiing the Lod Indication of Testing Ma:
chines AST Sed 74, 1978
"WC: Dion Co, Van Noy. Cale
LB Macurdy. Performance Tet for Balances, mt. & Com, Sy pp 127-13, Sepember

-- 423 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 417
YORCE, TORQUE, AND SHAFT OWI MEASUREMENT 398
micrometer) @ very sensitive indicator of unbalance. For the low end of a par
ticular instrument's range, often the beam deflection is used as the output reading
rather than attempting to null by adding masses or adjusting the arm length of a
poise weight. This approach is faster than mulling but requires thatthe deflection
"angle unbalance relation be accurately known and stable. This relation tends to
'ary with the load on the balance, because of deformation of knife edges. etc. but

The pendulum scale is a deflection-type instrument in which the unknown
force is converted to a torgue that is then balanced by the torque of a fixed
standard mass arranged as a pendulum, The practical version of this principle
Utilizes specially shaped sectors and stel tapes to linearize the inherently nonlin-
'car torque-angle relation of & pendulum, The unknown force F, may be applied
irectly as in Fig. 52 or through a system of levers, such as that shown for the
platform scale, to extend the range. An electrical signal proportional to force is
fuisily obtained from any angular-displacement transducer attached to measure
the angle 0,
"The platform scale utilizes system of levers to allow measurement of large
forces in terms of much smaller standard weights. The beam is brought to null by
'4 propor combination of pun weights and adjustment of the poise-weight lever
arm along its calibrated scale. The seale can be made self-balancing by adding an
electrical displacement pickup for null detection and an amplifer-motor system
10 position the poise weight to achieve null, Another interesting feature is that if
wh = cid the reading of the scale is independent of the location of F, on the
Platform. Since this quite convenient, most commercial scales provide this
feature by use of the suspension system shown or others that allow similar results.
'While analytical balances are used almost exclusively for "weighing" (really
determining the mass of) objects or chemical samples, platform and pendulum
scales are employed also for force measurements, such a8 those involved in shaft
power determinations with dynamometers. All three instruments are intended
mainly for static force measurements
Method 2, the use of un accelerometer for foroe measurement, is of somewhat
limited application since the force determined isthe resultant force on the mass,
'Often several unknown forces are acting, and they cannot be separately measured
by this method,
The electromagnetic balance? (method 3) utilizes a photoelectric (or other
displacement sensor) null detector, an amplifier, and a torquing coil in a servosy
stem to bulance the difference between the unknown force F, and the grav
force on a standard mass Its advantages relative to mechanical balances are ease

'of use, less sensitivity to environment, faster response, smaller size, and ease of

'microprocessors! allow even greater convenience, versatility, and speed of use by

Method 4 is illustrated in Fig. 52 by hydraulic® and pneumatic load cells.
Hydraulic cells are completely filled with oil and usually have a preload pressure
of the order of 30 Ibjin?. Application of load increases the oil pressure, which is
read on an accurate gage. Electrical pressure transducers can be used t0 obtain
aan electrical signal. The cells are very si, deflecting only a few thousandths of an
inch under full load. Capacities to 100,000 lbf are available as standard while
special units up to 10 million Ibf are obtainable. Accuracy is of the order of 0.1
percent of full scale: resolution is about 0.02 percent. A hydraulic otalizer' is
available to produce a single pressure equal to the sum of up to 10 individual
pressures in multiple-<el systems used for tank weighing, ete, (se Chap. 10).

'While all the previously described force-measuring devices are intended
mainly for static of slowly varying loads, the clastic deflection transducers of
method 5 are widely used for both static and dynamic loads of frequency content
'up to many thousand hertz. While all are essentially spring-mass systems with
{intentional or unintentional) damping. they differ mainly in the geometric form
fof "spring" employed and in the displacement transducer used to obtain an
electrical signal. The displacement sensed may be a gross motion, or strain gages
may be judiciously located to sense free in terms of strain. Bonded strain gages
have been found particularly useful in force measurements with elastic elements.
In addition to serving as force-to-deflection transducers, some elastic clements
perform the funtion of resolving vector forces or moments into rectangular
Components. As an example, the parallelogram flexure of Fig 52s extremely rigid
{insensitive} to all applied forces and moments except in the direction shown by
the arrow. A displacement transducer arranged to measure motion in the sensi-
tive direction thus will measure only that component of an applied vector force
which les along the sensitive axis, Perhaps the action of this lexure may be most
'easily visualized by considering it as a four-bar linkage with Mexure hinges at the
thin sections a , cand d.
'Because of the importance of elastic force transducers in modem dynamic
measurements, we devote a considerable portion of this chapter to their consider=
'ation. Although they' may differ widely in detail construction, their dynamic
response form is generally the same, and so we treat an idealized model
representative of all such transducers in the next section. Discussion of methods 6
and 7 is deferred to the end of the chapter since they are not as common as
methods I through 5

For transducers that do not measure a gross displacement but rather use
strain gages bonded to the "spring" K,. the output strain e may be substituted
for x, i K, is reinterpreted as force per unit strain rather than force per unit

ceannot be distinguished because the elasticity and inertia are distributed rather
'than lumped. In these cases, for design purposes the natural frequency must be
calculated from the appropriate formulas' for the geometric shapes involved
father than by employing Eg. (5.10. Once the transducer is constructed, its

'tically from strength-of-materials or elasticity formulas, whether it relates 10 a
gross deflection or a local unit strain. Once the transducer is constructed, it
should be given an overall calibration relating electrical output to foree input
nee none of the theoretical formulas is suliciently accurate for this purpose.

Since the dynamic response of second-order instruments has been fully dis-
'cussed, we concentrate mainly on details peculiar to specific force transducers
Bonded-Strain-Gage Transducers
|A typical construction for a strain-gage load cell for measuring compressive
forces is shown in Fig. $4 (Cells to measure both tension and compression
require merely the addition of suitable mechanical fitings at the ends) The
Toad-sensing member is short enough to prevent column buckling under the rated
load and is proportioned to develop about 1,500 je at full-scale loud (typical
design value for all forms of oil gage transducers). Materials used include SAE
"4340 steel, 17-4 PH stainless steel, and 2024-T4 aluminum alloy, with the last
being quite popular for "homemade" transducers. Foil-type metal gages are
bonded on all four sides: gages | and 3 sense the direct stress due to F,. and
tages 2 and 4 the transverse stress duc to Poisson's ratio . This arrangement
gives a sensitivity 21 + p) times that achieved with a single active gage in the
bridge. [See Eq, (108) for bridge-ircuit behavior] It also_provides primary
temperature compensation since all four gages are (at least for steady temper
atures) at the same temperature, Furthermore, the arrangement is insensitive 10
Se ce
finer
{eine en 2
Figure $4 Strange lou el

-- 430 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 424
400 weasunine nevices
bending stresses due to F, being applied off center or at an angle, This can be
seen by replacing an off-center force by an equivalent on-center force and a
couple. The couple can be resolved into x and y components which cause bend-
ing stresses in the gages. Ifthe gages are carefully placed so as to be symmetric,
the bending stresses in gages 1 and 3 will be of opposite sign, and by the rules of
bridge circuits the net output e, due to bending will be zero. Similar arguments
hold for gages 2 and 4 and for bending stresses due to F, being at an angle. The
side-force stiffener plate also reduces the effects of angular forces, since itis very
stiin the radial (x,y) direction but very soft inthe = direction
The deflection under full load of such load cells is of the order of 0.001 to
(0015 in, indicating their high stiffness. Often the natural frequency is not quoted
since itis determined almost entirely by the mass of force-carrying elements
'external to the transducer. This is especially true in the many applications where
the load cells used for weighing purposes. The high sifiness also implies @ low
sensitivity. To inerease sensitivity (in low-force cells where itis needed) without
sacrificing column stability and surface area for mounting gages, a hollow (square
'on the outside, round on the inside) load-carrying member may be employed.
To achieve the high accuracy (0.3 to 0.1 percent of full scale) required in
many applications, additional temperature compensation is needed." This is ac-
'complished by means of the temperature-sensitive resistors R,. and Ra, shown in
Fig 54, These resistors are permanently attached internal tothe load cell 50 as to
assume the same temperature as the gages. The purpose of Ry, is to compensate
forthe slighty different temperature coefficients of resistance of the four gages.
'The purpose of Ry is to compensate for the temperature dependence of the
'modulus of elasticity of the load-sensing member. That is, although we wish to
'measure fore, the gages sense strain; thus any change in the modulus of elasticity
will give a different strain (and thus a different e,) even though the force is the
same. Since all metals change modulus somewhat with temperature, this effect
'causes a sensitivity drift. The resistance Ry. compensates for this by changing the
excitation voltage actually applied to the bridge by just the right amount to
'counteract the modulus effec
Two additional (non-temperature-sensitive) resistors are often found in com
'mercial load cells. They are R,,, which is adjusted to standardize the sensitivity
for a nominal e,, to a desired value, and R,,, which is used to adjust the input
resistance toa desired value
When adequate sensitivity cannot be achieved by use of tension/compression
'members, configurations employing bending stresses may be helpful These gener-
ally provide more strain per unit applied force, but at the expense of reduced
sliness and thus natural frequency. Of the many possibilities, two are shown in
Fig. 5.5a. The canilever-beam gage arrangement provides 4 times the sensitivity of

"Transducers using shear loading! can be designed to be very compact (in the
load application direction) and have litle sensitivity to offaxis forces and mo-
'ments, good symmetry for tension/compression, long fatigue life, simple overload
protection, and high stifness. Figure 5.6 shows one possible design, where four
'gages (only two show) oriented at 45 deg with the load axis are cemented to the
shear webs between the holes to pick up the tension and compression strains
produced by the applied shear stress. With two gages in compression and two in
tension, a full-bridge circuit can be used. The nature of the overload stop allows
'stacking" of a high-range (say 100,000 Ibf) and a low-range (say 10.000 Ibf) cell
to provide « dual-range transducer whose accuracy is maintained at loads below
10 percent of high range. That is, at loads below 10.000 Ibf we use the output of
the low-range cell, but as load goes above 10,000 Ibf, this cell "bottoms out"
(safely) and we take our readings from the high-range cell

When maximum output is desired for any strain-gage transducer, one should
consider the possible use of low-modulus materials (such as aluminum) to in-
crease strain per unit force, several gages (or one high-resistance gage) per bridge
leg (if space allows}. the intentional introduction of stress concentrations at the
gage locations, and/or use of semiconductor gages.' However, such techniques
also present associated problems. Low modulus reduces stiffness and natural
frequency, and some low-modulus materials have excessive hysteresis and low
fatigue life. Stress concentrations also lower fatigue life, and thei effet may be
difficult to calculate for design purposes. Semiconductor gages present instal
lation and temperature compensation problems.
Differential-Transformer Transducers
Figure 5.7 shows the design of a family of LVDT load cells available in ranges
from 10 g to 1000 Ibf which use two helical flexures (each machined from one
solid piece) as the elastic element. An external threaded ring allows convenient
'ero adjustment by changing the axial postion of the spring-loaded LVDT coil
form, Fullscale deflections are about 0020 100.030 in, nonlinearity is better than
(02 percent full scale, and operating temperature range is -65 to + 200 deg F.
Piezoelectric Transducers
"These force transducers have the same form of transfer function as piezoelectric
accelerometers. They ate intended for dynamic force measurement only, although
some types (quartz pickup with electrometer charge amplifier) have sufficiently
large + to allow short-term measurement of static forces and static calibration
Just as in the piezoelectric accelerometers of Chap. 4 force transducers ate aval
able with or without built-in microelectronics. Typical units have about | percent
nonlinearity and very high stifess (10" to 10" lbf/in) and natural frequency
(10.000 to 300.000 #2), though, as with any force transducer, the system natural
frequency will be lower because of the unavoidable mass of necessary attached
'components. A single transducer often is useful over a very wide range of forces
and preloads because the 1 percent nonlinearity applies to any calibrated range
and the natural "leak-off" of output voltage for steady loads gives a convenient
zero reading. Useful temperature ranges and sensitivity to temperature inputs
'depend on piezoelectric material used, design details, and whether built-in elec-

'Studies of structural impedance actually are quite useful and common in

Variable-Reluctance/FM-Oscillator Digital Systems
While the electrical signal from any force transducer can be converted to digital
{form by suitable equipment sce Chap. 10}, some types of pickups are specifically
designed with this in mind. We here discuss briefly one such type which has been,
used in rocket-engine testing where digital data provide advantages of accuracy
and case of performing computations automatically. The elastic member is a
proving ring with a two-arm variable-cluctance bridge displacement transducer.
'The signal from this transducer is used t0 change the frequency of a frequency-
'modulated (FM) oscillator. The frequency change (from some base vale) of this
'oscillator is directly proportional to displacement (and thus to force). A digital
reading of force is accomplished by applying the output signal ofthe oscillator to
an cleetronic counter over a known time interval. The total number of pulses
'accumulated is thus # digital measure of force. Ina typical unit, a change of force
from minus full scale to plus full scale causes the frequency to change from 10.000
to 12500 Hz. For a I-s counting period, a full-scale force will thus cause a
'counter reading 1250 above the base value of 1,250.
Computing advantages of such a system arise from the desire to know the
total impulse of the rocket engine and to be able to add and subtract various
forces in multicomponent test stands. The total impulse is the integral of force
with respect to time: this is simply the total number of counts over the desired
integrating time. When several forees must be added or subtracted, each has its
'own Force pickup and 10,000 to 12,500 Hz oscillator. The oscillator output sige
nals are combined in an electronic adder unit, which produces a single output
whose frequency isthe sum (or difference) of the input fFequencies. The output of
the adder unit i a signal witha frequency of 30,000 to 37.500 Hz; counting this
'over a timed interval gives the algebraic sum of the measured forces. Integration
of this sum signal can be easily accomplished by the same method used for a
single signa. Figure 5.10 shows block diagrams of these systems. By applying the
dc output voltage to a voltage-to-requency converter (see Chap. 10), these digital
'methods can be extended to any load cell with de output,

or impedance, and the associated flow variable is either displacement or velocity

be infinite for the static case. The generalized input stiffness S,, of the
system of Fig. 53 is given by

$4 RESOLUTION OF VECTOR FORCES AND MOMENTS
INTO RECTANGULAR COMPONENTS
In a number of important practical applications, the force oF moment to be
'measured is not only unknown in magnitude but also of unknown and/or va
able direction. Outstanding examples of such situations are "balances" for mea-
suring forees on wind-tunnel models." dynamometers (force gages) for measuring
cotting forces in machine tools? and thrust stands? for determining forces of
rocket engines. Elastic force transducers of either the bonded-strain-gage of
sgrossdeflection variety are employed in these applications. Ingenious use of
various types of flexures for isolating and measuring different force components
characterizes the design of these devices. Depending on the degree to which the
force oF moment direction is unknown, force-resolving systems of varying degrees
'of complexity may' be devised. The most general situation (measurement of three
'mutually perpendicular force components and three mutually perpendicular
'moment components} is regularly aosomplished with high accuracy.
Figure 5.12 shows a six-component thrust stand* used in testing rocket en-
ines. Load cells 1,2, and 3 are mounted at the corners of an equilateral triangle,
and load cells 4, 5, and 6 are in the sides of a concentric, smaller equilateral
triangle. The engine to be tested is rigidly fastened at the common center of both
triangles and produces a force of unknown magnitude and ditection (which can
bbe expressed in terms of components FF, and F,) and a moment of unknown
'magnitude and direction (which can be expressed in terms of components M,,
M, and M.). The rocket forces are transmitted from the mounting plate to the
rigid foundation through the six load cells and their associated Mexures. The
action of the suspension system is most clearly seen if we consider each flexure as
4 pin joint, Actually pin joints are not used because of their lost motion and
friction. A static analysis of the force system gives the following results, which
allow calculation of the rocket forees and moments from the measured load-cel

'The indicated additions and subtractions of forces are (in the thrust stand de-

'A combination of bonded strain gages, Wheatstone-bridge circuits, and flex-
ible elements of various geometrics has proved a versatile tool in the development
'of multicomponent-force pickups of small size and high natural frequency. Figure
5.134 shows a beam with three separate bridge circuits of gages arranged 10
'measure the three rectangular components of an applied force. All bridge circuits
are temperature-compensated and respond only to the intended component of
force: however, the point of application ofthe force must be atthe center of the
'beam cross section. An eccentricity in the y direction, for example, would give F,
4 moment arm, causing bending stresses in the y ditection that would be indis-
tinguishable fcom those due to F,. If side loads F, and F, are presen, the end of
the beam will deflect, causing just such eccentricities; thus beam stiffness must be
adequate to keep deflection sufficiently low. This stiffness tends, of course, 10
reduce sensitivity. While many strain-gage pickup configurations are possible
and have been used, the octagonal ring' of Fig. 52 has particularly interesting
properties. The reference gives information on its design and use.
Piezoelectric techniques also are well adapted to the problem of component
resolution of vector forces and moments? as briefly shown earlier (Fig. 443),
where the x, x. and = components of a vector foree were measured. The three-
'component loud cell shown there can be used also as a building block in con-
structing measuring systems for specific purposes, such as the three-component
'dynamometer for machining research in milling and grinding operations illus:
trated in Fig. S.14a. (Dynamometer is another word used for force transducer,
particularly in Europe) Four individual three-component cells share the total
load, with all four-component erystals paralleled electrically to give a single x
'output signal, and similarly for y and :. This arrangement produces an output
which measures the force components correctly even when the force application
Point moves (as it would, in a milling operation). The reading of each of the four
cells varies with force position, but their sum (which is what is produced by the
clectric parallel connection) gives the correct total force, irespective of position
'This scheme is carried even one step further in the milling operation of Fig. 5.14,
where two complete dynamometers support the workpiece and are electrically
Paralleed (externally) to achieve the desired force measurement, The individual
sensing elements can be constructed from single disks of quartz or rings of disks,

Robotic manufacturing and assembly operations may be improved by adding

5 TORQUE MEASUREMENT ON ROTATING SHAFTS.
"Measurement of the torque carried by a rotating shaft is of considerable interest
for its own sake and as a necessary part of shaft power measurements. Torque
transmission through a rotating shaft generally involves both a source of power
and a sink (power absorber or dissipator, as in Fig. 5.16. Torque measurement
'may be accomplished by mounting either the source or the sink in bearings
(eradling") and measuring the reaction force F and arm length L: or else the
torque in the shaft itself is measured in terms of the angular (wist or strain of the
shafl (ora torque sensor coupled into the sha.
'The cradling concept is the basis of most shaft power dynamometers, These
are utilized mainly for measurements of steady power and torque. by using
Pendulum or platform scales to measure F. A free-body analysis of the cradled
member reveals error sources resulting from friction in the cradle bea

forces due to bending and/or stretching of power lines (electric, hydraulic. etc)
attached to the cradled member. To reduce frictional elfets and to make possible
'dynamic torque measurements, the eradle-bearing arrangement may be replaced
by a Mlewure pivot with strain gages to sense torque.' as in Fig. 5.17. The crossing
point ofthe flexure plates defines the effective axis of rotation of the flexure pivot
Angular deflection under fll load is typically less than 0.5. This type of cross-
spring flexure pivot is relatively very still in all directions other than the rotation-
lone desired, just as in an ordinary bearing. The strain-gage bridge arrangement
also is such as to reduce the effect of all forces other than those related to the
torque being measured. Speed-torque curves for motors may be obtained quickly
and automatically with such a torque sensor by letting the motor under test
accelerate an inertia from zero speed up to maximum while measuring speed with
ade tachometer? The torque and speed signals are applied to an XY recorder to
ive automatically the desired curves,
'Another variation (see Fig. 5.18) on the cradle principe is found in « null-
balance torquemeter using feedback principles to measure small torques in the
range 0 t0 10 02 in. In this device the test object is mounted on a hydrostatic
air-bearing table 10 reduce beating frition to exceedingly small values. Any
torque on the test objet tends to cause rotation of the air-bearing table, but this
rotation is immediately sensed by a differential-transformer displacement pickup.
'The output from this pickup is converted to direct current and amplified to
provide the coil current of a torque motor, which applies opposing torque t0
keep displacement at zero. The amount of current required to maintain zero
displacement is a measure of torque and is read on a meter. The servoloop uses
integral control' to give zeo displacement for any constant torque. Approximate

Even though they require additional equipment to transmit power and signal
between rotating shaft and stationary readout, strain-gage torque sensors are
very widely used. Figure 5.20 shows the basic principle. (Chapter 11 gives data on
Slip rings rotary transformers, and radiotelemetry systems to deal with the data

matched gage characteristics} is temperature-compensated and insensitive 10
'bending or axial stresses. The gages must be precisely at 45_with the shaft axis,
'and gages 1 and 3 must be diametrically opposite, as must gages 2 and 4.
Accurate gage placement is facilitated by the availabilty of special rosettes in
'which two gages are precisely oriented on one sheet of backing material. In some
ceases the shaft already present in the machine to be tested may be fitted with
'ruin gages. In other cases a different shaft oF a commercial torquemeter must be

ages on a square, rather than round, cross soetion of the shaft has some advan-
tages, The gages are more easily and accurately located and more firmly bonded

'on a flat surface, Also, the corners of a square section in torsion are stress-free

shaft natural frequencies.
'The torque of many machines, such as reciprocating engines is not smooth

'member and the low-frequency response of the platform or pendulum scales used
to measure F perform the same averaging function.

Commercial strain-gage torque sensors are available with built-in slip

3% 10" in Ibf with full-seale output of about 40 mV. The smaller units may' be

fof the 10 o2 » in unit is 112 in Ibf/rad while a 600,000 in - Ibf unit has 40
10 deg in -Ibf'rad. Nonlinearity is 0.1 percent of fll scale while temperature effect

'The dynamic response of elastic deflection torque transducers is essentially
slightly damped second-order, with the natural frequency usually determined by
the stillness of the transducer and the inertia ofthe parts connected at either end
Damping of the transducers themselves usually is not attempted, and any damp-
ing present is due to bearing fiction, windage, etc, of the complete test setup.
'A good example ofthe application of torque measurement (and several other
-mcasurement system concepts of general interest) is found in a dynamic test
system used in the design and development of front-whesl-drive components for
automobiles (ee Figs, 5.22 through .24)? An alternative to conventional dyna
mometer loading systems called the fou-square principle, is utilized here to con-
serve energy and allow fas-response testing. Rather than connecting the shaft test
specimens between a power source and a power sink, as in Fig. 5.16 an arrang
ment which wastes all the shaft-transmitted power and couples large, slow
responding inetias to the specimen, the four-square principle (Fig. $224) induces
44 "locked-in" torque into the system by counter-twisting the flanges of a pre-
Toding coupler at assembly. Now the geared assembly can be driven at any
desired spoed hy. say. a de motor drive, and the shaft specimens will he subjected
to the desired torque speed conditions, hut the drive supplies only fiction losses
Iplus accelerating power if speed is changed), nor the shaf-transmitted power
{product of locked-in torque and shaft speed).
The basic four-square principle just deseribed has been widely utilized for
'ome time: however, several new features were needed in the present application.
I was desired (© be able to maintain a certain level of locked-in torque in the
face of inevitable component wear. creep. slippage. oF fatigue, which
progressive "relaxation" of torque with time in the basic four-square system.
Also. it was nocessary to dynamically vary the locked-in torque in response to
clectical commands. By replacing the mechanical preloading coupler with a
rotary hydraulic actuator (Fig. $22h), measuring shaft torque with a rotating
torque sensor. and driving the actuator from an electrohydraulic servovalve
sponding to the error between commanded and measured torque, the desied
features are achieved
To simulate acial diving conditions, the testing machine includes means for
subjecting the frontwheel-drive shafls to "jounce" (up-and-down) motions and

steering motions, all simultaneous with speed and torque variations (see Fig.
5.23) All these testing modes are implemented by using electrohydraulc oF elec
tromechanical feedback control system, each of which requires appropriate mea
suring instruments for the controlled variable.
The remote parameter control (RPC) feature ofthe system is a further refine-
iment (see Fig. 5.24) Input commands to all the torque and motion control loops
fie derived from tape recordings obtained from instrumented automobiles driven
'actual roads oF test tracks. Since the various feedback control systems
not be designed to have "perfect" dynamic response, inputting a certain
'command waveform for, say, shaft torque will nor result in exactly that waveform
in the actual torque. To compensate for these unavoidable system response defi
'iencies, the RPC method measures them and then alters the input command so
that the desired response is obtained. This is accomplished by implementing
frequeney-domain methods (explained in Chap. 3) on a minicomputer, using fast
Fourier transform (FFT) algorithms. Overall this system isan excellent example
'of how concepts from the areas of measurement, system dynamics, automatic
Control, and computer-aided engineering are becoming a vital part of advanced
design methods
56 SHAFT POWER MEASUREMENT (DYNAMOMETERS)
'The accurate measurement of the shaft power input or output of power-
enorating. transmitting. and -absorbing machinery is of considerable interest.
While the basic measurements torque and spscd-have already been discussed
their practical application to power measurement is considered briefly here. The
term dynamometer generally is used to deseribe such power-measuring systems
although its also used as a name for elastic force sensors.
"The type of dynamometer employed depends somewhat on the nature of the
machine to be tested. Ifthe machine is a power generator. the dynamometer must
be capable of absorbing its power. If the machine is a power absorber, the
dynamometer must be capable of driving it. Ifthe machine isa power transmitter
for transformer, the dynamometer must provide both the power source and the
load, unless a four-square method is feasible.
Perhaps the most versatile and accurate dynamometer is the de electri type
Here a de machine is mounted in low-friction trunnion bearings (see Fig. 5.16b)
and provided with field and armature control circuits." This machine can be
'coupled to either power-absorbing or power-generating devices singe it may be
connected as ether a motor of a generator. When itis employed as a generator,
the generated power is dissipated in resistance grids or recovered for use. The de
"PS Pts and PT, Sshuerman. How to Choose Electic Dypamomeers. Mach Des. p12

Figure $28 Serocontole dynamometer
'dynamometer can be adjusted to provide any torque from zero to the maximum
design value for speeds from zero to the so-called base speed of the machine. At
this speed the maximum torque develops the maximum design horsepower. At
speeds above base speed, torque must be progressively reduced so as to maintain
horsepower less than the design maximum. The controllability of the de dyna-
'mometer lends it particularly to modern automatic load and speed programming,
applications.
Figure 525 illustrates such a situation. Tape recordings of engine torque and
speed measured under actual driving conditions for an automobile are utilized to
reproduce these conditions in the laboratory engine test. Two feedback systems
'control engine speed and torque. A tachometer generator speed signal from the
'dynamometer is compared with the desired speed signal from the tape recorder: if
the two are different, the dynamometer control is automatically adjusted 10
'change speed until agreement is reached. Actual engine torque is obtained from a
Yoad cell on the dynamometer and compared with the desired torque from the
tape recorder. If these do not agree, the error signal actuates the engine throttle
control in the proper direction. Both systems operate simultaneously and con-
tinuously to force engine speed and torque to follow the tape-recorder com-
'mands.

'runnion-mounted induction motor becomes a versatile dynamometer' for both

driving and absorbing applications. Features include fast response, flexible con-
trol, simplified maintenance, and energy conservation, since up to 85 percent of,
the energy absorbed is returned (0 the ac power line.
Dynamometers capable only of absorbing power (see Fig. 5:26) include the
eddy-current brake! (inductor dynamometer) and various mechanical brakes em-
Ploying dry friction (Prony brake) and fuid friction (air and water brakes). The
eddy-current brake is easily controllable by varying a de input, but it cannot
produce any torque at zero speed and only small torque at low speeds. However,
Iris capable of higher power and speed than a de dynamometer. The power
'absorbed is carried away by cooling water circulated through the air gap between
rotor and stator. The Prony brake isa simple mechanical brake in which friction
torque is manually adjusted by varying the normal force with a handwhee.
Torque is available at zero speed, but operation may be jerky because of the
basic nature of dry friction, Water and air brakes utilize the churning action of
paddle wheels or vanes rotating inside a fuid-fled casing to absorb power. A
flow of air or water through the device is maintained for cooling purposes. No
torque is available at zero speed, and only small torques ate available at low
Speeds. High speed and high power can be handled well, however, with some
water brakes being rated at 10,000 pat 30,000 r/min.
Tn all the above power measurement applications, torque and speed are
'measured separately and then power is calculated manually. This calculation can,

While the second-order term of Eq (5.31) is undamped, small damping (ition)

i, Figere $30 Solution of gmbstock problem.
produce a precessional angular velocity 0 in direct proportion according to 0 =
IKT,. This wil be true only as long as 0 is small, however, and since a constant
torque produces a constant relacits, eventually @ must become large. This will
'ultimately (when @ reaches 90 deg ) lead to what is called "gimbal lock." The gyro-
scopic precession actually depends on the component of the torque vector that is
perpendicular to the spin angulat-momentum vector. For small angles, this com-
ponent is directly proportional to torque. For large angles, itis proportional 10
the product of torque and cos 0: this becomes smaller as # increases and disap-
pears completely at 0-= 90", Thus the precession produced by a constant
Torque T. gets smaller and smaller as @ approaches 90 deg . At 90 deg a torque T,
produces' no precession at all; rather, the inner and outer gimbals both rotate
together about the x axis.
To prevent gimbal lock and thus achieve a useful torque-sensing instrument,
4 simple mechanical solution is available. The requirement that torque vector
'and spin angular-momentum vector always be perpendicular is met by the con-
figuration of Fig. 5.30, The equation # = KT, now holds for any angle , and we
can measure torque by measuring J or measure the time integral of torque by
means of a simple revolution counter attached t0 the @ shaft. A commercial
'weighing system based on the above principles (with refinements} is available."

'58 VIBRATING-WIRE FORCE TRANSDUCERS
Method 7 in the list of basic forcesensing schemes is based on the well-known
formula from classical physics for the first natural frequency w of a string of
length L and mass per unit length m,, which is tensioned by the force F to be
measured:
(534)
Since w varies smoothly with F, the measuring principle is basically analog;
however, the frequency is easily measured with conventional digital counters, so
'the transducer is sometimes described as a digital device. Temperature sensitivity
(variation in L) and the nonlinear «/F relation cause some inconvenience in
practical use."
'The temperature and linearity problems above have been overcome by clever
system design in a sensor used as part of industrial material feeding controllers?
(Fig. 531), While the wire-tensioning force determines the natural frequency of
vibration, some vibration exciting system must be provided in any vibrating-wire
Sensor to make up frictional losses and to maintain wire vibration at a fixed
amplitude. The "wires" (actually thin tapes of electrically conducting but non-
"KS Lon,"Inrumentation in Scien Resereh,"p. 3-85, McGraw-Hill, New York, 1959
* M. Glo and G. Busan, The Benefits of Mass Meaarement for Gravineic Feeding of Blk
Sols in Indust Environments, K-Troa Corp, Glastbor, Ni

-- 463 of 1000 --

{ORCE, TORQUE, AND SHAFT ROWER MEASUREMENT 433
'magnetic metal) are placed in the fields of permanent magnets In this way. they
serve as both velocity-to-voltage transducers (used in one leg of a bridge circuit
{o provide an oscillatory input voltage to an amplifier) and currentto-force
transducers, which accept an oscillatory output current from the amplifier and
'produce vibration-exciting transverse forces on the wires
By proper design, these feedback systems maintain constant-amplitude wire
vibrations at a frequency determined by the force carried by each wire. When the
'measured force is zero, a fixed reference mass M, applies a preload tension F.,
'which is equal in each wire, causing both wires to vibrate at the same frequency
land making the frequency ratio «/« = 1.0. If temperature changes, both wires
fexperience the same length and frequeney changes, but the frequency ratio is
Unaffected, which provides temperature compensation, To make the output signal
'oj/erg linear with F,, a force proportional to F, is applied through the (noavi=
brating) wire at an angle x such that an inerease in F, increases F, (the tension in
wire 1) but decreases F. Choice of angles fi and x allows us to adjust the relation
between AF, and AF, over a wide range. Analysis shows that the formulas for Fy

PRESSURE AND SOUND MEASUREMENT
6.1 STANDARDS AND CALIBRATION
Pressure is nota fundamental quantity, but rather is derived from force and area,
Which in turn are derived from mass, length, and time, the latter three being
fundamental quantities whose standards have been discussed earlier. Pressure
"standards" in the form of very accurate instruments are available, though, for
calibration of less accurate instruments. However, these "standards" depend
ultimately on the fundamental standards for their accuracy. The basi standards"
for pressures ranging from medium vacuum (about 10 mmHg) up to several
hundred thousand pounds per square inch are in the form of precision mercury
columns (manometers) and deadweight piston gages. For pressures in the range
10 deg to 10"? mmHg, the McLeod vacuum gage is considered the standard. For
pressures below 10 deg ? mmHg, a pressure-dividing technique allows flow through
4 succession of accurate orifices to relate the low downstream pressure to @
higher upstream pressure (which is accurately measured with a McLeod gage)"
"This technique can be further improved by substituting a Schulz hot-cathode
'oF radioactive ionization vacuum gage for the MeLeod gage. Each of these must

covered later.
62 BASIC METHODS OF PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
Since pressure usually can be easily transduced to force by allowing it to act on @
Known area, the basic methods of measuring force and pressure are essentially the
Same, ewcept for the high-vacuum region where a variety of special methods not
» accuracy in Meaturements and Cabbrations, NBS Tech, Noe 262 1965
2 NBS Spec Publ 2501987

-- 470 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 464
440 seasunina devices
directly related to force measurement are necessary. These special methods are
'described in the section on vacuum measurement. Other than the special vacum
techniques, most pressure measurement is based on comparison with known
<deadweights acting on known areas or on the deflection of elastic elements sub
jected {0 the unknown pressure. The deadweight methods are exemplified by
'manometers and piston gages while the elastic deflection devices take many
Ulifferent forms.
63 DEADWEIGHT GAGES AND MANOMETERS
Figure 62 shows the basic elements of a deadweight or piston gage. Such devices
are employed mainly as standards for the calibration of less accurate gages of
transducers. The gage to be calibrated is connected to a chamber filled with fluid
whose pressure can be adjusted by some type of pump and bleed valve. The
chamber also connects with a vertical piston-cylinder to which various standard
Weights may be applied. The pressure is slowly built up until the piston and
weights are seen to "float." at which point the fluid "gage" pressure (pressure
above atmosphere) must equal the deadweight supported by the piston, divided
by the piston area,
For highly accurate results, a number of refinements and corrections are
necessary. The frictional force between the cylinder and piston must be reduced
to a minimum and/or corrected for. This is generally accomplished by rotating
either the piston or the ylinder. If there is no axial relative motion, this rotation
should reduce the axial effects of dry fiction to zero, There must, however, be a
small clearance between the piston and the cylinder and thus an axial flow of
fluid from the high-pressure end to the low-pressure end. This flow produces
LC wig od en
be
Lot tn oy
== Goge aera sit
Pique 62 Desdnciht page cla

-- 471 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 465
viscous shear force tending to support part of the deadweight. This effect can be
estimated from theoretical calculations.' However, it varies somewhat with pres-
sure since the piston and cylinder deform under pressure, thereby changing the
clearance. The clearance betwen the piston and eylinder also raises the question
of which area isto be used in computing pressure. The effective area generally is
taken as the average of the piston and cylinder areas. Further corrections are
needed for temperature effets on areas of piston and cylinder, air and pressure-
'medium buoyancy effets, local gravity conditions, and height differences between
the lower end of the piston and the reference point for the gage being calibrated
'Special designs and techniques allow use of deadweight gages for pressures up 10
several hundred thousand pounds per square inch. An improved design, the
controlledclearance piston gage,? employs a separately pressurized cylinder
jacket 10 maintain the effective area constant (and thus achieve greater accuracy)
'at high pressures, which cause expansion and error in uncompensated gages.
Since the piston assembly itself has weight, conventional deadweight gages
are not capable of measuring pressures lower than the piston weight/area ratio
(Ctare? pressure). This difficulty is overcome by the tlting-piston gage? in which
the cylinder and piston can be tilled ftom vertical through an accurately mea
sured angle, thus giving a continuously adjustable pressure from Olbin® gage up
to the tare pressure, The described gage uses nitrogen or other inert gas as the
pressure medium and covers the range 0 to 600 Ibjin? gage, having two int
Changeable piston-cylinders and 14 weights. The accuracy is OOL percent of
reading in the range 0.3 10 15 Th/in? gage and 0015 percent of reading in the
range 210 600 Ib in? gage. The tilting feature is used for the ranges 0 10 03 and 0
to 20 Ibjin? gage; higher pressures are obtained in increments by the addition of
discrete weights.
'A very convenient pressure standard* (although not really a deadweight
sage) combines a precision piston gage with a magnetic null-balance laboratory
scale® (Fig. 63). The gas or liquid pressure to be measured (generated and
regulated by a system external to the pressure standard) i applied to one end of a
rotating piston; the other end of the piston is supported by the "weighing plat-
form" of the laboratory scale, which measures the pressure force and gives a
digital readout of 40,000 counts full scale, Tungsten piston carbide eylinders
jow clearances less than 1 ym: the high hardness and elastic modulus maintain,
precision in the face of potential wear and pressure expansion. Piston-cylinder
pairs are easily interchanged to give five full-scale ranges from 80 to 1,200 lbyin*

Since deadweights are nor utilized to measure the pressure force, periodic reca
bration against a set of four precision masses is required. However, ths is made
'quick and easy by using the scal's auto-tare feature and a simple screwdriver
Span adjustment, Instrument uncertainty on the 80 Ibjin? range (other ranges are
proportional) i +(0.008 + 10-p) lin', where p is the actual pres
sure in pounds per square inch and repeatability is +1 count.
Deadweight gages may be employed for absolute- rather than gage-pressure
measurement by placing them inside an evacuated enclosure at (ideally) 0 Ibn?
absolute pressure, Since the degree of vacuum (absolute pressure) inside the en-
closure must be known, this really requires an additional independent measur
ment of absolute pressure.
"The manometer in its various forms is closely related to the piston gage, since
both are based on the comparison of the unknown pressure force with the gravity
force on a known mass. The manometer differs, however, in that it is self
balancing, is a deflection rather than a null instrument, and has continuous
rather than stepwise output. The accuracies of deadweight gages and manometers
'of similar ranges are quite comparable; however, manometers become unwieldy
'at high pressures because of the long liquid columns involved. The U-tube man-
'ometer of Fig. 644 usually is considered the basic form and has the following
relation between input and output for static conditions

where y 4 local gravity and p ® mass density of manometer fuid. fp, is atmo-
spheric pressure, then his direct measure ofp, asa gage pressure. Note that the
cross-sectional area of the tubing (even if not uniform) has no effect. At a given
location (given value of g) the sensitivity depends on only the density of the
manometer fluid, Water and mercury are the most commonly used fluids. To
realize the high accuracy possible with manometer, often a number of correc
tions must be applied. When visual reading of the height h is employed, the
engraved scales temperature expansion must be considered. The variation of p

with temperature for the manometer fuid used must be corrected and the local
value of g determined. Additional sources of error are found in the nonverticaity
of the tubes and the difficulty in reading h because of the meniscus formed by
capillaity, Considerable care must be exercised in order to keep inaccuracies as
small as 0.01 mmHg forthe overall measurement.
'A number of practically useful variations on the basic manometer principle
are shown in Fig. 65. The cistern or welltype manometer is widely utilized
because of its convenience in requiring reading of only a single leg. The well area
is made very large compared with the tube; thus the zero level moves very little
when pressure is applied. Even this small error is compensated by suitably distor-
ting the length scale. However, such an arrangement, unlike a U tube, is sensitive

to nonuniformity of the tube cross-sectional area and thus is considered some-

'Given that manometers inherently measure the pressure diference between
the two ends of the liquid colum, if one end is at zero absolute pressure, then h
{san indication of absolute pressute, This is the principle of the barometer of Fig.
(65. Although itis a "single-eg" instrument, high accuracy is achieved by setting
the zero level ofthe well atthe zero level ofthe scale before each reading is taken.
"The pressure in the evacuated portion of the barometer is not really absolute
'zero, but rather the vapor pressure ofthe fling fluid, mercury, at ambient tem-
perature, This is about 10-* Ibjin? absolute at 70 deg F and usualy is negligible as a
To increase sensitivity, the manometer may be tilted with respect to gravity,
thus giving a greater motion of liquid along the tube for a given vertica-height
change. The inclined manometer (draft gage) of Fig. 6.5 exemplifies this principle.
Since this is a single-leg device, the calibrated seale is corrected for the slight
changes in well level so that rezeroing of the scale for each reading is not re-
'quired.
'The accurate measurement of extremely small pressure differences is accom-
plished with the micromanometer, a variation on the inclined-manometer prit
ciple. In Fig. 65 the instrument is initially adjusted so that when py = p2. the
'meniscus in the inclined tube is located at a reference point given by a fixed
hairline viewed through a magnifier. The reading of the micrometer used to
'adjust well height is now noted. Application of the unknown pressure difference
causes the meniscus to move off the hairline, but it cam be restored to its initial
position by raising or lowering the well with the micrometer. The difference in
initial and final micrometer readings gives the height change h and thus the
pressure. Instruments using water as the working fluid and having a range of
either 10 or 20 in of water can be read to about 0.001 in of water." In another
instrument in which the inclined tube (rather than the well) is moved and which
uses butyl alcohol as the working Muid, the range is 2 in of alcohol, and read-
ability is 0.0002 in. This corresponds to a resolution of 6 x 10-* Ibjin®
While manometers usually are read visually by a human operator, various
schemes for rapid and accurate automatic readout are available, mainly for cali
bration and standards work using gaseous media. The sonar manometer* em-
ploys a piezoelectric transducer at the bottom of each 1.S-in-diameter glass tube
to launch ultrasonic pulses, which travel up through the mercury columns, are
reflected at the meniscus, and return to the bottom to be received by the trans-
ducers. The pulse from the shorter column turns on a digital counter, while that
from the longer one turns it off. Thus a digital reading is obtained that is pr
portional to the difference in column height and thus to pressure. Resolution is
10,0003 in Hg, and accuracy is 0.001 inHg or 0003 percent of reading, whichever is
* Mera Instrument Co. Cleveland, Oba
2 DE. Van Dyk, Sonic Measurement of Manomster Colum Height, Wallace & Tiernan Div
Bete No

-- 476 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 470
Fig 64 Manometer mode,
er. Since temperature effects on sonic velocity and column length cause
additive errors, a feedback control system keeps instrument temperature at
95 + 005 deg F.
'Another instrument! employs two large mercury cisterns (one fixed, one
vertically movable by an electromechanical servasystem) connected by flexible
tubing to create a U-tube manometer. Each cistern has a capacitor formed by a
'metal plate, the mercury surface, and a small air gap between them. The two
capacitors are connected in an electric circuit which exhibits a null reading when
the air gaps in both cisterns are equal and produces an error voltage when they
are not. This error voltage causes the servosystem to drive the movable cistern 10
an elevation where balance is again achieved. A digital counter on the servosy~
stem motor shaft reads out position to the nearest 0.0001 inHg. System accuracy
is +-0.0003 inkg +0003 percent of reading. For manometers such as the two
above, accessory automatic systems for generating and regulating the pressures of
the gaseous (usually air or nitrogen) calibration media usually are available.
Manometer Dynamics
While manometers are utilized mainly for static measurements, their dynamic
response is sometimes of interest. The general problem of the oscillations of
liquid columns is an interesting (and rather difficult) question in fuid mechanics
and has received considerable attention in the literature Here we take a con-
siderably simplified view of the problem which nonetheless gives results of practi-
cal interest. In the U-tube configuration of Fig. 66a, the unknown pressures py
* Schwein Engineering. Pomona, Cai.
21. Ury, Viscous Damping in Oscillating Liguid Columns, Its Magnitude and Limits, nt J
Mech Set, ol. 4 p39, 1962; PD. Ricardo, Comments on Vicous Damping i Osilaing
igi Columns, nt J. Meck Set vol $9 415, 1963,

-- 477 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 471
PRESSURE AXD SOUND MEASUREMENT 487
land p, are exerted by @ gas whose inertia and viscosity may be considered
negligible compared with those of the manometer liquid. Ifthe pressures vary
with time, the reading of the manometer varies with time: we are interested in the
fidelity with which the manometer reading follows the pressure variation. The
motion of the manometer liquid in the tube is caused by the action of various
forces, I we consider the manometer liquid in its entirety as a free body and
search fr forces acting oni, the following forces come to mind
1. The gravity force (weight) distributed uniformly over the whole body of fluid
2. A drag force due to motion of the fluid within the tube and related to the
wall shearing stress
1, The forces on the two ends of the free body due to pressures p, and py
44 Distributed normal pressure of the tube on the fuid
5. Surface-tension effects at the two ends ofthe body of uid
[A detailed analysis of all these effects would lead to rather complex and
'unwieldy mathematics. Fortunately, useful results may be obtained by a sim-
plified analysis. The intial step is the assumption that the system shown in Fig
{66h is dynamically equivalent to that of Fig. 66a. The "gravity spring" force of
Fig. 66 is explained as follows: In Fig. 66a, whenever x # 0, there is an unbal-
anced gravity force acting on the liquid column tending to restore the level to
(0. The magnitude of this force is -2nR*xy, where 7 & manometer-Auid
specific weight, in pounds force per cubic inch, We see that this force is pro-
portional to the displacement x and always opposes it; thus it has all the charac-
teristics of a spring force. When the liquid column is "straightened out" in Fig.
66h, we must include this "gravity spring" force in our equivalent system if we
are to prescrve the analogy of the two configurations. In comparing Fig. 665
with Fig. 664, we note that any effects of low curvature in the 180 deg bend ate los.
But these will probably be small if the diameter of the bend is large compared
'with the inside diameter of the manometer tube and ifthe total length L is large
'compared with the bend length. We also neglect any surface-tension effects at the
tends of the column, This is usually a good assumption if the column is long
relative to its diameter
In addition to the gravity-spring force and the pressure forces due to p, and
isthe liquid column is subjected to a drag force at the interface between the
Tiguid and the wall of the tube. This drag force is equal to the wall shearing stress
times the surface area ofthe liquid column. The motion ofthe liquid in the tube
may be thought of as an unsteady pipe flow. We assume that at any instant of
time the wall shearing stress can be computed from the instantaneous velocity of
the liquid by using the formulas commonly used for steady pipe flows.
'The flow of liquid in the tube may occur in the laminar, transition, oF
turbulent regimes. First let us assume laminar flow prevails. The pressure drop
'Ap due to pipe friction for both laminar and turbulent flow is given by

Since laminar flow was assumed in carrying out the above analysis, we

The most significant difference between Eqs. (627) and (6.28) is that (627) rep-
resents a nonlinear relation between shear stress and velocity whereas (628) is
ear. This means that when the force due to wall shearing stress is substituted
into Newton's law, the result is a nonlinear differential equation because of the
term (3)!7 deg , This nonlinear equation cannot be solved analytically, so the pres-
tence of turbulent flow leads to mathematical difficulties.
In working with oscillations of systems with nonlinear damping terms similar
to the (x)! of this example, engineers have developed an approximate method
'of analysis which is quite useful. This approach is based on the observation that
while the linear damping term X and the nonlinear term, such as()*, are quite
different mathematically, the general form ofthe oscillation in the two systems is
not radically different in experimental tess. Ifthe linear system is excited by a
sinusoidal exciting force, it will espond with a sinusoidal motion, whereas the
nonlinear system's motion will not be purely sinusoidal. However, observation
shows that the deviation from pure sinusoidal motion is usually quite small
Using these facts as a basis, then, we might reason as follows: Ifa system with
rnonlincar damping is executing steady oscillations of fixed amplitude, during
each cycle the damping force will dissipate a certain amount of energy. If we
know from experience that the waveform of the nonlinear oscillation is nearly
sinusoidal, we can compute approximately the energy dissipation per cycle. This
is done as follows: Suppose there exists a steady oscillation of amplitude ¥ and
frequency w. Ifwe assume the waveform to be sinusoidal, we can write
y= ¥sinor (629)
and Y= Yo cos ot (620)

Original PDF Page 476

product of velocity and damping force. Ifthe damping force is a known function
of velocity /(j), we can write

Many assumptions were made in the above analysis. The formulas for steady
pipe flow were employed for an unsteady situation. In an oscillating flow, velocity

-- 484 of 1000 --

454 wasunine devices
actually goes to zer0 twice each cycle, no matter how great the amplitude or
frequency; thus one wonders whether part of such a cycle is turbulent and part
laminar. In the analysis above, turbulent equations were used for the whole eyle
'The nonlinear differential equation containing (x)"7® actually has no closed-form
analytical solution. Thus what is the meaning ofa, and ano, attached to such
'process? Such questions and others may be at least partially resolved by more
'complex analyses or experimental studies. To provide some idea of the degree of
validity of our simplified analysis, some experimental results are given. They were
'obtained at The Ohio State University by undergraduate students who study
'manometer dynamics in a simple experiment in a measurement course
'The experiment consists in part of suddenly releasing an ait pressure applied
to a mercury manometer and observing the resulting oscillations. The process is
slow enough that ¢, can be estimated from the size ofthe frst overshoot and «,
by counting and timing cycles with a stopwatch. The manometers used have the
'numerical values quoted earlier inthis section. A step pressure input of 10 inHg
(x= 5 in) is utilized; thus turbulent flow may be expected. If laminar low were
assumed, the theoretical values would be {= 0.007 and w= 4.7 rad/s. For tur
bulent flow, «becomes 5.4 rad/s. To calculate {, from Eq. (6.48), the frequency
'and amplitude ofthe oscillation must be known. For a step input the frequency is.
the damped natural frequency rather than «,; however, these are practically
identical for the small damping present, and so we use @,.. We experimentally
'measure {rom the first overshoot, and So the proper amplitude to employ in the
theoretical calculation might be an average of the initial amplitude and of that at
the first overshoot. Only the initial amplitude (Sin) is known, though, and so this
is used, Equation (6.44) then gives ¢, = 0.0905. By timing and counting cycles
(about 8 cycles ofthe decaying oscillation can be measured easily) the experimen-
tal value of o, is $2 rad/s. This lies between the values calculated for turbulent
and laminar low and thus is not unreasonable, since several of the 8 cycles used
were of quite low amplitude because of the decay of the oscillation. The first
'overshoot is about 405 in, giving an experimental value of £ of 0.067. Therefore
the theoretical estimate of 0.0505 is fairly good. If we now use the experimental
value of « (52) and the average amplitude of the first half-cycle (4.53) in Eq
(6-4), then the predicted ¢, is 0082, which compares even more favorably. Based
'on even these limited restlts, a certain amount of confidence in the theoretical
predictions is established,

idequacies in the original nonlinear model, inaccuracy in numerical values of

tiometer, differential transformers, ete. The basic element in all the various forms
isa tube of noneircular cross section. A pressure difference between the inside
'and outside of the tube (higher pressure inside) causes the tube to attempt 10
'attain a eieular cross section, This results in distortions which lead to a curv:
Tinear translation ofthe fee end in the C type and spiral and helical types and an
angular rotation in the twisted type, which motions are the output. The theor-
tical analysis of these effects is difficult, and practical design at present still
makes use of considerable empirical data. The C-type Bourdon tube has been
Utilized up to about 100,000 Ib in?, The spiral and helical configurations are
attempts to obtain more output motion fora given pressure and have been used
'mainly below about 1,000 Ibjin?, The twisted tube shown has a crossed-wire
Stabilizing device which is stiff in all radial directions but soft in rotation. This
feduces spurious output motions from shock and vibration. Figure 69 shows
Construction of a higher accuracy (0. percent) C-type Bourdon test gage. Also
shown isan optional bimetal temperature compensator which maintains accu-
Taey over the range -25 to +125 F. This device corrects for both thermal zero
shift and span shift
Flectrical output pressure transducers are available in forms combining
various elastic elements with most of the displacement transducers described in
Chap. 4 ie. potentiometers, strain gages, LVDTS, capacitance pickups, eddy-
current probes, reluctance and inductance pickups, piezoclectric elements, ee.
Space does not permit detailed discussion ofall types, so we choose specific ex-
amples that illustrate features we feel to be particularly significant and interest
ing
Figure 6.10 shows a sensor utilizing an infrared LED (light-emitting diode)
and two photodiodes to optically measure displacement (0.020 in fll scale) ofthe
pressure-sensitive elastic clement, The reference and measurement photodiodes
fre on the same chip and thus are equally affected by temperature changes.
Changes in LED output due to temperature or age also cancel, since both diodes
share the same illumination and a ratiometric integrating analog/digital con-
verter is employed to obtain a digital output sensitive to only diode-lluminated
areas Ay and Ay and pot settings 2 and fl, While diode signals exhibit nonlin
ferity which varies from unit to unit, this nonlinearity and all others are lin.
'earized in the analog digital converter using a look-up table resident in a pair of
PROMs (programmable read-only memories). Thus each sensor has its individ
tual nonlinearities linearized by programming its PROM at calibration time. This
'design is characteristic of recent trends which, rather than making a great effort,
to obtain linearity in basic sensors. accept their nonlinearity (as long as itis
repeatable) and then use programmable electronic compensation tailored fo each
'unit to achieve overall linearity. The referenced unit is available in ranges of 0 10
50 in H,0 to 60.000 Ibn? gage and 5 Ibjin® absolute to 60,000 byin* absolute
with accuracy 0.1 percent of span and temperature effect on span of +0004
percent) F from 0 to 160'F. An automaticzero feature in the analog/diital
'Converter minimizes thermal zero shifts. Interestingly, the manufacture also uses
the same optical sensor as a null detector in a force-balance type of pressure

(6:6) may be used to estimate the degree of nonlinearity. The stress situation on

exist simultaneously. This allows use of a four-active-arm bridge in which all
effcts are additive (giving large output) and gives temperature compensation
also. Gages 2 and 4 are placed as close to the center as possible and oriented to
read tangential strain, since itis maximum (positive) at this point, Gages 1 and 3

'radial strain has its maximum negative value at that point. The laws of bridge
circuits show that the pressure effects on all four gages are additive. In computing
the overall sensitivity, Eqs. (647) and (6.48) cannot be employed directly t0

Figure 6.14 shows construction of a piezoresstve (semiconductor *
age") pressure transducer of flush-diaphragm design with full-scale ranges from
0 10 2 10 0 to 200 bars. The measuring cell is a welded assembly of two silicon
chips forming a sealed chamber containing the reference pressure (or vacuum for
absolute pressure sensing). One chip is bored out on one side to form a diaph-
ragm of thickness suited to the desired range, while the reverse side has @ com-
plete bridge of strain-sensitive regions diffused into the silicon surface. Use of
'constant-current bridge excitation (see figure) enhances primary temperature
compensation, which together with external discrete compensating. resistors
borings the overall thermal zero shift to less than 001 percent of fll-seale/"C and
the sensitivity shift to less than 0.02 percent/"C. Pressure is transmitted from the
thin metal diaphragm through an oil fill (which also provides damping) to the
silicon capsule, Natural frequency is greater than 70 kHz, transducer compliance
{0.001 mm/bar, and acceleration sensitivity is 10-* bar/e.
A line of piezoresstve pressure transducers® available in ranges from 2 to
50,000 Ibvin? applies the Auid pressure directly to a silicon diaphragm with a
'complete Wheatstone bridge of strain elements diffused into the diaphragm.
'Chemical etching processes are employed to produce a "sculptured" diaphragm
(rather than the usual fat configuration) with thick and thin sections carefully
'designed to concentrate strain at the locations where the gages will be diflused
io the silicon. A further innovation employs the piezoresistive material in a
ransvers, rather than longitudinal, configuration, which gives improved linearity
since the transverse gages exhibit compensating nonlinearities for the tension and
compression legs of the bridge. While the transducers are specified for use with
dry nonconductive gases, short-term applications in water? have been successful

periodically be exposed (momentarily) to some known reference pressure (perhaps
the most common example would be a gage pressure unit, which could be vented
{o atmosphere, giving a known O lbvin® gage input). The automatic-reference
circuit measures and "remembers" the voltage output for zero pressure input,
and when the transducer is reconnected (o its usual pressure signal, the circuit
adds or subtracts this value, thereby correcting for bias errors from all sources, (If
'wo different reference pressures can be employed, the technique can correct for
slope errors too) Figure 6.156" shows block diagrams for "hardware" and "soft-
ware" (microprocessor) versions of automatic-referencing (consult the reference
for detail
Most designs of pressure transducers are available in gage (psig), absolute
(sia), diferental (psid), and sealed (psi) reerence versions. A sealed reference
transducer is similar (0 the absolute pressure version, except that the sealed
reference chamber contains a nonzero pressure rather than 0 Ib/in® absolute
Since this reference pressure will change with temperature, applications must take
this fact info account. Some transducer designs expose sensitive electrical ele-
'ments to the measured fluid and thus may be limited to dry noncorrosive gases.

Figure 6.16 shows a unit whose sensing diaphragm forms the moving plate of
2 diflerential capacitor; the motion is transduced to a proportional direct curtent
by using an ac-excited bridge circuit, demodulation, and a feedback-type current
regulator. Stainlss-stee! (316 SS) isolating diaphragms protect the sensor from
process fluids while transmitting the measured pressures through silicone-fuid
fills to the sensing diaphragm. Overpressure causes the sensing diaphragm to
(safely) bottom out on the glass backup plates, causing less than 0.25 percent of
span error when normal operation resumes after a 2,000 Ibn? overload.
Figure 6.17 shows a unit using diffused silicon "strain gage" technology. A
single silicon crystal is eut to 1/4-in® by 1/10 in thick, and the center of one side is
'ground out and etched to form an integral diaphragm of thickness suitable for
the desired pressure range (3 in,O to 10,000 Ibjin'), Then boron is diffused into
the other side of the silicon chip to form four complete Wheatstone-bridge
cuits, each having four strain-sensitive elements properly located on the diaph-
ragm. A computerized test system checks each of the four bridges and selects for
further processing only the best one. (This approach is typical of integrated-
circuit manufacturing technology in which the most cost-effective approach often
involves high-rate production with a certain percentage of defectives, which are
later weeded out by automated test equipment) Isolation diaphragms. and
-one-lud fills ate employed to protect the sensor from process Nuids. Over-
load protection is provided by valving (actuated by an overload bellows) which
'equalizes pressure on both sides of the silicon diaphragm when the diferential
pressure approaches unsafe valves,

diagrams. Previous discussions of feedback-type instruments maybe helpful

{Kept in continuous oscillation by using a limit-cycling feedback system exactly as
in the densitometer of Fig. 747. Changes in the measuted gas pressure cause related
