66 DYNAMIC EFFECTS OF VOLUMES AND.
CONNECTING TUBING
We have mentioned the possible strong effect of fd properties and "plumbing"
configurations on the dynamic behavior of pressure-measuring systems. In this
Section some of these problems are investigated. First, it should be pointed out
that if maximum dynamic performance isto be attained. then a Nush-diaphragm
Transducer mounted directly at the point where a pressure measurement is
'wanted should be used if at all possible (see Fig. 619). Any connecting tubing or
Volume chambers will degrade performance to some extent. The fact that this
Uegradation is studied in this section indicates that in many practical circum-
Stances a flush-diapheagm transducer is not applicable.

Liquid Systems, Heavily Damped, Slow-Acting
In the system of Fig. 620, the spring-loaded piston represents the flexible element
of the pressure pickup. For the present analysis, the only pertinent characteristic
'of the pressure pickup is its volume change per unit pressure change, the com.
pliance C,» [in'(lbjin')]. This can be caleulated or measured experimentally.
(The transducer of Fig. 6.14, for example, has C,, ~ 0.001 mm /bar) For systems
that are heavily damped (a criterion for judging this is given shorly) or subjected
to relatively slow pressure changes, the inertia effects of both the fluid and the
'moving parts of the pickup are negligible compared with viscous and spring
forces. We show that under these conditions the measured pressure p follows the
desired pressure p, with a first-order lag. For steady laminar flow in the tube we
have

is available, In the system of Fig. 620, any change in pressure 7, must be

5 lbvin® step function gave 022. The thooretial value was 0.065, suggesting that
the theory might be quite accurate for sufficiently small inputs, Detailed dis

Gas Systems with Tube Volume a Small Fraction of Chamber Volume

ofthe gas in volume V becomes the major spring effect when the pressure
kup is at all stiff Tis reasonable, then, to assume that the volume V is

rather low frequencies. This allows treatment as a lumped-parameter system with
fluid properties considered constant along the length of the tube for small dis-
turbances. The validity of this viewpoint is shown by noting that pressure-wave
propagation inthe gas follows the general law of wave motion:

'The Infinite-Line Pressure Probe?
'The configuration of Fig. 622, called an infnte-line probe, in some cases can be
used to achieve a gas pressure-measuring system of very-high-frequency response.
'The basic idea behind this design is tha, for a sufficiently long (infnite") line,
pressure fluctuations at the measuring station will have attenuated to small enough
values at the far end that their reflections back to the measuring station will be
very small, giving negligible measurement errors. The fat-end "termination" of the
line may be an open end, a closed end, or a terminating volume, depending on

the needs of the particular experiment. The reference gives calculation methods,

Which gave useful measurements to about 100 kHz.
Conclusion
The results ofthis section are to be thought of as practical working relations. The
seneral problem treated here is quite complex and has been the subject of many
Intecate analyses and experimental studies, some of which are found in the
Bibliography ofthis chapter. Most ofthe dificultis encountered ate inthe area
of very high fequencies. where the lumped-parameter models usd inthis section
ae inadequate and give faulty predictions
67 DYNAMIC TESTING OF PRESSURE-MEASURING
SYSTEMS.
To determine the regions of accuracy of theoretical predictions or to find accu-
humeral values of system dynamic characteristics for critical applications,
recourse must be made to experimental testing, This commonly takes the form of
'impulse, step, oF frequency-response tests, with stepfunction tests being perhaps
the most common. A comprehensive review? of this subject is available. Here we
ean mention only few high points.
For step-function tests of systems in which natural frequencies are not
areater than about 1,000 Hz, the bursting of a thin diaphragm subjected to gas
pressure i often satisfactory. A general rule for step testing is that the rise time of
the "step" function must be less than about one-fourth of the natural period of
the system tested if itis to excite the natural oscillations. Thus a 1,000-Hy system
requires a step witha rise time of 0.25 ms or less. Figure 6.23a shows schematically
the principle of such devices. The pressutes p, and p, are each individually
adjustable. The volume containing pis much smaller than that containing p,
'thus when the thin plastic diaphragm is ruptured by a solenoid-actuated knife,
the pressure p; rises top, very quickly. Ifa decreasing step function is wanted, p;
'ean be made larger than p,. Construction and operation of such devices are quite
simple, and they have been utilized widely in their range of applicability
Rise times of about 20 pcan be achieved withthe Aronson dynamic calibrator
'of Fig. 6234.?* Postive- or negative-going gas pressure steps of up to 1000 Ibjin?

diaphragm testers are not capable of exciting the natural oscillations, and the
pickup output is simply an accurate record of the terminated-ramp pressure
input. To achieve suficiently short pressure-rise times, the shock tube is widely
used, Figure 6.24 shows a sketch of such a device. A thin diaphragm separates the
high-pressure and low-pressure chambers, and the transducer to be tested is
'mounted flush with the end of the low-pressure chamber. When the diaphragm is
'caused to burst, a shock wave travels toward the low-pressure end at a speed that
may greatly exceed the speed of sound (5,000 fs is not unusual) From one side
of this shock front to the other, there is a pressure change of the order of 2: 1
'over a distance which may be'of the order of 10 deg * in. (At the same time, a
rarefaction wave travels from the diaphragm toward the high-pressure end)
When the shock front reaches the end of the tube where the transducer is
'mounted, it is reflected as a shock wave with more than twice the pressure
difference of the original shock wave. The transducer is thus exposed t0 a very
sharp (~10 * s) pressure rise which is maintained constant for a short interval
* Mode 907A, PCB Piesotroies, Depew, NY.

-- 516 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 510
before various reflected waves arrive to confuse the picture. The length of this
interval may be controlled to a certain extent by proper proportioning and
'operation of the shock tube. Some numerical characteristics of a typical shock
tube! are as follows: high-pressure chamber 7 long, low-pressure chamber 15 ft
Jong, tubing inside dimensions 1.4 in® (wall thickness 4 in), maximum high pres-
sure 600 Ibjin®, operating Muid air, maximum pressure step 350 Ibjin?, burst
diaphragm 0001- to 0.005-in-thick Mylar plastic, and duration of constant pres-
sure 001 s, For a pressure pickup of, say, 100,000-Hz natural frequency, a
pressurestise time of less than 0.25 x 10. sis required, This is readily met by the
tube described above. The 001-s step duration would give time for about 1,000
AA simple impulse-type test method applicable to lat, fush-diaphragm trans-
ducers utilizes small stel ball dropped onto the diaphragm. The impact excites
'the natural oscillations, which are recorded and analyzed for natural frequency
and damping ratio. Although this input is a concentrated force rather than a
'uniform pressure, the results have been found? to correlate quite well with shock-
tube tests. Another impulse method employs the shock wave created by dis-
'charging 25,000 V across a spark gap. The spark gap and transducer are located
about 3 in apart in open air. A pressure impulse of 0.2 rise time and 100 yin?
peak can be obtained,
Figure 6.254 shows one method of constructing a frequency-response tester
using liquid as the pressure medium. The vibration shaker applies a sinusoidal
fotce of adjustable feequency and amplitude to the piston/diaphragm to create
Sinusoidal pressure in the liguid-flled chamber. Such vibration shakers are
readily available in industry and cover a wide range of force and frequency. The
average pressure about which oscillations take place may be adjusted by regulat-
ing the bias pressure on the air side ofthe eylinder. Since usually itis not possible
to predict accurately and reproducibly the pressure actually produced by such an
arrangement as frequency and/or amplitude is varied, itis customary to mount a
reference transducer at a location where it will experience the same pressure as
the transducer under test. The reference transducer must have a known Mat
frequency response beyond any frequencies to be tested. This ean be determined
bby some independent method, such as a shock tube. In testing another transdu-
cer, one merely calculates the amplitude ratio and phase shift between the refer-
ence and test transducer to determine the test-transducer frequency response. A
different version' of the shaker method uses an open, vertical uid column (Fig.
£6256) to generate the test pressure,

area is modulated approximately sinusoidally with time. This is accomplished by
rotating a disk containing holes in front of the exhaust port so that outflow
periodically is cut on and off, This produces a periodic (nearly sinusoidal) vari
jan in chamber pressure, which is measured by both a reference transducer and
the test transducer, Varying the speed of the rotating disk changes the frequency.
The amplitude of pressure oscillation of such a device drops off with frequency.
For the system described, with helium gas at a supply pressure of 121 Ibyin
absolute, the peak-to-peak pressure amplitude goes from about 15 Ibjin® at
1,000 Hz to about 2Ibvin® at 11,000 Hz. In addition to this reduction in ampli-
tude, an inerease in Frequency also brings into play the resonant acoustical fre
'quencies of the chamber. When these resonances occur, one cannot depend on
the pressures being uniform throughout the chamber. This uniformity is a neces
sity when the method used is based on comparison ofa reference transducer with
the test transducer. The acoustic resonant frequencies depend on the chamber
size (smaller chambers have higher frequencies) and the speed of sound in the gas
{higher sound speed gives higher frequencies), The use of helium in the above
'eximple is based on this last consideration. A system of the above type was found,
to be usable for dynamic calibration up to about 10.000 Hz
Tn summary it should be pointed out that the dynamic testing of very-high-
frequency pressure pickups involves a number of complicating factors. It has been
found extremely difficult to generate high-frequency pressure sine waves that are
'of large enough amplitude to give a relatively noise-ree transducer output signal
Small-amplitude pressure waves (such as are applicable to sound-measuring sy'-
tems) ean be produced relatively easily with loudspeaker-type systems. but their
amplitude is far below the levels needed for pressure pickups whose full-scale
range is tens, hundreds, or thousands of pounds per square inch, Step testing with
4 shock tube thus has been widely used since the fst rise time and large pressure
Steps result in a transient input with strong high-frequency content. The pickups
themselves present problems since atthe high natural frequencies involved, many
complex wave-propagation and reflection effects make the response deviate con.
siderably from the simple second-order model. Also, these pickups generally have
Iitle damping (¢ = 001 to 0.08), which makes them particularly prone to ringing
at their natural frequency if any sharp transients occur.

JRE (VACUUM)
Two commonly employed units of vacuum measurement are the torr and the
micrometer, One torr is pressure equivalent to 1 mmHg at standard conditions:
Dre micrometer ic 10" torr, Manometers and bellows gages are usable to about
ft tore, Bourdon gages to 10 torr, and diaphragm gages to 10 * torr. Below
these ranges other types of vacuum gages are necessary
MeLcod Gage
The MeLsod gage is considered & vacuum standard since the pressure ean be
computed from the dimensions of the gage. It is not directly usable below about
TH! tore: however. presureividing techniques (see Sec. 61) allow its use as a
bration standard for considerably lower ranges. The multiple-compression
technique" i also being studied to extend its range. The inaecuracy of McLeod
tuages rarely kee than 1 persent and may be much higher at the lowest
pressures
'OF the many variations of MeLeod gages. here we consider only the most
basic. The principle of all MeLeod gages is the compression of a sample of the
fonw-pressure gas to a pressure sulficiently high to read with a simple manometer.
Figure 6.28 shows the basic construction. By withdrawing the plunger, the mer.
ccury level is lowered to the postion of Fig. 628«, admitting the gas at unknown
pressure p,, When the plunger is pushed in, the mercury level goes up sealing off
gas sample of known volume V in the bulb and capillary tube 4. Further
motion of the plunger causes compression of this sample, and motion is con-
tinued until the mercury level in capillary B is at the zero mark, The unknown
pressure is then calculated, by using Boyles law, as follows:

In using a McLeod gage it is important to realize that if the measured gas
'contains any vapors that are condensed by the compression process, then the
Pressure will be in error. Except for ths effect, the reading of the McLeod gage is
'not influenced by the composition of the gas. Only the Knudsen gage shares this
desirable feature of composition insensitivity. The main drawbacks of the
MeLcod gage are the lack of a continuous output reading and the limitations on
the lowest measurable pressures. When itis employed to calibrate other gages, a
liquid-air cold trap should be used between the MeLeod gage and the gage to be
calibrated to prevent the passage of mercury vapor.
Knudsen Gage
Although the Knudsen gage is little utilized at present, we discus it briefly since
itis relatively insensitive t0 gas composition and thus gives promise of devel-
'opment into a standard for pressures too low for the McLeod gage. In Fig. 629
the unknown pressure p, is admitted to a chamber containing fixed plates heated
to absolute temperature T;, which temperature must be measured, and a spring
restrained movable vane whose temperature T, also must be known. The spac.
ing between the fied and movable plates must be les than the mean free path of
the gas whose pressure is being measured. The kinetic theory of gases shows that
2s molecules rebound from the heated plates with greater momentum than from
the cooler movable vane, thus giving a net force on the movable vane which is
'measured by the deflection of the spring suspension, Analysis shows thatthe force
is directly proportional to pressure for a given 7; and 7,, following a law of the

'Composition except for the variation of accommodation coefficient from one gas
fo another, The accommodation coefficient is a measure of the extent {0 which @

Knudsen pages at present cover the range from about 10-* to 10 7 tort.
Momentum-Transfer (Viscosity) Gages
For pressures less than about 10"? tort, the kinetic theory of gases predicts that
the wscosity of a gas will be directly proportional to the pressure. The viscosity
may be measured, for example, in terms of the torgue required to rotate, at
Constant speed, one concentric cylinder within another. (For pressures greater
than about 1 torr, the viscosity is independent of pressure) The variation of
Viscosity with pressure is diferent for different eases; thus gages based on this
principle must be calibrated for a specific gas. While gages based on viscosity
principles can measure to about 10 deg " torr, such ranges are characteristic of
Taboratory-type equipment requiring great care in its use.
"A typical commercial gage, shown schematically in Fig. 6.30 is calibrated for
dry air and covers the range from 0 to 20 tort. The range from 0 to 0.01 torr
'ceupies about 10 percent of the total scale. The scale is nonlinearly calibrated

'Thermal-Conductivity Gages
Just as for viscosity, when the pressure of a gas becomes low enough that the
'mean free path of molecules is large compared with the pertinent dimensions of
the apparatus, a linear relation between pressure and thermal conductivity iS
Predicted by the kinetic theory of gases. For a viscosity gage, the pertinent
'dimension is the spacing between the relatively moving surfaces. For a conduc
tivity gage, itis the spacing between the hot and cold surfaces. Again, when the
pressure is increased sufficiently, conductivity becomes independent of gus pres.
sure, The transition region between dependence and nondependence of viscosity
and thermal conductivity on pressure is approximately the range 10-2 to | torr
{or apparatus ofa size convenient to construct.
The application of the thermal-conductivity principle is complicated by the
simultaneous presence of another mode of heat transfer between the hot and cold
surfaces, namely, radiation, Most gages utilize a heated element supplied with a
Constant energy input. This element assumes an equilibrium temperature when
hheat input and losses by conduction and radiation are just balanced. The conduc.
tion losses vary with gas composition and gas pressure: thus fora given gas: the
equilibrium temperature of the heated element becomes a measure of pressure,
sand this temperature is what is actually measured. Ifthe radiation losses are a
* Viscovas;Inicon Leyold-HeracusE Syracse, NY.

-- 525 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 519
PRESSURE AND SOUND MEASUREMENT. 495
major part of the total, then pressure-induced conductivity changes will cause
'only a slight temperature change, giving poor sensitivity. Analysis shows that
tadiation losses may be minimized by using surfaces of low emissivity and by
imaking the cold-surface temperature as low as practical. Since conduction and
radiation losses depend on both the hot- and cold-surface temperatures, the cold
Surface may be maintained at a known constant temperature if overall accuracy
'warrants this measure. A further source of efror isin the heat-conduetion loss
through any solid supports by which the heated element is mounted. The relative
importance of the above-mentioned effects varies with the details of construction
'ofthe gage. The most common types of conductivity gages are the thermocouple,
resistance thermometer (Pani), and thermistor.
'Figure 631 shows in schematic form the basic elements of a thermocouple
'vacuum gage. The hot surface is a thin metal strip whose temperature may be
'varied by changing the current passing through it. For a given heating current
land gas, the temperature assumed by the hot surface depends on pressure; this
temperature is measured by a thermocouple welded to the hot surface. The cold
Surface here is the glass tube, which usually is near room temperature. Often the
"accuracy of such gages is not high enough to warrant measurement or correction
or changes in room temperature. Thermocouple gages of one type or another are
available to measure in the range 10 deg to tore,

In the resistance-thermometer (Pirani) gage, the functions of heating and.
{temperature measurement are combined in a single clement. A typical construc:
tion is shown in Fig. 6.32. The resistance element is in the form of four coiled
tungsten wires connected in parallel and supported inside a glass tube to which
the gas is admitted. Again, the cold surface isthe glass tube. Two identical tubes
'generally are connected in a bridge circuit, as shown. One of the tubes is evacu
ated to a very low pressure and then sealed off while the other has the gas
admitted to it.The evacuated tube acts as a compensator to reduce the effect of
bridge-excitation-voltage changes and temperature changes on the output
'reading. Current flowing through the measuring element heats it to a temperature
depending on the gas pressure. The electrical resistance of the element changes
with temperature, and this resistance change causes a bridge unbalance. Gener.
ally the bridge is used as a deflection rather than a null device. Te balance the
bridge initially, the pressure in the measuring element is made very small and the
balance pot set for zero output. Any changes in pressure will cause a bridge
unbalance, OF course, the gage must be calibrated against some standard. Cali
bration is nonlinear and varies from one gas to another. Pirani gages cover the
range from about 10-* to 1 torr.

'Thermistor vacuum gages operate on the same principle as the Pirani gage
'except that the resistance elements are temperature-sensitive semiconductor ma-
terials called thermistors, rather than metals such as tungsten or platinum. Ther-
rmistor gages are used in the range 10. to I tort.
onization Gages
{An electron passing through a potential difference acquires a kinetic eneray
proportional to the potential difference, When this energy is large enough and the
leetran strikes gas molecule, there isa definite probability that the electron will
"ve an electron ovt of the molecule, leaving it a positively charged on, In an
fonization gage, a stream of electrons is emitted from a cathode. Some of these
Strike gas molecules and knock out secondary electrons, leaving the molecules as
positive ions. For normal operation of the gage. the secondary electrons are a
hegligible part of the total electron current: thus, for all practical purposes
tlectton current i, is the same whether measured at the emitting point (cathode)
fF the collecting point (anode), The number of positive ions formed is directly
proportional to j, and directly proportional to the gas pressure. If, is held fixed
{as in most gages). the rate of production of positive ions (ion current) is. for &
fiven gas a direct measure of the number of gas molecules per unit volume and
thus of the pressure. The positive ions are attracted to a negatively charged
electrode, which collects them and carries the ion current. The "sensitivity" S of
'an ionization gage is defined by

boeing used. A main advantage of ionization gages in general is thei linearity:
that ts the Sensitivity $ is constant for a given gas over a wide range of pressures,

Dual-Gage Technique?
When large amounts of water vapor (common during early stages of evacuation)
'oF helium (from a leak-detection system) are present, simultaneous readings from
'an ionization gage and a thermocouple gage (both of which will read incorrect»)
can be processed to not only get a correct vacuum reading but also determine the
amount of moisture or helium present.
6.10 SOUND MEASUREMENT
'The measurement of airborne and waterborne sound is of increasing interest 10
engineers. Airborne-sound measurements are important in the development of
less noisy machinery and equipment, in diagnosis of vibration problems, and in
the design and test of sound recording and reproducing equipment. in large
rocket and jet engines. the sound pressures produced by the exhaust may be large
'enough to cause fatigue failure of metal panels because of vibration ("acoustic

fatigue"). Waterborne sound has been applied in underwater direction and range-
finding equipment (sonar), Since most sound transducers (microphones and hy-
érophones) are basically pressure-measuring devices, i is appropriate to consider
them briefly in this chapter.
"The basic definitions of sound are in terms of the magnitude of the flucta-

"The ims value of the fluctuating component of pressure is employed because
most sounds are random signals rather than pure sine waves The value
(0.0002 ybar is an accepted standard reference value of pressure against which
'other pressures are compared by Eq, (695) Note that when p = 020002 bar. the
Sound pressure level is 0 dB, This value was selected somewhat arbitraily, but
Tepresents the average threshold of hearing for human beings if a 1,000-He tone is
used. That is, the O-dB level was selected as the lowest pressure fluctuation
normally discernible by human beings. Since O dB is about 3 x 10 * Ibn', the
remarkable sensitivity of the human ear should be apparent. The decibel (loga-
Fithmic) scale ts used as @ convenience because of the great ranges of sound
pressure level of interest in ordinary work. For example, an office with tabulating
frachines may have an SPL of 74d8 (Iyibar) The average human threshotd of
pain is 144 dB. Sound pressures close to large rocket engines are on the order of
170 dB (1 Ibvin'). One atmosphere (147 Ibn!) is 194 dB. The span from the lowest
to the highest pressures of interest is thus ofthe order of 10-* to I, tremendous
range.
Sound-Level Meter
"The most commonly utilized instrument for routine sound measurements is the
sound-level meter This is actually a measurement system made up of « number
of interconnected components. Figure 6.34 shows a typical arrangement. The
Sound pressure pis transduced to a voltage by means of the microphone. Mi-
Crophones generally employ a thin diaphragm to convert pressure to motion. The
motion is then converted to voltage by some suitable transducer, usually a ca-
pacitance, piezoelectric, oF moving-coil ype. Microphones often have a "slow

'This is necessary because the (slow) hour-to-hour and day-to-day changes in

Which the microphone must respond. (Note that the eustachian tube of the
hhuman ear serves a similar function.) The presence of this leak dictates that
'microphones will not respond to constant oF slowly varying pressures. This is
usualy no problem since many measurements involve a human response to the
sound, and this is known to extend down to only about 10 to 20 Hz. Thus the
'microphone frequency response need go only to this range, not to zero frequency.
The output voltage of the microphone generally is quite small and at a high
'impedance level thus an amplifier of high input impedance and gain is used at
the output of the microphone. This can be a relatively simple ac amplifier, since
response to static or slowly varying voltages is not required. Capacitor micro-

'microphone housing. This close coupling reduces stray capacitance effects by

seers Gpained from measurements on human beings. showing that the fee=
tqueney response of the human eat is both "nonflat" and nonlinear. Each curve is
ipeted with 1 loudness unit called a phon, with 0 phon corresponding to the
+ Katz Primer om Sound Level Meter and Acoustical Caltraton, Bruel & Kae nstrments

-- 532 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 526
threshold of hearing. The ordinate (sound-pressure level in decibels) tells wh
pressure amplitude must be applied at any given frequency so that the human
'observer will perceive a sensation of equal loudness. For example, at a $0-phon
loudness level, $8-dB SPL. at 100 Hz sounds as loud as a 50-dB SPL. at 1,000
Hz. which demonstrates the nonflatness of the ear' frequency response. Its non:
linearity is manifested by the need for a family of curves for various loudness
levels. rather than just single curve. Since the main use ofa sound-level meter i
'not the accurate measurement of pressure, but rather the determination of the
loudness perceived by human beings, a flat instrument frequency response is not
really wanted. The weighting networks of Fig. 6.4 are electrical filters designed
to approximate the human car's response at three different loudness levels, so
'that instrument readings wil reflect perceived loudness, Usually three filters
(approximates 40-phon ear response), B (70-phon), and C (100-phon)~are pro-
vided, and Fig. 6.3Sb shows the frequency response of these filters (dashed lines
show tolerances allowed on "precision soundclevel meters"). Some meters also
Drovide a "fat" setting i true pressure measurements are wanted; if not, the C
'network is a good approximation. Actually, many practical measurements are
'made by employing the 4 scale since itis a simple approach which has given
'200d results in many cases and has been writen into many standards and codes.
Readings taken with a weighting network are called sound level rather than
sound-pressure level
'The output of the weighting network is further amplified and an output jack
Provided to lead this signal to an oscilloscope (i observation of the waveform is
desired) oF to a wave analyzer (if the frequency content of the sound is to be
determined). If only the overall sound magnitude is desired, the rms value of ey
'must be found. While true rms voltmeters are available, their expense is justifiable
'only in the highest-grade sound-level meters. Rather, the ateraye value of e, is
determined by rectifying and filtering, and then the meter scale is calibrated to
ead rms values. This procedute is exact for pure sine waves since there is &
Precise relation between the average value and the rms value ofa sine wave. For
onsinusoidal waves this is not true, but the error is generally small enough to be
acceptable for relatively unsophisticated work. The filtering is accomplished by
both a simple low-pass RC filter and the low-pass meter dynamics. Some meters
hhave a slow-fast response switch which changes the filtering. The slow position
ives a steady, easy-to-read needle position, but masks any short-term variations
Inthe signal. If these short-term variations are of interest, they may be visually
observed on the meter by switching it to fast response. While the meter is actually
reading the rms value of es (and thus ofp), itis calibrated in decibels since Ea,
(695) establishes a definite relation hetween sound pressure in microbars and
decibels,
Microphones
'While the design of microphones is a specialized and comple field with a large
technical literature, here we can point out some of the main considerations
Frequency response is still of major interest; however, the effects on frequency

-- 533 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 527
PRESSURE AND SOUND MEASUREMENT SDB
response of sound wavelength and direction of propagation are aspects of dyna-
mic behavior not regularly encountered in other measurements. The pressure
response of & mictophone refers to the frequency response relating a uniform
Sound pressure applied at the microphone diaphragm to the output voltage of the
'microphone. The pressure response of a given microphone may be estimated
theoretically or measured experimentally by one of a number of accepted
methods
What is usually desired is the freefeld response of the microphone. That is,
what is the relation between the microphone output voltage and the sound
pressure that enisted at the microphone location before the microphone was
Introduced into the sound field? The microphone distorts the pressure field be-
'cause its acoustical impedance is radically different from that of the medium (ai)
in which itis immersed. In fact, for most purposes, the microphone (including its
diaphragm) may be considered as «rigid body. Sound waves impinging on this
body give rise to complex reflections that depend on the frequency, the diretion
of propagation of the sound wave, and the microphone size and shape. When the
wavelength of the sound wave is very large compared with the microphone
dimensions (low frequencies), the effect of reflections is negligible for any angle of
incidence between the diaphragm and the wave-propagation direction, and the
fre-field response isthe sime as the pressure response. At very high frequencies,
where the wavelength is much smaller than the microphone dimensions, the
microphone acts as an infinite wall and the pressure at the microphone surface
[for waves propagating perpendicular to the diaphragm (0 deg angle of incidence)] is
twice what it would be if the microphone were not there. For waves propagating.
parallel to the diaphragm (90 deg incidence angle), the average pressure over the
Giaphragm surface is zer0, giving no output voltage. Between the very low and
very high frequencies, the effect of reflections is quite complicated and depends on
sound wavelength (frequency), microphone size and shape, and angle of inidence.
For simple geometric shapes such as spheres and cylinders, theoretical results
are available? Experiments on actual microphones give results such as those
shown in Fig. 6.36, Note that for suficiently low frequencies (below a few thous-
'and hertz) there is little change in pressure because of the presence of the micro
phone; also the angle of incidence has litle effect. Ths flat frequency range can
be extended by reducing the size of the microphone; however, smaller size tends
to reduce sensitivity, The size effect is directly related to the relative size of the
microphone and the wavelength of the sound. The wavelength / of sound waves
inair is roughly 13,000/finches, where fis the frequency in hertz. When 2 becomes
Comparable to the microphone-diaphragm diameter, significant reflection effects
can be expected. For example, a I-in-diameter microphone would not be ex
pected to have good response much above 13,000 Hz. (These limitations can be
relaxed to some extent by clever use of acoustical mechanical techniques.*)

'The lower part of Fig. 636 shows a curve labeled "random incidence" This
refers to the response toa difuse sound field where the sound is equally likely 10
'come to the microphone from any direction, the waves from all directions are
{equally trong, and the phase of the waves is random at the microphone position.
Such a field may be approximated by constructing a room with highly irregular
walls and placing reflecting objects of various sizes and shapes init. A source of
sound placed in such a room gives rise to a diffuse sound field at any point in the
room. Microphones calibrated under such conditions are of interest because

'Microphones used for engineering measurements are usually piezoelectric.
'capacitor. oF electret types. Figure 6.384" shows construction details of a pi
'erocletrie unit which uses PZT (lead zirconate titanate) as a bending beam
coupled to the center of a conical diaphragm of thin metal foil. An electret type is

in the next section; however. they require no polarizing voltage since their charge
{is permanently "built into" the polymer film which forms the diaphragm. Since
the unsupported polymer film would sag and creep excessively, a backup plate
'with "raised points" is used. Such microphones? are less expensive than the
'capacitor type, can be used under high-humidity conditions (where the capacitor
type may are over) and result in instruments of smaller size and power consump-
tion. A version® which preserves the desirable features ofan all-metal diaphragm
also has been developed.

Pressure Response of a Capacitor Microphone
'OF the several types of microphones in common use, generally the capacitor type
's considered capable ofthe highest performance. Figure 640 shows in simplified
fashion the construction of a typical capacitor microphone, The pressure re
sponse is found by assuming a uniform pressure p, 0 exist all around the micro-
phone at any instant of time, This is actually the case for sufficiently low sound
frequencies, but reflection and diffraction effeets distort this uniform field at
higher frequencies. as pointed out earlier
"The diaphragm is generally a very thin metal membrane which is stretched
by a suitable clamping arrangement. Diaphragm thickness ranges from about
'0.0001 to O42 in, The diapheagin is deflected by the sound pressure and aets as
the moving plate of « capacitance displacement transducer. The other plate of the
capacitor 18 stationary and may contain properly designed damping holes
Motion of the diaphragm causes air Now through these holes with resulting uid
friction and energy dissipation. This damping effect is utilized to control the
resonant peak of the diaphragm response. A diaphragm actually has many natu-
ral frequencies: however, only the lowest i of interest here. For frequencies near
fr below the lowest natural frequency. the diaphragm behaves essentially as a
Simple spring-mass-dushpot second-order system and may be analyzed as such,
'A-capillary air leak is provided to give equalization of steady (atmospheric)
pressure on both sides of the diaphragm to prevent diaphragm bursting. For
ound) pressures the eapillary-volume system results in the varying com:
ponent of pressure acting only on the outside of the diaphragm and thus causing
the desired diaphragm deflection.
The variable capacitor is connected into a simple series cicuit with a high
resistanoe R snd "polarized" with a de voltage F, of about 200 V. This polarizing
voltage acts as cirevit excitation and determines the neutral (zero-pressure)
diaphragm position because of the electrostatic attraction force between the Cae
pacitor pl nt diaphragm deflection, no current flows through R

flow through R and an output voltage exists. The voltage c, usualy is applied to

Next we model the microphone as in Fig. 643. The mass M and spring K,
'must be such as to give the same natural frequency as the lowest natural fre
quency of the diaphragm. The dashpot B must be such as to give the same
fesonant peak as in the microphone's measured pressure response, The capacitor
plate area and air gap (with no external forces acting) must be such as to give the
same capacitance as is measured for the microphone under similar conditions
The spring constant K, and capacitor dimensions also must be such that the
force f, causes a capacitance variation equal (atleast for small motions) to that
'caused in the actual microphone by a pressure difference p,~ p, = fay. Ill the
above conditions are met, the simplified model of Fig. 643 will respond essen-
tially in the same way as the microphone itself While the equivalent system
described is defined in terms of experimental measurements on an existing mi
'crophone, microphone designers have theoretical formulas for estimating these
parameters before new microphone is bul,

{ence an attractive force and will move to a new position xy such that the spring

{4's applied, motion will take place around xy as an operating point. To find xp,

|GNAL MULTIPLEXING SYSTEMS
In certain applications, many channels of pressure data must be repetitively
transduced to voltage signals. For example, when a pneumatic process control
'system is retrofited for electronic computer control, there may be hundreds of 3

-- 552 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 546
522, suasunne vevices
Fire 653 Probe orettion and zfrequeney elation.
to 15 Ib/in? gage air pressure signals to be interfaced to the computer. In wind:
tunnel and fluid machinery testing," hundreds of test points distributed over the
surface of the model or machine may be instrumented for static or stagnation
Pressure sensing. When the needed data rates are sufficiently slow, a costefective
solution timeshares a single transducer/amplifier by multiplexing the pressure
lines using a pneumatic scanning device,
The classic device in this field is the Scanivalve (Fig. 654), a multiported
rotating valve which sequentially connects each of many (up to 64) pressure lines
distributed around its circumference to a single fush-diaphragm transducer. By
Keeping volume below 0001 in', the response time is sufciently fast to allow
scan rates up to 10 or 20 channels per second. Unlimited numbers of channels
'ean be accommodated by using as many Scanivalves as necessary; however, each
'equires its own pressure transducer. One 12-port model is designed with integral
gearing to allow convenient interconnection of multivalve systems (24 valves and

-- 553 of 1000 --

PRESSURE AND SOUND MEASUREMENT $23
ive
Output Signe!
Stotoe
Pot
~ Rotor
Thrvt Beoring [/-From stepper Drive
igre 684 Pressure ins! mulpeer. (Cares Scale Crp, San Diego. Call)
288 channels are not unusual) which are driven by a single electric or pneumatic
Stepping motor. Often, two channels of a valve are used for ealibration purposes
by leaving them connocted to, respectively, zero pressure and a stable reference
pressure, Modest computing capability in the electronics then allows periodic
Correction of transducer defts, When multiple Scanivalves are employed, the
'output of each electrical transducer may be multiplexed electrically into the com-
puter. thus combining pneumatic/mechanical scanning with electrical multi:
plexing and increasing the system scan rate in proportion to the number of valves
used.
'A diferent approach! to multichannel pressure scanning is somewhat more
costly, but achieves a higher scan rate (64 channels per second) and produces an
tlectrical output in digital form. Rather than using single analog pressure trans-
'ducer, as in the Scanivalve, each channel is provided with its own pressure
'comparator a diferental-pressure switch which provides a contact closure when-
fever the pressure differential across its diaphragm crosses zero (+ 0.005 Ijin'.
For each channel, the unknown pressure is applied to one side ofits diaphragm
while the other side is connected to a chamber common to all the switches. In
this chamber a pacumatic system produces a precision pressure ramp which rises
linearly from zero to, say. 20 lin? gage in 0.800 s, discharges to zero, and then
* Model SSP-D 64 MK. Scie Cor, San Digs. Cal

-- 554 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 548
524 weasunina nevices
repeats this eycle once per second. A binary electronic counter is synchronized
with this ramp: thus its contents at any time during the 0.800-s eycle are pro-
Portional to the ramp pressure. When the ramp pressure passes through any
particular unknown pressure of the 64 channels, that particular pressure com-
parator switches, an event whose timing relative to the counter ramp measures
the unknown pressure. Since all 6$ pressures are within the range ofthe ramp, 64
individual digital pressure readings are obtained each cycle and put into a "two-
page" memory, so that one page is being updated while the other can be asynch-
ronously accessed by the computer. Again, 2 of the 64 channels may be utilized
for calibration purposes by applying fixed precision pressures to them,
'When required data rates are very high, systems using a separate analog
transducer for each channel, with electronic multiplexing into a single ana-
log/digital converter, have been available for some time, but at considerable
expense. Recently silicon semiconductor pressure transducers with 32 individual
sensors packaged in a 5-07 box of size I x 2 x 2. in have become available! at
reasonable cost. These transducers are presently available for gases only and do
'ot meet as stringent accuracy specifications as those used in Scanivalve systems.
However, the accuracy problem is overcome in complete measurement systems?
by including a single precision vibrating-eylinder transducer as a reference and
using microprocessor computing power to periodically recalibrate not only the
'transducers but also amplifiers and analog/digital converters against thre precision
pressure values. This three-point calibration correts for transducer nonlinearity
(which otherwise would be a problem in transducers of this type) and thermal
zero and sensitivity shifts. Systems of up to $12 channels with acquisition rates to
14,000 measurements per second and throughput of $00 measurements per second
are available,

CHAPTER
SEVEN
FLOW MEASUREMENT
7.1 LOCAL FLOW VELOCITY, MAGNITUDE
AND DIRECTION
In many experimental studies of fuid flow phenomena, itis necessary to deter-
mine the magnitude and/or direction of the flow-velocity at @ point in the fluid
'and how this varies from point to point. That is, a description of the low field is,
desired, Various methods of flow visualization allow us to gain an overall view of
flow patterns. Sometimes the qualitative information available from direct visual
'observation is sufficient; however, most methods also allow quantitative analysis.
'Once (by flow visualization or other sources of information) localized regions of
particular interest have been pinpointed, it may be necessary to insert velocity
probes to obtain accurate point measurements. Such probes (pitotstatic tubes,
hot-wire anemometers, and laser-doppler velocimeters are the most common)
always involve a sensing volume of finite size, Thus true "point" measurements
fre impossible. However, sensing volumes can be made suificiently small to pro-
vide data of practical utility
Flow Visualization'
'The majority of low visualization schemes are based on one of two basic prin-
ciples: the introduction of tracer particles or the detection of low-related changes
in fluid optical properties. In liquids, colored dyes and gas bubbles are common
"W, Merch, Making Fi Flows Visible, Am Se, vol 67, pp. 380-336, May-June 1979,
WW. Mersich "Flow Visuation." Academe, New York, 197 T.ASanuma (e, "Fow Vinal
aon Hemiopet, New York 1979 Flow Vivian vol. 3, Hemisphere, New York, 1988
=

-- 559 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 552
$28 weasuaino dewcrs
tracers. A line of hydrogen bubbles, for example, can be formed in water by
applying a short electric pulse to a straight wire immersed in the flow. Photo-
raphy with steady illumination shows the bubbles as short streaks whose length
«an be measured to obtain velocity data, while stroboscopic light gives a series of
dots whose spacing gives similar information. For gas flows, smoke, helium-filled
soap" bubbles, or gas molecules made luminous by an ionizing electric spark
hhave served as traces,
Shadowgraph, schlieren, and interferometer techniques! employ, in different
ways, the variation in refractive index of the flowing gas with density. For eom-
Pressible lows (Mach number above about 0.3), density varies with veloct
sufficiently to produce measurable effects. In shadowgraph and schlieren me
thods, light and dark patterns related to flow conditions are produced by the
'bending of light rays as they pass through a region of varying density. The optical
apparatus for these two methods is relatively simple to use, and they are widely
utilized, mostly for qualitative studies. For quantitative results, the more difficult
interferometer approach may be necessary, Here the light/dark patterns are
formed by interference effects resulting from phase shifts between a reference
'beam and the measuring beam. For no flow, a regular grid of light/dark fringes is
present, When flow occurs, this grid is distorted and numerical values of density
'can be calculated from the fringe displacements.
Versions of the holographic techniques? discussed in Chap. 4 have been
developed for flow measurements. Combinations? of several visualization me
'thods are sometimes helpfl in difficult problems,
Velocity Magnitude from Pitot-Statie Tube
In some situations the direction of the velocity vector is known with sufficient
accuracy without taking any measurements. Ifthe direction is not known, it may
be found in several ways discussed later. Let us assume the direction is known, so
'that a pitot-statie tube may be properly aligned with this direction, as in Fig. 7.
'Assuming steady one-dimensional flow of an incompressible frictionless fluid, we
can derive the well-known result

In an actual pitotstatic tube, deviations from the ideal theoretical result of Ea.
(7.1) arise from a number of sources. Ifp is accurately known, the errors can Be
'raced to inaccurate measurement Of Py ANd Pa
The static pressure is usually the more difficult to measure accurately. The
diflerence between true (74) and measured (Puy, «) Values of static pressure may
bbe due to the following:
|. Misalignment of the tube axis and velocity vector, This exposes the static
{aps to some component of velocity.
2. Nonzero tube diameter. Streamlines next to the tube must be longer than
'those in undisturbed flow, which indicates an increase in velocity. This is
accompanied by a decrease in static pressure, which makes the static taps
read low. A similar (and possibly more severe) effect occurs if a tube is
inserted in a duct whose cross-sectional area is not much larger than that of
the tube
3. Influence of stagnation point on the tube-support leading edge. This higher
Pressure causes the static pressure upstream of the leading edge also to be
high. If the static taps are too close to the support, they will read high
because of this effect. Note that this error and that of item 2 above tend to
cancel. By proper design, effective cancellation may be achieved! (see also
Prandt! pitot tube, Fig. 2.176) Figure 7.2 shows the nature of both these
errors as revealed by experimental tests?

'An important application of the pitot-statc tube is found in aircraft and
rissles' Here the stagnation- and static-pressure readings of a tube fastened to a
vehicle are used to determine the airspeed and Mach number while the static
reading alone is utilized to measure altitude. If altitude is to be measured with an
error of 100 ft, the static pressure must be accurate to 0.5 percent.? To achieve
this accuracy, methods for compensating errors ofthe type mentioned in items 1,
2, and 3 of the above have been developed and reported.' An interesting and
'Useful result of these studies isa simple method for reducing error from angular
'misalignment. It was found that by locating the static-pressure taps as in Fig 7.3,

Single vane to rotate about its longitudinal axis and automatically locate the taps
37.5" from the eross-low stagnation point." Itis also possible to design multiple:

'These probes act in a fashion similar to a weather vane (except that they have
angular freedom about two axes) and thus align themselves with the velocity
vector. A conventional ring of evenly spaced static taps then gives accurate
feadings. By measuring the rotations of the gimbals, such probes also provide
information on the direction ofthe velocity.

Velocity Direction from Yaw Tube, Pivoted Vane, and Servoed Sphere
In addition to laboratory studies of low processes in uid machinery, ducting,
cle. flow-velocity direction information is of interest in flight vehicles! where
"HL Koch, "Handbook of Astonatcl Engineing" p. 13.31, McGinw-Hil New York,

angle-ofattack measurements are utilized in attitude measurement and control,
stability augmentation, and gust alleviation systems,
So-called yaw tubes! of one form or another conventionally are employed to
determine the direction of local flow velocity. Perhaps the simplest form, useful
for finding the angular inclination in one plane only, is shown in Fig. 78a, Taps 1
land 3 are connected to a differential-pressure instrument that reads zero when
the tube is aligned with the flow. A central tap 2 is often included to read the
stagnation pressure after alignment is attained (valid only ifthe angle of attack is
zero). The claw tube of Fig. 78 operates on similar principles, but may be
utilized in regions where the flow direction changes greatly, since its sensing holes
'may be located very close together. The two-axis probes of Fig. 78¢ and d
conceivably could be designed to allow rotation about each axis; however, the
'complexity and size of such a design are generally prohibitive. Thus probe oper-
ation consists of rotation about the probe axis to balance taps | and 3. Then
pressures 2 and 4 are each measured, and calibration charts give the angle of
attack. Tap $ does not read stagnation pressure directly; this can be obtained
from calitration charts. Any of these probes may be made automaticaly self
aligning by using the pressure difference p, - py asthe error signal in a servosy-
stem which rotates the probe until a null is achieved. The details of a system of
this type are shown in Fig. 7.10.

Clearly, if Cy, A, and p are known, then V may be found by measuring F,. If al
directional components of V are to be equally effective in producing drag force, a
body with spherical symmetry must be employed. If this is done, measurement of
the x, y.and z components of F completely defines the magnitude and direction
of V. Since the drag coefficient ofa smooth sphere is somewhat dependent on the
Reynolds number (and thus V), wire roughening rings are attached to the sphere
surface to ensure turbulence. The drag coefficient of the roughened sphere is

of the total drag force into three rectangular components is accomplished by
'mounting the sphere on a force-resolving flexure assembly. Figure 7.12b shows
the x-axis mechanism of this structure. (The y and = axes are identical except for
their orientation) The force components are applied to flexure-plate springs 10
'produce proportional motions which are then measured by suitable displacement
transducers, Sensors ofthis type have been used for both wind and ocean current
measurement.

'A miniaturized drag probe! for one-dimensional air velocity measurements,
using flat plate target 1.5 x 1.5 mm mounted on a commercially available
semiconductor strain-gage beam, exhibited frequency response to 10 kHz. A
'two-dimensional version? utilized a hollow spherical glass target of 0.153-in,
diameter mounted on the stylus ofa stereo phonograph pickup. Response to 26
kHz was estimated theoretically for 700 fs ir flow.
Hot-Wire and Hot-Film Anemometer
Hot-wire anemometers commonly are made in two basic forms: the constant-
'current type and the constant-temperature type. Both utilize the same physical
principle but in different ways. In the constant-current type, a fine resistance wire
carrying a fixed current is exposed to the flow velocity. The wire attains an
equilibrium temperature when the ?R heat generated in itis just balanced by the
convective heat loss from its surface. The circuit is designed so that the R heat
is essentially constant; thus the wire temperature must adjust itself to change the
convective loss until equilibrium is reached. Since the convection film coeficient
is a function of flow velocity, the equilibrium wire temperature is a measure of
velocity. The wire temperature can be measured in terms of its electrical resis-
tance. Inthe constant-temperature form, the current through the wit is adjusted
to keep the wire temperature (as measured by its resistance) constant, The current
required to do this then becomes a measure of flow velocity.
For equilibrium conditions we can write an energy balance for a hot wice as

In calibration, Vis set at some known value ¥;. Then R, is adjusted to set
hot-wire current [at a value low enough to prevent wire burnout but high enough
to give adequate sensitivity to velocity. The resistance R, will come to a definite
temperature and resistance. Then the resistor R, is adjusted to balance the bridge
'This adjustment is essentially a measurement of wire temperature, which is held
fixed at all velocities. The frst point on the calibration curve is thus plotted as
J, VV. Now Vis changed to a new value, causing wire temperature and R,, to
change and thus unbalancing the bridge. Then R,, and thus wire temperatue,
is restored to its original value by adjusting / (by means of R,) until the bridge
balance is restored (R, is not changed). The new current J and the corresponding
V may be plotted on the calibration curve, and this procedure is repeated for as
'many velocities as desired.
'Once calibrated, the probe can be employed to measure unknown velocities
by adjusting R, until bridge balance is achieved, reading 1, and obtaining the
corresponding ¥ from the calibration curve. This assumes that the measured uid
is at the same temperature and pressure as for the calibration. Correction me-
thods for varying temperature and pressure are fairly simple, but are not di
'cussed here. For the above constant-temperature mode of operation, Eqs. (7.12)

'We see that the voltage follows the flow velocity with a firsorder lag. The time
'constant « cannot be reduced much below 0001 s in actual practice, which would
limit the lat frequency response to less than 160 Hz. This is quite inadequate for
turbulence studies since frequencies of $0,000 Hz and more are of interest. This
limitation is overcome by use of electrical dynamic compensation (see Chap. 10
for a general discussion). Circuits whose frequency response just makes up the
deficiency in the hot wire itself ate employed, as in Fig. 7.15. The overall system
then has a flat response to almost 100,000 He. The main difficulty in applying this
technique is that the correct compensation depends on t, whose value is not
known and varies with flow conditions. The next paragraph explains a method of
'overcoming this difficulty.
The basic idea of the scheme is to force a square-wave current through the
hhot wire while it is exposed to the flow to be studied (se Fig. 7.16). We show that
'the outputvoltage response to this current signal has exactly the same time
constant asthe response to the flow-relocty signal. Thus ifthe compensation can
be adjusted to be correct for the current signal, it will be correct forthe velocity
input also, The "correctness" of the adjustment may be judged by the degree 10
Which the output voltage corresponds to a square wave. In the cicuit of Fig. 7.16,
4 good approximation to linear behavior may be expected for small input signals

'current and velocity inputs may be considered separately, If the square-wave
current is turned of, R, adjusted to give the desired hot-wire current Ia, and Ry

We sce now that the response of the voltage e to impressed current signals has
the identical time constant ¢ as the response to flow-velocity signals. Thus the

'The operation of the constant-temperature type of instrument for steady
velocities is explained earlicr in relation to Fig, 7.13. This mode of operation can
bbe extended to measure both average and fluctuation components of velocity by
'making the bridge-balancing operation automatic, rather than manual, through
the agency of a feedback arrangement. The advantages of this scheme are such
'that most instruments now employ it. A simplified functional schematic of such &
system is shown in Fig. 717, With zero flow velocity and the bridge excitation
shut off (i, = 0} the hot wire assumes the fuid temperature. Then the variable
resistor Ry is adjusted manually so that R, > R., thereby unbalancing the
bridge. When the excitation current is turned on, the unbalanced bridge produces
dg lnc oye

-- 581 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 574
fan unbalance voltage ¢,, which is applied to the input of a high-gain current
amplifier supplying the bridge excitation current, The curtent now flowing
through R, increases its temperature and thus its resistance, AS Ry, increases, it
approaches R, and the bridge-unbalance voltage e, decreases. If the current
amplifier had an infinite gain, the bridge current required to heat R,, to match Ry
precisely could be produced with an infinitesimally small error voltage e,, and
thus perfect bridge balance would be attained automatically. The current (which
is a measure of flow velocity V) produces an output voltage in passing through
the readout resistor R,,. Since an actual amplifier has finite gain, the bridge-
unbalance voltage cannot go to zero; but it can be extremely small. This also
'means that R, will be very close, but not exactly equal, to Rs
The above-described self-adjusting equilibrium will come about at any
steady-flow velocity, not just zero, with the equilibrium current in each case being
'a measure of velocity. Ifthe velocity is fluctuating rapidly, perfect continuous
bridge balance requires an infinite-gain amplifier. Lacking this, practical systems
exhibit some lag between velocity and current. System dynamic response for
'small velocity fluctuations can be obtained by superimposing previously obtained
results for response to velocity (wire-current constant) and response to curtent
(ow-velocty constant, since in the constant-temperature mode both current and
velocity are changing simultaneously. The effect of velocity on r, (the varying
'component of Ris obtained from Eq, (7.24) as

Note that r, the time constant of the constant-temperature anemometer system,
is always less than ¢ (the time constant ofthe wire itsel) and in actual practice is
'much Wess, since a very high value of amplifier gain K, is used. A more compre
hensive analysis of the system, which models the amplifier as a second-order
'dynamic system and adds a capacitor (used to "tune" system for optimum re
sponse) in parallel with R, leads to a third-order closed-loop differential equation.
This allows the usual Routh-crterion analysis for stability and allowable loop
ain, As in all feedback systems, too high a loop gain will cause instability:
hhowever, careful design allows sufficiently high gain to make ry of the order of
'sof + oF less, A typical instrument has fat (within 3 dB) frequency response 10
17.000 Hz when the average flow velocity is 30 ft/s, 30,000 Hz for 100 f/s, and
0,000 Hat 300 0s,
"The stated preference forthe feedback-type constant-temperature instrument
is based on a number of considerations. In the eonstant-current type, the current
'must be set high enough to heat the wire considerably above the fluid temper-
ature for a given average velocity. Ifthe flow suddenly drops to a much lower
velocity or comes to res, the hot wire will burn out since the convection loss
cannot match the heat generation before the wire temperature reaches the melt-
ing point. The constant-lemperature type does not have this drawback because
the feedback system automaricaly sets wire current to maintain the desired (safe)
wire temperature for every velocity
'A further advantage of the constant-emperature method lies in the nature of,
the dynamic compensation. In the constant-current method, the compensating
network must be reset (using the square-wave current) whenever the average

velocity changes appreciably. Furthermore, if velocity fluctuations about the
'average are large (more than, say, $ percent of the average velocity), the dynamic
'compensation will not be complete since the value of + varies with V and thus the

{temperature system provides more nearly correct compensation for large velocity
fluctuations. While large velocity changes result in nonlinear response in both
types of instrument, the nonlinearity ofthe constant-temperature feedback system
appears algebraically on the output signal, where itis easily linarized with an
'external electronic linearizer. Usually these linearizes utilize a polynomial func-

adjustable to suit the probe and flow conditions. These units are fairly simple 10
'adjust and are in wide use, but no similar apparatus has been devised for the
constant-current type. Figure 7.18" shows system output voltage, both nonlinear

Practical problems in the application of hot-wire anemometers ate found in
the limited strength of the fine wires and the calibration changes caused by ditt
accumulations. Unless the flow is very clean significant calibration changes can
'occur in a relatively few minutes of operation. Larger dirt particles striking wires
'may actually break them. At high speeds, wires may vibrate because of acro-
dynamic loads and flutter effects. Applications are mainly in gas flows: however,
special probes have been developed and successful results obtained in both elec.
really conducting and nonconducting liquids, such as water and oil. In com.
Pressible flows, the film coefficient his really related to pV thus calibrations such
as that of Fig, 7.13 must be adjusted when a probe calibrated at one density is
employed at another. Further complications arise when uid temperature varies,
either as a steady change from calibration conditions or dynamically during
'measurement; however, various compensation techniques? are available.
A Nariation of the hot-wire anemometer intended to extend its utility is the
hot-film transducer. Here the resistance element is a thin film of platinum de-
Posited on a glass base. The film takes the place of the hot wire; the requited
Circuitry is basically similar to that used in constant-temperature hot-wire ap=
'roach. The film transducers have great mechanical strength and may be used st
very high temperatures by constructing them with internal cooling-water pass
ages, Various configurations of sensors are possible; Fig. 720 shows two. pos-
sibilities,
In addition to measurement of velocity magnitude and direction, hot-wire
'and hot-film instruments may be adapted to measurements of fluid temperatures,
turbulent shear stresses, and concentrations of individual gases in mixtures." By
Using a probe with two wires or films in an X configuration and two channels of
electronics, velocity fluctuations in two perpendicular directions and the direc
tional correlation (Reynolds stress) can be measured. For three-dimensional flows
where flow direction is completely unknown, a system* employing six film sen-

Hot-Film Shock-Tube Velocity Sensors
fiments, the propagation velocity of the shock wave down the
tube offen must be measured. Of the various means available for making this
'measurement, hot-film temperature sensors are in wide use. The passage of the
'Shock wave past a particular section of the tbe is accompanied by a step change
in gas temperature. By locating thin resistance films Mush with the inside of the
tube, the instant of wavefront passage may be detected as a temperature (and
therefore resistance) change, If two such film sensors are mounted a known
distance apart, the average wave velocity may be computed from the time inter-
val between the two sensor responses. The films are operated at constant current
by the simple circuit of Fig 721, and a differentiating circuit is employed to
sharpen the pulses for greater timing accuracy. With systems of this type.' shock=
'wave velocities have been measured with an accuracy of I percent for shock
'Mach numbers as high as 75 t0 10

Laser Doppler Velocimeter*
{In the area of flow measurement, the most important recent advance is probably
the laser doppler velocimeter. This instrument measures local flow velocity and is
thus in competition with pitot tubes and hot-wire anemometers. Its main advan.
tages relative to these more well-established techniques include:
1, Measurement of velocity is direct rather than by inference from pressure
(pitot tube) or heat-transer coefficient (hot-wire).
2. No "physical object" need be inserted into the flow; thus flow is undisturbed
bby measurement
3. Sensing volume can be very small (a cube 0.2 mm on a side is not unusual).
4, Very-high-frequency response (to megahertz range) is possible.
Disadvantages involve the necd for transparent flow channels, the necessity for
'racer particles in the fluid, and the cost and complenity of the apparatus. In
brie, the operating principle involves focusing laser beams at the point where
"1. Dra, "The Laser Doppler Technique." Wiy-ntrience, New York, 198, TS. Dur
'ani and C. A Grated, "Laser Systems in Flow Mentoremen" Plo, New York. 1976: HD.
'Thompion and W:H. Stevenson, "Laser Velocietry and Partie Sng" Hemisphere New York,
1979: Durst A Meling and JH. Whitlam, "Princes and Pracce of Laser Anemowsety =
Academic New York. 176

oe
Fiore 722 Layout of hse doppler veocneter
velocity is to be measured and then sensing with a photodetector the light sca
tered by tiny particles carried along with the fluid as it passes through the laser
focal point. The velocity of the particles (assumed to be identical to the uid
velocity) causes a doppler shift ofthe scattered lights frequency and produces a
photodetector signal directly related to velocity.
'Actually, artical tracer particles are not always necessary; the microscopic
particles normally present in liquids may sufie; however, gas lows often need to
be seeded. Under extreme conditions, particles may not perfectly follow the flow,
'but studies have shown highly accurate following in many practical cases. A
simple analysis' accurate for many gas flows, which models the particle as a
spherical body of radius r and density p immersed in a fluid of viscosity 1, uses
'Stokes' law forthe viscous foree to obtain the differential equation
marty - ) 43)
'This gives a first-order dynamic response with time constant + @ 2pr?/(9u) rela-
'ing particle velocity v, to fluid velocity ey. For water droplets of (typical) 1-yam
diameter in air, we get + ~ 3 4, which allows fat (within 1 percent) frequency
response to about 7.400 Hz. More correct (and complex) models for particle
'motion, needed when Eq, (74) becomes inaccurate, ae available *
Laser doppler velocimeters (LDVs) have been operated in several different
'configurations; Figs, 7.22 to 7.24a deg give some details on the popular dual-beam
(differential Dopplet) or "fringe" mode. While a careful physical explanation of
the device rests on a detailed study of the doppler-shif effect, many workers in

flats (2a, 24) cause laser beam 1 to be split into two equal-intensity, parallel
'beams, 3 and 4. Lens $ causes these beams to cross and focus at common point F.
Light 6, scattered from particles moving through the fringe pattern is selectively
collected by the lens/pinhole aperture combination 7 and 8 and then detected by
pPhotosensor 9. Light intensity versus time is displayed on scope 10 and simul-
taneously processed by doppler burst signal processor 11 to derive flow-velocity
data, The frequency f of the electric signals (shown in Fig. 7.24a) produced by a
particle moving across the dark and light fringe pattern with a velocity compo-
rent V normal tothe fringes is given by

72 GROSS VOLUME FLOW RATE
The total flow rate through a duct or pipe often must be measured or controlled,
Many instruments (lowmeters) have been developed for this purpose. They may
be categorized in various ways; a useful overall classification divides devioes into
those which measure volume flow rate [(R? or m?)time] and those which mea-
sure mass flow rate [Ibm or kg)time], The total flow occurring during a given
time interval also may be of interest. This measurement requires integration with
respect {© time of the instantaneous flow rate. The integrating function may be
Performed in various ways, Sometimes being an integral part of the flowmetering.
'oneept and other times being performed by a general-purpose integrator more
fr less remote from the flowmeter.

Calibration and Standards
Flow.rate calibration depends on standards of volume (length) and time oF mass
And time: Primary calraton, in general s based onthe establishment of steady
flow through the flowmeter to be calibrated and subsequent measurement ofthe
volume or mass of flowing fuid that passes through in an accurately timed
Interval If steady ow exists, the volume or mass flow rate may be inferred fom
Such a procedure. Any stable and precise flowmeter calibrated by such primary
tmethods then itself becomes a secondary flow-rate standard against which other
{iss accurate) flowmeters may be calibrated conveniently. As in any other cai
tration significant deviations of the conditions of use fom those at calibration
invalidate the calibration. Possible sources of error in flowmeters include
'Variations in uid properties (density viscosity, and temperature. orientation of
the meter, pressure evel, and particularly low disturbances (such as elbows, tes
valves, et) upstream (and toa lesser extent dovenseam) ofthe meter. A com
terial calibrator for presise primary calibration of fowmeters using tiuids is
'Shown in Fig, 7252 These units use a convenient dynamic weighing? scheme, are
available in models to cover the range 0.5 to 150,000 Ibm/h, and have an overall
accuracy of + 0.1 percent
"Another type of calibrator, called a ballistic flow rover, is particulary useful
for fastresponse, high-resolution flowmeters such as turbine, positive:
displacement, vortex-shedding, ete, types. where steady state can be achieved
Guickly and the integration of the flow rate to gt total flow is accomplished
Sevurately by accumulating the meter pulse-ate output in @ counter. (The inte=
tration gives accurate foal ow even i the ow rate i not perfectly steady) One
Such calibrator' uses a Teflon-sealed airdriven free piston traveling down a
Precision-honed tube 10 dispense a presse volume of calibration fluid through
the attached flowmeter to be calibrated. Precise time and displacement measure-
iments on the moving piston are utilized in a microprocessor data-reduction
System {0 achieve a claimed flow-rate accuracy of + 0.02 percent. The smal low
Yolume (about 5 gal for a 700 gal/min full-scale unit) involved in checking a
Single low rate allows rapid calibration. 20 repeats of single point typically
being achieved in 8 minutes for 8300 galmin flow rate. Units ae available with
'maximum flow rate of 100 to 6000 gal min, each unit being usable over about a
11000:1 range. A simular type of calibrator (except that the piston is driven by
lecric motor is available for gas flows (see Fig. 7.266)" Detailed uncertainty
malyes are available" for these calibrators

How MEASUREMENT $68
'The calibration of lowmeters to be used with gases often can be carried out
with liquids as long as the pertinent similarity relations (Reynolds number) are
'maintained and theoretical density and expansion corrections are applied. If this
procedure is felt to be of insufficient accuracy, a direct calibration with the actual
'gas to be employed can be carried out by means of the gasometer system of Fig.
7 26a. Here the gas flowing through the flowmeter during a timed interval is
trapped in the gasometer bell, and its volume is measured. Temperature and
pressure measurements allow calculation of mass and conversion of volume to
"any desired standard conditions. By filling the bell with gas, raising it tothe top,
and adding appropriate weights, such a system may be used as a gas supply to
'drive gas through a flowmeter as the bell gradually drops at a measured rate. By
'using a precision analytical balance to measure the mass accumulated in a sto-
rage vessel overtime, accuracies of + 0.02 percent were obtained! for flow rates
Lup to 9 kg/s (20 Ibm/s) A volumetric method (corrected for pressure and temper-
ature) is also explained inthis reference. The sizeof the equipment makes it more
'economical for large flow rates; however, the accuracy is less (+0.08 percent).
'When the above primary calibration methods cannot be justified, compari-
son with a secondary standard flowmeter connected in series with the meter to be
calibrated may be sufficiently accurate. Turbine flowmeters and theit associated
digital counting equipment have been found particularly suitable for such second-
fry standards. With attention to detail, such standards can closely approach the
'accuracy of the primary methods themselves. The Navy Primary Standards Lab-
oratory at Pensacola, Florida, has such a system? with an inaccuracy ofthe order
(of 02 percent. For gas flow, a flow nozzle discharging air to the atmosphere can
be very accurately calibrated? for mass flow rate by means of pitot-tube traverses
at the discharge. Then this nozzle can be connected in series with any flowmeter
to be calibrated and used as an accurate standard. This can be done with very
small eror up to Mach number about 0.9.
Figure 7.27" summarizes the flow-rate calibration capabilities of the National
Bureaus of Standards (now NIST)
Constant-Area, Variable-Pressure-Drop Meters ("Obstruction"" Meters)
Perhaps the most widely used flowmetering principle involves placing a fixed
area flow restriction of some type in the pipe or duct carrying the fluid. This low
restriction causes a pressure drop which varies with the flow rate; thus measure-
iment of the pressure drop by means of a suitable differential-pressure pickup
allows flow-rate measurement. In this section we briefly discuss the most

'The sharp-edge orifice is undoubtedly the most widely employed flowmeter-
ing element, mainly because ofits simplicity, its low cost, and the great volume of
research data available for predicting its behavior. A typical lowmetering setup is
shown in Fig. 7.28. If one-dimensional low of an incompressible frictionless fluid

deviates from the assumptions of the theoretical model sufficiently to require
experimental correction factors if acceptable fowmetering accuracy is to be
tained. For example, Ay, and Az, are areas of the actual flow cross section,
which are nor, in general, the same as those corresponding to the pipe and orifice
diameters, which are the ones susceptible to practical measurement. Furthermore,
Ayy and Azy may change with flow rate because of flow geometry changes. Also,
there are frictional losses that affect the measured pressure drop and lead to a
Permanent pressure loss. To take these factors into account, an experimental
calibration to determine the actual flow rate Q, by methods such as that of Fig.
725 is necessary. A discharge coefficient C, may be defined by

flow nozzle is practically identical with that ofan orifice, because, to get the same
4p. the flow nozzle must have a smaller f ratio, and losses increase with decreas-
ing f ratio, The venturi tube also requires a smaller f fora given Ap, but because
of its streamlined form, its losses are low and nearly independent of f. The
Permanent pressure los is of the order of 10 to 15 percent of the measured Ap
'over the range 0.2 < fi<038; thus a venturi gives a definite improvement in
power losses over an orifice and often is indicated for measuring very large flow
Fates, where power losses, though a small percentage, become economically sig-
nificant in absolute value. The initial higher cost of a venturi over an orifice thus
ray be offset by reduced operating costs. The Dall ow tube has the unexpected
(though desirable) features of a high measured Ap (similar to an orifice) and a low
Permanent pressure loss (similar to, and sometimes better than, a venturi). These
apparently inconsistent virtues have been checked experimentally, but are not
fully explained theoretically." The permanent pressure loss of a Dall tube is of the
'order of $0 percent or less of that of a venturi tube with the same Ap. Other
factors to consider in choosing among the orifce, flow nozzle, venturi, and Dall
tube include freedom from pressure-tap clogging resulting from suspended solids
(enturi is best), loss of accuracy due to wear (venturi, ow nozale, and Dall tube

'A single capillry tube is capable of handling only stall flow rates at laminar

tubes. This particular instrument is designed 0 give a Reynolds number of 20 or

of low-pressure air, and standard models are available in ranges from 0.1 10
2.000 ft""min at pressure drops of 4 to 8 in of water. All the above laminar
clements have the advantages accruing from a linear (rather than square-root)
relation between flow rate and pressure drop; these are principally a large accu
rate range of as much as 100:1 (compared with 3: 1 or 4:1 for square-root
devices, accurate measurement of average flow rates in pulsating flow, and ease
of integrating Ap signals to compute total low. The laminar elements also can
measure reversed flows with no difficulty. They are usually less sensitive to up-
um and downstream flow disturbances than the other devices discussed. Their

sensors by the constant-temperature principle of Fig. 7.7. A commerical version?
for gas mass flow uses specially designed, rugged probes which include both the
flow sensor and a temperature-compensating resistance thermometer in the Ry
location. The flow sensor thus provides a temperature-compensated mass velocity
(pV) signal, which is linearized before being summed with those from the other
sensors in a mass flowmetering array. Good accuracy is obtained by basing the
individual probe locations on a preliminary study of the duct-velocity profile with
'a traversing probe. Typically a duct 2 x 2 ft uses four probes while a6 x 8 ft duct
'might require eight,
