Constant-Pressure-Drop, Variable-Area Meters (Rotameters)!
[A rotameter consists of a vertical tube with tapered bore in which a "Boat"
assumes a vertical postion corresponding to cach flow rate through the tube (see
Fig. 34). For a given flow rate, the float remains stationary since the vertical
{otees of differential pressure, gravity, viscosity, and buoyancy are balanced. This
balance is selfmaintaining since the meter flow area (annular arca between the
float and tube) varies continuously with vertical displacement; thus the device
'may be thought of as an orifice of adjustable area. The downward force (gravity
'minus buoyancy) is constant, and so the upward force (mainly the pressure drop
times the float cross-section area) must be constant also. Since the float area is
constant, the pressure drop must be constant. For a fixed flow area, Ap varies
with the square of flow rate, and so to keep Ap constant for differing Row rates,
the area must vary. The tapered tube provides this variable area. The float
postion is the output of the meter and can be made essentially linear with flow
rate by making the tube area vary linearly with ghe vertical distance. Rotameters
thus have an accurate range of about 10: 1, considerably better than square-
root-type elements. Accuracy is typically +2 percent full scale (1 percent if
calibrated) with repeatability about 0.25 percent of reading. Assuming incom
pressible low and the above described simplified model, we can derive the result
=a fia 76)

'Turbine Meters
If turbine wheel is placed in a pipe containing a lowing fluid, its rotary speed
depends on the flow rate of the fluid. By reducing bearing friction and keeping
'other losses 10 @ minimum, one can design a turbine whose speed varies linearly
'with flow rate; thus a speed measurement allows a flow-rate measurement. The
speed can be measured simply and with great accuracy by counting the rate at
'which turbine blades pass a given point, using a magnetic proximity pickup to
produce voltage pulses, By feeding these pulses to an electronic pulse-rate meter,
'one can measure low rate; by accumulating the total number of pulses during a
timed interval, the total flow is obtained. These measurements can be made very
accurately because of their digital nature. If an analog voltage signal is desired,

[At low flow rates, linearity is degraded by both viscous effects and magnetic
pickup drag. The later can be reduced by using « modulated carrier type of

-- 611 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 604
Pickoff. These are recommended! for all gas applications in meters of size | in or
less and for any liquid applications where extended linear ranges are necessary.
'This technique makes the graph of pulse rate versus flow rate more nearly a
straight line; however, this line does not go through the origin since the turbine
will "stall" before low goes to zero. By electronically adding" a bias fequency 10
the pickoff output, this defect can be corrected, which allows proper flow totaliza
tion over a wide range. While Fig. 7.36 shows linearity improving at high flows,
this end of the range can be extended only at the expense of excessive pressure
drop and reduced bearing life: but short-term overranging of 100 percent is
'usually possible without damage.*
'Commercial turbine meters are available with full-scale flow rates ranging
from about 0.01 to 30.000 gal/min for liquids and 0.01 t0 15,000 f/min for ar.
Nonlinearity within the design range (usually about 10: 1) ean be as good as
(005 percent in the larger sizes. The output voltage of the magnetic pickups is of
the order of 10 mV rms at the low end of the fow range and 100 mV at the high.
Pressure drop across the meter varies with the square of flow rate and is about 3
to 10 Ibyin® at full low. Turbine meters behave essentially as first-order dynamic
systems for small changes about an operating point However, the time constant
typically 2 to 10 ms at maximum flow) is inversely proportional to the operating:
point flow rate. Ifa frequency-to-voltage converter is used to get an analog
voltage output, however, its response speed may not be negligible since the
'operating frequencies of turbine meters are ofthe order of 100 to 2,000 Hz. That
is, the frequeney-to-voltage converter requires low-pass filtering, which rejects
frequencies somewhat below the turbine operating frequency and is thus limited
in transient response also. For example, ifthe turbine is putting out 500 Hz, the
low-pass filter will have to cut off at least at SOO Hz and probably considerably
lower. Ifa first-order filter is designed to attenuate 20 dB at $00 Ha, it will have @
time constant of 0.0032, thus adding this much tothe lag ofthe overall system,
Positive-Displacement Meters
"These meters are actually positive-displacement Muid motors in which friction
and inertia have been reduced to a minimum. The flow of a uid through volume
'chambers of definite size causes rotation of an output shaft, Figure 7.37 shows a
self-porting four-piston design with fluid entering at P3 and leaving at El, caus-
ing a clockwise shaft rotation which sequentially brings pairs (P4-E2, PI-E3,
P2-£4, P3-E1) of inlet/outlet ports into operation through the three-way valve

cai)
action of each piston. Such meters exhibit litle sensitivity to viscosity (accuracy
actually improves with increased viscosity, because of decreased leakage) and can
give high accuracy over wide flow ranges (up to 1,000: 1, see Fig. 7.38). Since
the meters have a maximum allowable pressure drop [typically 20 to 40 tbyin®
differential (140 to 290 kPa)}, high viscosity reduces the maximum allowable low
rate for a given meter (Fig. 738b). As a result of precision clearances in the
instrument, fuid filtration at the 10-ym level is recommended. Meter construc
tion materials also limit the fluids that can be metered, To climinate friction-
producing fluid seals, the meter shaft is coupled to the speed sensor magnetically
through a nonmagnetic wall. This "soft" coupling can introduce errors for un-
steady flows. Since the crankshaft kinematics produces an epicycloidal speed
variation for a steady flow rate, special analog tachometers that compensate for
this fluctuation are available.
Digital (pulse-rate) output is also available that uses photo-optic techniques
'or rotary differential-iransformer methods. The latter method does not use the soft
magnetic coupling and thus follows unsteady crankshaft motions faithfully. Both
methods provide direction sensing, so that reverse flows actually subtract pulses,
to give true net flow in totalizing systems using up-down counters. For larger
flow rates and for food/beverage applications requiring a cleanable, "pocketless"
design, a meter based on helical rotors (Fig. 739) is available with performance
similar to that of the four-piston design. An advantage shared by positive-
displacement meters in general is their insensitivity to distorted inlet/outlet flow
profiles; thus flow straighteners and/or long runs of straight pipe upstream /down-
stream of the meter usually are not required. Dynamic response specifications are
'ot usually quoted; however, the basic principle implies fast response since the
'output rotation is (ideally) "kinematically coupled" tothe ud low rate. Deviations
from ideal behavior which lead to response lags include fluid compressibility and

'An interesting variation' on the positve-displacement concept eliminates
both the leakage present in all such meters and the loading effect of the meter on
'the measured flow process. Both effects are chargeable to the meter pressure
<rop, and would disappear if it were zero. This is accomplished by using an active
feedback scheme in which the meter shaft actually is driven by an electric servo:
"Laboratory Equipment Corp. Moores, Ind. model PLY,

Lowe MEASUREMENT SEB
motor whose drive voltage is obtained from the output of a differential-pressure
ransducee connected across the meter. Whenever this differential pressure is not
oto, the motor either speeds up or slows down until the pressure is again zero:
thus the meter speed "tracks" the impressed flow rate without extracting any
energy from the flow.
Metering Pumps
A variable-dsplacement positivesisplacement pump. if properly designed, can
Serve both to cause a flow rate and to measure it simultaneously. The prineiple
again is merely that a positive-displacement machine, except for leakage and
compressibility, delivers a definite flow rate of fluid at gi
pumps of this kind, the operating speed is fied and the Nlow rate is varied by
Changing pump displacement, usually with some form of mechanical linkage
Since these pumps are used often in automatic control systems, many are de-
input signals which adjust the pump
isplacement ina linear fashion, The flow rate of such a system can be set with
fan accuracy of the order of I percent.

While ac and switched de systems predominate, "pure" de types have been
employed in metering liquid metals, such as mercury. Here, no polarization prob.
lem exists. Also, an insulating pipe liner is not needed, since the conductivity of
the liquid metal is very good relative to that of an ordinary metal (stainless-steel)
Pipe. This means that a metal pipe isnot very effective as a "short circuit" for the
Voltage induced in a liquid-metal flow. Also, no special electrodes are necessary.
the output voltage being tapped off the metal pipe itself at the points of maxi
'mum potential difference. Small, inexpensive pure de meters using a permanent
'magnet field and special sintered siver-silver chloride electrodes. which greally
cease the classical electrode instability problems of de meters. recently have
Appeared.' Originally developed for blood flow, they are usable with many
Tiguids with conductivity greater than a few microsiemens per centimeter, Be-
«ause of the permanent-magnet field and de operation, the electronics can be very
simple, giving a fest-esponse instrument of modest stability and accuracy useful
in various research applications,
'Some general features of electromagnetic flowmeters include the lack of any
ow obstruction; ability to measure reverse flows; insensitivity to viscosity, den-
sity, and flow disturbances as long as the velocity profile is symmetrical; wide
linear range: and rapid response to flow changes (instantaneous for a "pure" de
system; limited by the field frequency in an ac or switched de system).

Ultrasonic Flowmeters
Small-magnitude pressure disturbances are propagated through a fluid at a
definite velocity (the speed of sound) relative to the fluid. IF the fluid also has a
velocity, then the absolute velocity of pressure-disturbance propagation is the
algebraic sum of the two, Since flow rate is related to fluid velocity. this effect
may be used in several ways as the operating principle of an "ultrasonic?
flowmeter." The term ultrasonic refers to the fact that, in practice, the pressure
disturbances usually ate short bursts of sine waves whose frequency is above the
range audible to human hearing, about 20,000 Hz. A typical frequency might be
To Miz,
The various methods of implementing the above phenomenon all depend on
the existence of transmitters and recsivers of acoustic energy. A common ap-
proach is to utilize piezoelectric erystal transducers for both functions. In a
transmitter, eletrical energy in the form of a short burst of high-frequency
voltage is applied to a crystal, causing it to vibrate. If the crystal is in contact
with the fluid, the vibration will be communicated to the Muid and propagated
through it. The receiver crystal is exposed to these pressure fluctuations and
responds by vibrating. The vibratory motion produces an electrical signal in
"4.?.Lynmworth Utsoni: Flower chap. 5, "Phycal Acoust" W:P. Mason and R.N.
'Thurston (is Academie, NY 19.

-- 621 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 614
590 wwasunine pevices
proportion, according to the usual action of piezoelectric displacement trans-
ducers. The need, in most instruments, for a fairly narrow, well-defined acoustic
'beam explains the use of high (10 MHz) frequencies. Fora crystal to be an efficient
transmitter of acoustic energy, its diameter D must be large compared with the
wavelength 2 ofthe oscillation. The conical beam projected from a circular crystal
has a halfeangle «given by sin « = 1.24/D: thus the desired small angles also require
1 small £/D ratio. Since compactness requires reasonably small (= | cm) values of
D, we need to use wavelengths on the order of I mm. Water, for example, has
A= 15 x 10%/f mmf 4 frequency, Hz), so the need for megahertz frequencies is
apparent.
Figure 7.434 shows the most direct application ofthese principles. With zero
flow velocity the transit time fo of pulses from the transmitter to the receiver is

ch is independent of ¢ and thus not subject to errors due to changes in c, This
Technique is practical and forms the basis of many commercially available ut

Sommon: the "sing-around" and the up-down counter. In the sing-around
'method, the two signals of different frequency are multiplied, giving an output
'with sum and difference frequencies. Filtering then extracts the difference fre
'quency. This approach gives fast response to flow changes, but requires pulses
with well-defined leading edges,' which are not always available, particularly in
the clamp-on type of meter, discussed below. The up-down counter scheme ac-
teurmulates the two frequencies separately for 5 to 20 s and then subtracts them,
fiving an averaging effect which may reduce spurious noise problems.

'when unequal deposit layers build up), we avoid the bias error (Af #0 when
V = 0) and independence of cis retained. Inthe "clamp-on" type! of ultrasonic
meter (Fig. 743), the transducers are outside the pipe, which eliminates the
fouling problems just described and gives an extremely convenient installation
devoid of transducer/Auid compatibility problems. Generally. the existing pipe
'ean be employed, and the transducers are attached by mechanical clamping. or
«adhesive bonds, leaving the pipe intact and obstruction-ree, Clamp-on meters
exhibit their own problem areas, such as "acoustic short circuiting" (receiving
transducer feels both liquid-path and pipe-path signals) and changes in beam
path du to clamp slippage, temperature expansion, ete. However, these can often
bbe overcome by proper design or use of compensation methods.
AA problem common to both clamp-on and "wetted transducer" meters is
'sensitivity to flow velocity profile. Unlike electromagnetic meters, which read the
worrect average velocity as long as the flow profile is axisymmetric, ultrasonic
meters of the type described above will give different readings for axisymmetric
Profiles of different shape but identical average velocity (Eq. (7.70) assumed a
tuiform profile). The reason is that the pulse transit time is related to the integral
'of fluid velocity along the path. Thus 1 em of path near the wall is weighted
equally with 1 em near the centerline, even though the contributions of the
annular flow areas of these two regions would not be the same. If we defined a
"meter coefficient" to be 1.0 for a uniform profil, this would drop to 0.75 for
laminar flow (Ng < 2000) and vary between 0.93 and 0.96 for turbulent flows,
with 4.000 < Ny'< 10 deg . Rather than using the "tilted diameter" signal path as.
sumed so far in our discussion if one utilizes a chordal path, it has been shown?
that the meter coeficient is nearly the same for both laminar and turbulent flow.
'Another approach is to employ several signal paths and average their results,
'Als, if flow conditions are reproducible, computed corrections always can be
applied even for a meter witha single diametral path
'The other main category of commercial ultrasonic meters uses the doppler
principle. Whereas the "transit time" meters discussed above require relatively
lean fuid to minimize signal attenuation and dispersion, doppler meters will nt
'work at all unless sufficient reflecting particles and/or air bubbles are present. For
'exampl," in an 8-in pipe, 10 percent by volume of 40-um reflectors of 02 percent

Because of the availabilty of meters for both clean and dirty fluids, little or

Vortex-Shedding Flowmeters
'The phenomenon of vortex shedding ("Karman vortex street") downstream of an
immersed solid body of "blunt" shape when a steady flow impinges upstream is
Well known in fuid mechanics and isthe basis of the vortex-shedding flowmeter,
When the pipe Reynolds number Ny exceeds about 10,000, vortex shedding is
reliable, and the shedding frequency is given by
Na
fx
where V 4 fuid velocity
4 & characteristic dimension of shedding body
Nu & Strouhal number, an experimentally determined number, nearly con-
stant in the useful lowmetering range (for example, 0.21 for eylinders)
By proper design of the shedding body shape, Ny can be kept nearly constant
'over a wide range of Ng (and thus flow rate) making f proportional to Vand
thus giving a "digital"'fowmetering principle based on counting the vortex
shedding rate (see Fig. 7.45). Various shedder shapes and frequency sensing
Schemes have been developed by several manufacturers. The vortives cause alter:
rating forces or local pressures on the shedder; piezoelectric and strain-page
methods can be employed to detect these. Hot-film thermal anemometer sensors
buried in the shedder ean detect the periodic flow-velocity fluctuations. The
interruption of ultrasonic beams by the passing vortices ean be used to count
them. Vortex-induced diferential pressures will cause oscillation ofa small caged
ball whose motion can be detected with a magnetic proximity pickup. Figure 746
shows yet another scheme which senses differential pressure with an elastic di
phragm.
'A wide variety of liquids and gases and steam may be metered. Linear ranges
of 15:1 are common, with 200: 1 sometimes possible. Vortex frequencies at
'maximum flow rate are the order of 200 to 500 Hz, and the frequency responds to
changing flow rate within about 1 cycle. Ia frequency-to-vollage converter is
used t0 realize an analog output voltage, then its low-pass filter will determine
'overall system response, which can be quite fast at high flow rates but must

MiscellaneousTopics
We briefly mention here three topics that cannot be treated in depth because of
space limitations but which are felt worthy of more than just a bibliographic
entry. Two-phase flow' occurs in a number of practical applications and has been
of particular interest for steam/water and air/water mixtures in steam power
plants. Severe environments and lack of well-established calibration standards
'make instrument development particularly diffeult. Few commercial instruments
are available, and special designs or adaptations of standard devices usually are
necessary:
Metering of cryogenic fluids such as liquid oxygen? and hydrogen (used in
rocket engines), liquefied natural gas, ete, presents problems of extreme low
temperatures and explosion hazards. Use of safer liquid nitrogen as a surrogate
fluid for calibration purposes* has been established as a valid procedure and is
the basis of a calibration facility which provides a mass flow uncertainty of about
#02 percent over a flow range of 20 to 210 gal/min at temperatures of 70 10
90K.
'The use of cross-correlation as a basic Rowmetering principle is being re-
searched and may lead to a family of commercial instruments since it can be
'implemented in several ways* by utilizing "tagging signals" already present in the
flow of intentionally introduced. Briefly, the signals from two sensors separated
axially in the pipe by a distance L and responding to the tagging signals are
cross-correlated by using a range of delays. Since the tagging signals are swept
from one sensor to the other at flow velocity V, the cross-correlation function
should exhibit a peak at a delay equal to L/V seconds, which allows calculation
of ¥ since L is known. Turbulent eddies naturally present and detected by
doppler ultrasonic methods, or thermal pulses intentionally injected and then
sensed by thermocouples are only two of many possible schemes being con-
sidered. The practicality of the method hinges strongly on the availability of
low-cost correlators based on integrated-circuit and microprocessor technology.

73 GROSS MA'
5 FLOW RATE
In many applications of flow measurement, mass flow rate is actually more
significant than volume flow rate. As an example, the range capability of an
raft or liguid-uel rocket is determined by the mass of fuel, not the volume.
Thus flowmeters used in fueling such vehicles should indicate mass, not volume.
In chemical process industries also, mass flow rate is often the significant quan-
tiv.
Two general approaches are employed to measure mass flow rate. One in-
volves the use of some type of volume flowmeter, some means of density measure-
ment, and some type of simple computer to compute mass flow rate. The other,
more basi, approach is to find flowmetering concepts that are inherently sens
tive to mass Now rate, Both methods are currently finding successful application
in various forms
'Volume Flowmeter Plus Density Measurement
Some basic methods of fid-density measurement are shown in Fig. 7.47. In Fig
7.47a a portion of the lowing liquid is bypassed through a still well. The buoyant
force on the float is directly related to density and may be measured in a number
'of ways, such as the strain-gage beam shown. In Fig. 7.476 a definite volume of
lowing liquid contained within the U tube is continuously weighed by a spring
and pneumatic displacement transducer. Flexible couplings isolate external forces
fom the U tube. A pneumatic force-balance feedback system also can be used 10
measure the weight." This minimizes deflection and thus reduces errors due £0
'ariable spring effects of flexible couplings and flexure pivots
Figure 747e shows a method of measuring gas density by using a small
'centrifugal blower (run at constant speed) fo pump continuously a sample of the
flow. The pressure drop across such a blower is proportional to density and may
the measured with a suitable differential-pressure pickup. Ultrasonic volume fow-
meters offen use an ultrasonic density-measuring technique when mass flow rate
is wanted. In Fig. 747d the erystal transducer serves as an acoustic-impedance
efector. Acoustic impedance depends on the product of density and speed of
Sound. Since a signal proportional to the speed of sound is available from the
volume flowmeter, division of this signal into the acoustc-impedance signal gives
'2 density signal? The attenuation of radiation from a radioisotope source de-
pends on the density of the material through which tbe radiation passes (see Fig.
F'47e) Over a limited (but generally adequate) density range, the output current
of the radiation detector is nearly linear with density, for a given flowing fluid.

through the tube. The first bending mode of vibration of the U tube is well ap-
proximated by standard cantilever-beam formulas. The beam sifiess is unaffected
by the internal liquid, but the mass per unit length varies directly with the liquid

AAn intentionally unstable, amplitude-timited feedback system maintains contin-
'uous oscillation ata limit-eycting frequency." This frequency is very close to the
tube's density-dependent natural frequency and "tracks" it asthe natural frequency
changes with density. Standard electronic frequency-measuring schemes read out
'the density with high resolution and accuracy, while microprocessors can be easly
used the frequency counters are "digital" to linearize the frequency-density relation
and/or provide temperature compensation from temperature-sensor signals. Design
criteria include a desire for both high frequency (for good resolution over short
'counting intervals) and high sensitivity of frequency to density. For given E, L,
Ry py, and p,, the natural frequency f, exhibits a maximum with respect to R,
which can be found by calculus. For L = 550 in, R, = 0.30, water lowing, an
'stainless-steel tube, R, = 0.2293 in, maximizes f, at about $00 Hz, Maximization
of sensitivity (dj/dp,) becomes analytically difficult, so we use numerical differ
entiation and find R, = 0.291; and since frequeney for this R, was sill close to
500 Hz, we choose R, = 0.291. Stress analysis for 1,000 Ibjin? working pressure
showed an adequate safety factor. To check the final design, a CSMP simulation
was run for the feedback system of Fig. 747g, with K,=K,=K,~ 10,
K, = 1,000, r = 105, y = 005, and density varies over a 425 percent range. The
limit-eycling frequency was found to change from 467 to 537 Hz as the density
varied over its complete range
In computing mass flow rate from volume flowmeter and densitometer
(density-measuring device) signals, the necessary form of computer vaties some.
what, depending on the type of flowmeter. For "head" meters (those producing @
differential pressure or electrical signal proportional to p¥3,, such as an orifice)
the computer multiplics the p signal by the pV3, to form p2¥ and then takes the
square root of this 10 get pV, which is proportional to the mass flow rate. For
"Velocity" flowmeters, such as the turbine and electromagnetic types, the avail
able signal is proportional to ¥,,; thus the computer simply must multiply this
by the p signal, and the square-root operation is unnecessary.
Direct Mass Flowmeters
While the above indirect methods of mass-low-rate measurement often are satis:
factory and are in wide us, itis possible to find lowmetering concepts that are
more directly sensitive to mass flow rate. These may have advantages with te-
spect to accuracy, simplicity, cost, weight, space, etc, in certain applications. We
discuss briefly some of the more common principles in terms of the practical
hhardware through which they have been realized

Coriolis type meter which has been successfully reduced to commercial practice'

Qystem This is a selfexcited drive system which always runs at beam natural

back which compares sense-coil voltage (velocity) with a fixed reference signal
The mechanical "tuning fork? configuration minimizes the vibratory force into
the frame.

4
Flom ote meenred
of a single, small, low-power sensing tube as the basic element for a whole family
'of meters, up to those for very high flows. To ensure a fixed ratio between the
'main flow and the small bypass flow, both flow passages ate designed to have
laminar flow over the entire range ofthe meter. Since both elements fel the same
pressure drop at all times, the main flow rate is always proportional to the sensed
bypass rate, making the total flow (what we want to measure) also proportional
In the referenced instruments, the ratio of total flow to bypassed flow ranges
from 1 (smallest meter) to 100,000 (largest meter).
"The sensor tube has a diameter of 0031 in and a length 50 to 100 times this
amount, giving laminar flow for the entire measuring range, with the maximum
sensor-tube flow always being about 20 em?/min. The usually much larger main
flow requires a larger flow atea while still maintaining laminar conditions. Thin,
rectangular flow channels milled into the periphery of a cylinder (see Fig. 7.53)
are used here. Two electric windings on the outer surface of the sensor tube act
'as both heaters and resistance-temperature detectors and provide a constant-hes
input to the fluid at all flow rates. For zero flow rate (ii, = 0) the system is
"thermally symmetric," giving the symmetric temperature profile shown in Fig.

-- 639 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 632
7.53, and making AT 4 T, - Tr; equal to 2er0, The two windings are in adjacent
legs ofa bridge circuit, and bridge resistances are selected to give a balanced bridge
(no output voltage) for the zer0-flow condition. At this no-low condition, all the
'electric heating is being used to balance the heat losses from the tube to its sur-
roundings, which are assumed to remain at a fixed ambient temperature T,. If we
now allow a steady fluid mass low rate mi, from left to right and wait for the
new thermal equilibrium, the new temperature profile will be unsymmetric, with
T, dropping a large amount but T staying nearly constant, Temperature T,
drops because the mass flow of hot fluid leaving this region carries energy away
at a rate vi, requiring an increase in the local heat-transfer rate (from tube
to fluid) to support this energy loss and maintain equilibrium. To allow this heat-
transfer rate to increase, the AT between tube and fluid must increase, which
requires a drop in T, proportional to vi. Temperature T,, changes little when
flow occurs since ths region both receives and loses heat (at about the same rate)
from the flowing fluid. Although the desired mass-flow signal is associated mainly
with TT, use of the T;~T, signal available from the bridge circuit has the
advantages of giving a zero output for zer0 flow rate and making the instrument
less sensitive to ambient temperature, since T, and T are affected about equally
'by changes in T,. For very large flow rates (beyond the instruments design range),
the cooling effect of the fluid makes both T', and T much lower and also more
nearly equal, since a uid particle passes so rapidly from region { to region 2 that
litle temperature change can occur.
In the referenced commercial instruments, calibration of each instrument
'against standards plus use ofa built-in five-break-point electronic linearize gives
accuracy of +1 percent of full scale, including nonlinearity. Step response has &
2 percent seiting time of 1 to 3. The meter pressure drop at maximum flow
ranges from 008 to 1.5 Ibjin?, depending on meter size,

CHAPTER

TEMPERATURE AND HEAT-FLUX
MEASUREMENT
8.1 STANDARDS AND CALIBRATION
'The International Measuring System sets up independent standards for only four
fundamental quantities: length, time, mass, and temperature. Standards forall
'other quantities are basically derived from these. We discussed previously the
standards of length, time, and mass; we consider now the temperature standard."
1K should be noted frst that temperature is fundamentally different in nature from
length time, and mass. That is, if two bodies of like ength are "combined," the
total length is twice the original; the same is true for two time intervals or two
'masses. However, the combination of two bodies of the same temperature results
in exactly the same temperature. Thus the idea of a standard unit of mass, length,
or time that can be divided or multiplied indefinitely to generate any arbitrary
'magnitude of these quantities cannot be carried over to the concept of temperature.
Also, even though statistical mechanics relates temperature to the mean kinetic
energies of molecules, these kinetic energies (which are dependent on only mass,
length, and time standards for their description) are not measurable at present,
'Thus an independent temperature standard is requited
'The fundamental meaning of temperature, just as for all basie concepts of
physic, is not given easily. For most purposes the zeroth law of thermodynamics
"RP, Benet, "Fundamental of Temperature, Pere, and Flow Menorements" 24 et,
on

-- 644 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 637
TEMPERATURE AND HEATILUX SUASUREMENT 613
{ives a useful concept: For two bodies to be said to have the same temperature,
they must be in thermal equilibrium; that is, when thermal communication is
possible between them, no change in the thermodynamic coordinates of either
fecurs. The zeroth law says that when wo bodies are each in thermal equilib
rium with a thied body, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other. Then, by
efinition, the bodies are all at the same temperature. Thus if we can set up a
reproducible means of establishing a range of temperatures, unknown temper-
atures of other bodies may be compared with the standard by subjecting any type
'of "thermometer" successively to the standard and to the unknown temperatures
'and allowing equilibrium to occur in each case. That 3s, the thermometer is
calibrated against the standard and afterward may be used to read unknown
temperatures.
Tn choosing the means of defining the standard temperature scale, con-
'civably we could employ any of the many physical properties of materials that
vary reproducibly with temperature. For instance, the length of a metal rod
varies with temperature, To define a temperature scale numerically, we must
choose a reference temperature and state a rule for defining the difference be-
tween the reference and other temperatures. (Mass, length, and time measure>
'ments. do not require universal agreement on a reference point at which each
{quantity is assumed to have a particular numerical value. Every centimeter, for
'example, ina meter isthe same a every other centimeter)
'Suppose we take a copper rod 1m long, place it in an ice-water bath which
wwe have taken as our reference temperature source, and measure its length, Let us
'choose to call the iee-bath temperature 0 deg . We are now free to define any rule we
Wish to fix the numerical value to be assigned to all lower and higher temper-
atures, Suppose we decide that each additional 0.01 mm of expansion will corre
spond to +10" on our temperature seale and each 001 mm of contraction 10
1.0". Ifthe expansion phenomenon were reproducible, such a temperature scale
'would, in principle, be perfectly acceptable as long as everyone adhered to it
Would it be cortect to say that each degree of temperature on this scale was
equal" to every other degree? That depends on what we mean by "equal." If
"equal" means that each degree causes the same amount of expansion of the
'copper rod, then all degrees are equal. I, instead, we consider the expansion of,
say, iron rods, then equal amounts of expansion would not, in general, be caused
by a I" (copper scale} change from -6 t0 -S" as by al change from 100 to
TOI". Or, suppose we based our scale on conduction heat transfer in silver for
'example. Ia temperature difference from 100 to 200 deg causes a given heat-ransfe
rate, will the same rate be caused by a temperature difference from -50 to
450-7 The answer is, in general no.
'The point of the above discussion is that, while our arbitrarily defined tem
perature scale is, in principle, as good as any other such scale based on some
'material property, its graduations have no particular significance with regard to
physical laws other than the one used in the definition. We measure temperature
for some reason, such as computing thermal expansion, heat-transfer rate, electri
cal conductivity, gas pressure, ete. The forms of the equations employed to make

-- 645 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 638
such calculations depend on the nature of the standard used to define temper-
ature. A temperature scale that gives a simple form to thermal-expansion equa-
tions may give complex forms to all other physical relations involving temper-
ature. Since this difficulty is common to all standards based on the properties of a
particular substance, a way of defining a temperature scale independent of any
substance is desirable.
'The thermodynamic temperature scale! proposed by Lord Kelvin in 1848
provides the theoretical base for a temperature scale independent of any material
Property and is based on the Carnot eycle. Here a perfectly reversible heat engine
transfers heat from a reservoir of infinite capacity at temperature T, to another
such reservoir at T,. Ifthe heat taken from reservoir 2 is Q, and that supplied to
reservoir I is Q,, for a Carmot cycle Q3/Q, = Ty/T;s this may be taken as a
definition of temperature ratio. If, also, a number is selected to describe the
temperature of a chosen fixed point, then the temperature scale is completely
defined. At present, the fixed point is taken as the triple point (the state at which
soli, liquid, and vapor phases are in equilibrium) of water because this is the
'most reproducible state known. The number assigned to this point is 273.16 K
since this makes the temperature interval from the ice point (273.15 K) to the
steam point equal to 100 K. This would thus coincide with the previously es-
lablished centigrade (now called Celsius) scale as a matter of convenience."
While the Kelvin absolute thermodynamic sale is ideal in the sense that itis
independent of any material properties, it is not physically realizable since it
'depends on an ideal Carnot cyele. Fortunately it can be shown? that a temper-
ature scale defined by a constant-volume or constant-pressure gas thermometer
using an ideal gas is identical to the thermodynamic scale. A constant-volume gas
thermometer Keeps a fixed mass of gas at constant volume and measures the
Pressure changes caused by temperature changes. The perfect-as law then gives
the fact that temperature ratios are identical to pressure ratios. The constant
pressure thermometer keeps mass and pressure constant and measures volume
changes caused by temperature changes. Again, the perfect-gas law says that
emperature ratios are identical (© volume ratios. These ratios are identical to
those of the thermodynamic scale; thus ifthe same fixed point (the tripe point of
water is selected for the reference point, the two scales are numerically identical
However. now there is the problem that the ideal gas is a mathematical model,
ot a real substance, and therefore the gas thermometers described above cannot
ually be built and operated,
To obtain a physically realizable temperature scale, real gases must be util
ied in the gas thermometers: the readings must be corrected, as well as possible,
for deviation from ideal-gas behavior: and then the resulting values are accepted
as a definition of the temperature scale. The corrections for non-ideal-gas beha-
Vior are obtained for a constant-volume gas thermometer as follows: The ther-

'original value, The pressure will be higher now: we call itp,,. For an ideal gas,
the ratio ofthe steam-point and ice-point temperatures also would be given by
the pressure ratio p/p. If we repeat this experiment but use a different mass of

616 saasunine devices
'generating the curve A of Fig. 81h, Since we cannot use zero mass of gas, the
2ero-pressure point on this curve must be obtained by extrapolation. This zero:
Pressure point is taken as the true value of the pressure ratio corresponding to
'the steam-point/ice-point temperature ratio. If this experiment is repeated with
different gases (B, C in Fig. 8.16), all the curves intersect at the same point,
showing that the procedure is independent of the type of gas used. Actual results
ive the numerical value pp, = 1.36609 + 0.00004, If we take 7,7, = pJ/p,. the
'choice of a numerical value for any chosen reference point (such as calling 7, -
273.15 K) completely fixes the entire temperature scale. Such a seale, unfortu
day temperature measurements since the
Procedures involved are extremely tedious and time-consuming. Also, gas ther
mometers actually have a lower precision (repeatability) than some other
temperaturesmeasuring devices, such as resistance thermometers. This situation
led to the acceptance in 1927 of the International Practical Temperature Scale
which. with revisions in 1948, 1954, 1960, and 1968, isthe temperature standard
today.
'The International Practical Temperature Scale is set up to conform as closely
4s practical with the thermodynamic scale. At the Liple point of water, the two
scales are in exact agreement, by definition. Five other primary fixed points are
used. These are the boiling points of liquid oxygen (~ 182:962 deg C) and water
(100 C) and the freezing points of zine (419.58 deg C), silver (961.93 deg C). and gold
(1068.43 deg C). Various secondary fixed points also are established, withthe lowest
boeing the triple point of hydrogen (~259.34'C), which also is the lowest value
defined on the scale (the gold point 1064.43 deg C is the highest defined fited point).
'Im addition to the fixed points, the International Practical Temperature Scale
also specifies certain instruments, equations, and procedures to be utilized to
interpolate between the fixed points. From ~259.34 to 630,74 deg C, a platinum
resistance thermometer is the interpolating instrument; however, since one equa-
'ion will not serve the entire range, equations for several subranges are defined.
Each equation contains certain constants whose values must be obiained from
the readings of that particular thermometer at specific fixed points, Once these
constants have been found, the equation allows us to calculate temperature
values from thermometer readings at any point within the given subrange. A
similar procedure is employed between 630.74 and 106443 deg C, except now the
interpolating instrument is a platinum/10% rhodium and platinum thermo-
couple
'Above the gold point, the International Practical Temperature Scale is de-
fined and uses a narrow-band radiation pyrometer("optical" pyrometer) and the
lanck equation to establish temperatures, The formula is

"The quantity Jj/Jq is measurable with the pyrometer and is the ratio of the
spectral radiance of @ blackbody at temperature ¢ to one at temperature tay.
Since 2 can be determined for a given pyrometer, Fg, (8.1) allows calculation of ¢
when JyJuy has been measured. In principle, this method can be applied to
Arbitrarily high temperatures, but in practice few reliable results above 4000 deg C
are known.
"The highest meaningful temperatures, existing inthe interior of stars and for
short times in atomic explosions, are inferred from kinetic theory to be in the
Tange 10 deg to 10" K. Definition of temperature, much less measurement is dificult
it these extremes, although spectroscopic methods have given useful results. At
the other extreme, temperatures of 10-* K have been produced by using the
'Concept of nuclear cooling. Magnetic susceptibility of certain materials has been
"employed to measure temperatures in the extremely low ranges
"The question of the accuracy of temperature standards may be considered
from two viewpoints. First, how closely can the International Practical Temper-
ature Seale be reproduced: sevond, how closely does it agree withthe thermodyn-
amie absolute scale? The highest reproducibility of the International Practical
'Temperature Seale occurs at the triple point of water, which can be realized with
41 precision of a few ten-thousandths of a degree, giving an accuracy of about |
Pom. For either lower or higher temperatures the accuracy falls off Figure 82a!
Pummarizes these data and shows calibration uncertainties for various instru-
ments, with more recent data in Fig. 82h The question of agreement among the
'various empirical seales (such as the International Practical Temperature Scale)
land the absolute thermodynamic scale involves the fact that, in general, the
thermodynamic seale is considerably less reproducible than the empirical scales
For example, the steam-point temperature is reproducible to 0.0005" witha plati=
'num resistance thermometer, but to only 0.02" with a gas thermometer. The dis-
'agreement between the International Practical Temperature Scale andthe absolute
thermodynamic scale has been estimated in Celsius degrees as"

Calibration of a given temperature-measuring device generally is accom-
plished by subjecting it to some established fixed-point environment, such as the
frelting and boiling points of standard substances, or by comparing is readings
inith those of some more accurate (secondary standard) temperature sensor which
itself has been calibrated. The latter generally is accomplished by placing the two
"Uovices in intimate thermal contact in a constant-temperature-controled bath, BY
Varying the temperature ofthe bath over the desired range (allowing equilibrium
Ji cach point) the necessary corrections are determined. Accurate resistance
thermometers? thermocouples, oF mercury-in-glass expansion thermometers Ben-
'rally are useful as secondary standards, Fixed-point standards using the melting
points of various metals and the triple point of water are commercially available,

'Combination of this equation with appropriate strength-of-materials relations

Since there are no practically usuble metals with negative thermal expansion,
the B element is generally made of Tovar, a nickel stect with a nearly zero
[17 x 10" infin C)) expansion coefficient. While brass was employed or
finally, a variety of alloys are used now for the high-expansion strip, depending
fn the mechanical and electrical characteristics required, Details of materials and
bonding processes are, in some cases, considered trade secrets. A wide range of
'configurations has been developed to meet application requirements (Fig. 8.36).
Bimetallic devices are wilized for temperature measurement and very widely
'as combined sensing and control elements in temperature-control systems, mainly
Of the onvoff type. Abo they are used as overload cutout switches in electric
"pparatus by allowing the current to flow through the bimetal, heating and
expanding it and causing @ switch to open when excessive current flows. Further
"applications are found as temperature-compensating devices? for various instru
mmnts that have temperature as a modifying or interfering input. The mechanical
'motion proportional to temperature is employed to generate an opposing com-
pensating effect. The accuracy of bimetallic elements varies greatly, depending on
the requirements of the application. Since the majority of control applications are
rot extremely citical, requirements can be satisfied witha rather Low-cost device
For more critical applications, performance can be much improved. The working
temperature range fs about from - 100 to 1000'F. Inaceuracy of the order of OS
to 1 percent of scale range may be expected in bimetal thermometers of high
'quality. Recently, bimetal elements have been combined with conductive plastic
potentiometers for automotive sensing applications.»

Liquid-in-Glass Thermometers!
The well-known liquid-in-glass thermometer is adaptable to a wide range of
applications by varying the materials of construction and/or configuration, Mer-
ccury is the most common liquid utilized at intermediate and high temperatures;
its freezing point of ~ 38 deg F limits its lower range. The upper limit isin the region
of 1000 deg F and requires use of special glasses and an inert-gs fil in the capillary
space above the mercury. Compression of the gas helps to prevent separation of
the mercury thread and raises the liquid boiling point. For low temperatures,
alcohol is usable to -80'F, toluol to ~130 deg F, pentane to 30 deg F, and a mis
ture of propane and propylene giving the lower limit of -360 deg F.
'Thermometers are commonly made in two types: total immersion and partial
immersion. Total-immersion thermometers are calibrated to read correctly when
the liquid column is completely immersed in the measured fluid. Since this may
'obscure the reading, a small portion of the column may be allowed to protrude
with litte error, Partial-immersion thermometers are calibrated to read correctly
whien immersed a definite amount and with the exposed portion at a definite
{emperature. They are inherently less accurate than fullimmersion types Ifthe
'exposed portion is at a temperature different from that at calibration, then a
'correction must be applied. Corrections for full- and partil-immersion thermo
meters used at conditions other than those intended are determined most accu.
Fately by the use of a special "faden" thermometer, designed to measure the
'average temperature of the emergent stem. If such a thermometer is not avail-
able, the correction may be estimated by suspending a small auxiliary thermome.

Pressure Thermometers!
Pressure thermometers consist of a sensitive bulb, an interconnecting capillary
tube, and a pressure-measuring device such as a Bourdon tube, bellows, or dia-
phragm (Fig. 8.5). When the system is completely filled with a liquid (mereury
find aylene are common) under an initial pressure, the compressibility of the
Tiquid is often small enough relative to the pressure gage AV/Ap that the
measurement is essentially one of volume change. For gas or vapor systems, the
reverse is true. and the basic effect is one of pressure change at constant volume.
Capillary tubes as long as 200 ft may be used for remote measurement
'Temperature variations along the capillary and at the pressure-sensing. device
zencrlly require compensation, except in the vapor-pressure type, where pressure
cpends on only the temperature atthe liquid' free surface, located at the bulb.
'A common compensation scheme using an auxiliary pressure sensor and capillary

is shown in Fig. 86. The motion of the compensating system is due to the
interfering effects only and is subtracted from the total motion of the main
system, resulting in an output dependent on only bulb temperature. The "trim-
'ming" capillary (which may be lengthened or shortened) allows the volume to be
'changed {0 attain accurate case compensation by experimental test. Bimetal ele-
'ments also are used to obtain case and partial capillary compensation,
Liquid-filled systems cover the range - 150 to 750 deg F with xylene of a similar
liquid and -38 10 1100 deg F with mercury. Response is essentially linear over
ranges up to about 300 deg F with xylene and 1000 deg F with mercury. Elevation iff
'ences between the bulb and pressure sensor diferent from those at calibration
'may cause slight errors. Gas-filled systems operate over the range -400 to
1200 'F with linear ranges as great as 1000 deg F; errors due to capillary temperature
variations usually are small enough not to justify compensation. Case compensa
tion is accomplished with bimetal clements. Vapor-pressure systems are usable in
the range -40 to 600 deg F. The calibration is strongly nonlinear: special linearzing
linkages are needed iftinear output is required. Characteristics of the system vary.

Figure BA Basic thermocoupe and junction pes
'connected, a current 1 is measured. The magnitude of the voltage E depends on
'the materials and temperatures 7, and Ts. The current 1 is simply E divided by
the total resistance ofthe circuit, including the ammeter resistance. If currents
allowed to flow, electric power is developed; this comes from a heat fow from the
Surroundings to the wires. Thus a direct conversion of heat energy to eleciric
energy is obtained. The effect is reversible, so that forcing a current from an
external source through a thermoelectric circuit will cause heat flows to and from
the circuit, While we are concerned here with this thermoelectric effect only as a
'means of sensing temperatures, modern developments in materials have made the
Principle of practical application in electric power generation, heating, and cool.
ing. though only on a small scale at present.
'The overall relation between voltage E and temperatures T, and Ts, which is
the basis of thermoelectric temperature measurement, i called the Seebeck effect.
'Temperatures 7; and 7 refer to the junctions themselves, whereas when using «
thermocouple, we are trying to measure the temperature of some body in contact
with the thermojunction. These two temperatures are not exactly the same if
'current is allowed to flow through the thermojunction, since then heat is genet-
'ated oF absorbed at the junction, which thus must be hotter or colder than the
Surrounding medium whose temperature is being measured. This heating and
cooling process is related to the Peltier effect! Ifthe thermocouple voltage is
" P-HLDike,~Thermocitrie Thermomtr Leads & Northrup Co, Philadephia, Pa, 1954

-- 658 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 651
"TEMPERATURE AND HEAT-LUX MEASUREMENT 627
'measured with a potentiometer, no curtent flows and Peltier heating and cooling
are not present. When a millivolimeter is used, cutrent flows and heat is absorbed
fat the hot junction (requiring it to become cooler than the surrounding medium)
While heat is iberated at the cold junction, making it hotter than its surrounding,
medium. These heating and cooling effets are proportional to the current and
fortunately are completely negligible' when the current is that produced by the
thermocouple itself in a practical milivoltmeter circuit. These ertors are even less
when the thermocouple is connected (as is very common) to an instrumentation
amplifier with high input impedance (110 1,000 MQ)
"Another reversible heatfow effect, the Thomson effect? influences the tem=
perature of the conductors between the junctions rather than the junetions them-
Selves. When current flows through a conductor having a temperature gradient
{and thus a heat flow) along its length, heat i liberated at any point where the
csurrent flow is in the same direction as the heat flow, while heat is absorbed at
'any point where these are opposite. Since this effet also depends on current flow,
it is not present if a potentiometer is used. Even if « millivoltmeter is employed,
the effect of the heat flows on conductor temperature is completely negligible
Finally, it should be noted that in any currentcarrying conductor, F7R heat is
generated, raising the circuit temperature above its local surroundings. Again,
potentiometric and high-input-impedance voltage measurements give negligible
terror. Errors in millivolimeter circuits usually are negligible also, but can be
estimated if heat-iransfer conditions are known,
'The above physical effects can be analyzed? on a macroscopic scale by class-
'eal thermodynamics, with fewer assumptions by irreversible thermodynamics,
and qualitatively on a mieroscopic basis by solid-state physics, Thermodynamic
Approaches are based on the two experimentally observed reversible energy
'conversion processes, the Peltier and Thomson effects, and neither require nor
ive any explanation of the basic atomic mechanisms. The total emf produced is
made up of a part due to the Peltier effect, which is localized at each junction,
'and a (usually much smaller) part caused by the Thomson effect, which is distrib-
lted along each conductor between the junctions. The Peltier ems are assumed
proportional to the junction temperature, while the Thomson emf are pro-
portional to the difference between the squares of the junction temperatures. For
the total voltage, the equation takes the form

Unfortunately, the assumptions made in the analyses leading to Eq, (8.6) are
not exactly satisfied in practice; thus equations such as (8.7) cannot usually be
used to predict accurately temperatures from measured voltages. Rather, a given
thermocouple material must be calibrated over the complete range of temper-
atures in which it isto be used. In this calibration only the overall voltage is of
interest, and the separate contributions of Peltier and Thomson effects are not
determined, Temperature measurement by thermoelectric means i thus based
'entirely on empirical calibrations and the application of so-called thermoelectric
laws" which experience has shown to hold. These laws, quoted below, are
adequate for analysis of most practical thermocouple citcuits. In those cases
where the circuit configuration does not lend ise to ditect application of these
laws, alternative approaches! are available.
The laws of thermocouple behavior may be stated as follows:
1. The thermal emf of a thermocouple with junctions at T; and T; is totally
unaffected by temperature elsewhere in the eireuit ifthe two metals used are
'each homogeneous (Fig. 89a),
2. Ifa third homogeneous metal C is inserted into either A or B (see Fig. 896),
4s long as the two new thermojunctions are at like temperatures, the net emf
of the citeuit is unchanged irrespective of the temperature of C away from the
junctions
3. If metal C is inserted between 4 and B at one of the junctions, the temper:
ature of C at any point away from the AC and BC junctions is immaterial. As
long as the junctions AC and BC are both atthe temperature T, the net emt
is the same as if C were not there (Fig. 89).
4. I the thermal emf of metals A and C is E4c and that of metals B and C is
Ecys then the thermal emf of metals A and Bis Ec + Ecy (Fig. 89d).
5. Ifa thermocouple produces emf E, when its junctions ate at T, and T;, and
E, when at T; and T,, then i¢ will produce E, + Ey when the junctions are at
T, and T, (Fig. 890)
'These laws are of great importance in the practical application of thermo-
'couples. The frst states that the lead wires connecting the two junctions may be
safely exposed to an unknown and/or a varying temperature environment with=
'out affecting the voltage produced. Laws 2 and 3 make it possible to insert a
voltage-measuring device into the circuit actually to measure the emf, rather than
just talking about its existence. That is, the metal C represents the internal circuit
(usually all copper in precise instruments) between the instrument binding posts
The instrument can be connected in two ways, as shown in Fig. 896 and c. Law 3
also shows that thermocouple junctions may be soldered or brazed (thereby
introducing a third metal) without affecting the readings. Law 4 shows that all
possible pairs of metals need not be calibrated since the individual metals can
'each be paired with one standard (platinum is used) and calibrated. Any other
'combinations then ean be calculated; calibration is not necessary.

In considering the fith law, we should note that, in using a thermocouple to
measure an unknown temperature, the temperature of one ofthe thermojunetions
{called the reference junction) must be known by some independent means. A
voltage measurement then allows us to get the temperature of the other (measur-
ing) junction from calibration tables. These calibration tables were obtained by
'maintaining the reference junction at a fixed known value (usually 32 deg F, the ice
point), varying the measured junction over the desired range of temperatures
{known by some independent means}, and recording the resulting voltages. Thus
'most calibration tables are based on the reference junction being atthe ie point
'When a thermocouple is used, the reference junction may or may not be at the
ice point. If itis, the calibration table may be employed directly to find the
measuring-junetion temperatuee, If itis not, the fi law allows use of the stan-
Gard table as follows: Suppose the reference junction is at 70 deg F and the voltage
reading is 1.23 mV. In Fig. 89e we take T, = 32 deg F, T; = 70 deg F, and T, is un.

1.94 mV in the standard table; itis 100 deg F.
Common Thermocouples
'Thermojunctions formed by welding, soldering, or merely pressing the two ma-
terials together give identical voltages. If curtent is allowed to flow, the currents
'may be different since the contact resistance differs for the various joining me
thods. Welding either gas or electric) is used most widely although both silver
solder and soft solder (low temperatures only) ate used in copper/constantan
couples. Special capacitor-discharge welding devices (particularly needed for
very-finewire thermocouples) are available, Ready-made thermocouple pairs are,
'of course, available in a wide range of materials and wire sizes.
'While many materials exhibit the thermoelectric elect to some degree, only &
small number of pairs are in wide use, They are platinum rhodium, Chrome)
Alumel, copper/constantan, and iron/constantan, Each pair exhibits a combi
ration of properties that suit it to a particular class of applications. Since the
thermoelectric effect is somewhat nonlinear, the sensitivity varies with temper-
ature, The maximum sensitivity of any of the above pairs is about 60 4V/C deg for
ccopper/constantan at 350 deg C. Platinum/platinum-rhodium is the least sensitive
about 6 nV/C deg between 0 and 100 deg C.
'The accuracy of the common thermocouples may be stated in two different
ways. If you utilize standard thermocouple wire (which is nor individually eal
brated by the manufacturer) and make up a thermocouple to be used without
calibration, you ate relying on the wire manufacturer's quality control to limit
deviations from the published calibration tables. These tables give the average
characteristics, not those of a particular batch of wire, Platinum/platinum-
thodium isthe most accurate; error is of the order of +0.25 percent of reading.
Copper/constantan gives 40.5 percent or +1.SF deg (whichever is larger) between
~75 and 200'F and +£0.75 percent between 200 and 700 deg F. Chromel/Alumel
ives SF" (32 to 660 deg F) and £0.75 percent (660 to 2300 deg F). Iron/constantan
has £66 WV below S00'F and +1.0 percent from $00 to 1S00 deg F. If higher
accuracies are needed, the individual thermocouple may be calibrated. An indica
tion of the achievable accuracy is available from National Bureau of Standards
(now NIST) listings! of the results they will guarantee, At the actual calibration
Points, the error ranges from 0.05 to 0.5C". Interpolated points are less accurate:
04 to 10C*, except platinum/platinum-rhodium at 1450 deg F, 20 to 20C? The
realization of this potential accuracy in applying such calibrated thermocouples
to practical temperature measurement is, of course, dependent on the application
'conditions and is rarely possible,

igre 410 Thermocouple temper
Platinum/platinum-rhodium thermocouples are employed mainly in the
ange 0 to 1500 C. The main features of this combination are its chemical inert=
hess and stability at high temperatures in oxidizing atmospheres. Reducing
itmospheres cause rapid deterioration at high temperatures as the thermocouple
metals are contaminated by absorbing small quantities of other metals from
nearby objects (such as protecting tubes), This diffieulty causing loss of calibra
tion, is unfortunately common to most thermocouple materials above 1000 deg C.
'Chromel (NiygCry,)/Alumel (NiggMnAl,Si) couples are useful over the
ange 200 to +1300.C. Their main application, however, is from about 700 to
1200 deg C in nonreducing atmospheres. The temperature/voltage characteristic is
uite linear for this combination (see Fig. 8.10)
'Coppet/constantan (Cis Nig) 8 used at temperatures as low as ~ 200 deg C: its
upper limit is about 350 deg C because of the oxidation of copper above this range
Iron constantan is the most widely utilized thermocouple for industrial appli-
cations and covers the range ~ 150 to + 1000 deg C. It is usable in oxidizing atmos-
pheres to about 760 C and reducing atmospheres to 1000 deg C.
'Thermocouple manufacturers have a wealth of experience concerning the
application of thermocouples to diverse temperature-measuring problems and
'Should be consulted if special types of problems are foreseen in a particular case

igre R12 fothermal lok reference junction techni
Figure 8.12 sketches a reerence-junction technique widely utilized (in various
versions) for digital thermometer instruments, data loggers, and data acquisition
systems. Wires from the measuring junction are screwed directly to an isothermal
block terminal strip. The temperature of this block (which has no active temper
ature control) drifis with ambient temperature; but because of careful thermal
'design, a all times it is uniform over its length (within about +005 deg C). This
reference temperature is measured by independent means [often a junction semi
conductor sensor (Sec. 8.5)}, and compensation circuitry' develops a voltage
Eco Which is combined with that from the measuring junction so that the net
voltage presented to the voltmeter represents Tay.- In multichannel instruments
'with microprocessor computing power, the isothermal block can accept many
thermocouple pairs (of mixed types, if desired) since the Tyg sensor now sends its
temperature data to the computer, which computes the necded voltage correction
for each different thermocouple and applies it digitally as each channel is
scanned, Such a "software" correction scheme, of course, must be intermittent
(limiting response speed) because of the sampled nature of digital computation,
while the "hardware" technique of Fig. 8.12 is continuous and does not signti=
cantly degrade sensor dynamics.

'The main requirements in such applications are that the thermojunction be of

distributions are not distorted. In Fig 8.16a' the thermojunetion is formed by

Sensing tip. This action flows metal from one thermocouple element to the other
since the 0.0002-in mica insulation is easily bridged over, thus forming numerous

'Thermocouples in common use are made from wires ranging from about
(0020 to 0.1 in in diameter; the larger diameters are requited for long life in severe
environments. Since speed of response, conduction and radiation etrors, and
precision of junction location all are improved by the use of smaller wire, very-
fine-wire thermocouples are utilized in special applications requiring these at-
tributes and where lack of ruggedness isnot a serious drawback, Such couples are
available ready-made! in most common materials and wite sizes from 0.0005 to
(0015 in diameter. The time constant of an iron/constantan couple of 0.0005:
diameter wie fora step change from 200 to 100 F instill water is about 0.001 s.
Several thermocouples may be connected in series or parallel 10 achieve
useful functions (Fig. 8.18} The series connection with all measuring junctions at
fone temperature and all reference junctions at another is used mainly as a means
of increasing sensitivity? Such an arrangement is called a thermopile and for m
thermocouples gives an output m times as great as a single couple. Atypical
Chromel/constantan thermopile has 25 couples and produces about 1 mV/F
Common potentiometers can resolve 1 s:V, thus making such an arrangement
sensitive to O.001F". The parallel combination generates the same voltage as a
single couple if all measuring and reference junctions are at the same temper-
ature. If the measuring junctions are at different temperatures and the thermo-
couples are all the same resistance, the voltage measured is the average of the
individual voltages. The temperature corresponding to this voltage is the average
femperature only if the thermocouples are linear over the temperature range
being measured. A 20-junction thermopile with the two sets of junctions built
into two standard pipe fittings is available® for sensitive measurements of dif
'ential temperatures in flowing fluids.
By inserting a short section of tubing made of butt-welded thermocouple ma
terials into a pipeline, the temperature of the flowing fluid may be measured with
durability, fast response, and high accuracy in a nonintrusive way. Ready-made
Sensors in a variety of materials and configurations are commercially available*
(Fig. 8.180),

CTRICAL-RESISTANCE SENSORS
'The electrical resistance of various materials changes in a reproducible manner
with temperature, thus forming the basis of a temperature-sensing method. Ma-
terials in actual use fall into two main classes: conductors (metals) and. semi-
'conductors. Conducting materials historically came first and traditionally have
ben called resistance thermometers. [More recently the terminology resistance

Bridge circuits used with resistance temperature sensors may employ either
the deflection mode of operation or the null (manually or automatically balanced)
mode. If the null method is used, resistor Ry in Fig. 821a is varied until balance
is achieved. When the highest accuracy is required, the arrangement of Fig. 821
is prefered since the (variable and unknown) contact resistance in the adjustable
fesistor has no influence on the resistance of the bridge legs. If long lead wires
subjected to temperature variations are unavoidable, errors due to their resist-
lance changes may be canceled by use of the Siemens three-lead circuit of Fig.
'82le. Three lead wites of identical length and material exhibit identical resistance
variations, and since one of these leads is in each of legs 2 and 3, their resistance
'changes cancel. Resistance change in the third wire has no effect on bridge
balance since itis in the null detector cicuit for null-mode operation. For deflec:
tion operation, its effect is negligible if the indicating instrument draws Tittle
current

'not necessarily linear for large percentage changes in resistance. Unlike the case

sent directly to data acquisition systems, reducing their computation load since

'connected to measure differential temperature between two probes.
Resistance-thermometer bridges may be excited with either ac or de voltages.
'The direct or ems alternating current through the thermometer is usually in the
range 2 to 20 mA. This current causes an IR heating which raises the temper-
ature of the thermometer above its surroundings, causing the so-called self-
heating etror. The magnitude of this error depends also on heattransfer con-
ditions and usually is quite small. A 430-9 platinum clement of open construction
'carrying 25-mA current has a self-heating error of 0.2F deg when immersed in liquid
'oxygen. Actually, by using an unsymmetric pulse type of excitation voltage whose
rms (heating) value is small compared with its peak value, quite large instan-
taneous currents (and thus large peak output voltages) may be obtained without
significant selP-heating. Such pulse-exitation voltages (Fig. £22) can be obtained
by commutating # de source: this also allows timesharing of the bridge among
several resistance sensors. As much as a S-V full-scale bridge output signal can be
'obtained from resistance sensors by using this technique or others*

digital thermometers and data acquisition systems, where sensor nonlinearity is
'corrected in the computer software. Since a precise current source (usually afew
milliamperes) is utilized, resistance changes in these two lead wires have no effect

Bulk Semiconductor Sensors (Thermistors)"
'The earlier types of bulk semiconductor resistance temperature sensors were
'made of manganese, nickel, and cobalt oxides which were milled, mixed in proper
proportions with binders, pressed into the desired shape, and sintered. These were
given the name thermistor and are in wide use today. Compared with conductor-
type sensors (which have a small positive temperature coefficient), thermistors
have a very large negative coefficient. While some conductors (copper, platinum,
tungsten) are quite linear, thermistors are very nonlinear. Their resist-
ance/temperature relation is generally of the form

details on a commercially available transistor-based device! (Fig. 828) that is
produced as an integrated-cireuit (IC) chip and available in chip form, in small
metal ean or ceramic Nat-pack packages. oF in ready-made probes. The met
tion diagram (Fig. 828a) of the chip shows the small size of the basic sensor,
while Fig. 28h shows internal details. It is a fundamental property of silicon
transistors that if two identical transistors are operated at a constant ratio r of
collector-current densities, then the difference in their base-emitter voltages is
(kT /q) In r. Since Boltzman's constant K and the charge q of an electron are
'constants, the resulting voltage is directly proportional fo absolute temperature
T. In the referenced device, transistors Qy and Q,, produce the voltage, resistors
Ry and Ry convert it {0 a current, and Qyo (whose collector current tracks those
in Q, and Q,,) supplies all the bias and substrate leakage current for the rest of
the circuit, forcing the total current (Mowing between plus and minus power
supply terminals) to be proportional to temperature. Resistors Ry and Ry ace
laser-trimmed on the wafer to calibrate each device to give 1 wA/C at 25 deg C.
'The simplest application circuit would require only an unregulated supply of
410 30 V, « microammeter, and the device, all connected in series. (Figure 8.28
shows the insensitivity to supply-voltage drifts; between 5 and 15 V, the effect is
only 0.2 A/V) For such a circuit and a design range of ~ 55 to +155 deg C, the
sccuracy (including nonfineatity) of the medium-grade device (five aecuraey/price
trades are available) would be about +42 C. This basic device accuracy cam be
Improved by adding "trim" circuitry and calibrating at known. temperatures.
Figure 828d shows a simple one-temperature trim circuit (Which also gives a
voltage, rather than current output) that improves accuracy to + 1.5 deg C. The
twortemperature trim circuit of Fig. 828e (which also provides some amplii-
cation) gives the curve of Fig. §.28/ (accuracy ++0.5 C), (The ADS81 isa precision
reference voltage supply: AD3OIAL is an op-amp) Just as in other electric sen-
sors, self-heating effects are present and should be checked. For example, the
metal-can package at 25 deg C in a stirred oil bath has a 006 C rise, while instill air
this would be 0.72. Note that ifthe trim circuit techniques mentioned above
are employed with calibration heat-ransfer conditions the same as actual appli
cation conditions, self-heating causes no error

'The main advantages of junction semiconductor temperature sensors are
linearity, simple external circuitry, and good sensitivity. However, their upper
range is restricted to about 200 deg C by the damage limits of silicon transistors,
86 DIGITAL THERMOMETERS
{In most applications the nonlinearity of thermocouples and RTDs is suficently
{reat thal we must use calibration tables (or curve-At formulas derived from
them) to convert voltage oF resistance measurements to the corresponding
yeratures. When many measurements are to be made, this is inconvenient,
lime-consuming. and. prone to efror. Also, with thermocouples (TCS). the
reference-junction temperature must be accounted for, Because of these consider-
ations (which are not present in most other sensors), the fact that temperature is
industry's most measured quantity, and the popularity of TCs and RTDs (market
share in 1980; TCs, about $0 percent; RTDs, about 27 percent; thermistors,
about 23 percent; junction semiconductors, les than 5 percent; radiation pyrom-
'ters, less than 5 percent." special-purpose digital voltmeters called digital thermo-
meters have been developed and found a ready market.
'Thermometers for RTDs generally use four-wire ohmmeter (rather than
bridge) resistance measurement methods and provide the needed current source
excitation (two- and three-wite sensors can be accommodated, but at reduced
accuracy). Linearization methods for both TC and RTD thermometers can be
SMrictly analog (see Fig 10.54, nonmicroprocessor digital, or microprocessor digi-
{al (the most common). Simple units provide linearization for only a single type
of TC (or RTD), while microprocessor linearization techniques allow switch
selection from among six types and Fahrenheit or Celsius readout in more so-
Phisticated instruments. Reference-junction compensation in TC thermometers
usually uses an isothermal block whose temperature is monitored by a junction
semiconductor sensor, which sends these data to the same microprocessor that
does the linearization. Use of dual-slope analog/digital conversion for low noise
and high resolution (RTDs: 0.01" low range, 0.1" high range; TCs: 01 deg ) plus
'microprocessor computing lags, limits sampling rates to two oF three readings per
second, Instrument accuracies are consistent with the quoted resolutions; how=
'ver, accuracy of the actual temperature measurement will be considerably less
bbeeause of deviations of the individual TC or RTD from published tables and the
usual problems of sensing any physical variable precisely. Sharing of «single
digital thermometer among many TCs or RTDs is possible by using stanner
accessories. Since digital information is already available, interfacing to printers,
computers, etc, is also convenient,
"DM. Mackenve und W. Kehr. Review of Temperature Messrement Techniques nt
Teck. 43 4k September 1916, pp. 49 $4 November 978

{TMPERATORE AND HEAT-LUX MEASUREMENT 6ST
8:7 RADIATION METHODS'?
All the temperature-measuting methods discussed up to this point require that
the "thermometer" be brought into physical contact with the body whose tem=
perature is to be measured. Also, except for the pulsed thermocouple of Fig. 8.14,
the temperature sensor generally is intended to assume the same temperature as
the body being measured. This means that the thermometer must be capable of
withstanding this temperature, which in the case of very hot bodies presents real
problems, since the thermometer may actually melt at the high temperature
Fequired, Also, for bodies that are moving, a noncontacting means of temperature
sensing is most convenient. Furthermore, f we wish to determine the temperature
variations over the surface of an object. a noncontacting device can readily be
"scanned" over the surface. Radiation thermometers discussed in this section are
widely used in manufacturing control systems in many industries. Footnote 2
'below gives « particularly useful discussion of the selection and application of a
radiation theemometer for a computer-based system.
To solve problems of the type mentioned above, a variety of instruments
based in one way or another on the sensing of radiation have been devised. These
might, in general, be called radiometers; however, common usage employs terms
such as radiation pyrometer. radiation thermometer, optical pyrometer. ete. 10
"describe particular type of instrument. Since this terminology is mot standard
ized, you must inquire into the basic operating principle ofa given instrument 10
be sure what its characteristics are, eather than relying on the name given to the
instrument
Other important applications of infrared radiation include missile guidance,
sitllte attitude sensing, and infrared spectroscopy. In missile guidance (the Side=
winder missile is an outstanding example) the missile is designed to "home on
the infrared radiation emitted by the target, often the hot jet exhaust ofthe target
airerats engine. A scanning system in the missile locates the target and produces
'error signals that steer the missile into the target. For satelite attitude sensing
the infrared sensors are able to distinguish the radiation from the earth, the
'moon, of a planet from the background of space and thus generate accurate
"orientation signals for control purposes. Infrared spectroscopy® involves the use
of infrared principles for the analysis of gases, liguids, and solids to identify and
'determine the concentration of molecules or molecular groups. Given the thermo-
'clastic effec, radiation methods can be used for stress measurements in cyclically
Toaded materials *
"Vane "Pratl Appisins of tered Techniques" Wiley, Now Yor. 1972
£1. Rison aD. P Dewitt. Applications of Radation Thermsmetty ASTM TP 95.1985
SHC Holman: Mol! Temperate Measurement for Glut Prning Proce, ASTM STP 99S,
