Radiation Fundamentals,
Radiation-temperature sensors operate with electromagnetic radiation whose
wavelengths lie in the visible and infrared portions of the spectrum, The visible
spectrum is quite narrow: 03 to 0.72 ym. The infrared spectrum generaly is
defined as the range from 0.72 to about 1,000 gm. Bordering the visible spectrum
fon the low-wavelength side are the ultraviolet rays, while microwaves border the
infrared spectrum on the high side. Radiation-temperature sensing devices utilize
mainly some part of the range 0.3 to 40 ym
Physical bodies (solids, liquids, gases) may emit electromagnetic radiation or
subatomic particles for a number of reasons. As far as temperature sensing is
concerned, we need be concerned with only that part of the radiation caused
solely by temperature. Every body above absolute zero in temperature emits
radiation dependent on its temperature. The ideal thermal radiator is called a
blackbody. Such a body would absorb completely any radiation falling on it, and
for a given temperature, emit the maximum amount of thermal radiation pos-
sible. The law governing this ideal type of radiation is Planck's law, which states
that

The quantity 1; is the amount of radiation emitted from a flat surface into a
hemisphere, per unit wavelength, at the wavelength 2. Equation (815) thus gives
the distribution of radiant intensity with wavelength. That is, a blackbody at a
certain temperature emits some radiation per unit wavelength at every wave
length from zero to infinity, but not the same amount at each wavelength, Figure
8.304 shows the curves obtained from Eq (8.15) by fixing T at various values and
plotting W, versus i. The curves exhibit peaks at particular wavelengths, and the
peaks occur at longer wavelengths as the temperature decreases. The area under
cach curve is the total emitted power and increases rapidly with temperature,

While the concept ofa blackbody is a mathematical abstraction, real physical
bodies can be constructed t0 approximate closely blackbody behavior. Such
radiation sources are needed for calibration of radiation thermometers and gener-
ally take the form of a blackened conical cavity of about 15 deg cone angle. The
temperature is adjustable automatically controlled for constaney, and measured
bby some accurate sensor such as a platinum resistance thermometer. A typical
unit! covers the range S00 to 1000 K with 1 K accuracy and emittance
(0.99 4 001 (blackbody has 1.00). While itis possible to construct a nearly perfect
blackbody, the bodies whose temperatures are to be measured with some
radiation-type instrument often deviate considerably from such ideal conditions

the measured body:

properties such as densities, but rather depend on size shape, surface roughness,
angle of viewing, etc. This leads to uncertainties in the numerical values of

'measurement, Real-world objects are characterized by emittance ¢, reflectance r,

When r and/or ris not zero, we have nonblackbody behavior, and measurement
'errors as in Fig. 831a are possible. Commercial radiation thermometers usually
include an emittance adjustment with a range from about 02 to 10. Thus if
'material emittance is known, it can be compensated for. The most reliable tech-
nique for determining emittance for such purposes requires that at some time we
be able to measure specimen temperature (simultaneous with the eadiation
measurement) by some independent means, such as a thermocouple. The radi-
'ometer emittance dial is adjusted until the temperatures indicated by the two
instruments agree. If there are no changes in conditions, this emittance setting
fan be used for any future radiation temperature measurements without, of
'course, requiring the presence of the thermocouple. Since emittance ean vary with
temperature, such an emittance calibration may be necessary over the desired
temperature range. In such a case, a calibration in which the ¢ dial is left fixed at
itrary value, and a table of indicated versus true temperatures is devel-
'oped, might be more useful for correction purposes. One commercial instrument*
measures (and corrects for) target emittance by impinging a laser beam on the
target and measuring the fraction of energy reflected.
Plastic film manufacture? makes wide use of radiation temperature measure
ment and is a good example for emittance considerations based on Eq, (8.23.
Reflectance ris about 004 forall plastics in the infrared range and is independent
'of film thickness. Transmittance is given for cellulose acetate film (as measured
by a spectrophotometer) in Fig. £32. Note that in the range 8 ¢0 10 jam, film of
any thickness between 0.001 and 0.1 in behaves almost as a blackbody: thus we
'hou! design our radiometer for sensitivity to only this range.
'Another source of error is the losses of energy in transmitting the radiation
from the measured object to the radiation detector. Generally the optical path
consists of some gas (often atmospheric air) and various windows, lenses, of
'mirrors used to focus the radiation oF protect sensitive elements from the env
ronment. In atmospheric air the attenuation of radiation is due mainly to the
resonance-absorption bands of water vapor. carbon dioxide, and ozone as well as
the scattering effect of dust particles and water droplets. The combined absorp-
tion effect of HzO, COs, and O, is roughly as shown in Fig. 8.33. Since the
absorption varies with wavelength, a radiation thermometer can be designed to
respond only within one of the "windows" shown, thus making it insensitive to
these effects, Since the absorption varies with the thickness of the gas traversed

While infrared radiation follows the same optical laws used for lens and mirror

trisulfde, for example) are opaque to vsible-light wavelengths.
To "see around corners." span interfering atmospheres between target and
detector, and isolate system electronics feom hostile sensing environments, Nen-
ible fiber optic" cables are available in lengths to 10 m to transmit infrared
radiation from target to detector. Such systems work best at high temperatures
(using silicon detector) since fiber transmission is limited to the shorter wave.
lengths.
Radiation Detectors
In all radiation thermometers (other than the disappearing-filament optical py-
Fometer), the radiation from the measured body is focused on some sort of
radiation detector (se Fig. 8.38*) which produces an electric signal. Detectors may
be classified as thermal detectors or photon detectors. Thermal detectors are
blackened clements designed to absorb a maximum of the incoming radiation at
all wavelengths. The absorbed radiation causes the temperature of the detector to
ise until an equilibrium is reached with heat losses to the surroundings. Thermal
detectors usually measure this temperature, using a resistance thermometer, ther-
'istor, oF thermocouple (thermopile) principle. (Pyroelectric thermal detectors
'operate on a different principle, as discussed below)
Resistance-thermometer and thermistor elements are made in the form of
thin films oF flakes and are called holometers. Performance criteria for both
thermal and photon detectors include the time constant {most detectors behave
roughly as firstorder systems}, the responsivity (volts of signal per ws
dent radiation), and the noise-equivalent power, The noiseequivalent power
ives an indication of the smallest amount of radiation that can be detected,
'which is limited by the inherent electrical noise level ofthe detector. That is, with
'n0 radiation whatever coming in, the detector still puts out a small random
voltage due to various electrical noise sources within the detector itself The
'amount (watts) of incoming radiation required to produce a signal just equal in
strength to the noise (signal-to-noise ratio of 1) is called the noise-equivalent
power. Thus a low value of noise-equivalent power is desirable. An evaporated.
nickel-ilm bolometer of about 35 mm? area has a resistance of about 100 2 a
time constant of 0.005 s, responsivity of 0.4 V/W, and noise-equivalent power of
3 10 deg deg W. A thermistor bolometer of 0.5 mm# area might have 3-MO resis
ance, a time constant of 0.004 to 0.030 s, responsivity of 700 to 1.200 V/W, and
nnoise-equivalent power of 2x 10-' W. Thermopiles of an arca from O4 to
10 mm" have resistance of the order of 10 to 100 0, time constants in the range
"0. W. Usvecni and FG. Polack, High-Resolution Surface Temperature Mesaremens on
Rotating Turbine Blades with an tured Pycomeer, NAS TN DADS Te

'A class of thermal detectors of increasing importance since the mid-1960s is
the pyroelectrics, useful over the spectral range 0.001 (soft x-rays) to 1,000 jm (far
infrared). These devices can have a wide spectral response (similar to thermopiles
and bolometers), but do not suffer from the slow response speed characteristic of
'other thermal detectors. The basis of the improved speed lies inthe fact that the
electrical output depends on the time rate of change in detector temperature
rather than on the detector temperature itself. Materials that exhibit the pyroelec-
trie effect include lithium tantalate crystals, ceramic barium titanate, and poly
vinyl Ruoride plastic films." Figuee 8.35 shows spectral response for coated and
'uncoated lithium tantalate sensors? Black absorbing coatings, which increase
spectral uniformity and overall sensitivity, ae used often with infrared radiation;
however, they reduce speed of response by introducing a -10 dB decade roll
in amplitude ratio at high frequencies, with a typical breakpoint being at about
2000 Hz." When coatings are not employed, very fast response (rise times on the
'order of 170 ps}* are possible since the electrical output can be shown' to
respond to the instantancous volume-averaged detector temperature and thus
does not have to "wait" for the high surface temperature created by a fast

In Fig, 8364 a pyroelectric erystal is shown with thin metal film electrodes,
'one of which faces the radiation (edge-type electrodes also are possible). In some
ceases face electrodes, while sometimes thin enough to be essentially transparent
to the impinging radiation, may significantly effect spectral response and detector
speed. Since pyroelectric materials are dielectrics, the electrode/erystal sandwich
is a capacitor, in addition to being a radiation-sensitive charge generator. Pyro-
clectric materials exhibit a permanent clectric polarization (charge) which is sens
tive to temperature. Often a "poling" process which involves application of an
external electric field at room or elevated temperature to obtain maximum align
'ment of the materials domains is employed to develop the strongest effet. Py-
roelectric materials all exhibit a Curie temperature (610 deg C for lithium tantalate,
above which the effect is ost and repolarization would be required,

Since connection of any measuring circuit to the sensor electrodes allows a
flow of charge which ultimately neutralizes the polarization effect, the sensor,
unlike thermopiles and bolometers is not able to directly measure steady radi
ation (however, addition of a chopper system allows this capability). You may
'ote that this behavior is analogous to that of the piezoclctrc effect discussed in
Chap. 4: and, in fact, one of the earliest pyroelectric materials studied (barium
titanate} is employed also in piezooletric applications. Both pyroelectric and
piezoelectric behavior require the material to be ferroelectric; it must exhibit
permanent electrical polarization. This piezoelectric response can cause spurious
readings in pyroelectric detectors whose housings are subject to mechanical oF
'acoustical excitation and even for nonvibrating housings when rapidly changing
radiation input (such as short laser pulses) excites detector vibration,

Equations (8.28) and (8.32) clearly show that the current-mode electronics
'exhibits the same form of transfer function as the voltage-mode, although each
'mode can be designed with widely varying numerical values. For the values given
as typical above, Fig. 837 shows the frequency response (recall that an additional
~10 dB decade rollo would be present at high frequency if an absorbing coat-
ing were present), The voltage-mode system of this example shows no range of
fat response, although, of course, it could be obtained easily by changes in
'numerical values, Actually, a flat response is not always necessary or desirable.
For example, o obtain response to steady radiation (which neither voltage mode
nor current mode directly provides), a chopper is utilized to convert the steady
radiation flux to a periodic variation. Since the chopping is ata fixed frequency,
such an instrument need only have adequate amplitude ratio at that frequency,
but there is no requirement for fat amplitude ratio. Also, some applications
require measurement of the total energy of a radiation pulse. This requires inte-
'gration, with respect fo time, ofthe input signal W" (which is power), and this is
accomplished if the pulse-frequency content lies mainly in the downward-sloping
(20 aB/decade) region of the frequency response. Finally if an overall system
with flat amplitude ratio is desited in which the detector and preamp provide a
=20 dB/decade slope, then the preamp can be followed with a dynamic com-
pensator (approximate diflerentiator) to achieve this.

of electrical noise, always important in practical design studies. Useful data on
this area may be found in the literature

In the various types of photon detectors, the incoming radiation (photons)
frees electrons in the detector structure and produces measurable electrical
clfect. These events occur on an atomic or a molecular time seale rather than on
the gross time scale involved in the heating and cooling of thermal detectors.
Thus a much higher response speed is possible. However, photon detectors have
44 sensitivity that varies with wavelength; thus incoming radiation of different
wavelengths is not equally treated. Typical spectral response of some common
types is shown in Fig 8.38, Photon detectors commonly in use operate in the
pphotoconductive, photovoltaic, or photoelectromagnetic (PEM) modes.
Photoconductive types exhibit an electrical resistance that changes with the
incoming radiation level. Photovoltaic eells, also called harrier phorocells, employ
aa photosensitive barrier of high resistance, deposited between two layers of con-
'ducting material. A potential diffrence between these two layers is built up when
the cell is exposed to radiation, In photoclectromagnetic detectors the Hall effect
is utilized. A semiconductor crystal is subjected 10 a strong magnetic field, and
'radiation is applied to one side. A potential difference is developed across the
tends of the crystal. Lead sulfide photoconductve cells are the most frequently
used type by far, typical units of 1 to 35 mm? area having resistances of 10 deg 10
2 x 10 deg 0, time constants of 2 to 0.04 ms, responsivity of 5,000 to 150.000 V/W.
land noise-equivalent power of 4 x 10" to4 x 10 deg "? W. An indium antimonide
photoelectromagnetic cel of 100-0 resistance may have a time constant less than
1 ys, responsivity of | V/W. and noise-equivalent power of 10 deg W.

Some type of circuit must be employed to realize a usable electrical signal
(generally a voltage) from a radiation detector. Thermopile devices generally
'work with an uninterrupted stream of radiation and require no circuitry other
than the usual reference junction, whichis commonly left at ambient temperature.
Bolometers and photoconductive cells often employ a chopper to interrupt the
radiation at a fixed rate of the order of several hundred hertz. This leads to an
acctype electric signal and allows use of high-gain ac amplifiers. Typical circuits
for such arrangements are shown in Fig. 838.
'Unchopped (DC) Broadband Radiation Thermometers
We begin our study of complete radiation-sensing instruments by consideration
fof the most common ype used in day-to-day industrial applications." These
instruments employ a blackened thermopile as detector and focus the radiation
bby means of either lenses or mirrors. Figure 8.40 shows in simplified fashion the
construction ofthis class of instruments. The reference of footnote | gives very
complete analysis of such devices
Basically for a given source temperature T;, the incoming radiation heats the
'measuring junction until conduction, convection, and radiation losses just
balance the heat input. The measuring junction temperature usually is less than
40 C above its surroundings even ifthe source is incandescent. An oversimpliied

'number of junctions has less mass and thus faster response, bit lower sensitivity
limits application to high temperatures. Response is roughly that of a frstorder

2 s (low-temperature systems). Instruments of this cass are available to measure
{emperatures as low as 0'F; that is, actually the thermopile is cooler than the
ambient temperature and reads a negative voltage. Theoretically there is no
'upper limit to the temperatures that can be measured in this way. Commercial
instruments usable to 3200 deg F are readily availabe

'Conceivably, an instrument of the above type could be constructed with no
focusing means. ie, no lens or mirror. A simple diaphragm with a circular
apertute (Fig, $42) would define the target from which radiation is received. To
define smaller target areas for a given target distance, a smaller aperture could be
utilized; however, there would be a proportionate loss in incoming radiation and
thus sensitivity. The reading of such an instrument is independent of the distance
'between the target and the instrument, since the amount of radiation received is
limited by the solid angle of the cone defined by the aperture and detector, and
this is always the same, However, as the target distance inereases, the target area
necessary to fill the cone increases. If, Because of nonuniformity of target temper-
'ature or small target size, we wish to restrit the target atea to small values, &
very small ape quired, giving very low sensitivity
"The basic purpose of lens or mirror systems is to overcome this restriction,
thus allowing the fesolution of small targets without loss of sensitivity. Thus, in

Fig. 843, radiation emanating from the point P on the target is focused on the
corresponding point P' of the detector. If a simple diaphragm, rather than a
focused lens, had been used, the same amount of energy would be spread out over
the area 4,, with the detector receiving only a fraction ofthe radiation, Commer-
cial lens- oF mirror-iype instruments generally have parameters such that targets
2 ft or more distant are adequately focused with a fixed-focus lens, and the
instrument calibration is independent of distance as long as the target fils the
field of view. Minimum target size to fill the field of view is of the order of

ATWMPERATURE AND MEATHLUX MEASUREMENT. 675
'one-twentieth of the target distance for common instruments, For targets closer
than 2 f, focusing is necessary and may affect the calibration, depending on
instrument construction and closeness of target. Target diameters of 1 to 0.3 i
at target distances of 4 to 12 in are available. Since the focal length of a lens
'depends on the index of refraction, which in turn varies with wavelength, not a
wavelengths are focused at the same point. In particular, if one focuses a lens
visually (using visible light) the longer infrared wavelengths, which contribute @
large portion of the total energy at lower temperatures willbe out of focus. Such
an instrument may have to be focused by adjusting for maximum thermopile
'output rather than for sharpest visual definition. Another effect of lenses is selec-
Live transmission, as shown in Fig. £34. The use of mirrors rather than lenses is
aan attempt to alleviate some of these problems. However, instruments of both
types are employed widely with success,
Chopped (AC) Broadband Radiation Thermometers,
'A number of advantages acerue when the radiation coming from the target to the
detector is interrupted periodically (chopped) at a fixed frequency; therefore
many infrared systems employ this technique. When high sensitivity is needed,
amplification is required, and high-gain ac amplifiers are easier to construct than
their de counterparts. This is usually the main reason for using choppers. Ad-
ditional benefits related to ambient-temperature compensation and referer
source comparison also may be obtained. Systems employing thermal (broad:
band) and photon (restricted-band) detectors and choppers are in common use.
Here we consider those utilizing thermal detectors,
'The time constants of adequately sensitive thermopile detectors are generally
too long to allow efficient use of chopping: thus the faster bolometers, usually the
thermistor type, ate employed. We consider two specific forms of this class of
instruments: the blackened-chopper type and the mirror-chopper type. Figure
844 shows the basic elements of a blackened-chopper radiometer. A mirror
focuses the target radiation on the detector; however, this beam is interrupted
Periodically by the chopper rotating at constant speed. Thus the detector alter-
nately "sees" radiation from the target and radiation from the chopper's black-
ened surface. For high target temperatures, suffcefit accuracy may be achieved
by leaving the chopper temperature at ambient. Higher accuracy, particularly at
low target temperatures, is obtained by thermostatically controlling the chopper
temperature
'An arrangement similar to that of Fig. 839 is used for the detector circuit,
"The output voltage of the detector circuit is essentially as shown in Fig. 844, By
amplifying this in an ac amplifier, the mean value (which is subject to drift) is
discarded, and only the difference between the target and chopper radiation levels
is amplified. I the chopper radiation level is considered as a known reference
value, the target radiation and thus its temperature may be inferred. To provide a
de output signal related to target temperature and suitable for recording oF

'While the response time ofthe detector itself may be of the order of a milisecond,
the chopper frequency and necessary demodulator fiter time constant greatly
reduce the overall system speed. High chopper speeds allow faster overall re-
sponse, but reduce sensitivity ifthe detector time constant is too large, since the
detector does not have time to reach equilibrium during the time when either the
target or the chopper i in view.

In the mirror-chopper instrument? of Fig. 845, 1wo thermistor detectors are
utilized. Also, an accurate blackbody source whose temperature is controlled
automatically and measured accurately (say by a thermocouple) is provided
within the radiometer. A chopper operating at 77 Hz alternately exposes each of
the two detectors to the target radiation and the blackbody source of known
temperature. In Fig. 845 detector 1 is receiving target radiation while 2 is receiv
ing blackbody. When the chopper disk rotates 90 deg so that a sold sector isin the
line of sight, detector 1 receives blackbody radiation reflected from the rear
mirror surface of the chopper while detector 2 receives target radiation reflected
from the front surface. Circuitry similar to that of Fig. £44 can be employed to
develop a de output signal related to target temperature, Such a system can be
utilized for very accurate static measurements by using it in a null method of
operation. The blackbody source temperature is adjusted until no output is ob-
tained. Then the target and blackbody are at identical temperatures, provided the
target emittance is 10, Ifthe emittance is not 1.0 but is known, a correction may
be applied. Such a null method makes the reading independent of detector sensi
tivity (which may dri) and amplifier gain. A system of this type employing
ILS x 15 mm thermistor detectors has a 0.5 by O'S field of view (I-in® target at
10 f) and an overall system time constant of 0.016 s (bandwidth 10 Hz), and will
elect (signal-to-noise ratio is LO) a target temperature change of 04C. By
sacrificing response speed for sensitivity, heavier filtering can be switched in 10
give time constant of 0.16 or 1.6 , with corresponding increase of resolution to
0.14 and 0.04C". The instrument can be focused on targets from 4 f to infinity:

'While photon detectors are themselves selective with respect to wavelength,
the use of optical bandpass filters provides a further (and more controllable)

types of radiation thermometers are inherently nonlinear because of the T* rela-

the various detectors. The total instrument nonlinearity can be compensated
'electronically by either analog or digital computer means, and such linearization
is widely available
Automatic Null-Balance Radiation Thermometers
Figure 849 shows an interesting variation of the chopped, selective band type of
instrument. Here the rotating chopper exposes the detector alternately (150 Hz)
© nae nds Sata Barbara, Cl
> toa, ne Chica,

-- 713 of 1000 --

[Blank page or no OCR text detected]

-- 714 of 1000 --

{THMPERATURE AND HEAT-TLUX MEASUREMENT 63
to radiant lux from the target and from a feedback-controlled reference souree
[an infrared light-emitting diode (LED)), Thus the detector output signal is @
sauare wave whose mean value i the average of the two flux levels and whose
amplitude is their difference. The dilference signal is extracted and amplified by
fn ac amplifier (acting as a high-pass filten, and the amplitude of this ac error
signal is converted to a de level (the feedback-system error signal) by a syn-
cchronous rectifier (phase-sensitive demodulator). Whenever the reference-source
radiant fx is not equal to that from the measured target, the error signal drives
the reference Mux in the direction to null the error. By using suficiently high gain
in the feedback system, the reference Mux almost perfectly tracks that from the
measured target, and thus measurement of the LED drive signal is equivalent 10
measurement of the target lux. Such a system is largely unaffected by drifts in
detector sensitivity because the detector is located in the forward path of the
feedback system. The stability requirements, of course, are transferred from the
detector to the LED reference source, but careful selection and aging give excel
Tent stability here. The LED response is sufficiently fast that overall system
'dynamics is determined by the low-pass filter required in the demodulator, just as
in conventional (nonfeedback) chopper instruments. (Figure 8.49 also shows lin-
cearizing, peaksholding, and current output features which are not necessary for
'operation of the basic feedback principle)
Monochromatic-Brightness Radiation Thermometers
(Optical Pyrometers)
'The classical form of this type of instrument is the disappearing-filament optical
pyrometer. I is the most accurate of all the radiation thermometers: however, it
{s limited to temperatures greater than about 700'C since it requires a visual
brightness match by a human operator. This instrument is used to realize the
International Practical Temperature Scale above 1063 C
"Monochromatic-brightness thermometers utilize the principle that. ata given
wavelength 7 the radiant intensity ("brightness") varies with temperature as
aiven by Ea. (8.15), Ina disappearing-flament instrument (Fig, 8.50), an image of
the target Is superimposed on a heated tungsten filament. This tungsten lamp.
which is very stable, has been calibrated previously, so that when the current
through the filament is known, the brightness temperature of the filament is
know, (Such a calibration is obtained by visually comparing the brightness of a
blackbody source of known temperature with that of the tungsten lamp) A red
filter which passes only a narrow band of wavelengths around 0.65 ym is placed
between the observer' eye and the Wungsten lamp and target image. The observer
controls the lamp curtent until the filament disappears in the superimposed
target image. Then the brightnesses ofthe target and lamp are equal, and we can

'Two-Color Radiation Thermometers

schemes for alleviating this difficulty, Although no universal solution has been
found, the two-color concept* has met with some practical success. The basic
concept requires that W, be determined at two different wavelengths and then the
ratio of these two W's be taken as a measure of temperature. For the usual
conditions of practical application, the terms e""" are much greater than 1.0,

Blackbody-Tipped Fiber-Optic Radiation Thermometer
Earlier we mentioned the use of optical fibers to transmit radiation for temperature
'measurements, In this section a distinctly diferent use of fiber optics isthe basis
of @ unique, new type of radiation thermometer. Originally developed at NBS, it
is now commercially available? and is intended mainly for the range of 500 to
2000 deg C in hot gases such as in gas-turbine combustors. The sensing probe is made
from a single-crystal aluminum oxide (sapphire) optical fiber 0.25 to 2 mein diam-
ler and 5 to 100 em long. A cup-shaped blackbody cavity is formed at the sensing
tip by vacuum-sputtering an opaque 2-umn-thick film of platinum or iridium which
precisely defines the sensing region. The cup-shaped film may have a squared or
Angled end and use length/diameter ratios from about 2 to 80, with the larger
ratios more closely approximating blackbody conditions but the smaller ones
giving better spatial resolution. A protective sapphire film is also formed over the
'metal sensing film. When the probe is immersed in a hot gas, the sensing film
receives heat by convection, conduction, and radiation and its temperature rapidly
(due to large area and small mass) approaches the gas temperature. Just as in
'other probes, of course, conductive and radiative heat losses prevent the sensing
film from exactly reaching the gas temperature; however, the present probe exhibits
significant advantages since the conductivity of sapphite is 14 times smaller than
that of typical thermocouple metals and the fibers optical transparency also reduces
radiation errors, Whatever temperature the sensing film achieves, it acts asa black
body cavity, projecting radiation characteristic of that temperature along the
sapphire fiber toward a silicon photodiode or photomultiplier detector.
The sapphire fiber on which the sensing tip is formed can withstand the re-
quired high temperatures but is rigid and expensive, soit is optically coupled to
an inexpensive, flexible, low-temperature fiber to transmit the optical signal up to

Infrared Imaging Systems
Most of these systems have the function of providing a tclevisionlike visual
display in which the shades of gray represent various temperature levels of the
surface of some two-dimensional object (target) on which the infrared camera is
focused. While these shades of gray are accurately related to infrared energy
levels emitted fom the target, recall that all nfrared temperature sensing requires
Knowledge of the emittance of the target surface to convert the detector signal 10
degrees of temperature.

-- 720 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 713
Figure #83 Inared camera
In some recent systems, emissivity measurement and correction have been
'implemented by using computer-aided thermal-image storage and processing In
small-target applications such as microelectronic devices, emissivity measurement
fof each individual pixel (spatial resolution of 0.038 mm) has been achieved. The
procedure requires the device to be heated sequentially to two known low-level
Temperatures. (The device temperature here is spatially uniform since the devie's
clectric power is not applied.) Actualy, a "radiation reading" at one known tem-
perature is suficent to calculate emissivity. Two readings can improve statistical
Teliability and detect emissivity trends with temperature, which can be extrapolated
to the higher temperatures existing when the final measurement is made (and for
emissivity) corrected withthe device's power applied so as to reveal its temperature
'istribution under normal operation. By comparing images of each device as it
"comes off the production line" with computer-stored images of acceptable devices,
this technique provides a valuable nondestructive quality-control test
Figure 853 shows the arrangement of the camera for a unit of Swedish
manufacture? Scanning of the target surface is accomplished by focusing the
tadiation first on & plane mirror osilating about & horizontal axis at 16 Hz. This
Scans the line of sight vertically over the target surface. The image from the plane
mirror is focused on an eight-sded prism (rotating at 200 r/s) which provides the
horizontal scanning and results in a "picture" with a frame rate of 16 frames'
and 100 lines per picture. An indium antimonide (InSb) detector (cooled by liquid
nitrogen to reduce its noise level) produces an electrical signal proportional to the
incident radiant flux. To produce a thermal image ofthe target on a TY picture
tube, horizontal and vertical deflection signals are picked off the scanning-system

Produces an image composed of a raster of 64 horizontal lines at a tate of 1

Fluoroptic Temperature Measurement
See Figure 921
88 TEMPERATURE-MEASURING PROBLEMS.
FLOWING FLUIDS
In attempting to measure the static temperature of flowing fuids (particularly
high-speed gas flows), we encounter certain types of problems irrespective of the
Particular sensor being used. These have to do mainly with errors caused by heat
transfer between the probe and its environment and the problem of measuring
Static temperature ofa high-velocity flow with a stationary probe.
Conduction Error?
Let us consider the so-called conduction error first. Figure 8350 shows a
'common situation. A probe has been inserted into a duct or other flow passage
and is supported at a wall. In general the wall wil be hotter or colder than the
* Porolecne SeltScaming Infared Detector Artays, Appl. Note AN-IO, Spigon, nc. Logan,
ah 198
RP. Benedict, "Fundamentals of Temperature, Presure, and Flow Measurements" Wily,
'ew York, 1977. chap. 12: FD, Werner eal. Slam Conduction Eto ft Tempertare Senne
'ul 9622 Rosemount Inc. Mingcapas, Mine

-- 724 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 717
{TEMPERATURE AND HEATHLUX MEASUREMENT 693
"t
Figure 888 Probe configuration and condution-ror analysis
flowing fluid; thus there will be heat transfer, and this leads to a probe temper-
ature different from that of the fluid. We analyze a simplified model of this
Arrangement to find what measures can be taken to reduce and/or correct the
terror to be expected in such a cas. Figure 8.55b shows the simplified model to be

Probes wit some form or another of radiation shield are employed widely to
reduce radiation errors. Figure 837 shows a probe with single shied The
Principe ofall radiation shields is to imerpose between the probe and the wall a
body (the shield) whose temperature is loser tothe uid temperature than is the
wall Thus the probe "sees" the shield rather than the walland ithe temperature
Oo the shield is close to uid temperature, the probe radation-heat Tone wil be
small. For pure-raiation-heat transfer, it e489 to show that inerposing a single
sereen between a body and its surroundings will reduce the heat loss to one-half
the former value, since the screen comes to a temperature Ti,-- = (Tay +
Tnsading!2. For m sereens the heat loss is reduced t0 Iitn si} of the n-
sercond Value. Al these results are forthe simplest casein which the sereens and
surroundings completly enclose the body. For actual probe shields, various
gsometric and emittance factors complicate the situation. Also, additonal hea.
'ng of the shield by convection raise its temperature and reduces probe error
Experimenta tets' with concentric circular cylinder shields (Fig. 88%) have
shown the following:
1. A significant decrease in error may be achieved by adding more shields, at
least up to about four.
2. Little is gained by increasing the length/diameter ratio beyond 4:1 in ate
{tempting to reduce the unshielded angles at the open ends.
3. For multiple shields the spacing between shields must be sufficiently large to
prevent excessive conduction heat transfer between shields and to allow high
enough flow velocity for good convection from gas to shields. A double shield
with only h-in spacing acted almost as a single shield
To illustrate the need for shielding, an unshielded probe exposed to 1800 deg F gas
flow at 270 tbmft? min) may exhibit an error of I60F". A suitable quadruple
shield can ceduee ths to about 20F deg
" W. J. King Measurement of High Temperatures ia High-Velocy Gas Steams, Trans. ASME:

Original PDF Page 722
Another shielding technique employs an electrically heated shield with an
additional temperature sensor fastened to the shield. The heat input to the shield
is adjusted until the probe sensor and shield sensor register identical temp
es. At this point, probe and shield should both be at the fluid temperature,
'with the heat los from the shield to the cooler wall being replaced by the shield
heater.
Velocity Effects'
is often necessary to determine the static temperature of a lowing gas, since its
'physical properties depend on this temperature, To measure this temperature
irectly with a probe, however, requires that the probe be stationary with respect
to the Muid; thus i must be moving at the same velocity as the Aid. Since
Usually this is impractical, various indirect methods of measuring static temper-
tures are in use If we ean measure static pressure and density, sound velocity, oF
index of refraction, formulas allow calculation of static temperature. Experimen-
tal techniques based on each of these principles have been developed. However
for routine measurements a diferent approach is employed. This involves placing
4 stationary probe in the stream and calculating the static temperature from the
readings of this probe, by using suitable corrections. Ideally, if « perfect gas is

For bare thermocouple sensors, the numerical value of r usually lies in the
range 0.6 to 09, depending on the form of the junction (butt-welded, twisted, or
spherical bead) and the orientation (wire parallel to flow or transverse to flow)
'To gta high value of rand one relatively independent of flow conditions such as
velocity magnitude and direction, sensors (usually thermocouples or resistance
thermometers} are built into probes that have been specifically designed to ap-
proach ideal stagnation conditions. Figure 8.59 shows two examples! of such
probes. Desirable characteristics of probes include the following:
1. Low heat capacity in the sensing element fora fast response.
Conduction loss of lead wires minimized by exposing enough length of the
lead tothe stagnation temperature.
3, Radiation shield of low thermal conductivity and low surface emittance,
4. Vent holes provided to replenish continuously the fluid in the stagnation
chamber: otherwise, it would be cooled by conduction and radiation. This
flow must be kept small enough, however, that stagnation conditions are
essentially preserved, The increased convection coefficient caused by the flow
speeds the response and reduces the radiation error.

89 DYNAMIC RESPONSE OF TEMPERATURE SENSORS
Since the conversion from sensing-element temperature to thermal expansion,
thermoelectric voltage, or electrical resistance is essentially instantaneous, the
dynamic characteristics of temperature sensors are related to the heat-transfer
and -storage parameters that cause the sensing-element temperature to lag that of
the measured medium. When a fluid-immersed sensing element is used "bare"
(not in a protective wel), often the model of Fig. 861 is adequate (see Fig. 8.17
for sensors attached to solids). Here heat losses are neglected, resistance to heat
transfer is lumped in a single element, and energy storage is lumped in a single
element. Conservation of eneray gives

Dynamic response tests of temperature sensors im the laboratory are not
always accurate predictors of behavior when the sensor is installed in the process
nvironment. Response degradations may be present in new sensors (if im-
properly installed) or may develop over time as a result of cracking of insulating
ements or relocation of insulating powders used in probe construction, If actual
installed response is critical to process safety and/or performance, inst dynamic
testing can provide the desired confidence. For both thermocouple and RTD
'sensors the loop current step response (LCSR) technique! developed for nuclear
power plant testing provides an in situ test method ifthe sensor lead wires may
be disconnected for 1S to 60 min. The sensor circuit is subjected to a step increase
lor decrease) of current, causing a heating (or cooling) effect, whose response can
be analyzed to extract sensor time-constant information.
Dynamic Compensation of Temperature Sensors
When environmental conditions require a rugged temperature sensor, the mass
may be so high as to cause a sluggish response. It may be possible to obtain an
improved overall measuring-system response by cascading an appropriate dyna-
mic compensation device with the sensor. Such schemes have been applied in
practice with considerable success and may be implemented in a number of
'ways? An RC network such as that shown for hot-wire anemometer compensa-
tion in Chap. 7 may be used ifthe sensor is essentially first-order, Second-order
Iso may be compensated," operational-amplifier networks provide a
Convenient means of implementation. Since the compensation is correct only for
Specific sensor dynamics. changes in numerical values or form of transfer function
'Guused by changes in operating conditions can lead to loss of compensation. In
tener, increased speed of response is traded for overall sensitivity in such com-
[pensation schemes. This can be made up by additional amplification, but only up
to-a point: then the inherent noise level prevents further improvement. However,

'equilibrium temperature difference is rapidly established which is proportional to

Application Considerations
'The introduction of the sensor into the wall locally alters the thermal properties
'of the wall and causes the measured heat flux to differ from that which would
'occur if the sensor were not present. Thus it is desirable to match, insofar as
feasible, the thermal properties of sensor and wall. For a Gardon gage, there is a
radial temperature gradient over the disk which, if excessive, causes a variation in
local convection coefficient and thus an error. By sacrificing sensitivity (and then
recovering it by external amplication, if necessary) the temperature gradient and
associated error may be reduced. When only the radiation component ofthe total
flux is desired, the front of the sensor is covered with a thermally isolated sap
phire window which passes the radiation lux but blocks the convective flux

9.1 TIME, FREQUENCY, AND PHASE-ANGLE,
MEASUREMENT.
'The fundamental standaed of time is discussed in See. 4.2. The United States
Frequency Standard is a cesium-beam resonator whose precision is of the order
'of | part in 10", By radio-broadcasting signals related to the frequency of the
standatd, the National Bureau of Standards makes these frequency and time
Mandards available 10 any other laboratory equipped to receive the signal,
Stations WWVB and WWVL broadcast signals whose precision as received is
about I part in 10! Time-code signals are also available from the GOES
satellites"
Perhaps the most convenient and widely utilized instrument for accurate
Imeasurement of frequency and time interval is the electronic counter-timer

n a temperature-controlled oven. A typical frequency is 10" Hz while the di

drift can cause errors; thus highly accurate measurements require periodic recali-

signal whose frequency is 6:843,169 Hz. This is accomplished by allowing the

Sometimes it is more desirable to measute the period (rather than the fre-
'queney) of a signal, Figure 9.1h shows the arrangement used for this measure
ment. The trigger-level control is adjusted so that triggering occurs on the steep-
'st part of the signal waveform to reduce error. There is usually provision for
triggering on cither a positive slope or a negative slope. as desired. Since there is
'am inherent potential error of 41 count in turning the gate on and off requency-
mode metsurements are more accurate for high-frequency signals. whereas
period-moxle measurements are more accurate for low-frequency signals. For
'example, a 10-H signal measured in the frequency mode with the usual 1-s time
interval gives only 10 counts: thus an error of 4 1 count is a 10 percent error.
The same signal measured in the period mode with a 10-MHz counter gives 10 deg
'counts and an error of only 00001 percent. Thus for a given counter there is
some frequency below which period measurements should be employed and
above which feequency measurements should be utilized. For a 1-s sampling
period, this frequency f, is given by
fon Jie oy
where £4 frequency of crystal oscillator ("elock? frequency). Thus a 10-MHz
lock-frequeney counter has fy = 3.160 Hy.
"Meastiements ofthe time interval between {wo events are very important in
many experimental studies. The basic building blocks described above can be
interconnected in slightly different fashion, as in Fig. 9.1¢ and d, to accomplish
this. In Fig. 9.c two separate events have been transduced to electrical pulses
'one event pulse is used to open the gate, and the other to close i, thereby timing
the interval between them, Considerable versatility in triggering is obtained by
providing trigger-level and slope controls on each input. By using the above
rangement but only one input signal (Fig. 9.14), the widths of pulses may be
determined. Sometimes additional cicuits are provided to send to an oscilloscope
pulses that show the exact point on the incoming signals at which triggering is
initiated. These are helpful in adjusting the trigger-level and slope controls and in
interpreting the resulting information,
'Often measurement of the phase angle between two sinusoidal signals of the
same Frequency J, is required, A general-purpose digital counter-timer ean be
'employed for such measurements, as shown in Fig. 9. To use this method, the
amplitude of the two signals must be made equal and the triggering point of the
two channels adjusted to be the same, Then the phase angle ean be read directly
with a resolution of 1 for the setup shown, oF 0.1" ifthe reference frequency is set
a0 3.600 f,
To prevent false counts as a result of the unavoidable noise on input signals,
'counter input citcuits use a Schmitt trigger type of circuit with a built-in hyster=
xis effect (see Fig. 9.2). When frequency is measured, to cause one count in the
'counting register, a signal must cross horh the upper and the lower hysteresis

-- 749 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 742
levels. [For period or time-interval measurement the gate is opened (or closed)
when the signal crosses a selected hysteresis level (ether upper or lower)] The
difference between the two hysteresis evel is called the counter sensitivity, and
{ypically this might be 150 mV peak to peak. This is really threshold sensitivity
since it fixes the amplitude of the smallest countable signal. But note that the
'optimum value is not necessarily the lowest, because of the false triggering pro-
blem mentioned earlier. The major error sources! in counter-timers are categor-
ined as:
1. The +1 count ambiguity, a random error
2. The time-base (clock) error, resulting from temperature and line-voltage
changes, aging, and short-term instability
3 The trigger error. a random error due to noise that causes early oF late
'rossing of the hysteresis level
4. Channel-mismatch error, a systematic error present in two-channel measure-
'ments when input-circuit rise times and/or propagation delays are not ident-
ical for the two channels,
Frequency measurements are subject only to the +1 count and time-base
errors. Period measurements have these plus the trigger ertor, while time-interval
'measurements suffer from all four. A high-performance instrument utilized within
| week oftime-base calibration and within the usual lin-voltage (10 percent) and
temperature (0 to 50 deg C) limits might have a time-base error of 1 part in 10"
CChannel-mismatch error might be {ns and could be reduced by calibration of
this effect. Analysis? ofthe tigger error gives the standard deviation ofthe timing.

ertors is very important, since total accuracy may turn out to be muck less than
resolution. This total error includes not only the contributions of the counter, but

'While the description of Fig, 9.1 explains the operation of most basic
'counter-timers, much more sophisticated instruments that employ micropro-
'cessor technology to extend capabilities, automate procedures, and provide caleu-
lating features are available The referenced instrument stil uses a 10-MHz
crystal clock as the basic timing source: however, an interpolation scheme in-
treases time resolution from the 100 ns of the clock itself to about 1 ns for the
instrument and removes the +1 count uncertainty always present in "ordinary"
'counters, Automatic trigger-stting citcuits allow implementation of many useful
Features, such as 10 t0 90 percent ise- (or fll) time measurements and slew-rate
measurements. Rise time is found by setting trigger levels at the 10 and 90 per-
tent levels (this is automatic) and making a time-interval measurement, which is
Stored in a temporary register. Then the two trigger levels are measured auto-
matically, and the mieroprocesser subtracts these and divides by the rise time to
'obtain and display slew rate in volts per second. Many other automatic features
Such as nonambiguous phase-angle measurement and various statistical caleulat-
ions may be selected by push-button.

92 LIQUID LEVEL
Measurement and/or control of liquid level in tanks is an important function in
'many industrial processes and in more exotic applications, such as the operation
and Tucling of large liquid'fuel rocket motors. Figure 94 illustrates some of the
'more common methods of accomplishing this measurement.
The simple float of Fig. 9.ta can be coupled to some suitable motion trans-
ducer to produce an electrical signal proportional to the liquid level. Figure 9.45
shows a "displacer" which has negligible motion and measures the liquid level in
{erms of buoyant force by means ofa force transducer. Since hydrostatic pressure
is related directly to liquid level, the pressure-sensing schemes of Fig. 9c and d
allow measurement of the liquid level in open and pressure vessels, respectively.
In the "bubbler" or purge system of Fig 9.4, the gas pressure downstream of the
flow restriction is the same as the hydrostatic head above the bubble-tube end.

'The flow of gas is quite small; a bottle of nitrogen used as a source of pressurized
'Bas may last six months or more,
Capacitance variation has been employed in various ways for level sensing.
For essentially nonconducting liquids (conductivity less than 01 yS/cm'), the
'bare-probe arrangement of Fig. 94 may be satisfactory since the liquid resistance
A is sufficiently high. For conductive liquids the probe must be insulated as in
Fig. 94g, to prevent short-circuiting of the capacitance by the liquid resistance
'The measurement of the capacitance between terminals ab may be accomplished
in several ways. However, high-frequency ac (radio-requency) methods offer sig-
nificant advantages. Capacitance leve-sensing techniques have been used with
'many common liquids, powdered or granular solids, liquid metals (high temper.
atures), liquefied gases (low temperatures}, corrosive materials such as hydro-
fluorie acid, and in very-high-pressure processes.
Figure 9.4 illustrates the use of radioisotopes for level measurement. Since
the absorption of beta-ray or gamma-ray radiation varies with the thickness of
absorbing material between the source and the detector, a signal related to tank
level may be developed. For analyzing such arrangements we may use the law.

This exponential relation of 1 and sh is nearly linear only for sufficiently small
values of h. For h as large as, say, 100 cm, the nonlinearity is quite apparent. This
can be overcome by using ether a radiation source of a detector in the form of &
Strip oriented vertically rather than a "point" source or detector. Such atrange-
ments are nonlinear for small values of sp%qaq. Therefore point-to-point con.
figurations are indicated for small ranges, whereas larger ranges require the more
'complex stripto-point type. For a strip source (or detector), the strength (sensit
vity Tor a detector) can be "tailored" to vary in just the right way with position
along the strip to give a linear tank-level/detector-signal relation,
Figure 944 shows the method of using hot-wire or carbon resistor elements
for the measurement of liquid level in discrete increments. The basic voncept is
that the heat-transfer coefficient at the sutface ofthe resistance element changes
radically when the liquid surface passes i. This changes its equilibrium temper-
'ture and thus its resistance, causing a change in bridge output voltage. By
locating resistance elements at known height intervals, the tank level may be
measured in discrete increments, Such arrangements have been used in filing fuel
tanks of large rocket engines with eryogenic liquid fuels.
Ultrasonic "range-finding" techniques, discussed in Chap. 4, ean be applied
to liquid-evel sensing, as in Fig. 94
An interesting variation on the classic Moat of Fig, 94a is found in the
servopositioned level gage of Fig. 94k," widely employed in deep tanks and
'underground caverns to S00-f depth. The thin displacer has density (relative to
liquid in tank) such that it would sink if it were not supported by the measuring,
wire, which applics just enough liting force to support the displacer at a design
immersion of | to 2 mm. The lifting force is maintained at this value (as the tank
level rises and falls) by the servodrive, whose error signal comes from the elastic
foree sensor which deflects from neutral in proportion to the difference between
" Ena Nona Service Corp, Bohemia, NY.

-- 756 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 749

lifting force and the design value set by adjustment of the balance springs. A
capacitance displacement pickofl transduces elastic deflection to an electrical
terror signal for the servomotor, Competing (nonservo) gages use a mechanical
constant-force" spring to supply the lifting force, being otherwise similar except
that displacer immersion is much larger, typically 10 mm. These simpler and
cheaper allemechanical devices suffer, however, from several error sources which
fare minimized in the servotype gage: increased friction, variation in constant-
force spring lik, grcater sensitivity to liquid-density change because of greater
immersion, and greater cable-weight effect as a result of using a heavier tape
{rather than a light wire) to suspend the displacer. Comparison of overall error
for a 20-maleep tank shows +135 mm for mechanical gages and +29 mm for
servogages. Since | mm in a 100-{-diameter tank is 46 bbl of product (at, say,
'S40 per Barre) the economic importance of accuracy in a system used for cus-
tody transfer of product becomes obvious
Hydrostatic tank gaging (HG), an advanced version of Fig. 9c and d, is

total contained mass ate required. Figure 9.4 shows a typical system using three
vibrating-wire digital") pressure sensors and a platinum resistance (RTD) tem-

93 HUMIDITY
Knowledge of the amount of water vapor in the air is important to the operation
'and/or automatic control of many industrial processes. This information may be
fithered and presented in a number of ways, depending on the needs of the
*D, lume and J. Myers, Smart Dy
InTech, pp 45-48, December 1986.
SC Rouimon, Hyarotn Tank Gaping. Inock pp 37-0, Fbraary 1988
2 Pro. Spec PSS 4A? A, Forboro Co. Foxboro, Mas.
PR Wiederhold Humity Measurement, Inst Teck, pp. 31-3, June 1975 pp. 45-50, August

-- 757 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 750
76 weasunine eviess
particular process and the measuring instrumentation utilized. In common use
are the relative humidity (ratio of water partial pressure to saturation pressure)
<dew-point temperature, mixing ratio or specific humidity (mass of water per unit
'mass of dry gas) and volume ratio (parts of water vapor per million pars of ain)
The ultimate standard for calibration of humidity-measuring devices is the
National Bureau of Standards standard gravimeteic hygrometer, This isa strictly
laboratory apparatus in which the water vapor in an air sample is absorbed by
suitable chemicals and then weighed very carefully. It directly determines the
'mixing ratio in grams per kilogram, covers the range 0.30 to 200, and has an
'uncertainty (systematic ror plus 3 standard deviations) of about 0.1 percent of
the reading. For less critical calibrations, the National Bureau of Standards uses
its two-pressure humidity generator.* This equipment generates air/water mix-
tures in the dew-point range ~70 C (uncertainty £1.2C) to 425 deg C (£010),
Felativeshumidity range 10 to 98 percent at temperatures ranging from - 55 deg C
(clative-humidity uncertainty 42.5 percent) to. +40 deg C (relative-humidity uncer
{tainty OS percent), mixing ratio in the range 0.0013 g/kg. (uncertainty
440.0003 g/kg) to 20 g/kg (uncertainty +0.5 percent), and volume ratio 2 ppm
(£055 ppem to 30,000 ppm (40.5 percent)?
Classically, relative humidity has been found from psychrometric charts and
the temperature readings of two thermometers. One, the dry-bulb thermometer,
reads the ordinary air temperature; the other, the wet-bulb, is intended to read
the temperature of adiabatic saturation. The later measurement requires that the
bulb be kept wet and a suitably high (about 1,000 fY/min) aie velocity be main-
tained over the wet bulb. Formulas are available? for calculating the relative
humidity from measured values of the wet- and dry-bulb temperatures and absolute
pressure.
For continuous recording and/or control of relative humidity, electrical
'transducers of the Dunmore type are employed widely. These were fist devel-
'oped about 1944 by F. W. Dunmore of the National Bureau of Standards and
are basically a resistance element that changes resistance with relative humidity
'The resistance element is constructed of a dual winding of noble-metal wires on &
plastic form with a definite spacing between them. When the windings are coated
with a lithium chloride solution, a conducting path is formed between the wind-
ings. The electrical resistance ofthis path is found to vary reproducibly with the
relative humidity of the surrounding air and thus may be used as a sensing
clement. Bridge-type resistance-measuring circuitry with ae excitation is em-
ployed normally. The resistance /relative-humidity relation is quite nonlinear, and
generally a single transducer can cover only a small range of the order of
10 percent relative humidity. Where large ranges (as great as 5 to 99 percent
relative humidity) are needed, seven or eight ofthe transducers, each designed for
"A. Wealer and R.D. Danes, Presure-Humiity Apparatus, J. Res. Na Bw Sd, vol
928, 982
* Humidity Carton Servi, Insram. Con. Spt 123, November 1964
> CRSHRI Hy-Cal Enginering 1 Monte Cale

-- 758 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 751
scHLLAMous MEASUREMENTS 727
48 specific part of the total range, are combined in a single package. A single
narrow-range sensing clement may have an inaceuracy ofthe order of 1S perce
relative humidity, resolution about 0.15 percent relative humidity, time constant
as small as 3's, and size 1 in in diameter by 2 in long. Since these units are
sensitive to temperature also, some form of temperature compensation may be
required. The sensors do not add or subtract moisture or heat from their environ-
ment in significant amounts and so may be utilized in sealed areas. Working
temperatures in the range of ~40 to + 150F are possible.
'Another electrical resistance type of sensor, the sulfonated polystyrene ion=
'exchange device called the Pope cel" exhibits a nonlinear resistance change from
4 few megohms at 0 percent relative humidity to about 1,000 2 at 100 percent,
and a single sensor can cover the entire range. Accuracy is comparable to that of
the Dunmore sensor. Proprietary semiconductor sensors. highly porous at a
molecular level, allow molecules of water vapor to drift freely in and out of the
structure, changing device impedance and resistance when water dipoles interact
with the lattice. single sensor (and associated electronics) temperature-
'compensated from 0 to 50 C covers the range 0 10 100 percent relative humidity
with a lincarized output and 4 percent accuracy. Thin-ilm capacitance
sensors?* have recently become quite popular
Dew-point temperature can be determined by noting the temperature of a
polished metal surface (mirror) when the fist traces of condensation ("fogging")
appear. Commercial devices are available in which this operation has been com-
pletely automated by means of a feedback system. The mirror is cooled thermo-
clectrically (Peltier cooling), with the coolers electrical input coming from an
amplifier whose input is the error signal of the feedback system. Lightemitting
'diodes (LEDs) provide light to the mirror surface, where itis reflected to photo-
transistors that generate a feedback signal which is compared with a set reference
voltage to generate the system error signal. The reference voltage is set ata value
that just barely maintains condensation (fogging) on the mirror. Any deviation
from this level of fogging causes an inerease (or a decrease) in light reflected to
the phototransistors and & proportional change in the cooling effort. Thus the
mirror surface is always kept at the dew-point temperature, which is sensed with
an embedded platinum RTD. For dew points below 0 deg C. the system tracks the
frost point. Figure 9.5 deg shows a microprocessor-controlled version of this instru
ment which uses a dual-mirror system to correct continuously for mirror con-
'amination, which causes errors and requires periodic shutdown and/or cleaning
in systems without this feature, The compensating mirror is controlled to slay

"dry" at a precise temperature differential above the "wet? mirror, and both
mirrors experience essentially the same contamination; so the dry mirror can be
employed as a reference channel (phototransistor changes also are corrected by
this scheme). Dew-point accuracy of +0.2 deg C over the range -75 to +60 deg C is
achieved. Addition of ambient temperature and/or pressure sensors allows micto=
processor calculation of relative humidity, parts per million, grains, or pounds.
'The lithium chloride sensor mentioned above under relative humidity can be
'modified to give a signal related to dew-point temperature.' The dual wire wind-
ings are supplied with ac power, causing a heating of the lithium chloride film
Lithium chloride shows a very sharp decrease in electrical resistance when its
relative humidity increases above about 11 percent. Thus, when surrounded by
'moist air, the lithium chloride momentarily absorbs moisture, and its resistance
<rops, allowing more current to flow and more heat to be generated. This raises
the temperature, driving off excess moisture, increasing the resistance, and re-
ducing the heating. Thus the sensor regulates its own temperatuee, so that the
relative humidity of the lithium chloride element stays near II percent no matter
what the moisture content of the surrounding aie is. It has been established that
the temperature attained by the lithium chloride element, while not equal to the
'dew-point temperature, is directly related to it. Thus by measuring this temper-
ature with some appropriate sensor. the dew-point temperature may be es-
tablished. Probes ofthis type cover a dew-point range of ~ 50 to + 160 deg F with an
error ofthe order of 1 oF 2F
'The final humidity sensor considered here is the electrolytic type. Here a
continuous flow of sample gas (typically 100 cm/min, regulated +2 percent is
passed through an analyzer tube. This tube has two platinum wires wound in a
"£1 Amd, Humidity Sensor. rm. Cont. Syst p. 9, Fane 1963.

double helt on the inside ofthe tube. The space between the wires is coated with
'a strong desiccant (phosphorous pentoxide), and a de potential is applied to the
Wire ends. When moisture in the sample gas is taken up by the P.O. the water is
lectrolyzed into hydrogen and oxygen gas and a measurable electrolyte current
ows. Such instruments have an inaccuracy ofthe order of S percent of full scale,
typical range 0 to 2.000 ppm, resolution better than 1 ppm, and a time constant
of the order of 30 s. They also have been adapted to the measurement of the
water content of liquids and solids.
9.4 CHEMICAL COMPOSITION
In years past, the chemical composition of materials was necessarily determined
by taking a sample to a laboratory and performing the required chemical tests,
usually with somewhat tedious procedures. Today, many important measure-
'ments of this type are made on a relatively continuous and automatic basis
without the need for a human operator. The need for such messuring systems is
dduc largely to the desire to automatically control produet quality directly in
terms ofits chemical composition, rather than infrsing it from measurements of
temperature, pressure, flow rate, ete. Even in manually controlled situations, the
desige to increase production rates while also maintaining or improving quality
leads to a need for rapid analysis methods. Rapid and accurate analysis tech-
higues are very useful in research and development problems. These needs of
industry have led to the development of a wide variety of instruments for measur-
ing various aspects of chemical composition and related quantities.
Some examples of measurements of the above type include analysis of prod
ucts of combustion, monitoring of the composition of dissolved gases in ol-wel
driling mud, detection of alcohol contaminant in a heavy-hydrocarbon liquid
Stream, detection of explosive solvents in the atmosphere within a uranium=
extraction Kettle, measurement of pH of industrial-waste effluent to control river
pollution, determination of alloying constituents in metals, outgassing of materi-
'als under high vacuum, use of rocketborne instruments for analyzing atmospheric
eases at high altitudes, air-pollution studies, and analysis of anesthetic gases in
blood. Most of the analyzers used for such measurements are rather complex
systems, rather than simple sensors, and a discussion of these methods adequate
for their selection and use is beyond the scope of this text. The references? serve
this purpose for the interested reader.
'There are, however, some simple sensors which do perform useful "chemical
analysis." and now we describe one such from the important area of combustion
control, Both industrial furnaces and automotive engine-control systems make

98 PARTICLE INSTRUMENTS AND
CLEAN-ROOM TECHNOLOGY
Many modern manufacturing processes require extremely lean operating environ-
'ments, with the manufacture of electronic integrated circuits a prime example of
great economic significance. Airborne "drt" particles cause much of the yield loss
in these processes. As miniaturization trends continue to reduce the minimum fea-
ture size used in these circuits, smaller and smaller particles can cause failure, and
so they become of practical interest to process designers and operators (see Fig.
9.7"). Particles can cause defects in many ways-by bridging over two adjacent
conductors (short-circuit), by masking of the film during an etching process (open
Circuit, et. In this section we briefly survey the means for reducing particle con-
centrations ("clean-room technology") and the measurement techniques for docu
menting the sizes and/or concentrations of particles that remain ("particle instru-
ments"). Particle instruments, of course, also find important applications outside
the clean-room field, such as atmospheric air quality, medical research, smoke/soot
studies of combustion, cloud physics, seeding studies for laser velocimetry. ete. In
addition to their measurement function, particle "instruments" are needed to pro-
'duce particles of controllable size and concentration for use in instrument testing
'and calibration?
Figure 9.8? relates various sources of airborne contaminants to particle size.
Two essential features of clean-room technology are HEPA (high-efficiency parti-
culate ait) filters and the laminar-airflow concept (see Fig. 994) Even in 1950
HEPA filters which removed 99.97 percent of all particles 0.3 yom and larger were
available; more recently 99999 percent removal for 0.12-ym particles has been
achieved. Since laminar (as compared to turbulent) fuid flow provides a more
controllable and predictable flow path, clean rooms use a laminar air flow, from
ceiling to floor, to wash away the contaminants generated by the manufacturing

wherein the particle travels an irregular path, with its postion at any time de-
pending on its most recent collisions. Due to their lower momentum, small particles
are affected more by such collisions than large ones, and particles larger than
about 0.3 ym are nearly unaffected. When particles travel through a fine metal
screen (typically 20-um-diameter stainless-steel wires with 20-um openings), smaller
particles will collide with and stick to the wires because of surface attractive forces,
while larger particles will continue lowing with the gas in which they are entrained.
In the difusion battery shown, the aerosol (entraining gas plus particles) is moved
through the device at about 4 L/min. The stages are separated by groups of screens,
the number of screens in a group increasing by 1 for each stage as the aerosol
progresses from the first (ine-particle) stage to the last (coarsest particles). The
first screen group has only 1 screen, the last has 10, for a total of 5S screens. An
stage selector valve is used to connect a particle counter to the original, un-
classified aerosol or any one of the 10 sizeclasified stages. By using calibration
data as in Fig 9.146, counter measurements such as in Fig. 9.14¢ can be processed
to yield the useful results of Fig. 9.144.
'A commercial condensation nucleus-particle counter is shown in Fig. 9.15."

'Japan has adopted a law limiting the allowable concentration of airborne
particles smaller than 10 um to less than 150 g/m? within most occupied b
larger than 3000 m?, Measurements of such total-mass concentr con
veniently made with another type of particle instrument, the piezobalance' of Fig.
9.16. Here the aerosol stream first impinges onan impactor? which collects particles
larger than 10 jam while smaller particles pass on to an electrostatic precipitator.
Inthe precipitator, particles are first electrically charged and then attracted to the
surface of a vibrating piezoelectric crystal, where they accumulate, gradually in-
creasing the mass and lowering the frequency of crystal oscillation. (The crystal
'vibration is controlled by an intentionally unstable feedback system in a manner
identical to that of the vibrating U-tube densitometer.) Mass concentrations of
(0.005 to 9999 mgm? for particles from 0.01 to 10 um are measured with a re
solution of 0.001 mg/m? and an accuracy of +10 percent of reading, +001 mg/m.

For "continuous" operation, the impactor and erystal surfaces must be periodically

wafers." Here the measuring crystal is placed in the same deposition chamber as
the wafers being processed and thus accumulates on its surface a film of "identical"
thickness at an "identical" rate, The crystals cost only about $5.00 each and ean
thus be replaced for each new batch of wafers. The accuracy of rate and thickness
'measurement is about 0 percent of reading.
'Our final instrument is not a measuring device but rather an aerosol generator,
for producing aerosols containing particles of controllable size and concentration
'OF the types listed in Fig. 9.13, we choose to give some details on the eibrating-
orice monodisperse® generator. (Polydisperse generators produce aerosols with &
wide range of particle sizes whereas monodisperse generators are intended to give
particles of mainly one size) A cylindrical liquid jet passing through an orifice
(typically of 10- to 20-um diameter) is inherently unstable and will break up into
droplets of nonuniform size. By vibrating the orifice at a fixed high frequency
(10 kHz to 1 MHz) it has been found that droplets of uniform size are produced,
'one droplet for each cyete of vibration, with the droplet volume given by Q/f.
where Q is the volumetric orifice flow rate and f is the vibration frequency. For
'example, a liquid feed rate of 0.14 em'/min with a 60-kHz vibration frequency (a
Piezoelectric crystal is used) produces 60,000 droplets/s with a mean diameter of
about 42 um and standard deviation of about | petcent for an 18-um orifice. When
sold-particle aerosols are wanted (assuming the solid can be dissolved in a suitable
volatile liquid), droplets of solution are formed as described above and injected into
«turbulent ar jet to prevent coagulation. Then mixing with a high volume of
'causes evaporation ofthe solvent, leaving the solid particles desired. A Krypton-85
radioactive source is sometimes used to ionize the aerosol gas, discharging any
clectrostatic charge picked up by the particles and thus keeping them from clinging
to apparatus walls

9.7 FIBER-OPTIC SENSORS
'Optical fiber technology, originally developed for data-transmission applications
(and still used mainly for that purpose, is finding increasing use in sensing. An
'optical fiber is a thin (0.002-in diameter) strand of material composed of two
layers, an inner core and an outer cladding, with the core material having a higher
index of refraction. By the principle of total internal reflection, this causes light
introduced to the core at one end to propagate with low loss to the other end by
reflection atthe core/cladding interface. Some fiber types achieve this propagation
without relying on cladding, by producing a "core" with a radial gradient in
refractive inden.
Optical signals carrying information related to many different physical vari-
ables can be produced by using fibers in many different ways." The simpler methods
use various forms of intensity modulation of light (attenuation, scattering, grating
plates, frustrated internal reflection, impurity diffusion, cladding modification, fuo-
rescence, or radiation loss) while phase-modulation methods employ some type of
interferometric approach (Mach-Zender, Michelson, Sagnac, polarimeter, ring r=
sonator, Fabry Perot, ot mode-miode). Four types of fiber are common: monomode,
'multimode, birefringent, and coated. Monomode fiber is required in interferometric
21S Medlock, Review of Modulating Techniques for Fer Optic Sensors. Meas & Convo
19, pp 4-17, February 1986 RP. DePaula and FL. Moore. Onerew of Fite Opel Sensor
NASH Took Brivo Tomo 7 Jaly/Apot 197, JPL Tne, Rept MPO. 168174309,

-- 775 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 768
44 seasunine evices
ont
eeure |
cae
Figure 920 Presre randacer using micrbend daplacement mor
methods since coherence of the light must be maintained, whereas intensity-
'modulating schemes can use multimode fiber. Polarizing techniques use birefringent
fiber, while coated fiber offers the opportunity for compound modulation, wherein
the coating provides a primary sensing effect (such as magnetic field sensing with
'8 magnetostrictive coating) which in turn modulates an optical signal inthe fiber.
Since fiber-optic transducers as yet hold only a small percentage share of the
market for sensing any particular variable, we limit our treatment toa few specific
'examples, referring the reader to the literature for more details. Since displacement
sensing is useful also for foree, pressure, and acceleration transducers, fist we
describe the microbend displacement sensor' (see Fig. 9.20). This NASA-sponsored
'esearch was aimed at developing nonmetallic pressure sensors for high-tempera-
ture applications in aircraft, and several fiber-optic techniques were studied in
addition to the microbend sensor described here. When a fiber is deformed into
convoluted shape, some of the light is lost to radiation and the loss in pro-
;pagated light can be sensed with conventional optical detectors. Theory predicts
this loss to be greatest when the convolutions have a spacing given by

where Af is the difference in propagation constants between propagating and
radiation modes, For the aluminum-coated multimode fiber used (60-um core
radius), the optimum spacing was about 3 mm, and this was verified by experiment.
'The pressure diaphragms were designed to deflect 2S yum at the full-scale design
pressure of 690 kPa, Although the transducer was temperature-limited 10 450 deg C
by available fibers and exhibited more hysteresis than desired, otherwise it showed
considerable promise for further development,
'A commerically available fiber-optic temperature sensor is the fluoroptic
system, Here a material (magnesium fluorogermanate activated with tetravalent
'manganese) exhibiting a long. exponentially decaying fluorescence either is applied
to the surface whose temperature is to be measured or is incorporated into the
tip of a probe which will be pressed against a surface (Fig. 9.21). A xenon flash
lamp source, filtered as shown in Fig, 9.22, sends a short pulse of "blue" light out
'long the optical fiber toward the fluorescent film, which then emits a longer-
'wavelength (red) fluorescence that propagates back along the fiber through another
filter into an optical detector. The decay time ofthe fuorescence isa reproducible
function of the temperature of the sensing film (Fig. 922) and is measured with
'conventional electronic timing circuitry. Good accuracy is easily achieved since the
time intervals are relatively long compared to the clock period (01 to 1.0 ys) of
standard electronic timers. About 10 readings (Nashes) per second are possible,
with single-flash precision being about +0.5 deg C, while averaging 20 or 30 flashes
