TWELVE
VOLTAGE-INDICATING
AND -RECORDING DEVICES
'The majority of signals in measurement systems ultimately appear as voltages.
Since voltage cannot be seen, it must be transduced to a form intelligible to a
human observer. The form in which the data are presented is generally that of a
pointer moving over a scale, a pen writing on a chart (including light beams
writing on photosensitive paper and electron beams writing on cathod
tubes), visual presentation of a set of ordered digits, or printout of digital data by
4 typewriter or similar device. We consider the most common types of such
indicating and/or recording devices.
12.1 STANDARDS AND CALIBRATION
Figure 12.1' gives information on the primary standards for voltage, resistance,
'capacitance, and inductance, For most routine purposes in engineering labora
lories, the secondary standards (voltage-calibration sources, precision resistor
decades, etc) availabe from many manufacturers are adequate.
122 ANALOG VOLTMETERS AND POTENTIOMETERS
While digital voltmeters are very popular, analog meters are still the prefered
choice for certain applications* The most widely employed meter movement for
cde and (with rectifiers) ac measurement in electronics and instrumentation work
is the classical D'Arsonval movement (see Fig. 12.2). This basically current-
sensitive device is used to measure voltage by maintaining circuit resistance
" ascracy in Measrements nd Calibration, NBS. Tech Nat 2621965
2), Hare Jr Anil Patel Meters Alive und Wel! Ist. Cont Spt pp. 19-28 July 197%;
J. ayes Dipl or Moving-Cot Meters? Mark Des pp 113-115 September 1074

-- 893 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 885
\OLTAGEINDICATING AND -RECORDING DEVICES 86
'constant by means of compensating techniques (see Fig. 17a). Relatively recent
improvements on this basic configuration include taut-band suspension (rather
than pivot-and.jewel bearings) individually calibrated seale divisions, and
'expanded-scale instruments. Taut-band suspension completely eliminates bearing
ion, reduces inertia and temperature effects, increases ruggedness, and results
in less loading on the measured circuit since the reduced frition requires less
power drain. The increased accuracy made possible by taut-band construction
an be provided at reasonable cost through the use of automatic calibration
systems, which print an individual scale for each and every. instrument
Expanded-scale instruments use a precision voltage-suppression circuit to mea-
sure @ small variation around a larger voltage. Thus if you need to measure a
100-V signal accurately. itis possible to do this with a meter whose scale goes
from 99 to 101 V. Statie inaccuracies of 0. percent are attainable in a rugged and
portable instrument with these methods.
Transistorized voltmeters (sce Fig. 323) sill utilize the D'Arsonval meter
'movement, but precede it by amplificr circuits. These inerease the input im-
pedance and overall sensitivity. Such instruments generally accept a wide range
'of de and ac input voltages and have static error of the order of | to 3 percent of
full scale,
When ac (not necessarily sinusoidal) voltages are to be measured with &
D'Arsonval movement, its nevessary to perform rectification. Depending on the
Circuitry used, a meter may be sensitive to the average, peak, or ms value of the
input waveform. Its common practice to calibrate the scale of the meter to read
fms value no matter What quantity is fundamentally sensed, This procedure is
accurate only if pure sinusoidal waveforms are being measured since, in this case
only, the peak, average, and rms values are all elated by fixed constants and thus
can be included in the scale calibration, For nonsinusoidal waveforms, peak- oF
average-sensing meters will not read the correct rms value. In some cases, peak oF
'average value #8 actually what is wanted: however, rms is desired most often, A
true rms voltmeter is complex and expensive: thus peak- and average-sensing
meters calibrated to read rms are in wide use and are generally satisactory,
except in the most critical applications
Figure 12.3 shows circuits for peak, average, and rms meters. In the peak
circuit, the capacitor is charged to the peak value of a periodic input voltage.
'This charge cannot leak off rapidly because of the one-way conduction of the
diodes and the high input impedance of the voltmeter (transstorized voltmeters
'often use peak sensing). The voltage across the meter thus stays near the peak
value of the input with only slight fuctuations resulting from diode reverse
leakage and meter noninfinite impedance. The meter reads the lurgest peak,
'whether positive oF negative. In the average-reading citcuit, the input is full-
'wave-retified, and the low-pass filtering characteristic of the meter movement is
'employed to extract the average value. The ems-reading circuit" approximates the

'When the most accurate measurements of de voltage are needed, poten-
tiometers rather than deflection meters are employed. The potentiometer is a
'ull-balance instrument in which the unknown voltage is compared with an
Accurate reference voltage, which can be adjusted until the two are equal. Since,
at the null point, no current flows, errors due to IR drops in lead wires are
'eliminated. Such 1R drops are always present when a D'Arsonval-type meter is
used to measure voltage directly. Figure 124a shows the basic potentiometer
circuit, We see that a galvanometer (just a very sensitive D'Arsonval movement)
is utilized as a null detector. It detects the presence or absence of current by
'deflecting whenever the unknown and reference voltages are unequal. However,
reed not be calibrated since it must indicate only the presence of current, not its
numerical value. The basic circuit of Fig. 12a is not practical since the aocuracy
'of the reference voltage picked off the slidewire is directly influenced by changes

-- 897 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 889
ire 123 Peak average and em ret
in the dry-cell voltage. Since the dry cell supplies power to the slidewire, its
voltage is bound gradually to drop off. This problem is solved in the practical
circuit of Fig. 12.4b by inclusion of an additional component, the standard cell
Figure 12.4 shows the Weston cadmium saturated standard cell which isthe
basic working standard of voltage. Its terminal voltage is L018636 V and is
reproducible to the order of 0.1 t0 0.6 ppm. Its accuracy in terms of the funda-
mental mass, length, and time standards can be established to only about
10 ppm, however. Its temperature coefficient is -40 V/C"; thus close temper-
ature control obviously must be employed in the most exacting situations. Since
its accuracy is destroyed if any appreciable current is drawn from it over a time

-- 898 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 890
Fmd)
Fite 124 Manvaly blued potentioneter
interval, a standard cell cannot be substituted for the dry cell of Fig. 124a. So the
standard cell must be utilized as an intermittent reference against which the
slidewire excitation voltage can be checked whenever desired. The unsaturated
Weston cell is employed in practical instruments since it is more portable. Its
terminal voltage varies from one unit to another. However, its drift at constant
temperature is only about ~0.003 percent per year; thus it is perfectly adequate
for most purposes. Is temperature coefficient is about - 10 xV/C*
'The operation of the circuit of Fig. 12.4 is as follows: When the slidewire
scale on the potentiometer was calibrated originally at the factory, slidewire
excitation-adjusting resistor R, was set at a fixed value and resistor Ry was
adjusted until, when loop abed was completed by the switch, no eurrent flowed in
the galvanometer. This means that the voltage drop across R, was just equal 10

the standard-cell voltage. Now the slidewire, Ry. and Ry are all ixed and stable
sistors: thus ifthe voltage across Ry is at its calibration value, the slidewire
jxcitation voltage also must be at its calibration value, Thus, whenever we wish
to check the calibration (this is called standardization), we merely complete the
loop abd momentarily (so as not to draw much current from the standard cell)
sand note whether the galvanometer deflects, If it does, we adjust the sldewire
'excitation with Ry until deflection ceases. Then we are assured thatthe slidewire
'excitation is at its original calibration value. The resistor Ry is merely a current-
limiting resistor to prevent drawing large curtent from the standard cell theough
the sldewire path, which is fairly low-tesistance
Fairly common and inexpensive potentiometers which can be read to the
nearest microvoll afe in wide use. More sophisticated instruments intended for
the most accurate calibration work provide greater accuracy and sensitivity. One
'Such commercially available instrument" measures in three ranges: 0 t0 1.611110
Vi steps of 10 pW. 0 t0 01641110 in steps of Ow, and 0 40 OOTGHTTIO in
steps of 001 2V. The total parasitic thermoelectric voltage is less than 0.1 AV.
The limit of error on the high range is +0.003 percent of reading +0.1 nV, while
'on the medium and low ranges itis +0008 percent of reading 0.1 nV. These
values approach the level of the National Standards achieved by the NIST, which
are about 0.001 pereent from 0.01 to 1,000 V.
123 DIGITAL VOLTMETERS AND MULTIMETERS
While analog meters equire no power supply, give a better visual indication of
trends and changes. suffer less from electric noise and isolation problems, and are
'Simple and inexpensive, digital meter offer higher accuracy and input impedance,
unambiguous readings at greater viewing distances, smaller size, and a digital
clectrical output (for interfacing with external equipment) in addition to visual
readout, The three major classes of digital meters are panel meters, bench-type
meters, and systems meters. All employ some type of A/D converter [often the
dual-slope integrating type (see Sec. 10.12)} and have a visible readout that
displays the converter output. Usually panel meters are dedicated to a single
function (and pethaps even a fied ranged, while bench and system meters often
ae multimers: that is, they can read ac and de volts and amperes as well as resis-
tance over several ranges. The basic circuit is always de volts; current is converted
to volts by passing it through a precision low-resistance shunt, while alternating is
converted to direct current by employing rectifiers and filters. For ohms' measure
'ment, the meter includes a precision low-current source that is applied across the
unknown resistor; again this gives a de voltage which is digitized and read out as
coms (se Fig. 12 deg ) Bench meters are intended mainly for stand-alone operation

to microcomputer data busses since any number of devices can be serviced by a

Bench-type meters range from inexpensive hand-held units with 3}-digit
readout and 0.5 percent accuracy to 54-igit (200,000 count) devices with 1-yV
resolution, Instruments designed for extremely low voltages are called digital
rnanovolimeters and they provide resolution down to about 10 aV (comparable
analog meters go to about 1 nV). Digital picoammeters? measure very small
currents and can resolve about | pA (analog instruments go to 3 fA}. When
extremely high input impedance is required for current, voltage, resistance, of
charge measurements, an electrometer? type of instrument is employed, Digital
electrometers can resolve 10-'7 A, 10 pV, and 1 {C and measure resistance as
high as 200 TO. Input impedance ean be as high as 10000 TA. Corresponding
analog instruments achieve 10" A, 10 nV, and 1 £C and can measure resistance
to 100 7.
Systems-type digital voltmeters (DVMs) or multimeters (DMMs) are de-
signed to provide the basic A/D conversion function in data systems assembled
by interfacing various peripheral devices with the DVM. Capabilities and costs
vary widely: here we describe briefly a meter* which utilizes a microprocessor 10
provide several useful mathematical functions in addition to managing meter
'operations. A modified dual-slope A/D converter is used with selectable integra
tion times from 0.01 to 100 powerline cycles to trade off speed versus accuracy,
[AL maximum speed (330 readings per second) accuracy is 40.1 percent, while
(0.37 reading por second gives 6}-igit resolution and 0.001 percent accuracy.
Modes available are de volts, ac volts, and resistance, Mathematical functions
include the following: with NULL, the first reading is subtracted from «ach
successive reading. and the difference is displayed (the first "reading" can be
centered manually from a built-in keypad). The function STAT accumulates read-
ings and caleulates mean and variance, With PASS/FAIL, any readings falling
'outside user-entered upper and lower limits will display Hi or LO and pull the
service-request line. With dBr(R), the user enters resistance R, and then all
readings are displayed as power dissipated in R in units of decibels referenced to
L-mW. With THMS'F (with the voltmeter in OHMS), the temperature of a
thermistor probe is displayed in degrees Fahrenheit (or Celsius). The function
(X ~ 2) provides offsetting and scaling with user-entered Z and Y constants (X
is the reading). The function 100(X ~ YV/Y finds the percentage deviation and 20
log (X/Y) displays X in decibels relative to the stored value Y. A builtin IEEE-
4488 type of interface allows versatile interconnection with other instruments and
'computers. An internal memory (RAM) can be used to store the results of
measurements and programs for taking the measurements.

124 ELECTROMECHANICAL SERVOTYPE
XT AND XY RECORDERS
Figure 12.7 shows the principle of servotype XT recorders employed for obtain-
ing indication and simultancous recording of a voltage ¢,(X) against time (7)
'The instrument servomeckanism is designed so that displacement x, tracks the
voltage e, accurately over the design frequeney range. Variations on this general
principle include use of ac or de amplifiers, ac or de motors, rotary oF trans
lational motors, various mechanical-drive arrangements (piano wire and pulleys,
te), assorted writing schemes, and diferent displacement transducers (poten:
tiometers, RVDTs, capacitance, etc}, Adjustable chart drive speeds (these es
tablish the time base) were obtained originally from constant-speed synchronous
'motors and pushbutton mechanical change gears; however, now the step-motor
drive of Fig. 127 is more common,
One subclass of this type of recorder utilizes rather wide charts (10 in) and is
intended for slowly changing inputs (<1 Hz for full-scale travel). Throw-away
fiber ink pens are employed most, but some recorders use heated styli and heat-
sensitive paper. With suitable preamps, most any desired voltage range can be
accommodated, with resolution to as litle as a few microvolts being common
Multichannel operation (a separate servo for each pen) is possible ether reduced
width side by side or full-width overlapping (if pens are staggered (0 avoid
mechanical interference): the limit is about six pens. Staggered pens give an
undesirable chart-displacement error betwoen channels, Some recorders! com=
pensate for this with a microprocessorimplemented interchannel time delay.
'When inputs change very slowly, another approach to multichannel operation
multiplexes the various input voltages into one servo and uses a numbered print
wheel (or thermal dot matrix printhead) to print an identifying number or symbol
foreach channel of data (24 oF 30 channels isa practical limit). Static accuracy of
this entire subclass of recorders is about 0.1 percent full scale
To cross-plot one variable against another, the XY configuration of Fig.
28a is available. Here the paper stands sil (held down by vacuum or elec:
trostatic attraction) while two independent servos move the pen horizontally and
vertically. Standard sizes are 8} x I and 11 x 17 in (metric equivalents are
available), and performance is very similar to the XT recorders just described
singe the mechanical motions are of a similar scale, Two ¥ pens on a single X
carriage (pens staggered) also are obtainable. Many XY recorders include a ramp
voltage generator, which can be applied as voltage input to one (or cither) of the
servos, creating a time base and allowing XT recording. Figure 12.8h? shows
internal details of typical XY recorder. Digitakinput X'Y plotters for computer
graphics work may employ open-loop linear step-motor drives with sophisticated

We see that the galvanometer isa second-order instrument. We can increase
sensitivity by increasing H, N, and b or by decreasing K,.,R,, and R,. The ux
ensity H is usually at the maximum value with ordinary permanent magnets,
Increases in NJ, and b result in increases in R, since a longer total length of wire
{nthe col results; thus the net effet on sensitivity is not obvious, Decreases in K,
increase K directly: however, speed of response i lost since wy decreases. Note
'also that sensitivity varies with R,; thus changing from one transducer to another
with a diferent resistance results in a loss of calibration. This is due to the fact
'that the galvanometer i a currentsensiive device. Equation (12.7) shows that the
mechanical viscous damping B can be eliminated completely and the system is
sill damped. The damping that remains is of electromagnetic origin and is caused
by the back emf proportional to ddr (mechanical-viscous-damping torque also
is proportional to did), In low-frequency, high-sensitivity galvanometers the
electrical damping is adequate to obtain the optimum { value of 06S, and no
intentional mechanical damping B is provided. Also such galvanometers may

The most important feature of this result is that ¢ depends on R,q the source
resistance. Thus such a qultanometer can be properly damped for only one value of
external resistance, This does not mean it can be used only with transducers that
hhave this value of R, ; but it does mean that a suitable resistance network may
hhave to be interposed between the transducer and galvanometer. Ifthe transducer
resistance is too high, a shunt resistor is needed: if too low, a series resistor (see
Fig. 12.11) is required. For high-frequency, low-sensitvity galvanometers the elec-
{tromagnetic damping is inadequate, and intentional viscous damping is provided.
The electromagnetic damping is a small percentage of the total, and so generally
such galvanometers can be used with any external resistance ina wide range.
To construct a recording oscillograph, it is necessary to provide chart paper
moving at a known speed, 10 give a time base, and a means of writing the
xgalvanometer motion on this paper. To realize the high-frequency response (up to

25 kHz) mentioned earlier. tiny mirror is rigidly fastened to the moving coil and
a light beam reflected from it. When the coil turns, the light beam, which is
focused as a spot on the moving chart paper, defects over the paper, leaving a
trace which develops under ordinary fluorescent room lighting. These records will
last for years if kept away from sunlight and ean be subjected to a simple liquid
fixing process if absolute permanence is require.

Since the light beams do not interfere with one another. as do mechanical arms,

126 FIBER-OPTIC CRT RECORDING OSCILLOSCOPE,
'The familiar cathode-ray tube (CRT) oscilloscope has extremely high-frequency
response, but permanent records normally require photography of the sereen and
are time limited 10 one sweep. By combining a special fiber optic CRT with an
'osellograph-type paper drive (which passes the paper over the CRT face, where it
is exposed by the light from the CRT phosphor), a recording osclloscope® with
uniquely useful characteristics is obtained. Ifthe paper is held stl, conventional
'oscilloscope operation allows single-sweep XT oF XY recording of signals from
direct current to 1 MHz, much like the use of standard camera recording tech-
niques, except that ordinary direct-print oscillograph paper is utilized. By employ~
ing both the CRT sweep and simultaneous paper drive (speeds up to about 100 in/s
'can be selected), the I-MH2 frequency response is retained: but now we can get
Permanent records of as many contiguous sweeps as we wish, because they are
simply "stacked" one above the other on the recording paper. This technique
tives an equivalent paper speed of up to 40,000 inys. Time skew, resulting from
paper motion during a single CRT sweep, is corrected electronically, but there
will be small "gaps" in the data between sweeps because of the CRT sweep
Fetrace time, Since the CRT allows beam intensity (Z axis) modulation, the
instrument can produce gray-scale pictures such as video images. A multichannel
version utilizes sampling techniques to obtain direct current to 5,000-Hz response
for up to 18 channels of data
12.7 THERMAL AND ELECTROSTATIC ARRAY RECORDERS
With these recorders, heat-sensitive or xerographic chart paper is fed past a
stationary printhead with an array of closely spaced (typically four per millime-
{er) heated or electrostatic elements. Analog input voltages are sampled and applied
Honey Cor, Denver, Clo
2 Moneynell Corp. Demet. Coe.

-- 912 of 1000 --

80, WANIPULATION, TRANSMISSION, AND RECORDING OF DATA
to the appropriate thermal or electrostatic elements, leaving a mark of a charge
(Electrostatic instruments require a toner application similar to xerographic copy-
ing machines) Time lines, grid tines, and other annotation are combined with
analog input-signal data, and a raster line which includes all these components is
printed at one time. A typical thermal instrument' employs 6-in-wide paper and
had 2 to 32 channels, static accuracy of 0.2 percent, and maximum paper speed
'of 50 mm/s. Dynamic response specification of such array recorders is complicated
by the sampling process utilized; however, the maximum paper speed dictates that
'8 S0-Hz signal could occupy only 1 mm per cycle. A 20-He sine wave actualy is
printed as 10 straight-line segments over a 25-mm length. Electrostatic instru
'ments® exhibit similar behavior, except that the printing process is about S times
as fast, allowing a maximum paper speed of 250mm/s, and now a 100-Hz sine
wave is printed as 10 segments over 2.5 mm.
128 CATHODE-RAY OSCILLOSCOPES AND
GRAPHIC DISPLAYS
Figure 12.13 shows in simplified fashion the functional operation of a typical
cathode-ray oscillosope." A focused narrow beam of electrons is projected from
aan electron gun through a set of horizontal and vertical deflection plates. Volt
'ages applied to these plates create an electric field which deflects the electron
bbeam and causes horizontal and vertical displacement of its point of impingement
'on the phosphorescent screen. By proper design this displacement can be made
closely linear with dflection-plate voltage. The phosphorescent screen emits light
'Which is visible tothe eye and may be photographed for a permanent record.
'The most common mode of operation is that in which a plot of the input
signal against time is desited, This may be accomplished by driving the horizon-
{al deflection plates with a ramp voltage, thus causing the spot to sweep from left
{o right at a constant speed. To ensure that the sweep and the input signal
applied to the vertical deflection plates are synchronized properly, the triggering
of the sweep can be initiated by energizing the trigger circuit from the leading
cedge of the input signal itself (see Fig. 12.13) This results in a loss of the first
instants of the input signal on the seteen, but generally this is not serious since
'only about 1 mm of deflection is needed to cause triggering. In those cases where
this loss is objectionable, oscilloscopes with signal delay (Fig. 12.14) are available,
'These delay the application of the input signal to the vertical deflection plates so
that the sweep stars before the rise of the input signal on the screen, Thus the
complete input signal is recorded. Most oscilloscopes also provide for triggering

Cathode-ray tubes are obtainable with a number of different phosphors on
the sereen.! The choice of phosphor controls the intensity of light available for
visual observation or photographic recording, as well as the persistence of the
trace after the electron beam has moved on. Both long- and short-persistence
phosphors are available. Long-persistence phosphors are useful in visual observa-
tion of transients since the entire trace is visible long enough for an observer to
note its characteristies. Persistence for several seconds is possible. When the
'moving-film method of trace photography is used, a very-short-persistence phos-
hor is necessary to prevent blurring. (In this method the electron beam is
'deflected vertically only, while the film is moved horizontally in front of the
screen at a fixed, known velocity.) Persistence of less than I ys is availabe
Dual-persistence phosphors provide either a long or short persistence, depending
'on the color of the filer utilized over the scope screen, The most common
method of photographing oscilloscope traces uses a still camera and the 10-5
Polaroid? film process. A common method of photographing transients employs
'4 double exposure to record both the trace and the grid lines. With the gri-line
illumination turned off and the camera shutter held open, the transient is trig
gered, thus recording its image on the film. Then the shutter is closed. Now the
R.A Bl, CRT Phosphor Slaton nt. & Com. St pp 86-9, March 1970.

'rid lines are turned on, and the shutter is snapped in the normal manner (say

To obtain multichannel capability in oscilloscopes, several approaches' are
taken, The dual-beam oscilloscope has two separate electron beams in one
cathode-ray tube, with separate deflection plates and amplifiers for each beam. In
some units both beams use the sime sweep system: thus the two traces are
Plotted against the same time base. Completely independent beams allowing
different time bases on each trace are available. The other approach (dual trace)
uses a single-beam eathode-ray tube and a high-speed electronic switeh to time-
share the beam among several input signals. Such multitrace systems are avail
able to give up to four traces on a single sereen
Versatility of operation is achieved in plug-in-type oscilloscopes by providing.
'a wide varity of functional plug-in units for a single mainframe. Typical plug-ins
available include dusl-trace and four-irace units, op-amps, carrier amplifiers,
spectrum analyzers, high-gain amplifiers, and time bases with special features
While most laboratory oscilloscopes have an 8.x 10 em sereen, larger screens
'up to about 21 in are available. These are useful for viewing by large groups,
presentation of many channels of data by bar-graph types of displays, ete. Large-
screen scopes usually cannot attain the high-frequency capability ofthe smaller
types.
'While the Polaroid photography process makes recording very convenient, a
'permanent retention ofthe trace on the scope sereen has certain advantages. This
Teatute is available in the various forms of storage oscilloscope*. Special cathode-
ray tubes are oblainable that retain a trace for long periods until i is erased
cletrically by pushing @ button. Oscilloscopes using such storage tubes cannot
achieve as high-speed performance as conventional types; however, they allow
fone to examine traces visually with ease: when a desired trace is noted, it can be
photographed, ogether with grid lines, in a single exposure.
Limited sereen size and inherent nonlinearity of the CRT deflection system.
make the error of the basic oscilloscope (both voltage and time) about 2 to
5 percent. In dual-beam or dusl-trace scopes, timing accuracy can be improved
bby applying an oscillatory signal of known frequency to one input as a reference.
Voltage accuracy can be increased similarly in a dual-trace instrument by apply:
ing a reference squate wave of accurately known amplitude to one input. Some

129 DIGITAL WAVEFORM RECORDERS AND
DIGITAL STORAGE OSCILLOSCOPES,
'The digital-memory waveform recordet' provides capabilitis dificult or impos-
sible to achieve by earlier methods. Figure 12.16 shows a block diagram for a
unit? capable of recording four independent signals simultaneously. The practical
and economic implementation of this concept rests on the ready availability,
small size, and reasonable cost of digital memories and associated digital
processing hardware brought about by explosive growth and intense competition
in the computer field. Consider a single channel. The analog voltage input signal
is digitized in a 10-bit A/D converter with a resolution of 0.1 percent (I part in
1,024) and frequency response to 25 kHz. The total digital memory of 4,096
words can be used for a single channel, 2048 can be employed for each of two
channels, or 1,024 for each of four channels. The analog input voltage is sampled
at adjustable rates (up to 100,000 samples per second), and the data points are
read into the memory; 4096 points maximum are storable in this particular
instrument. (Sampling rate and memory size must be chosen to suit the duration
Somers, New Way to Cptae ave Signals. Mach. Depp. 11-115. May 198
* Goal Ine, Bomation Div, Santa Clara Cal

-- 919 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 911
\OLTAGEINDICATING AND -RECORDING DEVICES 887
and waveform of the physical event being recorded. Some models have sampling
rates 10 200 MHz.)
'Once the sampled record ofthe event is captured in the memory, many useful
'manipulations are possible, since the memory can be read out without erasing it
{nondestructive readout), By reading the memory out slowly through a digtal-o-
analog converter (DAC), the original event (which could have been extremely
rapid) is reproduced as a slowly changing voltage that is easy to record per-
rmanently in large size on a slow XY plotter. If the memory is read out rapidly
'and reperiively an input event which was a "one-shot" transient becomes @
repetitive waveform that now may be observed easily on an ordinary (not stor-
age) oscilloscope. The digital memory also may be read out directly (without
going through the DAC) to, say, a computer where a stored program can ma-
nipulate the data in almost any desired way.
Pretriguer recording" allows the device to record the input signal preceding
the trigger point, a unique and often useful capability. In "ordinary" triggering
the recording process is started by the rise ofthe input signal (or some external
triggering signal) above some preset threshold value. If this threshold is set too
low, random noise will trigger the system inadvertently: too high a threshold
;revents recording ofthe inital rise of the desired signal. The digital recorder can
bbe set to record "continuously" (new data coming into memory push out old
data, once memory is full) until the trigger signal is received; then the recording
process is stopped, thus freezing in the memory the data received prior to the
rigger signal. An adjustable trigger delay allows operator control of the stop
point, so that the tigger may oocur near the beginning, middle, or end of the
'sored information
While digital-memory waveform recorders are marketed without attached
recording devices for their analog outputs, such packages are available as digital
memory oscillographs and oscilloscopes. The usual 40-Hz frequency response of
penjink oscillographs (Sec. 124) can be extended to 20 kHz with a plug-in
'module! using waveform-digitizing principles. This makes the convenience and
high recording quality of direct-writing instruments available for a very wide
range of signals, Digital-storage oscilloscopes? are available in processing and
ronprocessing forms. Processing types? include buil-in computing power, which
takes advantage of the fact that all data are already in digital form, Thus in-
'clusion of interfacing and a microprocessor provides a complete system for infor-
'mation acquisition, analysis, and output. Processing capability ranges from
simple functions (such as average, area, rms, etc) to complete FFT spectrum
'analysis capability. Units* with built-in hard-copy plotters are particularly useful

LATION, TRANSMISSION, AND RECORDING OF DATA
in that they can serve as a digital scope, high-speed oscillograph, tabular printer,
low-speed recorder, and XY plotter, all in one unit, with computing power and
8} < 11 in paper/ink printout
Nonprocessing digital scopes! are designed as replacements for analog in-
struments of both storage and nonstorage types. Their many desirable features
lead some proponents to predict that ultimately they will displace analog, scopes
'entirely (within the bandwidth range where digitization is feasible)" The basic
Principle again rests on the scheme of Fig. 12.16, However, the scope operating
controls are designed so that all confusing details are relegated to the back-
round, and one appears to be using a conventional instrument (in fact, some-
times digital scope panels are simpler). (Also, most digital scopes provide switch-
letable analog operation as one operating mode) One obvious advantage of
digital operation is the "storage scope" capability. provided by repetitively
reading out the stored waveform, thus making transients repetitive and allowing
their convenient display on the scope screen. (The CRT is an "ordinary," not a
storage, type) Furthermore, the voltage and time scales of the display are easily
'changed after the waveform has been recorded, which allows expansion (typically
10 64 times) of selected portions for resolution of greater detail A cross-hair
cursor can be positioned at any desired point on the waveform and the volt-
'ageytime values displayed digitally on the screen and/or read out electrically.
Some scopes use 12-bit converters, giving 0.025 percent resolution and 0.1 per-
'eent accuracy on voltage and time readings, which is much better than the 2 to
5 percent accuracy typical of analog scopes. "Splitscreen? capability (simul-
taneously displaying "live" analog traces and replayed stored ones) enables easy
comparison. The availabilty of pretrigger signal information (discussed under
waveform recorders) is also a significant advantage. Display of stored data in
both ¥T and XY format is possible. In addition to the fast memory readout used
for CRT display, slow readout is obtainable to produce hard copy with external
plotters. When more memory than the basic amount [typically 4.096 points
(words)) is needed, a magnetic disk accessory allows expansion to 32,000 points.
All digital-storage scopes are limited in bandwidth by the speed of their A/D
converters; however, 20-MHz digitizing rates available on some scopes yield
S-MHz bandwidth, which is adequate for many applications
12.10 DIGITAL PRINTERS/PLOTTERS,
Wide uiilization of computerized digital systems creates a large market for hard-
copy devices such as printers. Printers may be categorized as serial versus line,
dot matrix versus whole-character image, and impact versus nonimpact. Serial

printers produce a single character at a time, usually moving from left to right
across page (some recent printers move in borh directions, increasing efficiency),
Line printers appear to print an entire line simultaneously. Page princes print in
a line-at-a-time mode, but can be stopped and restarted only on a page basi
"Top speeds are about 200 characters per second for serial, 4,000 lines per minute
rnd 48,00 lines per minute for page printers.
ot-matrix printers form: the desired characters by selective printing of dots
ina row/column matrix (typically $ x 7, 7 x 7, oF 9 x 7), Whole-character print-
crs form the entire symbol at a single stroke, as in a traditional typewriter. Im-
pact printers bring the character element into forceful contact with the paper,
'sandwiching an inked ribbon between them. Nonimpact printers utilize
clectrophotographic ("aser/xerographic"), thermal,' ink-jet? electrostatic, or
clectrosenstive means to produce an image without contact of with minimal
pressure
'Whole-character impact printers are slow and noisy, but inexpensive, and
they give good print quality and produce multiple copies (carbons). Dot-matrix
'impact printers give higher speeds and have graphics capability. Drum, chain,
and band types of line printers offer high output rates. Thermal dot-matrix types
are slow, cannot produce multiple copies (true of all nonimpact types), and
require special paper: but they are quiet and inexpensive and have graphies
capability. Electrosenstive printers provide medium speed at low cost, but re
'Quire special paper and produce low print quality. Fletrostatic types have high
speed. with versatile fonts and graphies capability, but need special paper and wet
toner. Fleetrophotographic ("laser") printers give high speed, high resolution, and
uiet operation. Their high cost and maintenance have recently been greatly im-
proved, making them more popular. The several ink-jet types employ plain paper,
are quiet, and range from 30 characters per second to 45,000 lines per minute;
but they have reduced print quality and reliability at high speeds
Digital XY plotters? are intended mainly for graphics, but do provide alpha-
'numerics ("printing") as annotation for the graphics. Drum, flatbed, and hybrid
types are available. Ink-pen and electrostatic writing methods presently dominate
the market. Pen units provide high print quality and accuracy. ability to draw on
'both paper and plastic film, programmable multicolor capability, and low materi-
al cost: but they are slow and cannot easily color in large areas, Electrostatic
plotters offer high speed, but may produce somewhat lower quality

12.11 MAGNETIC TAPE AND DISK
RECORDERS/REPRODUCERS
'The magnetic recorder/reproducer has a number of unique features derived
'mainly from its ability to record a voltage, store it for any time, and then
reproduce it in electrical form esscmtally identical to its original occurrence
Recording methods include the direct, FM, pulse-duration modulation (PDM),
and digital techniques.* We consider first the direct and FM modes of operation
'applied to tape devices.
Figure 12.17 shows a functional diagram of a tape recorder/reproducer, and
Fig. 12.18 shows a closcup of the record and reproduce heads. A current i
proportional to the input voltage is passed through the winding on the record
hhead, producing a magnetic fux @ = Kyi at the recording gap. The tape (thin
plastic coated with iron oxide particles) passes under the gap, and the oxide
particles retain a state of permanent magnetization proportional to the flux exist
ng at the instant the particle leaves the gap. (Actually the applied flux and
induced magnetization are not proportional because of the nonlinearity of the
'magneti-hysteresis curve. Effectively, however, a close linearity i obtained by a
high-frequency bias technique.) Thus, with a sinusoidal input signal
i= ig sin 2 ft
and a tape speed of v inches per second, the intensity of magnetization along the
{ape varies sinusoidally with distance x according to
* Magnetic Tape Receding Tecnia! Fundamental, Bll & Howell Datatape Div, Pasadena,
ai. 199%

-- 923 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 915
eal
Soret jae
Figure 1218 Record and reprodoe ends
where m= Kad. The wavelength of the magnetization variation is then o/f
inches. For eximple, 60-H2 signal at a 60 in/s tape speed gives a wavelength of
Fin, I the tape with this signal on itis passed under the reproduce head, then a
voltage proportional to the rate of change of fux bridging its gap will be gener
'ated in its coil. Note that since the output voltage depends on the rate of change
'of flux, if direct current at the input had produced a constant tape magnetization,
the reproduce head would have given zero output
Thus the technique described above, the so-called direct recording process,
'can be used with varying input signals only, with about $0 Hz being the usual
lower limit of feequency. Furthermore, since the reproducing head has a differen-
Uiating characteristic, the reproduce amplifier must have an integrating character-
istic in order for the system output to be proportional to the input. An upper
frequency limit also exists, because at sufficiently high frequencies, for @ given
reproduce gap and tape speed, one wavelength of magnetization will become
'equal (o oF less than the gap width. Then the average magnetization in the gap

-- 924 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 916
892 WANIFULATION, TANSMESION, AND RECORDING OF DATA
will be ero, and no output voltage willbe generated. For example, at the fastest
'common tape speed, 240 in/s, and a gap width of 0.00008 in, this occurs at 30
MHz. Actually, the system is usable to only about half this frequency with
reasonable accuracy. The frequency range of the direct recording process is ap-
proximately within the band 100 Hz to 2 MH. The direct recording process does
'ot give particularly high accuracy. Essentially ths is limited by the signal-to-
noise ratio, which is of the order of 25 dB (about 18: 1). The rather high noise
level isthe result of minute defects in the tape surface coating to which the direct
recording process is sensitive,
'When more accurate recording and response to de voltages are required,
generally the FM system is employed. Here the input signal is used to frequency:
'modulate a carrier, which is then recorded on the tape in the usual way. Now,
hhowever, only the frequency of the recorded trace is significant, and tape defects
causing momentary amplitude errors are of litle consequence, The frequency
modulators employed hhere are similar in principle to those discussed under
voltage-to-frequency converters and subcarrier oxillators in Chaps. 10 and 11
However, the frequency deviation for tape recorders is +40 peroent about the
'carrier frequency. The reproduce head reads the tape in the usual way and sends
a signal to the FM demodulator and low-pass filter, where the original input
signal is reconstructed. The signal-to-noise ratio of an FM recorder may be of the
'order of 40 to 50 dB (100: I to 330: 1), indicating the possibility of inaccuracies
smaller than 1 percent. By using sufficiently high carrier frequencies (432 kHz),
the flat (+1 dB) frequency response of FM recorders may go as high as 80,000
Hz at 120 in/s tape speed. To conserve tape when high-frequency response is not
niceded, generally a range of tape speeds is provided. When the tape speed is
'changed, the carrier frequency is altered in direct proportion. This makes the
recorded wavelength of a given de input signal the same, no matter what tape
speed is being used, since +40 percent full-sale frequency deviation is utilized in
all cases. Signals may be recorded at one tape speed and played back at any of
the others without change in magnitude, but with a compression or an expansion
'of the time scale. A common set of specifications might be as follows

Multichannel tape recorders are found with up to 14 channels on one f-
wide tape. Input to tape recorders is generally at about a 1-V level, and so most
Uwansducers require amplification before recording. The maximum time-base
'change of about 60: 1 (120/13) shown in the table can be increased even further
by rerecording the signal For example, the original signal can be recorded at 120
im'. Then it is played back at 17 with the output of the reproduce amplifier
feeding the input of the record amplifier of another machine running at 120 in/s
If now this tape is played at 13, an overall slowdown of 4096 : | is achieved. An
'example application of tape slowdown is the recording of a 20.000-Hz signal on
20 in's and playback at 74 i's into an optical oscillograph (frequency
.000 Hz) for a permanent record, Also tape slowdown may be
ttilized when digital computations are to be performed on high-speed analog
data. The digital equipment is very accurate, bul may not handle rapidly varying
inputs: thus one can analog-tape-record the data and then play them into the
digital processing equipment at reduced spsed. Digital waveform recorders per=
form these slowdown speedup operations more conveniently than tape recorders,
as Tong as the data record is not to0 long. The storage and playback feature of
tape recording is employed widely in simulation. particular environmental
condition, such as vibration in an aircraft is measured and tape-recorded in the
cual environment. Then the tape is brought into the simulation laboratory
Where the environmental parameter (say, vibration) is repeated by playing the
tape into a vibration shaker
Wide utilization of digital data systems requires that digital data often be
directly recorded on magnetic-type oF -isk systems! Pulse-code modulation
'one of several coding schemes, is employed extensively to record
Jn are assumed to be in the binary form of Os and Is (a high voltage
level and a low level. The actual magnetie-recording technique is similar to direct
analog (rather than FM) in thatthe response does not extend to zer0 frequency.
Codes of the return-to-zero-evel type RZ) (see Fig. 12.19) are wasteful of band-
width since each bit requires two level transitions. Non-return-to-2ero-kevel
(NRZ-L) codes are more frugal of bandwith, but present recording problems
since a long string of 1s oF Os is essentially "de? which cannot be recorded
properly. The frequency content of the signal depends, of course, on both the
sequence of Is and 0s and the tape speed. A rule of thumb says that the number
'of level changes per unit time should be about 1.5 times the low-frequency cutof
'of the recorder. Thus fora 100-Hz cutoff, we would require a combination of tape
speed and level changes such that there would be (on the average) 150 level
changes per second. Most digital recording is done with the basic NRZ-L coding
scheme or improved versions of it. One such version separates the NRZ-L. data
stream into 7-bit words; inverts bits 2, 3,6, and 7; and adds a parity bit to make
an 8-bit word, The parity bit is chosen so as to make the total number of Is in
" Magnetic Tape Reseling Tecieal Fundamental, Bll & Howell Dattape Div Paden
{al 199, Paral Mode High Density Digtal Revrding Teche Fundamental, ll & Howell

igre 1219 Digital eowing formas
the 8-bit word an odd count. Advantages of such a scheme include reduction of
'de.content and a guarantee that there wll be atleast one transition every 14 bits.
Since digital recorder heads (just as analog recorders) can have multiple
1cks, data can be recorded in serial, parallel, or serial-parallel format. In serial
format, frequently used in instrumentation applications, data are recorded in a
continuous stream on a single tack. In parallel format, common in computer
applications, data are recorded simultaneously on several tracks and the data
have a relationship across the width ofthe tape, such as using an 8-track head to
record 8-bit words. Seral-parallel mode is a process in which two or more serial
datastreams, longitudinally recorded, also have a relationship across the width of
the tape. For example, the number 10011101 could be recorded on two tracks by
using the code that alternate bits employ alternate tracks; thus one track would
contain 1010, and the second would hold O11. This technique is utilized in
instrumentation recording at very high data rates.
Specifications for digital tape recorders vary widely, but it may be useful to
indicate present upper limits of performance. Both tape-packing density and
speed of data transfer are of interest. An 84-track (2-in-wide tape) high-density
recorder can transfer 600 millon bits per second at 150 in/s tape speed and store
440 billion bits on a 9,200-N reel of tape
While magneticisk recording' encompasses a wide range of equipment, we
'mention briefly only the flexible ("floppy") disk class since itis quite common in

12.12 SPEECH INPUT-OUTPUT DEVICES
[At the human-machine interface, the vast majority of communication relies on
the human sense of vision. Thus the meters, recorders, ete, of this chapter prod-
'uce a visible output. In some applications audible input/output would be advan-
tageous. Some manufacturing tasks require handling of parts and keyboard entry
'of computer data, Ifthe data entry were by voice, the operator' hands could be
kept fice for parts manipulation. As one might suspect, voice output! is much
casier to achieve than voice input® (speech recognition; thus cost/eapability has
progressed much further in the spoceh output area. Presently available digital
Yoice-output modules can duplicate accurately the natural inflection, intonation,
and expression of the original voice. Thus one ean supply the device manufac
turer with an audio tape ofthe desited message, as spoken by the desired speaker,
id they will produce a digital device which accurately reproduces the message
'on command. Also modules are available with a prerecorded and fixed "menu"
'of basic words (a typical one has numbers 0 to 12, letters A to Z, and 140 words),
These basic items can be strung together arbitrarily by a program to create
phrases and sentences. The sound of such devices is clearly intelligible, but wil
hot be as natural as that of devices "custom-made" from an audio recording
Both classes of devices rely on clever data-compression techniques to drastically
sduce the nceded digital memory. "Brute force" digital recording (as practiced in
high-fidelity music recording) would require about 96000 Bd, while the data
compression techniques reduce this figure to about 1200 Bd, This is what makes
the whole scheme practical, since now messages of useful length (minutes) can be
stored in memories of acceptable cost
tl voice-recognition modules can be obtained with vocabularies of 0 to
100 isolated words phrases. For good aecuracy, words must be clearly enunci
ated: however, some machines have "training" schemes that make them adaptive
to-a particular speakers voige so that machine accuracy improves as a word is

CHAPTER
THIRTEEN
ENGINEERED DATA ACQUISITION
AND PROCESSING SYSTEMS
{In the simpler measurement applications in which we monitor, control, or per
form experimental engineering analysis with only a few channels of data and/or
do only minor data processing, often a system "casually" assembled from avail-
fable sensors, amplifies, filters, recorders, etc, is adequate. However, when
requirements become more comprehensive and stringent, data acquisition and
processing systems of various types are commercially available and may be
necessary. Such systems vary with respect to several factor:
1. Is data processing mainly analog or digital?"
2 Is there one amplifier per channel, or is a single amplifier timeshared by
multiplexing?
4. I the system is digital-computer-based, is it sel-contained (includes the com-
puter) or just a "front end" that can be interfaced with various computers?
4, Is the system a general-purpose type that can be ordered off the shelf, ready
for immediate use, oF will it be tailored to the specific application and thus
require some system engineering before delivery and some debugging after
Selivery?
5. Are the signals changing relatively slowly, or is fast dynamic response re-
guired?
'To illuminate these and other questions, we describe a few examples of comme
cially available systems of several representative types.

-- 930 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 922
13.1 A VERSATILE, MODULAR SYSTEM.
EMPHASIZING ANALOG SIGNAL PROCESSING
Figures 13.1 through 13.5" show some selected features of system which, while
using digital control logic and digital readout (so it can be interfaced easily to a
digital computer), relies heavily on analog methods for data processing. A system
to meet the needs ofa particular application is assembled from a mainframe (Fig.
13.14), signal conditioning modules (Fig. 13.16 shows examples), signal processing
modules (examples in Figs. 132 and 133), and readout modules (example Fig.
134), Mainframes can accommodate up to 60 plug-in modules and provide the
necessary power supplies and interconnection facilites, Fifteen nondedicated con-
nections are provided for any special features or alterations that might be needed
'The 28 standard connections are divided into a "housekeeping" bus (power sup-
plies, system reference voltage, synchronizing and timing functions) the digital
communications bus (13 conductors used for intrasystem transmission of logic
signals, such as scaling commands, channel addresses, ete), and the called signal
us (transmits analog data signals "on request" from selected source modules 10
'common receiving element, usually a digital indicator or converter)
'A wide range (12) of transducer signal conditioning modules (some of which
are shown in Fig. 13.16) are obtainable. Thermocouple conditioners accept low-
level thermocouple signals and provide reference-junction compensation, ampli
cation, and linearization to produce a standard +5-V de fullscale output signal,
linear with temperature. For transducers that require excitation andjor bridge
'completion (strain gage, LVDT, resistance thormomete, ete) these facilites are
provided in the module. All modules offer two +5-V de outputs: a continuously
Available "prime output" and a "called output", which is transmitted onto the
system called signal bus when and only when the module receives a logic call
'command. [Some signal conditioner modules include modest processing capabil-
Fig. 13.2a shows a two-channel LVDT unit that provides sum (4 + 8) of
difference (4 ~ B} functions useful in thickness and taper gaging This approach
is more economical than employing two separate LVDT conditioners and
_general-purpose sum/difference module.} Note that a system of this type utilizes
the "amplifier per channel" design philosophy. This is more expensive than a
'multiplexed system, but it provides continuous (rather than sampled) data, which
Preserves all the speed of response inherent in the transducer /conditioner
'A comprehensive family of 27 signal processing modules which perform
analog operations and logical decision making also can be obtained. Figure 13.25
shows the use of three product/ratio modules in an electrie-motor test stand.
Funetions A(xy), ky/|.v} Ax, and ky/[x| may be chosen on these modules. Five
Additional modules are shown in The spring-testing system of Fig. 1334, An
'op-amp type of integrating module computes absorbed energy in the shock-
absorber test system of Fig. 13.35. When integration over many minutes or even

132 COMPACT DATA LOGGERS
Although this class of system accepts a number of options and accessories, it can
be properly considered an off-the-shelf item that can be put into service with a
minimum of effort and delay. The term compact is descriptive: @ typical unit
provides 60 channels of data in a 20 x 40 x 60 cm box weighing about 20 ke
'Most manufacturers offer local of remote add-on scanners to expand to about
1,000 channels. Sean rates are modest usualy (1 to 20 channels per second), and
though versatile signal conditioning is provided, signal processing capability is
limited to simple functions such as "(mx + b) scaling", time averaging of single
'channels, group averaging of several channels, and alarm signaling when preset
limits are exceeded. However, most units do allow interfacing to computers,
where versatile processing is possible.
Data loggers of this class utilize a built-in microprocessor to control the
internal operations and carry out calculations. The design philosophy isto multi
plex all the data channels through a single amplifer-A/D converter, which is

'automatically ranged or gain-switched under program control to accommodate
the signal level of each channel (see Fig. 136) This approach greatly reduces
'costs, but rules out applications in which fast-changing signals must be observed,
since a (typical) $ channel per second sean rate takes 12 s to scan 60 channels
before returning to any given channel. Also note that if we plan to calculate
density from a pressure on channel 1 and a temperature on channel 60, the"
skew" of 12 s can cause density errors if the signals change 100 rapidly. Often
'multiplexers (Scanners) are offered in both general-purpose (two-wire) and low-
level (two signal wires plus shield) versions since millivol-level signals, such as
from thermocouples, generally use a shiclded, twisted pair of conductors. A three-
wirescanner system ean reduce errors from about 10 to 1 yV. Electromechanical
feed switches ate used frequently in such scanners since speed requirements are
modest but low noise is important,
Since thermocouples are very common in data-logger applications, reference-
junetion compensation and linearization options are always available. Reference-
junction compensation can be offered economically and accurately for any
'mixture of thermocouple types by use of an isothermal connection block. This
thermocouple terminal block is designed to have a uniform (:+0.05 deg C) temper-
ature (the feference-junction temperature) over its length. Block temperature is
allowed to drift with ambient conditions, but is measured (often with a junction
Semiconductor sensor, since these work well near room temperature). This
Feference:junction temperature is sent to the microprocessor, where temper-
ature/voltage data for each thermocouple being employed are stored and the
necessary correction is calculated. The microprocessor also stores equations
Which curvest the thermocouple tables (over the desired range) for each of the

-- 941 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 933
[ENGINTERED DATA ACQUISITION AND PROCESSING SYSTEMS. 909
thermocouple types, providing software linearization. For resistance thermome-
ters the data logger provides constant-current excitation and software lin
'The system amplifier and A/D converter are crucial elements for overall,
system accuracy: Since data-logger inputs vary widely in voltage range. while the
ND input typically is fixed at + 10 V, the microprocessor sets the amplifier gain
'ata proper value as each channel is sampled (some loggers also provide automa
tic ranging), These range selections are entered from the front panel when the
logger is programmed for the particular application, Programming of this and
'other functions is very simple: no computer languages need be learned. A typical
set of ranges and resolutions would be +40 mV (I-uV resolution), +400 mV
(10 «Vi. £4. (100 pV}, and 40 V (1 mV}, with input impedance of 200 MQ
{except 10 MQ on 40-V range). Zero drifts kept negligible by an automatic 2er0
system, Frequently the A/D converters are of the dualslope or voltage-to-tre-
'quency type, since conversion speed is modest and these integrating converters
ive good noise rejection. Fast or slow scanning rates (say, 15 versus 3 channels
per second) may be chosen to allow a tradeoff between speed and accuracy since
integrating A/D noise rejection improves as integration time is increased
Readout by means of built-in digital indicator and two-color printer (prints
'alarm values in red) of channel number, data, and time of day i standard. When
'the builtin printer is employed, printer speed (2 to 4 channels per second) limits
'overall system speed, even though scanning without printing may be possible at
15 to 20 channels per second. Readout format is selectable by front-panel pro-
gramming. Some units provide 5-year nonvolatile program memory which pres-
ferves stored programs in case of power failure. Interface options for external
magnetic tape, punched tape. CRT terminals, line printers and computers usually
are obtainable. Eletrical programming (from a remote terminal, controller, or
'computer) which duplicates manual front-panel programming capabilities is pos-
sible als.
13.3 INSTRUMENT INTERCONNECTION SYSTEMS
Whereas the systems of Secs. 13.1 and 132 are intended mainly to coordinat
data gathering from basic sensing devices, in this section we are concerned with
the interconnected operation of instruments such as DVMSs, counters, digital
thermometers, signal generators, etc.--devioes which may themselves include sen-
sors but also are capable of higher-level system functions. Such an approach may
bbe advantageous for a number of reasons. If only S or 10 channels of data are
needed, a data logger with 60-channel capacity may be uneconomical. Perhaps
'many of or all the instruments were purchased for their individual capabilities
'and now we wish to be able to combine them in diferent ways at varidus times to
meet specific needs as they arise. Such interconnected operation has been greatly
facilitated by the almost-universal inclusion of microprocessors in electrical in-
struments for the purpose of "managing" instrument functions. And so instru
rent designers find it economical to build in comprehensive facilities for inter-
'connection even ifthe instrument is marketed mainly asa stand-alone device

-- 942 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 934
910. MANIPULATION, TRANSMISSION, AND RECORDING OF DATA,
'One such system! is based on a "computing printer" which includes a scan-
nner that connects with up to nine external instruments, The builtin micro-
'computer allows programming of many useful functions in addition to straight-
forward seanning and printing of the nine channels. One of the channels may be
chosen to carry out one of four special functions: limits, units, math, oF trac,
Limits isa standard high/low alarm function. Units converts (and annotates the
Printout) to any desired engincering units, Math calculates ether the difference or
Percentage difference between a selected reference value and the input data
Trace produces a graphic plot (rather than a printed table) against time for the
selected channel, with programmable values for the lef/right edges of the graph
and alarm outputs when the graph exceeds either edge. Such graphic output is
Possible because the printer is a thermal dot-matrix type. Six other print modes
'may be selected
Trace interval: graphic plot at programmed time intervals,
Interval: prints at programmed time intervals
Interval/imit: prints at programmed intervals; monitors for alarm when not
printing
Single: prints one reading
Continuous: continuously prints up to three readings per second
Remote: prints continuously when signaled from rear panel
When one of the nine instruments is itself a multichannel device (say, a 10-
'channel digital thermometer), addition of an external scanner to the computing
printer allows the system to scan and log the 10 thermometer channels before
'moving on to the next instrument
Instrument interconnection capability is further enhanced when the individ
ual instruments conform to IEEE-488, the standard digital interface for pro-
_grammable instrumentation, This industry standard, officially introduced in 1975,
has been incorporated into the products of many manufacturers to facilitate the
development of interconnected instrument systems. A number of computer-based
instrument controllers are availabe to take advantage of the IEEE-488 compat
bility now present in many measurement and control devices. One? such unit,
which combines minicomputer processing poser with IEEE-488 interconnection,
provides dual IEFE-488 busses and dual RS-232-C busses (The earlier RS-232:C
EIA (Electronic Industries Association) standard is sill much used for bit-serial
YO devices such as modems and data terminals) Provision of dual busses offers a
'number of advantages. The IEFE-488 standard allows 20 m of eable and 15
instruments per system; the dual bus doubles both these numbers in addition to
Providing redundancy in case of partial system failures. It also allows partitioning
* Computing Priner Mod 203A John Fluke Mig Ci, Moune Terie, Wash Appl
'Ami Jon Fiske Mig. Co
2H, Draye IEEE-188 Controller Promotes Baling of In-House ATE, Blom, pp. 147-182,

[ENGINEERED DATA ACQUISITION AND PROCESSING SYSTENS 91
the total system into "slow" and "fast" groupings to improve overall system
throughput. For example, a digital multimeter might require the controller's
tention only briefly to transmit a single reading. whereas a printer may hold
that attention for long periods while multiple data points are transmitted, Allo-
cating slow components to one bus and fast to another bus means that each can
'operate at an optimum rate.
"To ease programming, the system's minicomputer utilizes a modified version
'of the Basic programming language, as do most such systems. (A speci assembly
language can be obtained when maximum machine speed is necessary) The
'enhancements to standard Basic include a set of IEEE-488 commands for device
'Control and special CRT sequence commands for controlling a video display. The
main read/write memory is 60 kilobytes (24 kilobytes available to user) with a
builtin floppy disk providing 175 kilobytes of mass storage. Optionally, an ad-
ditional 128 oF 256 kilobytes of electronic RAM (transfer rate of 130 kilobytes per
second, 4 times as fast as a floppy) may be installed. Clearly, a system of this type
provides a much higher level of computing power than the "computing printer"
'escribed earlier. Operator/programmer input and output are by means of a
'detachable keyboard and builtin CRT display. A unique feature of the display is
'transparent, Couch-sensitive switch matrix in the form ofa thin plastic overlay
fon the CRT screen, Sixty rectangular areas, each acting as an independent,
touch-sensitive switch, are provided. By writing any desired "key label" and an
enclosing "box") on the CRT behind one of these areas, one ean create a "key:
board" whose labeling can be altered dynamically under program control, guid-
ing the operator through the desired test sequence. With such a device, the
keyboard may be utilized for program development by a skilled programmer
who builds into the program the CRT displays and prompts for operator re-
sponse that are needed to guide the test operator through all the steps. When the
debugged system is handed over to the test operator, the keyboard may be
'detached, if desired, preventing any inadvertent or intentional alteration of the
program. The CRT display/switch now acts as a user-friendly input/output
'device, simplifying the operator' tasks, reducing errors, and increasing testing
speed. Such a system meets the needs of both the skilled programmer and the less
sophisticated test operator.
134 SENSOR-BASED, COMPUTERIZED DATA SYSTEMS
In this section we describe hardware/software which is commercially available at
several levels of "completeness." ranging from single-board computer "front
tends" to stand-alone systems complete with powerful minicomputers and high-
level programming languages. As in any digital data system, the possibility of
aliasing must be carefully considered and suitable antialiasing filters provided if
necessary (see Secs. 10.3 and 10.13)
"The simplest and least expensive devices will require considerable elee-
tronics/computer expertise on the part of the user, access {0 @ microcomputer

-- 944 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 936
912 MANIPULATION, TANSMISION, AND RECORDING OF DATA
development system, and sufficient engineering time to integrate the interface and
computer into a working overall system. Clearly, ready-made complete systems
avoid these difficulties, but the user may pay the price of increased cost and
performance that may not have been optimized forthe specific application, These
'make/buy decisions are not always simple since the initial cost advantage of
assembling a system from "cheap" components may be lost as development time
'extends beyond original estimates, because of unforeseen hardware/softwate dif
culties,
Since microcomputer system design is a specialized area beyond the scope of,
this text, we give only a brief description of microcomputer interface boards, using
Fig 137" as an example. These are available from several manufacturers to
interface with most of the popular microcomputers, To reduce program storage
'equirements and execution times (at the expense of memory address area), often
'memory-mapped 1/0 is employed. Note that analog inputs (up to 32 single-ended
'oF 16 differential) are processed through a multiplexer, programmable-gain
amplifier, sample/hold, and A/D converter (ADC) in very similar fashion to those
'of the data logger of Fig. 13.6. However, since we wish to handle high-frequency
signals, a successive-approximation (rather than dual-slope) A/D converter (max-
imum throughput rate 28 kHz) and an clectronie (rather than reed switch) multi
plexer are necessary. Two (optional) D/A converters are obtainable for driving
analog recorders, generating analog control signals, et. All connections to the
'microcomputer are made simply by plugging both units into the same card cage
'While hardware problems are reduced to a minimum by use of such interface cards,
recall that a major effort will be required to develop the software, since many
'microcomputers do not support convenient high-level languages. Also the overall
system throughput rate must be les (and often much less) than the 28-kHz figure
ven above, since software execution time must be added to the A/D conversion
time
Figure 138? shows a single-board, microcomputer-based data acquisition
system designed to accept multichannel analog and digital inputs and provide
digital output to a host computer (usually a mini or mainframe supporting high-
level languages such as Basic or Fortran) through a standard serial communi-
cations port (RS-232C or 20-mA current loop). The on-board microcomputer
lunburdens the host computer by allowing supervisory control. It performs di
acquisition control, linearization, conversion to engincering unis, limit checking,
interface control, and data output formatting. The analog channels are scanned
'continuously (I5 or 30 per second), and the resultant data are stored in micro
'computer RAM. The data in RAM are refeshed on a continuous basis (the latest
data are Kept in memory), so that requests for data from the host are serviced
immediately. Upon receipt of a transmit command, the microcomputer (via the
ART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver Transmitter] begins transmitting a

For those applications in which a sensor-based measurement and control
system with comprehensive and easy-to-use computer processing is wanted with
4 minimum of user engineering effort, complete stand-alone systems such as that
'of Fig, 13.12! are available. Analog and digital input/output is through plug'
signal conditioning cards; space for 16 is provided (extension chassis allows
'expansion to 256 cards). A wide selection of card functions are available, allowing
casy interfacing with all kinds of sensors and control devices, Single cards often
are themselves multichannel devices with a multiplexer on the card: thus two
levels of computer-controlled multiplexing are present, slot multiplexing (chooses
the card slot desired) and card channel multiplexing (sclets the channel wanted
on the chosen card). For example, a single digital input card provides 16 channels
(bits) while the analog input card has 32 channels (single-ended) or 16 channels
(differential, This analog card has its own programmable-gain amplifier (PGA)
(gain = 1, 16,256) which, combined with the PGA of the central controller
(gain = 1, 2,4 8) allows versatile selection of channel gain under program con-
tol. Thermocouple cards are four-channel units which share a common
Feference-junetion compensation circuit and have fixed gain, Linearization is ac-

'A common requirement of many applications i the ability to perform several
'operations or "tasks." independently of one another, in the same program. Exam-
ples include the monitoring of several analog signals in the laboratory or control
'of several process loops. To provide for this requirement, Macbasic is structured
as a "multitasking" language, and it contains the necessary key words for im-
plementation. Tasks are groups of Macbasic language statements that are defined
asa task, and they are exccuted each time that task is activated unconditionally
for by the satisfaction of a condition (such as an external event) or on a periodic
basis. Up to 18 tasks may be defined ata given time, and if more than one task is.
tive at a given moment, the active {asks will share resources and run simul-
tancously unless priority is assigned to a particular task. Ifa task completes its
'operation, it can "DISMISS" itself and return its resources to the system until
the tas is reactivated
'A simple Macbasic program might go as follows:
1X =AIN&O) Acquire data on channel 0 of analog input caed in 1/0 slot

(CHAPTER

COMPUTER-AIDED EXPERIMENTATION
14.1 INTRODUCTION
Many applications of computer technology (both asa problem-solving design tool
and as built-in system components) have been given in this text. We now want 10
present, in considerable detail, a case study of computer-aided experimentation.
'This technique uses a computer, not just to gather and process data, but also to
actually run partially or totally) the experimental apparatus, and itis finding wide
use in both R&D laboratories and manufacturing facilities. We thus add to the
wellknown acronyms "CAD" (computer-aided design), "CAM" (computer-aided
manufacturing), "CAE" (computer-aided engineering), and "CAI" (computer-aided
instruction) the acronym "CAX." for computer-aided experimentation. Since all
'examples of computer-aided machines and processes share similar basic functions
{interfacing to sensors and actuators, programming logic, data processing and
display, and computer-human communications), our experimentation example is
{good preparation forthe design and implementation of any computer-aided physi-
cal system.
142 FUNCTIONAL DESCRIPTION OF THE SYSTEM
'The overall purpose of our example system is to run speed and torque tests on
rotary pneumatic motors (See Fig. 14.1). Such testing might be of interest in an
R&D program aimed at understanding motor behavior and suggesting or eval-
uating design improvements. Or it could be part of the motor-manulacturing
process, playing a quality-control role in which each motor (or statistically selected
sample) would be tested and compared with some benchmark performance spe
fications. We decide that the motor air supply will come from 100 Ibn? "shop ai
om

-- 955 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 947
Figure 1 Vanetyp rotary ar motor

-- 956 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 948
924 MANIPULATION, TRANSMISSION, AND RECORDING OF DATA
through a manually set filter/tegulatorlubricator with a pressure-fow capability
'adequate for out motors. Note that in "automating" any experiment or test, there
is a complete spectrum of degrees of automation to choose from, ranging from
'mainly manual systems with only minor computer assistance to the human opera
tor, to completely automatic systems which require no human action a all Choice
'of the optimum level of automation depends heavily on detailed technical and
economic factors and, asin all realistic design studies, requites considerable engi
neering experience and judgment. In our example we have chosen to make the
selection of motor supply pressure a manual (rather than computerized) function
'The reader may find it useful to make a list of pros and cons for manual, as com-
pared with computerized, air-pressure selection in this example, to appreciate the
type of reasoning involved in making such a choice,
With our supply-pressure regulator we can manually set the motor inlet pres-
sure as we desire, We now need some sort of adjustable mechanical load for the
'motor to work against. Since we want to produce torque/speed curves for steady-
state operation at a fixed supply pressure, this load will be set at several (sty 10)
different levels with the speed and torque measured at each. We decide that
computerized load setting can be justified here (why?) and we ty to find a suitable
Joading device. Let's assume the computer will have D/A converters (DACS), so
that computer commands to the loading device will be analog voltages of + 10-V
range. Of the many available loading devices, we choose a pneumatically actuated
friction brake which produces our desired range of torques for a 3to 1S Ib/in® gage
air-pressure range. We then need an electtopneumatic transducer (EPT) to convert
the computer' voltage commands to air pressure.
143 FUNCTIONAL DESCRIPTION OF THE,
COMPUTER SYSTEM
'The air-motor test system being described was actualy constructed for student
use in a measurements course required of all mechanical engineering seniors at
'The Ohio State University (OSU), General-purpose data acquisition and control
'computer systems ofthe type described in Fig. 13.12 were available, and they are
'good choice for the one-of-a-kind application at hand. Very little "computer
engineering" expertise is required, so a working system can be quickly realized by
'engineers who are familiar with the test equipment but who are not computer
specialists. When purchasing such a system, due to its modular nature, one can
configure it for modest or extensive capabilities. A simple system can also be
upgraded later by adding the desired modules. The systems? available at OSU
were originally purchased with a fairly versatile complement of modules since the
systems were intended to be used for data acquisition and control with many
diferent sets of apparatus for teaching and research purposes. Figure 14.2 details
"EO. Doebelin "Conta System Principe and Desi" p. 262, Wik, New York.

the system hardware, Of equal importance to rapid test-system development is the
software. In the Macsym systems the hardware and software were developed 3s a
unit and specifically optimized for data acquisition and control purposes. A high-
level language called Macbasic measurement and control Basic), developed speci-
fically for this purpose, allows rapid programming. Iti a high-speed version of
Basic with additional commands to easily perform the desired measurement and
conteol functions, Since many students already know Basic even from high school,
Macbasic comes quickly to most enginects.
Capability quite similar to that of the Macsym systems can be obtained by
'combining one of the popular personal computers with add-on data acquisition
boards ete available from a number of vendors. Special measurement and control
software is also available, and since personal computers are already in such wide
tse for engineering calculations this isa popular approach,
144 SENSORS
For speed sensing it was decided to use a tachometer encoder with 1S pulses/r
and a software counting timing scheme to calculate the revolutions per minute by
timing 200 pulses. At maximum motor speed of 1200 r/min, 200 pulses take about

-- 958 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 950
(0.67 s. The Macsym clock used here has a 2s resolution, so worst-case stop/start.
timing error of ys is possible, but a more probable error would be about.» sof
1 percent.
'To measure motor torque (maximum value 40 in Ib), we use a "homemade"
clastic torque sensor. A torque arm, bolted tothe fiction brake's bearing housing,
'acts the friction torque into the sensor spring (a cantilever beam), causing about
1 I-in deflection for the full-scale design torque of 45 in: Iby. An adjustable ait
damper attached to the other end of the torque arm suppresses torsional oscilla.
tions. To obtain a de voltage related to torque, the spring deflection is transduced
to potentiometer shaft rotation by using a slding-pin linkage, with I-in deflec-
tion giving about 90 deg of shaft rotation. The potentiometer is a conductive plastic,
1000-2, single-turn,1-W unit. The accuracy ofthis torque sensor willbe determined
by a computer-aided deadweight calibration which is part ofthe overall computer
rogram.

'mounted in graphite-bronze self-lubricating bearings so as to take both radial and

undergoes only small rotational motions as the motor torque causes clastic deflec-
