so that the motor, brake, and tachometer all rotate together.
'ince our system is a rather simple one, we utilize only fraction of the inter-
facing facilities available and shown in Fig. 142. The 12-bit DACS are £10-V
full-scale devices: so when we purchased our EPT, we tried to make it compatible
Fortunately a unit was found which gives a 3 to 15 Ibn? gage pressure range for
4 1- to 9-V input signal. Since 12-bit resolution is 1 part in 4096, the 3to 1S Ijin?
'gage pressure range will be resolved into 1688 parts, which is more than adequate
for this application. Since the EPT has @ 2500-0 input resistance, it will draw
3.6mA of current from the DAC at 9 V.(The DACs are rated at 20 mA maximum,
'but tests showed ths rating to be very conservative; earlier applications had drawn
fas much as 42 mA with no ill effects) Figure 14.5 shows this wiring. OF the six
'available slots in the computer, the analog output card (which includes four DACs,
channels 0, 1,2, 3) occupies the slot numbered 2. In the computer program, these
Slot and channel numbers must be used to properly route the signals,

'The tachometer encoder requires +5-V de excitation, while the potentiometer
'on the torque sensor could use any voltage up to its 35-V heating limit. To reduce
'cost and complexity, we decide to use the same 5-V de supply for both potenti-
'ometer and encoder. The maximum calibration torque of 4S in Ib gave about a
1-V change of potentiometer output in preliminary tests. The A/D converter(ADC)
card has a gain-slectable preamplifier (1,8, 64, 128 V/V) to allow the ADC to
'work near its design range of #10 V irrespective ofthe voltage applied to the card
input terminals. For a maximum potentiometer voltage change of 1 V, a gain of
8 V/V is the best choice. [In the Macsym card being used, gain setting requires
removal of the card and manual setting of some jumpers and switches. At addi-
tional cost, one can get cards in which the gain is computer-settabe from the
'rogram ("programmable-gain ampliir")]
'When potentiometers are used for motion measurement, we often add an inex-
pensive "trim pot" potentiometer to ereate a potentiometer bridge, which allows
'one to make the output voltage zero for any postion ofthe measuring pot's wiper.
We do this here (see Fig. 14.5) so that we can place our 1-V signal range at 0 to
1V rather than at, say, 4 t0 5 V, which could occur with S-V pot excitation ifthe
90" rotation happened to occur atthe high end of the potentiometer' total travel

-- 961 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 953
'COMPUTER-AIDED EXPERIMENTATION 929)
For a 0- to 1-V range we can use the 8 V/V preamplifier gain in the 12-bit ADC
'and resolve our signal span into (4096)0.540.8) = 1640 parts. The analog input
'card can be configured to provide either 32 channels of single-ended or 16 channels
fof more noise-olerant differential inputs. Since we need less than 16 channels in
four simple system, the differential mode is certainly acceptable and, infact, turns
'out to be necessary when we next look into the tachometer interfacing.
To count the tachometer pulses for use in our software speed-measuring
scheme, we connect the tachometer output to channel 9 of the computer's digital
VO card, installed in card slot 3. This card has 16 channels, cach of which can
bee set to either accept a logic (on/off) input or provide a logic output, by using
'one of various plug-in modules to meet the needs of the application, Since the
tachometer produces a square wave which switches between O and 5 V, we plug
jn a 0- to 16-V de input module and set jumpers to make channel 9 (we could
tse any channel) an input. When we write our program, the logic variable called
DIN:043, 9) will be 0 when the tachometer signal is "low" (near 0 V) and 1 when
itis "high" (near 5 V). Since we are sharing the 5-V de power supply between the
tachometer and the torque-sensor pot, and because the low sides ofthe tachometer'
excitation and output are common and necessarily connected to logic ground at
ital 1/0 eard, the low side ofthe power supply must be connected to signal
'ground (through 100-K0 resistor) at the analog input card. This means neither
'of the pot wipers may be connected to ground, so we must use differential input
(as mentioned earlier), connecting one wiper to high and one to low. We have now
'explained all the interfacing between external devices and the computer
146 OVERALL PLAN OF THE TEST SEQUENCE,
AND COMPUTER PROGRAM
Before commencing the detailed programming, we must decide exactly what func-
tions are to be performed. Suppose that our needs would be met by the following
sequence:
1. Use CRT screen messages fo guide the operator in preparing the apparatus for
use (connect wiring, turn on power, etc)
2. Guide the operator through a deadweight calibration of the torque sensor
3, Tabulate and plot the calibration data. Also store them in computer memory
{or later use in motor testing.
4, Set up air-motor test conditions.
5. Rum a speed torque test with 10 diferent torque values and fixed motor-supply-
pressure setting.
6, Tabulate and plot the speed/torque test data
7. Guide the operator through apparatus-shutdown procedures.
Figure 146 shows this plan as a flowchart

-- 962 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 954
930, MANIPULATION, TRANSMISSION, AND RECORDING OF DATA
|
sup
Umer |_| rinweednonve
<=
igre 146 Experiment flowchart.
We now need to fill in some details ofthis general plan. The fist part of the
program is devoted to some remarks which describe the purpose of the entire
'computer-aided apparatus. Next we enter an "interactive" section where the com=
puter screen prints, one at atime, a soquence of instructions which the operator
'must follow to prepare the apparatus for testing. When the operator completes each
step, pessing CARRIAGE RETURN brings the next steps instructions onto the
sereen. These steps involve "wiring and plumbing," which establish the needed elec-
'tie and pneumatic connections between apparatus, computer, and power supplies.
Next the computer leads the operator through the calibration of the torque
sensor with deadweights, This calibration is done with the brake released but with
the motor running ata speed approximately in the middle of the speed range to be
used in motor testing This "dynamic" calibration is felt to be more representative

-- 963 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 955
COMPUTER-AIDED EXPERIMENTATION 931
of the actual motor test conditions than would a "static? calibration performed
'with the motor stopped. Air-motor specifications show that the motor a the max
'mum desited pressure, is capable of about 40 in I of maximum torque, so the
calibration is designed to slightly exceed this range. A convenient length of the
calibration weight lever arm was chosen as 7:50 in, and six L-Ib weights applied in
increasing and deereasing fashion give a total of 14 calibration points. Since the
calibration arm rotates slightly with added weight, the lever arm varies from the
7S0:in radius; however, calculations showed this effect was negligible. (If it were
not, we could easily include a geometric correction in the computer program.)
'The tachometer encoder speed sensor is an "absolute" device and thus does
not itself require calibration against a speed standard. That is, it produces exactly
1S electric pulses/, and this number isnot affected by temperature, aging, or other
variables, Our "software method" of counting and timing pulses to derive the shalt
Speed is, however, nor perfectly accurate, as will be apparent when we discuss it
later; so the orerall speed-measuring system could be calibrated. Because this
possible speed error was shown to be negligibly small (by preliminary experiments
And calculations) and its stability with time acceptable, we chose to accept the
{small risk associated with nor performing a speed calibration as part of the motor
test program,
'Once the torque calibration has been carried out, tabulated, and plotted, we
are ready to start actual motor testing. Our goal is to obtain speed and torque
'curves for fixed settings of the motor pressure regulator covering a speed range
from about 200 to a maximum of 1050 r/min, Since we are concerned about pos-
sible dangerous overspeeding of the motors, we prefer to start each test at about
1000 r/min annd then let the computer increase the load to obtain progressively
lower speeds, The procedure will be to set the frction-brake air pressure at &
selected value by sending a selected voltage from the computer's DAC to the EPT.
(Some preliminary experimentation was used here in order to estimate frition-
torque air-pressure relations, numbers not amenable to accurate theoretical predic~
tion) A message on the sereen now instructs the operator to gradually increase
the motor supply pressure (manually, with the pressure regulator) until the motor
speed is between 950 and 1050 r/min. As soon as the motor pressure develops
{enough torque to overcome the brake friction initially set by the computer, the
'motor stars to rotate and the speed-measuring algorithm starts to report motor
speed on the CRT screen and on the printer. When a speed between 950 and
1080 r/min is reported, the operator stops manually changing the motor pressure,
and the CRT also displays a message that automatic testing ean now commence
'with operator input of @ CARRIAGE RETURN.
'Automatic testing consist ofthe computers increasing the brake torque above
the inital set value in 10 steps. After each step of torque increase, the program
waits 10 s for transients to die out, measures torque and speed, and then goes on
to the next step. The steps are of equal-size voltage from the DAC (and since the
EPT is quite linear, brake pressure control steps will also be of equal size), but
because the friction torque is not uniquely and precisely related to the brake air
pressure, the steps of torque are not exactly of equal size. Ths is no real problem,

-- 964 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 956
9382 MANIPULATION, TRANSMISSION, AND RECORDING OF DATA,
however, since our torque sensor measures the actual torque, whatever it might
be. The largest torque (last ofthe 10 steps) reduces the motor speed to a low value
but does not normaly stall the motor. When the last of the 10 speed and torque
readings has been taken, the brake pressure is maintained at this (largest) value
and a message on the screen tells the operator that testing is complete and that
4 CARRIAGE RETURN should be entered to initiate a motor-stall sequence in
the computer. Here, the brake pressure is "ramped" (o its maximum value (about
15 1b/in® page), which gives a brake torque larger than the motor can overcome,
stalling the motor. With the motor stalled, the operator gets a message on the
screen requesting a "manual" reading of the motor supply-pressure gage and @
typing in of this reading so that the computer will know the pressure at which
the test was run, Another screen message then requests the operator to reduce the
'motor supply pressure to zero and then enter a CARRIAGE RETURN. Further
screen messages prompt the operator to request tabulation, screen plotting. and
plotter plotting of the test data. Finally, the operator is led through the system-
shutdown procedure.

'Torque sensor calibration, completed prior to motor speed torque sensing pro-
'duces a set of data which might typically plot asin Fig. 148. The nonlinearity of

readily availabe for a wide variety of curve shapes, and the simple, smooth shape
in Fig. 148 probably could be well fitted with a quadratic or cubic polynomial
'A preliminary study showed this to be true but also revealed that an even simpler
'method gave sufficiently accurate results. In Fig. 149 we choose our calibration
eurve" as the set of straight-line segments defined there. These line segments are
easily calculated from the calibration data and easily used to convert measured
voltages to torques, as we shall see from program details late.

148 PROGRAMMING DETAILS.
We are now ready to show (Fig. 14.10) and discuss the complete program for this
application. While most readers will no know the Macbasi language, iis fairly
'simple language and close enough to ordinary Basic that only occasional explana
tory remarks will allow you to follow the program flow. If your programming
experience has not included computer-aided machine or process examples, this
application, though relatively simple, should give you a good idea of the general
Drinciples and procedures involved.

uses INPUT to cause a pause in the program, to allow the operator to carry out
that instruction and then hit RETURN to go on to the next step. Blank lines,

'Torque Sensor Calibration, Lines 410 to 870
We need to produce 14-clement arrays for torque values CALTRQ(N) and cot
responding potentiometer output voltages CALVLT(N) for the deadweight cal
bration. Line 450 dimensions these arrays, while lines 460 t0 510 caleulate the
torque values from the known lever arm (7.30 in) and known weights of Oto 6 bt
for both increasing and deereasing sequences of force application. The brake must
be released during calibration; line S40 docs this by setting the EPT input voltage
at 00 V, corresponding to about 1 Ibjin® gage brake pressure, which was found
insufficient to engage the friction surfaces.
'We wish to calibrate the torque sensor with the motor running, since the
calibration will be used (to convert measured voltages to torques) under similar
conditions. Also the sensor has less hysteresis because motor vibration reduces
'atic friction. The torque sensor will be used at motor speeds from about 100 to
1000 r/min, so we need to decide which speed should be used for calibration. Ac.
tually, the torque calibration might vary with speed, due to specd-sensitive dis-
turbing torques such as air drag, brake ball-bearing friction, etc. Preliminary studies
'on such effets showed that adequate accuracy was achieved by running the torque
calibration at a single speed near the midrange and that this corresponded to a
'motor supply pressure of 10 Ib/in* gage. Lines 580 and 560 instruct the operator
to perform this setting. The deadweights are applied, fist in increasing and then
in decreasing fashion, in lines $80 to 870. To get the data point at zero torque
(increasing), lines $80 to 590 instruct the operator, line 600 leaves a blank line
between successive screen messages, and line 610 reads the potentiometer output
voltage and places that reading in the array element CALVLT(1). Lines 620 and
{630 repeat this procedure for the 7.S-in Ibf (increasing) data point, fines 640 and
{650 do it for the 150i Ibf point (increasing), ete. To get the 45.0-in-Ibf point
(decreasing) data, we use lines 740 to 750, The rest ofthe procedure is clea from
the program statements.

is used for this, but this statement could come anywhere in the program. as long
fas it precedes actual use of the printer. "Printing" on the CRT does not require
'opening a software channel and uses the statement PRINT. "Printer printing"
tuses the statement PRINT:2, We first lay out our table for CRT display by using.
PRINT. To duplicate this on the printer, we simply repeat everything but use
PRINT:2. Line 900 sereen-prints a table ttle, line 910 leaves a blank line, lines
'920 and 930 print column headings, line 940 leaves another blank line, and lines

horizontal spacing of the printed material on the CRT screen is the same as in
program statements 900, 920,930, and 960. That is, the heading CALIBRATION

to left ofthe decimal point, and three digits to right ofthe decimal point. Lines

Preparation for graphing on the CRT the calibration data just tabulated begins

keyboard input from the operator. Whereas the general area of computer graphics
hhas developed capabilities of extreme sophistication, the needs of many data ac-
{uisition and control applications are met with relatively modest tools such as
basie coordinate graphs, bar charts, etc. In our present case we only want graphs
ofthe calibration and motor speed torque data, which are easily programmed in
"Macbasic. Line 1070 sets up some sereen graphing parameters which are common
to almost every sereen graphics example; this line will not vary from one example
to another. The WINDOW statement in line 1080, however, must be tailored t0
cach example. In our case the span (10, 63) ofthe x-axis window accommodates
the torque range of Oto 45 in Ibf with "a litte to spare" on each end for margins,
legends, ete. Choice of the WINDOW numbers requires same trial and error when
the program is being writen to get the spacing and appearance desired by the
programmer. Similarly, the y window (0.33, 1.7) accommodates the torque-sensor
voltage range of to 1 V. The rest ofthe CRT plotting programis put in asubroutine
that is called in line 1090. This subroutine could also have included lines 1070
and 1080, of We could have used no subroutine at all. The method used was just
4 personal choice of the programmer.
Picking up the subroutine at line 1980, we see that "standard methods" of
Macbasic are used to plot coordinate axes, tick marks, legends, and numerical

scales (lines 2000 to 2150). In producing this "framework" for the actual graph,
the same list of statements would be used for every example, but numerical values,
'names, ete. would, of course, be different. Next we plot the voltage/torque calibra
tion data (lines 2160 to 2350), using a solid line (LTYPE 9) for the increasing
orque values and a dashed line (LTYPE 3) for the decreasing values. Since the
solid line i also the default value, it need not be called before the increasing values

halted and the computer brought to the READY state by the simultaneous key-

restart it from its first line (which is what we want here), by entering RUN. Line

In Macbasi, a successful screen graphic can be converted to plotter graphic
with minimal effort. In line 2900, the GRAPHICS S and PAGE 5 statements are

ter size and COLOR 1 selects one of the four available pen colors. Note that

raph. The rest of the subroutine is essentially the same as that used for CRT
graphing, and we return to the main program at line 1120 from the subroutine

Speed/Torque Testing, Lines 1120 to 1480
Following the plan of Sec. 146, in line 1120 we set an initial brake torque by
sending a 4.5-V signal to the EPT. At this time the motor is still running at cal
bration conditions; however, the 4.5-V signal causes a brake torque suficient 10
Stall the motor. This happens a little before the plotter completes the calibration
_aphing because the plotters input buffer memory will accept the last few lines
'of the plotting subroutine and free the computer to continue the program, while
the "slow" plotter actually fnishes carrying out these last few "moves." Lines 1130
'and 1140 thus signal the operator via a sereen message to wait for plot completion
'and then load new paper, in preparation for later speed and torque plotting. Line
1150 then clears the CRT screen for further operator instructions in lines 1160 to
1190, which call for the operator to manually increase the motor supply pressure
until a speed between 950 and 1050 r/min is achieved, helping the stalled motor
to get started at about 40 Ibjin? gage, if necessary. Line 1200 calls « subroutine
Which measures the motor speed as outlined earlicr.

-- 975 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 967
COMPUTER-AIDED FXPEUNENTATION 943
'This subroutine starts (Line 2740) by sampling the digital 1/0 card at channel
'9 where the encoder output is connected. Recall that this voltage is a square-wave
signal between 0 and +5 V with 15 pulses. We wish to measure the time taken
for 200 pulses, so that we can calculate the average motor speed (over this time
interval) in revolutions per minute. The choice of 200 pulses is a tradeoff between
Target values, which give greater timing accuracy, and smaller values, which obtain
fan rpm reading more quickly and thus more nearly time-synchronized with the
torque reading. The ds resolution of the Macsym clock statement TIMER places
'4 basi limit on our timing accuracy. For example, a 1-8 interval can be in error
by as much as 1.7 percent if we assume that we "miss" the interval by "one clock
tick." Since our rpm subroutine always times 200 pulses, the worst timing accuracy
'occurs at the highest rpm value, since the 200 pulses then occur in the shortest
time, If we take the highest value as 1000 r/min, then 200 pulses will occur in O8 s,
while at 200 r/min it takes 40's. Estimation of speed-measurement accuracy in-
volves the following factors:
1. We really want simultaneous measurement of speed and torque, but this is im-
possible with any pulse-counting scheme.
2. Timing error occurs at both clock starting and clock stopping.
3, Our TIMER statement ("clock") has as resolution.
'4 Statements in the speed-measuring subroutine have finite execution times
'Numerical caleulation of the speed-measurement error is explored in the end-of-
'chapter problems.
Returning to the details of the speed-measurement subroutine, line 2740
'samples the tachometer encoder voltage. If its high (5 V), then DIN:0(3, 9) is on
(logic 1) and we go to line 2760, IFt is low (0 V), then DIN:0(3, 9 is off (logic 0)
land we go to line 2750, which returns us to line 2740 where we again check 10
'See ifthe voltage is high or low. The program thus cannot get beyond line 2750
luni the tachometer voltage is high. Once this happens (initially, or after looping
through lines 2749 and 2750 a few times), we go to line 2760 where we check
whether the voltage is going low. The purpose of lines 2740 to 2780 is to detect
transition a +b in Fig. 14.7 and to start the clock (set TIMER = 0) for timing
the 200 pulses. With TIMER started, ines 2790 and 2800 detect the transition
¢-+ d, and line 2810 inerements the pulse count (which started at zeto in line
2780) by 4, while lines 2820 and 2830 detect e -> f and line 2840 sets CT equal 10
1.0 since one cycle of the tachometer waveform (15 eycles/) is now complete. Line
2850 checks for completion of the 200 cycles used to measure speed, whi line
2860 creates & loop to continue the counting until this is achieved. Then line 28
'computes the average rpm value by reading the value of TIMER "immediately"
after counting the 200th cycle, and line 2880 returns us to the main program at
line 1210. For a pulse-counting scheme like this one to work, the repetition rate
'of loops like lines 2820 and 2830 must be somewhat faster than that ofthe pulses
to be counted (263 per second at 1050 r/min). By using an electronic square-wave
oscillator to simulate the tachometer input, preliminary experiments showed our

-- 976 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 968
Scheme to be accurate to about 400 Hz, which is more than adequate forthe fastest
value
We are now back in the main program at line 1210 where we print (to both
the CRT and the printer) the current rpm value of the motor. To give the operator
'some warning of overspeed operation (chosen conservatively at 1200 r/min) line
1220 prints an alarm message while lines 1230 and 1250 sound a buzzer 3 times
[PRINT CHRS(7)], which is part of the Macsym computer. For normal speeds,
line 1240 skips the alarm. If the speed is outside the desired range of 950 10
1050 r/min, line 1260 sends us back to line 1190, where the operator is reminded
by a sereen message that the motor speed needs adjustment. This loop cycles until
the speed is between 950 and 1050 r/min, whereupon lines 1270 and 1280 stop the
loop and display the message that data taking can now commence.
To prepare for taking the speed and torque data, line 1290 dimensions the
arrays MESRPM (measured rpm values) and MESVLT (measured voltages from
the torque sensor) to accept 10 values each. These arrays are both filled by the
FOR loop in lines 1300 to 1370. Line 1310 sets the brake torque at 10 stepwise
increasing values by applying, successively, voltages ranging from 4.62 to 5.70 V
to the eleetropneumatic transducer. We want the maximum torque in this sequence
to give a motor speed of about 200 r/min, the lowest speed of practical interest.
Because of the unpredictability of friction, the brake air pressure (and thus EPT
voltage) needed to give this speed must be found by preliminary experiments. This
is how the maximum voltage of 5.70 V was determined, [Note that, withthe pro-
gram as presently written, unanticipated increases in the friction coeflcient su
ficient to stall the motor for voltages of 5.70 V or less will cause the program 10
fail lock up") since the speed-measuring routine will "wait forever," trying to
'count 200 pulses. After setting the brake pressure in line 1310, we wait 10 (ine
1320) for transients to die out and then measure torque in line 1330 and speed in
lines 1340 and 1350 with an overspeed alarm "beeper" in line 1360.
'The motor supply pressure was manually set in lines 1160 to 1270 to achieve
'desired top speed between 950 and 1050 s/n, but we did not record this pres
sure at that time. Preliminary experiments revealed that, even though we use a
regulator to control the pressure and a gage snubber to minimize pressure-gage
oscillations, this pressure reading varies somewhat with motor speed and fuc-
tuates ata given speed. Since we wish to associate each set of 10 speed and torque
readings with a single representative motor pressure, we must decide how this
pressure will be defined and measured, We concluded that @ useful value would
'be obtained if we stalled the motor and then read the resulting steady pressure.
Lines 1380 to 140 gradually (0 prevent shock loads) increase the brake voltage
(pressure) t0 9 V, sufficient to stall the motor, whereupon lines 1450 to 1470 re-
'quest manual reading of the pressure, assignment of this value to the program
variable PMOT, and printing of this numerical value.
Since all the data have now been collected, line 1480 requests manual shut-
'off of the motor pressure while line 1500 initiates processing of the data. Earlier
(Fig. 149) we decided to use straight-line segments (rather than the more conven-
tional polynomial curve) to convert measured torque-sensor voltages to actual
torque values, based on the deadweight calibration. Inthe program, the endpoints

149 CONCLUSION
"Though relatively short and simple, our example of computer-aided experimenta
tion includes most of the features common to more complex applications and
'should provide useful guidelines forthe reader who needs to develop such systems
for use in R&D laboratories or manufacturing facilities. A common advantage of

-- 978 of 1000 --

Original PDF Page 970
946 MANIPULATION, RANSON, AND RECORDING OF DATA
such computer-based systems is their flexibility and adaptability to changing situ-
ations and requirements, a valuable feature as industry attempts to meet customer
'needs with shorter lead times and smaller production runs of more customized
Products. Much of this flexibility, of course, derives from the replacement of ha
Ware solutions by software solutions, wherever feasible, in designing the system's
basic functions. Whenever this is possible, future changes can be accommodated
rapidly and cheaply by program modifications rather than requiring the purchase,
installation, and debugging of new hardware. Such advantages accrue in even
simple systems such as that of our example, Since the student laboratory in which
these dynamometers are used employs a three-group format, four complete units
were built (one for backup in case of failure. Although the four commercial fric-
tion brakes purchased were all the same model, the nature of friction is such that
'each unit has a somewhat different numerical relation between air pressure and
friction torque. This normal sample-to-sample variation is easily accommodated
by software changes in ines 1120 and 1310, where the numbers 4 and/or 0.12
are changed to get the desired performance in each dynamometer.
'While the computer program given in our example well serves the educational
'goals of the laboratory experiment, itis, ofcourse, not the only possible, or neces-
sarily the functionally best possible, program. The end-of-chapter problems eX
plore some possible modifications and/or improvements in software, using the
existing hardware. We also investigate increasing the degree of automation by
adding hardware to perform functions presently accomplished manually. These
hardware changes naturally require associated reprogramming, so system redesign,
'can become quite extensive. While most readers will not know the Macbasic
language, a careful study of our example program and the associated explanation
in text should provide adequate familiarity to allow a useful solution to the prob
Jems. If you need a certain programming function which did not appeat in our
'example, if itis available in the version of Basic familiar to you, fel free to use
it I probably is available in Macbasic.
